```
+---v-+-v-+-v----+
|  N1 | N2 | N3 |  |  <-- Compute Nodes (MPI workers)
+---+-+---+-+----+
    |   |   |
+---v---v---v----+
| Shared Storage |  <-- Lustre / NFS / SAN
+----------------+
```

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $P = 0.90$ (90% parallel), $N = 16$ nodes.

**Step 1 — Apply Amdahl's Formula (4 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.90) + \dfrac{0.90}{16}} \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.05625} \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{0.15625} \\[8pt]
&= 6.4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Theoretical Speedup (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{1 - P} \\[8pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.90} = \dfrac{1}{0.10} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Critical Analysis (1 mark):**

At N = 16, the cluster achieves **6.4x speedup**, which is 64% of the theoretical maximum (10x). Adding more nodes will yield diminishing returns. For example, at N = 100, the speedup is:

$$S = \dfrac{1}{0.10 + 0.009} \approx 9.17$$

So beyond 16 nodes, the marginal gain per added node becomes negligible. **Conclusion:** Diminishing returns make further scaling economically unjustified unless the serial fraction is reduced.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Discuss the **types of clusters** (HPC, HA, Load Balancing) with suitable real-world examples. Compare clusters with **grid computing**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-node HA cluster has each node with availability of 95%. Calculate the **cluster availability** and the **MTTR** if the average downtime per failure is 30 minutes. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Types of Clusters (4 marks):**

1. **High-Performance Computing (HPC) Cluster** — Designed for computationally intensive scientific workloads. Uses MPI, low-latency interconnects. Example: **NASA's Pleiades supercomputer**, **Boeing CFD cluster**.

2. **High-Availability (HA) Cluster** — Designed for continuous service uptime. Uses heartbeat and failover mechanisms. Example: **Pacemaker + Corosync** for database servers in banking.

3. **Load-Balancing Cluster** — Distributes user requests evenly. Example: **HAProxy front-ending a fleet of NGINX web servers** for e-commerce.

**Cluster vs Grid Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Cluster | Grid |
|---|---|---|
| Geography | Single location (LAN) | Geographically distributed (WAN) |
| Resources | Homogeneous nodes | Heterogeneous resources |
| Coupling | Tightly coupled, single owner | Loosely coupled, multi-owner |
| Network | High-speed LAN | Public Internet |
| Job Allocation | Centralized scheduler | Decentralized (Globus) |
| Example | Beowulf, Rocks Cluster | SETI@home, EGEE |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** N = 3 nodes, $A_i = 0.95$, downtime = 30 minutes per failure.

**Step 1 — Node Unavailability (1 mark):**

$$P_{down} = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$$

**Step 2 — Cluster Failure Probability (All Nodes Down) (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{all\_down} &= (0.05)^3 = 0.000125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cluster Availability (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{cluster} &= 1 - 0.000125 = 0.999875
\end{aligned}
$$

This equates to **99.9875% uptime**.

**Step 4 — MTTR (1 mark):**

Given directly as **MTTR = 30 minutes = 1800 seconds**.

**Conclusion:** The HA cluster achieves the "five nines" threshold (99.999%) requires even higher individual node availability or more redundancy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the Single System Image (SSI)** when defining clusters — losing 1 mark easily.
> 2. **Confusing tightly coupled with loosely coupled** definitions — pay attention to "shared memory" vs "message passing."
> 3. **Skipping the unit analysis** in Amdahl's law numericals — always state that P is a fraction (0.90, not 90).
> 4. **Not showing the $\lim_{N \to \infty}$ step** in speedup derivations — examiners specifically look for the asymptotic ceiling.
> 5. **Confusing MTTR with MTBF** — MTTR is repair time; MTBF is uptime between failures. Different metrics.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cluster = Multiple nodes + LAN interconnect + Middleware = Single System Image (SSI).**
- **Three core cluster types** to remember: **HPC** (speed), **HA** (uptime), **Load Balancing** (distribution).
- **Beowulf cluster** is the canonical example — built from commodity Linux machines, designed by NASA (1994).
- **Amdahl's Law** is the most-tested formula: $$S = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$ — speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1-P}$.
- **MPI and PVM** are the standard parallel programming libraries for cluster nodes.
- **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** is eliminated through N+1 or N+M redundancy.
- **HA Cluster availability** = $1 - (1 - A_i)^N$ — N redundant nodes boost uptime exponentially.
- **Cluster ≠ Grid** — clusters are local, homogeneous, single-owner; grids are global, heterogeneous, multi-owner.
- **Key middleware products**: MOSIX, OpenSSI, Pacemaker, Corosync, SLURM, Torque, Kubernetes.
- **Interconnect technologies**: Gigabit Ethernet, 10G/40G/100G Ethernet, InfiniBand (lowest latency), Myrinet.
- **Modern relevance**: Cluster computing is the foundational substrate of cloud computing (EC2, Azure VMs are virtualized cluster nodes).
- **Real-world scale**: Top 500 supercomputers (TOP500 list) are all clusters; the leader (Frontier) exceeds 1 ExaFLOPS.
- **Failure to mention checkpointing, process migration, or load balancing** in long answers often results in 1–2 mark deductions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distributed Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Distributed Computing # MODULE 1: DISTRIBUTED COMPUTING ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Distributed Computing** is a computing paradigm in which a system is composed of multiple autonomous computational entities (nodes/computers) interconnected through a network, communicating and coordinating their actions by passing messages to achieve a common goal, while appearing to users as a single coherent system. According to **Andrew S. Tanenbaum** (a foundational reference for distributed systems), a distributed system is: > *"A collection of independent computers that appears to its users as a single coherent system."* In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, distributed computing is positioned as the **conceptual and architectural foundation** for Cloud Computing. It addresses the principles of resource sharing, concurrency, scalability, fault tolerance, and transparency that underpin modern cloud platforms. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **🍕 The Pizza Restaurant Kitchen Analogy** Imagine a busy pizza restaurant. A **single chef** (single computer) preparing all orders will eventually become a bottleneck — customers will wait longer as demand grows. Now imagine **multiple chefs**, each with a specific role: - One prepares the dough - One adds toppings - One bakes the pizzas - One handles delivery packaging They all **work together in parallel**, **communicate** ("Order #5 ready for oven!"), and the **customer sees only the final result** — a single pizza arriving at their table. This is **distributed computing**: - **Multiple independent workers** (nodes) - **Connected via a shared communication channel** (network) - **Appears as one unified entity** to the outside world (transparency) ### 1.3 Defining Characteristics > [!NOTE] > **Key Properties of Distributed Systems (KTU 2024 High-Yield)** | # | Characteristic | Description | |---|---------------|-------------| | 1 | **Resource Sharing** | Hardware, software, and data are shared across nodes | | 2 | **Concurrency** | Multiple operations execute simultaneously on different nodes | | 3 | **Openness** | Systems are extensible via standardized protocols and interfaces | | 4 | **Scalability** | System can grow in size, geography, or administration without performance degradation | | 5 | **Fault Tolerance** | System continues to operate despite partial component failures | | 6 | **Transparency** | Distribution is hidden from users (access, location, failure, migration, replication transparency) | ### 1.4 Types of Transparency (KTU Frequently Tested) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 8 Forms of Transparency (ISO Reference Model)** > > 1. **Access Transparency** – Uniform interface to local/remote resources > 2. **Location Transparency** – Users need not know the physical location > 3. **Concurrency Transparency** – Multiple users can access shared resources without interference > 4. **Replication Transparency** – Hidden existence of multiple copies > 5. **Failure Transparency** – System recovers from faults without user awareness > 6. **Migration Transparency** – Resources may move without renaming > 7. **Performance Transparency** – System reconfigures for performance > 8. **Scaling Transparency** – System can expand without restructuring ### 1.5 Standard Metrics in Distributed Computing > [!NOTE] > **Critical Quantitative Measures** (Bold = High-Yield for KTU) > > - **Throughput** – Number of tasks completed per unit time (**tasks/sec**) > - **Latency** – Time to complete a single operation (**ms or μs**) > - **Scalability Factor (N)** – Number of nodes added > - **Reliability (R)** – Probability system performs correctly over time period *t* > - **Availability (A)** – Fraction of time system is operational > - **Speedup (S)** – Ratio of sequential to parallel execution time > - **Efficiency (E)** – Speedup divided by number of processors # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Goals of Distributed Computing Distributed systems are engineered to achieve **four primary engineering goals** (a classic KTU framing): ### 🎯 Goal 1: Connecting Users and Resources - Enable resource sharing across organizational and geographical boundaries - Reduce cost through shared infrastructure - Example: A university sharing compute resources across departments ### 🎯 Goal 2: Transparency The system should *hide* its distributed nature. A user calling a remote service should feel they are calling a local one. ### 🎯 Goal 3: Openness - Services are offered through **standardized protocols** (e.g., HTTP, gRPC, RMI) - Systems expose well-defined **APIs (Application Programming Interfaces)** - Allows heterogeneity and vendor independence ### 🎯 Goal 4: Scalability A distributed system should remain effective as the number of users, resources, or nodes grows. There are **three dimensions of scalability**: | Dimension | Description | Mitigation | |-----------|-------------|------------| | **Size Scalability** | More users and resources | Replication, caching, partitioning | | **Geographical Scalability** | Users distributed across regions | CDNs, distributed databases | | **Administrative Scalability** | Multiple administrative domains | Federation, decentralized protocols | ## 2.2 Architectural Models of Distributed Computing ### A. Client–Server Architecture - **Definition**: A clear separation between service *providers* (servers) and service *consumers* (clients). - **Tiers**: - **1-Tier**: Standalone (DB + UI on single machine) - **2-Tier**: Client ↔ Server (e.g., browser ↔ web server) - **3-Tier**: Client ↔ Application Server ↔ Database - **N-Tier**: Multiple specialized middle layers ### B. Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Architecture - All nodes are **equals**; each can act as both client and server - High **fault tolerance** due to no single point of failure - Examples: BitTorrent, blockchain networks ### C. Three-Tier / Multi-Tier Architecture - **Presentation Layer** → UI - **Application/Logic Layer** → Business rules - **Data Layer** → Database/storage ### D. Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) / Microservices - **Loosely coupled**, **independently deployable** services - Communicate via lightweight protocols (REST, gRPC, message queues) - Foundation for **cloud-native** systems ## 2.3 Challenges in Distributed Computing > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 8 Fundamental Challenges (KTU Exam Favorite)** 1. **Heterogeneity** – Different hardware, OS, languages, networks 2. **Openness** – Balancing standardized vs. proprietary components 3. **Security** – Encryption, authentication, authorization across trust boundaries 4. **Scalability** – Avoiding centralized bottlenecks 5. **Failure Handling** – Detecting, masking, tolerating, recovery 6. **Concurrency** – Shared resources, race conditions 7. **Transparency** – Hiding distribution while preserving performance 8. **Quality of Service (QoS)** – Maintaining reliability, performance, adaptability ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 numerical problems. Memorize these units and boundary conditions. | # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | **Speedup (S)** | $S = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p}$ | Sequential time ÷ Parallel time | Dimensionless | | 2 | **Efficiency (E)** | $E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{T_s}{N \cdot T_p}$ | Speedup per processor | 0 ≤ E ≤ 1 | | 3 | **Amdahl's Law** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | Theoretical max speedup with parallel fraction P and N processors | Dimensionless | | 4 | **Gustafson's Law** | $S = N - (1 - P)(N - 1)$ | Scaled speedup for fixed-time workloads | Dimensionless | | 5 | **Throughput (Th)** | $Th = \dfrac{\text{Tasks Completed}}{\text{Time Interval}}$ | Tasks per second | **tasks/sec** | | 6 | **Latency (L)** | $L = T_{\text{transmit}} + T_{\text{propagate}} + T_{\text{process}}$ | End-to-end delay | **ms** | | 7 | **Propagation Delay** | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Distance ÷ signal velocity | **sec** | | 8 | **Transmission Delay** | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Packet size ÷ link bandwidth | **sec** | | 9 | **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures; MTTR = Mean Time To Repair | 0 ≤ A ≤ 1 | | 10 | **Reliability (R(t))** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | λ = failure rate | Dimensionless | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Distributed computing is **not an academic concept** — it underpins: - **Search Engines** (Google's MapReduce across thousands of nodes) - **E-Commerce** (Amazon's microservices handling millions of TPS) - **Banking** (Distributed ledgers, transaction processing) - **Scientific Computing** (CERN's LHC data processing) - **Social Media** (Facebook's TAO distributed data store) - **Streaming** (Netflix's distributed CDN) In **cloud computing**, distributed computing is the **bedrock** — every IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS offering is essentially a distributed system. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Amdahl's Law (KTU Highest-Yield Derivation) > **Scenario:** A program has a *parallelizable fraction* P (0 ≤ P ≤ 1) and a *serial fraction* (1 − P). When executed on N processors, derive the maximum achievable speedup. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1: Decompose the workload** Let $T_s$ be the total execution time on a single processor. Then: $$ T_s = T_{\text{serial}} + T_{\text{parallel}} $$ **Step 2: Express as fractions of total time** $$ T_{\text{serial}} = (1 - P) \cdot T_s $$ $$ T_{\text{parallel}} = P \cdot T_s $$ **Step 3: Compute parallel execution time $T_p$ on N processors** - The **serial portion** $(1 - P) \cdot T_s$ runs on **one processor** (cannot be parallelized). - The **parallel portion** $P \cdot T_s$ is divided across **N processors**, so it takes $\dfrac{P \cdot T_s}{N}$. $$ T_p = (1 - P) \cdot T_s + \dfrac{P \cdot T_s}{N} $$ **Step 4: Factor out $T_s$** $$ T_p = T_s \cdot \left[ (1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N} \right] $$ **Step 5: Apply the speedup definition $S = \dfrac{T_s}{T_p}$** $$ S = \dfrac{T_s}{T_s \cdot \left[ (1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N} \right]} $$ **Step 6: Simplify to obtain Amdahl's Law** $$ \boxed{\,S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}\,} $$ **Step 7: Boundary condition analysis (asymptotic limit)** As $N \to \infty$ (infinite processors): $$ \lim_{N \to \infty} S = \dfrac{1}{1 - P} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight (Exam Favourite):** No matter how many processors you add, the speedup is **bounded** by $\dfrac{1}{1 - P}$. If 10% of the code is serial (P = 0.9), the **maximum speedup is 10×**, even with infinite processors! --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Efficiency of Parallel Execution **Step 1:** Recall the definition of efficiency: $$ E = \dfrac{S}{N} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute Amdahl's speedup formula: $$ E = \dfrac{1}{N} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} $$ **Step 3:** Simplify the denominator: $$ E = \dfrac{1}{N(1 - P) + P} $$ **Step 4:** Validate boundary cases: - If **N = 1** (sequential): $E = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1$ ✓ - If **P = 1** (fully parallel): $E = \dfrac{1}{N}$ ✓ (linear efficiency) - If **P = 0** (fully serial): $E = \dfrac{1}{N}$ but speedup is also 1, so $E = 1/N$ (poor utilization) --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Network Latency (Transmit + Propagate + Process) When a message of size $L$ bits travels across a link of bandwidth $R$ bps over a distance $d$ at signal velocity $v$: **Step 1: Transmission Delay** (time to push all bits onto the wire) $$ T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} $$ **Step 2: Propagation Delay** (time for one bit to reach the receiver) $$ T_p = \dfrac{d}{v} $$ **Step 3: Total One-Way Latency** $$ L_{\text{one-way}} = T_t + T_p + T_{\text{process}} $$ **Step 4: Round-Trip Time (RTT)** (ignoring processing): $$ \text{RTT} = 2 \cdot \left( \dfrac{L}{R} + \dfrac{d}{v} \right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Used to calculate the response time of distributed applications — critical in cloud SLA (Service Level Agreement) design. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Architecture | Scenario | Recommended Architecture | Justification | |----------|-------------------------|---------------| | Centralized database with 1000s of users | **Client–Server (3-Tier)** | Simpler administration, strong consistency | | File sharing among millions of users | **Peer-to-Peer** | High fault tolerance, no central bottleneck | | Banking transaction system | **Multi-Tier with Replication** | ACID compliance, fault tolerance | | Real-time multiplayer gaming | **Hybrid (P2P + Centralized Matchmaker)** | Low latency for gameplay, fair matchmaking | | E-commerce web application (Amazon-like) | **Microservices** | Independent scaling, technology diversity | | Scientific computation (weather simulation) | **Cluster / Grid** | Massive parallel workloads, batch processing | --- ## 3.5 Worked Example (KTU Practice Style) **Problem:** A distributed system has 95% parallel code (P = 0.95) and runs on 16 processors. Calculate: (a) Speedup (b) Efficiency (c) Maximum theoretical speedup ### Solution **(a) Speedup using Amdahl's Law** $$ S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \dfrac{0.95}{16}} $$ $$ S = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.059375} $$ $$ S = \dfrac{1}{0.109375} \approx 9.14 $$ **(b) Efficiency** $$ E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{9.14}{16} \approx 0.571 \;\; (57.1\%) $$ **(c) Maximum theoretical speedup as $N \to \infty$** $$ S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - P} = \dfrac{1}{0.05} = 20 $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: High-Level Distributed Computing Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB ClientA[Client Node A] -->|Request| LB[Load Balancer] ClientB[Client Node B] -->|Request| LB ClientC[Client Node C] -->|Request| LB LB -->|Distribute| App1[Application Server 1] LB -->|Distribute| App2[Application Server 2] LB -->|Distribute| App3[Application Server 3] App1 -->|Read Write| DB1[(Database Primary)] App2 -->|Read Write| DB1 App3 -->|Read Write| DB1 DB1 -->|Replication| DB2[(Database Replica 1)] DB1 -->|Replication| DB3[(Database Replica 2)] App1 -.->|Heartbeat| Mon[Monitoring Service] App2 -.->|Heartbeat| Mon App3 -.->|Heartbeat| Mon ``` **Explanation:** This is a **classic 3-tier distributed architecture**. Clients never talk to the database directly — the load balancer distributes requests, and the database uses replication for fault tolerance. The monitoring service collects heartbeats for failure detection. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Comparison of Architectural Models ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CS[CLIENT SERVER MODEL] C1[Client 1] -->|Request| S1[Server] C2[Client 2] -->|Request| S1 C3[Client 3] -->|Request| S1 end subgraph P2P[PEER TO PEER MODEL] P1[Peer 1] --- P2[Peer 2] P2 --- P3[Peer 3] P3 --- P4[Peer 4] P4 --- P1 end subgraph MT[MULTI TIER MODEL] CL[Client Browser] -->|HTTP| WS[Web Server] WS -->|Query| AS[App Server] AS -->|SQL| DB[(Database)] end ``` **Explanation:** - **Client–Server**: Centralized control, easier to manage. - **P2P**: No single point of failure; every peer is both client and server. - **Multi-Tier**: Separation of concerns; each tier scales independently. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Failure Detection & Fault Tolerance Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant N1 as Node 1 participant N2 as Node 2 participant N3 as Node 3 participant COORD as Coordinator N1->>N2: Heartbeat PING N2-->>N1: PONG ACK N1->>N3: Heartbeat PING N3--xN1: No Response (Timeout) N1->>COORD: Report N3 as Suspected Failed COORD->>N3: Verification Probe COORD--xN3: No Response COORD->>ALL: Mark N3 OFFLINE COORD->>N2: Reassign N3 tasks ``` **Explanation:** This is the **heartbeat-based failure detection** pattern used in real distributed systems like **Apache ZooKeeper** and **etcd**. The coordinator uses a gossip protocol or heartbeats to detect failures, then redistributes work from the failed node. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Distributed Computing Stack (Conceptual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB L5[APPLICATION LAYER
Web Apps Mobile APIs Microservices] L4[MIDDLEWARE LAYER
Message Queues API Gateways Service Registry] L3[DATA LAYER
Distributed Databases Caches Object Storage] L2[COMPUTE LAYER
Virtual Machines Containers Serverless] L1[INFRASTRUCTURE LAYER
Physical Servers Network Storage Power] L5 --> L4 L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 ``` **Explanation:** This **layered stack** represents the building blocks of any modern distributed/cloud system. The course **PECCT631 — Cloud Infrastructure and Systems** will expand on each of these layers in subsequent modules. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Define distributed computing. List any four characteristics of a distributed system.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Distributed computing is a paradigm in which a system consists of multiple autonomous, networked computers that communicate and coordinate by passing messages to achieve a common goal, while appearing to the user as a single coherent system. **Four Characteristics:** 1. **Resource Sharing** – Hardware, software, and data can be shared across the network. 2. **Concurrency** – Multiple processes execute simultaneously on different nodes. 3. **Scalability** – The system can grow in size and demand without redesign. 4. **Fault Tolerance** – The system continues functioning despite partial hardware or software failures. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *"distributed means multiple computers"* without mentioning the **coordination via message passing** or the **single coherent system** aspect. Both keywords are mandatory for full marks. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Differentiate between Client–Server and Peer-to-Peer architectures. (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Client–Server | Peer-to-Peer | |-----------|--------------|--------------| | **Role of Nodes** | Clients request; servers provide services | Each node is both client and server | | **Centralized Control** | Yes — server is central | No central authority | | **Scalability** | Limited by server capacity | Highly scalable | | **Failure Impact** | Server failure = total system failure | Failure of one peer does not stop the system | | **Examples** | Web applications, email | BitTorrent, blockchain | --- ## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **Q (a):** Explain the various architectural models of distributed computing in detail with neat diagrams. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Distributed computing systems are organized using several architectural models. The major ones are: **1. Client–Server Architecture** - Clients send service requests; servers process them and return responses. - **Advantages:** Centralized management, security, ease of backup. - **Disadvantages:** Single point of failure, scalability bottleneck. - **Example:** Web browser → Web server → Database. **2. Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Architecture** - All nodes are peers; they share resources directly with each other. - **Types:** - *Pure P2P:* No central server (e.g., Gnutella). - *Hybrid P2P:* Central index + peer data transfer (e.g., early Napster). - **Advantages:** High fault tolerance, no bottleneck. - **Disadvantages:** Harder to secure, complex resource discovery. **3. Three-Tier / Multi-Tier Architecture** - **Presentation Tier:** User interface (HTML, React, mobile app). - **Application/Logic Tier:** Business rules (Java, Python, .NET). - **Data Tier:** Database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB). - **Advantages:** Separation of concerns, independent scaling, security (logic tier hides DB). **4. Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA) and Microservices** - Services are **loosely coupled** and communicate via standard protocols (REST, gRPC, SOAP). - Each service can be developed, deployed, and scaled independently. - **Advantages:** Agility, technology diversity, resilience. - **Examples:** Netflix, Amazon, Uber backend systems. > **Diagram:** A clean block diagram showing all four architectures side by side earns full marks. --- **Q (b):** A parallel program has 90% parallel code (P = 0.9) and runs on 8 processors. Using Amdahl's Law, calculate: (i) The speedup obtained (ii) The efficiency (iii) The maximum achievable speedup with infinite processors **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Given: $P = 0.9$, $N = 8$. **(i) Speedup (S) using Amdahl's Law** $$ S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} $$ $$ S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.9) + \dfrac{0.9}{8}} $$ $$ S = \dfrac{1}{0.1 + 0.1125} $$ $$ S = \dfrac{1}{0.2125} \approx 4.71 $$ **(ii) Efficiency (E)** $$ E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{4.71}{8} \approx 0.589 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 58.9\% $$ **(iii) Maximum Speedup ($N \to \infty$)** $$ S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - P} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.9} = \dfrac{1}{0.1} = 10 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** > 1. Many students forget to convert percentages to decimals (P = 0.9, not 90). This single mistake costs 2 marks. > 2. The **maximum speedup question** is often skipped entirely. It tests the asymptotic limit concept. > 3. Always include **units** for efficiency (dimensionless or %). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Q (a):** Discuss the major challenges in designing distributed systems. How does transparency help address some of these challenges? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Distributed systems must overcome a unique set of challenges arising from the **independence** of components and the **unreliability** of networks. **Major Challenges:** 1. **Heterogeneity** – Different hardware, OS, network protocols, and programming languages. *Solution:* Use middleware and standardized protocols (TCP/IP, HTTP, CORBA). 2. **Openness** – Need for interoperable and extensible systems. *Solution:* Publish APIs and use open standards. 3. **Security** – Confidentiality, integrity, availability across untrusted networks. *Solution:* Encryption (TLS/SSL), authentication (Kerberos, OAuth), authorization (RBAC). 4. **Scalability** – Performance must not degrade as the system grows. *Solution:* Replication, caching, partitioning (sharding), load balancing. 5. **Failure Handling** – Partial failures must be masked. *Solution:* Replication, redundancy, checkpointing, heartbeat-based detection. 6. **Concurrency** – Shared resources must be synchronized. *Solution:* Distributed locks, consensus algorithms (Paxos, Raft), transactions. **Role of Transparency:** Transparency means **hiding the distributed nature** from the user. The 8 forms (Access, Location, Concurrency, Replication, Failure, Migration, Performance, Scaling) help address challenges like heterogeneity, failure, and scalability by making the distributed infrastructure *invisible* to applications and end-users. --- **Q (b):** Explain the Client–Server architecture with a real-world example. Differentiate between 2-tier and 3-tier architectures. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Client–Server Architecture:** A model where clients (service requesters) and servers (service providers) communicate over a network. The server hosts, manages, and delivers resources or services requested by clients. **Real-World Example: Online Banking** - **Client:** Mobile app or browser - **Server:** Bank's application server - **Database:** Account, transaction data - **Flow:** User logs in → Client sends request → Server validates → DB query → Response sent back. **2-Tier vs 3-Tier Architecture:** | Feature | 2-Tier | 3-Tier | |---------|--------|--------| | **Layers** | Client + Database | Client + Application Server + Database | | **Business Logic** | Embedded in client | On a separate server | | **Scalability** | Limited | High (each tier scales independently) | | **Security** | Client directly accesses DB | DB is hidden behind application server | | **Example** | Desktop accounting app | Modern web applications (Amazon, Flipkart) | | **Performance** | Faster for small workloads | Better for large, concurrent workloads | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students confuse **tiers** with **layers**. In 2-tier, the *client* contains the *presentation + business logic*, while the *server* is just the *database*. Drawing the layered diagram is essential for full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist for Distributed Computing** - [ ] **Definition:** Multiple autonomous computers, communicating via message passing, appearing as a single system. - [ ] **8 Characteristics:** Resource sharing, concurrency, openness, scalability, fault tolerance, transparency, heterogeneity handling, security. - [ ] **8 Types of Transparency:** Access, Location, Concurrency, Replication, Failure, Migration, Performance, Scaling. - [ ] **4 Architectural Models:** Client–Server, Peer-to-Peer, Multi-Tier (3-tier/N-tier), SOA/Microservices. - [ ] **3 Dimensions of Scalability:** Size, Geography, Administration. - [ ] **8 Challenges:** Heterogeneity, Openness, Security, Scalability, Failure Handling, Concurrency, Transparency, QoS. - [ ] **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ — mandatory for numerical problems. - [ ] **Amdahl's Limit:** $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - P}$ (as N → ∞). - [ ] **Efficiency:** $E = \dfrac{S}{N} = \dfrac{1}{N(1 - P) + P}$. - [ ] **Network Latency:** $L = \dfrac{L}{R} + \dfrac{d}{v} + T_{\text{process}}$. - [ ] **Availability:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$. - [ ] **Reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (exponential failure model). - [ ] **Foundation for Cloud:** Distributed computing principles are the bedrock of IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. - [ ] **Real-World Examples:** Google Search (MapReduce), Amazon (Microservices), Netflix (CDN + Microservices), BitTorrent (P2P). > **Final Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam), expect at least one **Amdahl's Law numerical** and one **architecture comparison** in every paper for this module. Master these two and you secure 14 marks!

topics.title: Utility Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTURE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Utility Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Utility Computing** is a service-provisioning model in which computing resources — including processing power, storage, network bandwidth, and software — are packaged, delivered, and billed to users on a **metered, pay-per-use basis**, in the same manner as traditional public utilities such as electricity, water, or natural gas. The user consumes resources dynamically according to demand, and the provider scales the underlying infrastructure transparently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT631 Module 1):**
> Utility computing refers to the model in which computational services (compute, storage, network, applications) are provided to end-users as a metered, on-demand utility. It forms the **conceptual and economic foundation** upon which modern Cloud Computing is built. Cloud Computing is, in effect, the *realized implementation* of Utility Computing over the Internet.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you live in a house. You do **not** buy a personal power plant. Instead, you plug into the **electricity grid**. You pay only for the kilowatt-hours you consume this month. If your family hosts a wedding, you temporarily use more power; the grid supplies it. When the guests leave, your bill drops. You never worry about generator maintenance, capacity planning, or paying for a power plant that sits idle 90% of the time.

**Utility Computing applies the same logic to IT resources:**

| Traditional Electricity Utility | Utility Computing Equivalent |
| --- | --- |
| Electricity (kWh) | Compute (CPU hours / vCPU-seconds) |
| Water (litres) | Storage (GB-month) |
| Gas pressure | Network bandwidth (GB transferred) |
| Monthly meter reading | Usage telemetry & billing dashboards |
| Electricity provider (KSEB) | Cloud Provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** You rent capacity instead of owning infrastructure. The cost scales with consumption, not with a pre-purchased capital asset.

## 1.3 The Five Defining Characteristics of Utility Computing

> [!NOTE]
> These five traits are the **defining pillars** that KTU examiners repeatedly test in Module 1.

1. **Metered Usage** — Every unit of consumption (CPU-second, GB, I/O request) is measured, recorded, and reported.
2. **Pay-Per-Use (Usage-Based Pricing)** — No fixed licensing fee; the bill equals *quantity consumed × unit price*.
3. **Elasticity & On-Demand Self-Service** — Resources can be provisioned or released *automatically* without human provider interaction.
4. **Location Independence / Abstraction** — The consumer does not know or care *where* the physical server sits.
5. **Economies of Scale** — Providers aggregate massive demand, dropping per-unit costs through bulk purchasing and operational efficiency.

## 1.4 Historical & Theoretical Lineage

Utility Computing is **not a new invention** — it is the *re-purposing* of an old economic model (utility economics, dating back to the 19th-century gas/electricity era) for IT.

- **1961** — John McCarthy (MIT) speculated that *computation may someday be organized as a public utility*.
- **1990s** — The grid-computing movement (SETI@home, Globus) pioneered distributed resource sharing.
- **2006** — Amazon Web Services (EC2, S3) operationalised the utility model at hyperscale.
- **2008–Present** — NIST formally defines Cloud Computing as the *utility-driven realization* of this concept.

## 1.5 Utility Computing in the Cloud Computing Stack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hook:** The relationship between Utility Computing and Cloud Computing is the single most-asked 3-mark question in Module 1.

Utility Computing is the **economic philosophy**. Cloud Computing is the **technical implementation** of that philosophy over the Internet. All four NIST deployment models (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community) and the three service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) inherit utility-computing properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Logical Layers of the Utility Computing Stack

The utility-computing ecosystem is best understood as a **three-tier logical stack**, where each tier is provisioned on a metered basis:

### Tier 1 — Infrastructure Utility (Compute + Storage + Network)
- **Compute:** vCPU-hours, GPU-hours
- **Storage:** GB-month for block/object storage
- **Network:** GB of egress, public IP-hour, load-balancer-hour

### Tier 2 — Platform Utility (PaaS)
- **Runtime environments** (e.g., managed Kubernetes node-hour)
- **Database services** (RDS instance-hour + IOPS)
- **Message queues, caches, ML training platforms**

### Tier 3 — Application Utility (SaaS)
- **End-user applications** delivered over the browser (Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce)
- Pricing: per-user-per-month, per-transaction, or freemium tiers

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the three-tier view matters in KTU exams:** It directly maps onto the IaaS / PaaS / SaaS taxonomy that you must draw and explain in Part B 14-mark questions.

## 2.2 The Utility Computing Economic Model — Formal Treatment

Utility computing transforms IT from a **Capital Expenditure (CapEx)** model to an **Operational Expenditure (OpEx)** model.

- **Traditional (Own-and-Maintain) Model:**

  The organization purchases a server, a rack, an UPS, a cooling plant, and licenses, then depreciates the asset over 3–5 years. The cost is fixed whether the server runs at 2% or 98% CPU utilization.

- **Utility Model:**

  The organization provisions a virtual machine, runs it for *t* hours at rate *r*, and pays the bill at the end of the cycle. The cost is variable and tracks business demand.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates *all* quantitative relationships you must memorise for Module 1 numerical/theory questions. These are pure utility-computing economic equations and are tested as both short-answer and 14-mark sub-parts.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | **Total Utility Bill** | $\text{Cost} = \sum_{i} (U_i \times P_i)$ | INR / USD | $U_i$ = units of resource *i* consumed, $P_i$ = per-unit price of resource *i* |
| 2 | **Cost per vCPU-hour** | $C_{cpu} = \frac{\text{Instance hourly rate}}{\text{Number of vCPUs}}$ | INR per vCPU-hour | Used for comparing instance families |
| 3 | **Effective Storage Cost** | $C_{storage} = \frac{\text{Monthly storage charge}}{GB \text{ provisioned}}$ | INR per GB-month | Tiered pricing (Hot / Cool / Archive) |
| 4 | **TCO Comparison (CapEx vs OpEx)** | $\text{TCO}_{utility} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} C_{op}(t) \cdot (1+r)^{-t}$ | Currency (NPV) | $r$ = discount rate; $T$ = project life |
| 5 | **Mean Utilization (Queuing View)** | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu \cdot s}$ | Dimensionless | $s$ = servers, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate |
| 6 | **Elasticity Response Time** | $R_e = t_{scale\text{-}out} - t_{demand\text{-}spike}$ | Seconds | Lower is better |
| 7 | **Bill of a Spot Instance** | $\text{SpotBill} = P_{spot} \times T_{run}$ | Currency | $P_{spot}$ fluctuates with demand |
| 8 | **Cost per Transaction (SaaS)** | $C_{txn} = \frac{\text{Monthly SaaS fee}}{\text{Monthly transactions}}$ | INR / USD per txn | Used in unit-economics discussion |
| 9 | **Capacity Saved by Elasticity** | $S_{cap} = C_{peak} - C_{avg\text{-}actual}$ | Currency | Quantifies the elasticity dividend |
| 10 | **Egress Cost** | $C_{out} = GB_{out} \times P_{out}$ | Currency | $P_{out}$ is usually the *highest* unit price |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse the *storage retention* unit (GB-month) with the *network egress* unit (GB transferred). KTU examiners often give a problem mixing the two to test your unit awareness.

## 2.4 The Four Economic & Engineering Properties

> [!NOTE]
> These are the *why* behind utility computing and the most common 7-mark sub-part in 14-mark questions.

### 2.4.1 Pay-as-you-use (Elasticity)
- Compute scales from **1 vCPU to 10 000 vCPUs** in minutes.
- Storage scales from **1 GB to 1 PB** without provisioning tickets.

### 2.4.2 No Capital Lock-in
- The user does not buy hardware. A start-up with ₹0 in capex can serve 10 million users.

### 2.4.3 Operational Cost is Variable, Not Fixed
- Idle resources cost *almost nothing* (storage pennies, no compute).
- The CFO sees a clean OpEx line item rather than amortisation tables.

### 2.4.4 Workload-Aware Pricing
- Reserved instances, spot instances, savings plans, committed-use discounts — all variants of the utility model *with demand-commitment rebates*.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Utility-Computing Application |
| --- | --- |
| **Fintech** | Bursting risk-modeling workloads during end-of-day reconciliation |
| **Healthcare** | Storing petabytes of MRI/CT imaging on cold storage tier |
| **E-commerce** | Auto-scaling web servers during festive sales (10×–100× traffic) |
| **AI / ML** | Training on thousands of GPU-hours, paying only for run-time |
| **Media & Streaming** | Encoding pipelines that spin up at night and shut down at dawn |
| **Research (CERN, Genomics)** | Distributing compute across global data-centre regions |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Comparing Own-and-Maintain vs Utility Model

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Module 1, 14-mark style):**
> A startup expects its web application to serve **2 000 000 requests/month** with each request requiring **0.5 s of vCPU time** on a single 2-vCPU server. Compute the *monthly utility bill* and compare it with a CapEx server costing **₹4 00 000** with a **36-month amortisation** and **₹12 000/month** for power, cooling, and admin.

### Step 1 — Identify the demand model

- Total request-seconds per month:

$$\text{Total CPU-seconds} = 2\,000\,000 \times 0.5 = 1\,000\,000 \text{ CPU-seconds}$$

- Convert to vCPU-hours. Each request uses 1 vCPU fully, so:

$$\text{vCPU-hours} = \frac{1\,000\,000}{3600} = 277.78 \text{ vCPU-hours}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the utility-pricing formula (Formula #1 in cheat sheet)

Assume a public-cloud on-demand price of **₹8.00 per vCPU-hour** (illustrative AWS-mapped rate).

$$\text{Utility Bill} = U \times P = 277.78 \times 8.00 = ₹2\,222.24 \text{ per month}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the CapEx monthly equivalent

$$\text{Monthly CapEx} = \frac{₹4\,00\,000}{36} = ₹11\,111.11$$

Add OpEx (power, cooling, admin):

$$\text{Monthly CapEx+OpEx} = 11\,111.11 + 12\,000 = ₹23\,111.11 \text{ per month}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the saving (Formula #9 — capacity saved)

$$S_{cap} = C_{peak} - C_{avg\text{-}actual} = 23\,111.11 - 2\,222.24 = ₹20\,888.87$$

> **Result:** Utility computing is **~10.4× cheaper** for this demand profile. The saving widens further if traffic is *bursty*, since the CapEx server must be sized for peak load.

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Elasticity Dividend (Why Auto-Scale Saves Money)

> **Problem:** A photo-sharing app's CPU demand follows a 24-hour sinusoidal pattern: peak of **800 vCPUs at 20:00**, trough of **50 vCPUs at 04:00**. Average demand = **312 vCPUs**.
> (a) Compute the monthly bill of an *over-provisioned* (fixed) cluster sized at the peak.
> (b) Compute the monthly bill of an *elastic* cluster that scales hourly to the exact demand.
> (c) Compute the **elasticity dividend**.

Assume on-demand price = **₹6.00 per vCPU-hour**.

### Step 1 — Over-provisioned (fixed) cluster

$$\text{Fixed monthly hours} = 800 \times 24 \times 30 = 5\,76\,000 \text{ vCPU-hours}$$

$$\text{Fixed Bill} = 5\,76\,000 \times 6.00 = ₹34\,56\,000$$

### Step 2 — Compute total elastic demand across 30 days

We model the 24-hour demand as:

$$D(t) = 425 + 375 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{24} - \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$

Average value of the sine term over a full day is 0, so:

$$\overline{D} = 425 \text{ vCPUs (model mean)}$$

But the *empirical* average is given as 312. The integral under the curve over 30 days:

$$D_{total} = 312 \times 24 \times 30 = 2\,24\,640 \text{ vCPU-hours}$$

### Step 3 — Elastic monthly bill

$$\text{Elastic Bill} = 2\,24\,640 \times 6.00 = ₹13\,47\,840$$

### Step 4 — Elasticity dividend (Formula #9)

$$S_{cap} = 34\,56\,000 - 13\,47\,840 = ₹21\,08\,160$$

> **Conclusion:** Elasticity saved **~61%** of the bill. This is the **engineering heart** of utility computing: paying for *what you actually consume* rather than *what you might consume*.

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Storage Tier Optimisation

> **Problem:** A media company stores **20 TB** of data. 80% is older than 90 days and rarely accessed (archive); 20% is hot. Compute the monthly bill under three storage tiers.

Let the tiered pricing be:

| Tier | Price (₹ per GB-month) |
| --- | --- |
| Standard (Hot) | 2.50 |
| Infrequent Access | 1.00 |
| Glacier / Archive | 0.10 |

### Step 1 — Quantify per-tier allocation

$$\text{Hot data} = 0.20 \times 20\,000 = 4\,000 \text{ GB}$$
$$\text{Archive data} = 0.80 \times 20\,000 = 16\,000 \text{ GB}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the storage-cost formula (Formula #3)

$$\text{Hot bill} = 4\,000 \times 2.50 = ₹10\,000$$
$$\text{Archive bill} = 16\,000 \times 0.10 = ₹1\,600$$
$$\text{Tiered total} = ₹10\,000 + ₹1\,600 = ₹11\,600$$

### Step 3 — Counterfactual: all-hot baseline

$$\text{All-hot bill} = 20\,000 \times 2.50 = ₹50\,000$$

### Step 4 — Saving

$$\text{Saving} = 50\,000 - 11\,600 = ₹38\,400 \text{ per month} \;(\approx 77\%)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Storage-tier optimisation is the textbook example of *utility-computing cost engineering* and is often given as a 7-mark sub-part.

## 3.4 Decision Matrix — When to Choose Utility Computing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect you to *justify* your choice in 14-mark questions. Use the matrix below to structure your argument.

| Decision Criterion | Favours Utility Computing | Favours Own-and-Maintain |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Demand Predictability** | Unpredictable / bursty | Highly stable, near-100% utilisation |
| **Workload Lifetime** | Short-lived (< 2 years) | Long-lived (> 5 years) |
| **Data-Sovereignty** | Public data, generic apps | Highly regulated, classified data |
| **Network Sensitivity** | Tolerates 10–50 ms WAN latency | Sub-millisecond local latency |
| **Capital Budget** | Limited | Large capital available |
| **Team Skill Set** | Wants to focus on app code | Wants full hardware control |
| **Compliance Audit** | Trust in provider's certifications | Strict in-house audit trail |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Utility-Computing System

The following block diagram shows the canonical layered architecture of a utility-computing system, mapping the user-facing demand plane to the provider-side resource plane through a metering/billing control loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Demand Plane: Web Mobile Batch ML Workloads] --> B[Service Catalogue Portal]
    B --> C[Identity and Access Management Layer IAM]
    C --> D[Provisioning Orchestrator]
    D --> E[Resource Pool: Compute]
    D --> F[Resource Pool: Storage]
    D --> G[Resource Pool: Network]
    D --> H[Resource Pool: Platform Services]
    E --> I[Metering and Telemetry Collector]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Billing and Pricing Engine]
    J --> K[User Invoice and Cost Dashboard]
    I --> L[Capacity Planner and Elasticity Controller]
    L --> D
    J --> M[Provider Revenue and P and L]
```

**How to read this diagram (for the exam):**
- The **left side** is the consumer (pay-per-use requests).
- The **middle** is the provider's control plane (orchestrator + IAM).
- The **right side** is the metering/feedback loop — the *defining innovation* of utility computing.
- The arrow from `Metering and Telemetry Collector` back to `Provisioning Orchestrator` (via `Capacity Planner`) represents **auto-elasticity**.

## 4.2 Utility Computing vs Traditional IT — Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TraditionalIT
        T1[Capital Purchase: Buy Server] --> T2[Install in Datacenter]
        T2 --> T3[Run at Fixed Capacity]
        T3 --> T4[Depreciate Over 3 to 5 Years]
    end
    subgraph UtilityComputing
        U1[On Demand Request via API] --> U2[Provider Provisions VM or Service]
        U2 --> U3[Metered Consumption]
        U3 --> U4[Pay Only for What Was Used]
        U4 --> U5[Release Resources When Done]
    end
    T4 --> X[Total Cost of Ownership]
    U5 --> X
```

**Interpretation for KTU exam:** The traditional model is a *linear, single-pass flow* with no feedback. The utility model is a *cyclic, metered flow* where consumption information drives both billing *and* future provisioning.

## 4.3 Service-Model Mapping Within the Utility Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Hardware and Datacenter: Power, Cooling, Racks] --> L1[IaaS Utility: vCPU, RAM, Disk, Network]
    L1 --> L2[PaaS Utility: Runtime, DB, Queue, ML Platform]
    L2 --> L3[SaaS Utility: Web Mail, CRM, Office Suite]
    L3 --> L4[End User Browser or Mobile App]
    L0 --> M[Metering Layer Embedded at Every Tier]
    L1 --> M
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    M --> B[Unified Bill and Cost Dashboard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram is the single most useful schematic to draw in a 14-mark question. It directly addresses the KTU Module 1 syllabus outcome on "understanding service and deployment models."

## 4.4 Elasticity Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workload Demand Metric: CPU pct or QPS] --> B[Threshold Compare: Scale Out or Scale In]
    B --> C[Orchestrator Adds or Removes VMs]
    C --> D[Updated Capacity Available]
    D --> A
    C --> E[Metering Log: Usage Units]
    E --> F[Billing Event]
    F --> G[Customer Invoice]
```

**Why this loop matters:** It is the *embodiment* of utility computing's promise — your bill, your capacity, and your demand track each other in real time.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Define *Utility Computing*. List any **four** of its defining characteristics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Utility Computing is a service-provisioning model in which computing resources such as CPU, memory, storage, and network bandwidth are delivered to users on a **metered, pay-per-use basis**, just as electricity or water is delivered by a public utility.

Four defining characteristics (½ mark each + 1 mark for definition):

1. **Metered usage** — every unit of consumption is measured.
2. **Pay-per-use pricing** — the bill scales with consumption.
3. **On-demand self-service** — resources are provisioned without human provider interaction.
4. **Elasticity** — capacity expands or contracts dynamically with demand.
5. **Location independence** — the user is unaware of the physical location of the resource.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Utility Computing** and **Grid Computing** in any **three** dimensions.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per dimension):**

| Dimension | Utility Computing | Grid Computing |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Primary Goal** | Deliver IT as a billed, on-demand service | Coordinate distributed resources for a *single* large job |
| **Billing Model** | Metered pay-per-use | Usually free, project-funded |
| **Workload Pattern** | Many small, independent, *elastic* jobs | Few large, *batch* parallel jobs |
| **User Interaction** | Continuous, transactional | Burst-style, scientific |
| **Resource Ownership** | Single commercial provider | Federated across many institutions |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, every Module 1 question carries an internal choice. You must attempt **one** of the two given alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**
> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain in detail the **economic and architectural model of Utility Computing**. With the aid of a neat block diagram, illustrate how the metering and feedback loop enables elasticity.
> **(b)** [7 marks] A SaaS provider charges ₹120 per user per month. A client signs up 450 employees, but 30% of them are inactive in any given month and only consume *light* tier at ₹40. Compute the **expected monthly bill** for the client. Discuss the **two advantages** of pay-per-use from the provider's perspective.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Definition (1 mark):** Utility computing packages IT resources as a metered, pay-per-use service.
2. **Architectural components (3 marks):**
   - Service catalogue / portal
   - Identity & access management
   - Provisioning orchestrator
   - Resource pool (compute, storage, network)
   - Metering & telemetry collector
   - Billing engine
   - Capacity planner (elasticity controller)
3. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw the architecture from Section 4.1 of these notes. Label all components and the feedback arrow from metering → orchestrator.
4. **Economic properties (1 mark):** Conversion of CapEx → OpEx, variable cost, no idle waste.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

- Active users: $450 \times 0.70 = 315$ users.
- Inactive users: $450 \times 0.30 = 135$ users.

Billing for *light* tier: rate is given as ₹40. We interpret the question as 70% standard users and 30% on light tier:

- Standard full-rate users: 315 × ₹120 = ₹37 800
- Light-tier users: 135 × ₹40 = ₹5 400

$$\text{Total Monthly Bill} = 37\,800 + 5\,400 = ₹43\,200$$

**Two advantages to the provider (1 mark each):**

1. **Revenue smoothing** — usage data lets the provider right-size capacity planning.
2. **Reduced customer-acquisition friction** — low entry price removes the CapEx objection.
3. **Better risk-sharing** — provider's cost is also variable (they only pay cloud IaaS for active users).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Retest Paper]**
> **(a)** [7 marks] Compare **Utility Computing** and **Cloud Computing**. Is Cloud Computing merely a *technological implementation* of Utility Computing? Justify your answer with **five** points.
> **(b)** [7 marks] An e-commerce site experiences a **20× traffic spike** during a 6-hour Diwali sale window. Compute the *elasticity dividend* in INR if the site scales from a baseline of 40 vCPUs to 800 vCPUs for the 6 hours. Price = ₹7 per vCPU-hour.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| # | Point (1 mark each) | Justification |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | **Concept vs Implementation** | Utility Computing is the economic philosophy; Cloud Computing is the *Internet-scale implementation* of that philosophy. |
| 2 | **Delivery Medium** | Utility computing can theoretically run over a private network; Cloud computing is *Internet-delivered* by default. |
| 3 | **Service Scope** | Utility computing traditionally focused on raw compute; Cloud computing spans IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS, and DBaaS. |
| 4 | **Metering Granularity** | Both meter usage, but Cloud offers finer granularity (per-request, per-second billing). |
| 5 | **Elasticity Mechanism** | Both promise elasticity; Cloud delivers it via *virtualisation and orchestration* (OpenStack, Kubernetes). |
| 6 | **Ecosystem** | Cloud computing includes marketplace, serverless, managed AI — beyond a pure utility's scope. |
| 7 | **Verdict** | Cloud is *not merely* utility computing — it is utility computing **+ virtualisation + orchestration + global reach + rich ecosystem**. |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Baseline cost (without elasticity, must provision for peak all month):**
- 30 days × 24 hours = 720 hours.
- $\text{Fixed bill} = 800 \times 720 \times 7 = ₹40\,32\,000$

**Step 2 — Actual elastic consumption:**
- Baseline (24 h − 6 h = 714 h at 40 vCPUs): $40 \times 714 \times 7 = ₹1\,99\,920$
- Spike (6 h at 800 vCPUs): $800 \times 6 \times 7 = ₹33\,600$

$$\text{Elastic total} = 1\,99\,920 + 33\,600 = ₹2\,33\,520$$

**Step 3 — Elasticity dividend (Formula #9):**

$$S_{cap} = 40\,32\,000 - 2\,33\,520 = ₹37\,98\,480 \approx 94\% \text{ saving}$$

> **Interpretation (1 mark):** The 20× spike cost only 6 hours of extra capacity — exactly the engineering promise of utility computing.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students lose marks on Utility-Computing questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the unit "vCPU-hour" with "instance-hour".** A 4-vCPU instance running for 1 hour consumes 4 vCPU-hours, not 1. The KTU valuation key *will* deduct 1 mark for this unit error.
> 2. **Treating Cloud Computing and Utility Computing as synonyms.** They are *not* synonymous. Utility is the *economic model*; Cloud is the *implementation*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this conflation.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw a diagram.** A 14-mark answer without a labelled block diagram typically caps at 10/14.
> 4. **Confusing storage units.** "GB-month" (capacity × time) ≠ "GB transferred" (egress). Always state the unit.
> 5. **Omitting the feedback arrow** in the elasticity diagram. The arrow from metering back to orchestrator is the *defining feature* of the utility model — losing it costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Not justifying the choice** in compare/contrast questions. Every comparison line must end with a "**because…**" clause.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Utility Computing (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Utility computing = IT resources delivered on a *metered, pay-per-use* basis, analogous to electricity.
- **Five Pillars:** Metering, Pay-per-use, Elasticity, On-demand self-service, Location independence.
- **Cloud ↔ Utility Relationship:** Utility is the *economic philosophy*; Cloud is the *Internet-scale implementation*. They are related but **not** synonymous.
- **Three-Tier Stack:** Infrastructure (IaaS) → Platform (PaaS) → Application (SaaS); metering embedded at every tier.
- **Five Key Formulas:**
  - Total Bill = $\sum (U_i \times P_i)$
  - Cost per vCPU-hour = Instance rate ÷ vCPU count
  - Storage cost = GB-month × tier rate
  - Elasticity dividend = Peak cost − Actual consumed cost
  - Mean utilisation $\rho = \lambda / (\mu s)$
- **Three Industry Use-Cases:** Burst web traffic, archive storage, ML/GPU training.
- **Decision Rule:** Choose utility when demand is *unpredictable*, lifetime is *short*, and you want to *focus on application code* over infrastructure.
- **Storage Units:** GB-month (capacity) vs GB transferred (egress) — never mix.
- **Diagram Required:** Always include the elasticity feedback arrow (metering → orchestrator).
- **Historical Anchor:** John McCarthy (1961) predicted computation-as-utility; AWS (2006) delivered it.
- **CapEx → OpEx shift:** Utility converts a fixed capital asset into a variable operating expense.
- **Cost Engineering Wins:** Tiered storage (up to 80% saving), elastic scaling (up to 90% saving), spot instances (up to 90% off on-demand).
- **Common Exam Trap:** Don't call a "subscription" the same as "utility pricing" — a flat monthly SaaS fee is *not* utility pricing; per-user-per-active-day is closer to the true utility model.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Cloud Computing — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Computing** is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., **networks, servers, storage, applications, and services**) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction. — *Adapted from the NIST SP 800-145 Definition.*

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, cloud computing is the **on-demand delivery of IT resources over the Internet with pay-as-you-go pricing**, abstracting the underlying physical infrastructure from the end user. The user neither owns nor maintains the hardware; instead, resources are consumed as elastic, metered services.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity Grid" Model

Imagine cloud computing as the **electricity grid**:
- You do **not** own a power plant at home. You simply plug in an appliance, consume electricity, and pay only for the units (kWh) you used.
- If you need more power (e.g., for an air conditioner in summer), the grid scales automatically.
- The power company handles the generators, transformers, and transmission lines.

In the same way:
- The **Cloud Provider** (AWS, Azure, GCP) owns the data centers, servers, and storage.
- The **User** requests resources (a virtual machine, a database, storage) via the Internet.
- Billing is based on **usage metrics** (per hour, per GB, per request).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Cloud computing shifts capital expenditure (CapEx) to operational expenditure (OpEx). You trade ownership for *utility-style consumption*.

## 1.3 The Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud Computing (NIST)

| # | Characteristic | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **On-demand self-service** | Provision resources automatically without human interaction at the provider. |
| 2 | **Broad network access** | Available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms (laptop, mobile, tablet). |
| 3 | **Resource pooling** | Provider's resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a **multi-tenant model**. |
| 4 | **Rapid elasticity** | Capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released, scaling with demand. |
| 5 | **Measured service** | Resource usage is monitored, controlled, and reported, providing transparency for both provider and consumer. |

## 1.4 Why Cloud Computing Matters in Engineering

- **Scalability:** Handle traffic spikes (e.g., e-commerce during a festival sale).
- **High Availability:** **99.99% SLA** uptime offered by major providers.
- **Global Reach:** Deploy in 30+ geographic regions within minutes.
- **Cost Optimization:** Eliminates upfront hardware investments.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask the *five essential characteristics* and the *NIST reference model* — memorize this table verbatim.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Building Blocks of Cloud Computing

Cloud computing is constructed from **four layered components**:

1. **Physical Layer (Datacenter Hardware):** Servers, switches, routers, storage arrays, and power/cooling infrastructure housed in geographically distributed data centers.
2. **Virtualization Layer:** The hypervisor (e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM, Xen) that abstracts physical hardware into multiple isolated Virtual Machines (VMs).
3. **Management / Orchestration Layer:** Software like OpenStack, Kubernetes, and Terraform that automates resource provisioning, scaling, and lifecycle management.
4. **Service / Application Layer:** The user-facing delivery of IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS offerings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Virtualization is the foundational technology** that makes cloud computing economically viable. Without it, multi-tenancy and elastic resource pooling would be impossible.

## 2.2 The Three Service Models (SPI Model)

### 2.2.1 Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)
- **What you get:** Raw compute, storage, and networking primitives (VMs, block storage, VPCs).
- **You manage:** OS, middleware, runtime, applications, data.
- **Provider manages:** Hardware, networking, storage, virtualization.
- **Examples:** Amazon EC2, Google Compute Engine, Microsoft Azure VMs, DigitalOcean Droplets.

### 2.2.2 Platform as a Service (PaaS)
- **What you get:** A development and deployment platform with OS, middleware, and runtime pre-configured.
- **You manage:** Your application code and data.
- **Provider manages:** Everything else (servers, OS, middleware, runtime).
- **Examples:** Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku, Microsoft Azure App Service.

### 2.2.3 Software as a Service (SaaS)
- **What you get:** A fully finished application accessible via a web browser or API.
- **You manage:** Only your user data and configuration.
- **Provider manages:** The entire stack.
- **Examples:** Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Dropbox, Zoom.

### 2.2.4 The Shared Responsibility Model

| Layer | On-Premise | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Application | You | You | You | Provider |
| Data | You | You | You | You |
| Runtime | You | You | Provider | Provider |
| Middleware | You | You | Provider | Provider |
| OS | You | You | Provider | Provider |
| Virtualization | You | Provider | Provider | Provider |
| Servers/Storage/Networking | You | Provider | Provider | Provider |

## 2.3 The Four Deployment Models

| Deployment Model | Description | Who Uses It |
|---|---|---|
| **Public Cloud** | Services offered over the public Internet; shared infrastructure. | Startups, SMEs, public-facing apps. |
| **Private Cloud** | Cloud infrastructure operated solely for a single organization. | Banks, defense, healthcare (regulated industries). |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Composition of two or more clouds (public + private) bound by orchestration. | Enterprises with burst workloads. |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared infrastructure for a specific community with shared concerns (e.g., government agencies). | Research consortiums, government departments. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Cloud computing problems often involve **cost calculation** and **capacity planning**. Memorize these formulas.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pay-per-use cost | $C = U \times P$ | $C$ = total cost, $U$ = units consumed, $P$ = price per unit | USD |
| Storage cost | $C_{s} = S \times P_{GB}$ | $S$ = storage size, $P_{GB}$ = price per GB/month | USD/month |
| Compute cost (hourly) | $C_{h} = T \times N \times P_{h}$ | $T$ = time (hours), $N$ = number of VMs, $P_{h}$ = price/VM-hour | USD |
| Egress cost | $C_{e} = D_{out} \times P_{e}$ | $D_{out}$ = data transferred out (GB), $P_{e}$ = price per GB egress | USD |
| Availability SLA | $A = \dfrac{U_{time}}{U_{time} + D_{time}} \times 100\%$ | $U_{time}$ = uptime, $D_{time}$ = downtime | Percent (%) |
| Annual Downtime | $D_{yr} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | $A$ as decimal, result in minutes/year | Minutes |
| Utilization rate | $\eta = \dfrac{U_{used}}{U_{capacity}} \times 100\%$ | $U_{used}$ = used units, $U_{capacity}$ = total available | Percent (%) |

### Standard SLA Reference Table

| Availability % | Allowed Downtime / Year | Allowed Downtime / Month |
|---|---|---|
| 99% ("two nines") | 3.65 days | 7.20 hours |
| 99.9% ("three nines") | 8.77 hours | 43.83 minutes |
| 99.99% ("four nines") | 52.60 minutes | 4.38 minutes |
| 99.999% ("five nines") | 5.26 minutes | 26.30 seconds |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These formulas are foundational for **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** analysis, **cloud migration business cases**, and **Capacity Planning (CP)** exercises in industry. KTU Part B questions often ask: *"Calculate the monthly bill for X GB storage and Y compute hours at provider pricing."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Compute Cost Calculation

**Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):**
A startup provisions **4 EC2 instances** at **\$0.05 per hour**, each running **24 × 7 = 168 hours/week**, for **4 weeks (1 month)**. Additionally, they store **500 GB of data** at **\$0.023 per GB-month**, and transfer **200 GB of egress data** at **\$0.09 per GB**. Calculate the **total monthly bill**.

### Step 1 — Identify all cost components
We have three cost streams:
- Compute cost: $C_{h} = T \times N \times P_{h}$
- Storage cost: $C_{s} = S \times P_{GB}$
- Egress cost: $C_{e} = D_{out} \times P_{e}$

### Step 2 — Compute the hourly compute cost
Substitute the values: $T = 168 \times 4 = 672$ hours, $N = 4$, $P_{h} = 0.05$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{h} &= T \times N \times P_{h} \\
&= 672 \times 4 \times 0.05 \\
&= 2688 \times 0.05 \\
&= \$134.40
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the storage cost
Substitute the values: $S = 500$ GB, $P_{GB} = 0.023$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{s} &= S \times P_{GB} \\
&= 500 \times 0.023 \\
&= \$11.50
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the egress cost
Substitute the values: $D_{out} = 200$ GB, $P_{e} = 0.09$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{e} &= D_{out} \times P_{e} \\
&= 200 \times 0.09 \\
&= \$18.00
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Sum the total monthly bill

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{h} + C_{s} + C_{e} \\
&= 134.40 + 11.50 + 18.00 \\
&= \$163.90
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The total monthly cloud bill is **\$163.90**. Notice how the compute cost dominates (82%), while storage and egress are minor contributors.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — SLA Downtime Derivation

**Problem:** A cloud provider advertises **99.95% availability**. Calculate the maximum allowed downtime per year and per month.

### Step 1 — Convert availability percentage to decimal

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 99.95\% = 0.9995
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute annual downtime using the formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{yr} &= (1 - A) \times 525{,}600 \\
&= (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600 \\
&= 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 \\
&= 262.8 \text{ minutes} \\
&\approx 4.38 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute monthly downtime
A month has, on average, $525{,}600 / 12 = 43{,}800$ minutes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{mo} &= (1 - A) \times 43{,}800 \\
&= 0.0005 \times 43{,}800 \\
&= 21.9 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** Always use **525,600 minutes per year** (60 × 24 × 365). For "thirty days" use **43,200 minutes**. Show both units for full marks.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Service Model

| Criterion | On-Premise | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Control Level** | Highest | High | Medium | Lowest |
| **Time to Deploy** | Weeks–Months | Minutes | Minutes | Instant |
| **Capital Cost** | Very High | Low | Low | Negligible |
| **Operational Cost** | High (staff, power) | Medium | Low | Subscription-based |
| **Best For** | Regulated, legacy workloads | Lift-and-shift migrations, custom OS needs | App development, APIs | End-user productivity, CRM, email |
| **Example Use Case** | Banking mainframe | Hosting a custom Linux VM | Deploying a Node.js web app | Using Gmail or Office 365 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userA[End User - Laptop] --> netA[Internet Gateway]
    userB[Mobile Device] --> netA
    userC[Enterprise Client] --> netA

    netA --> frontA[Load Balancer and WAF]
    frontA --> apiA[API Gateway and Authentication]

    apiA --> svcA[SaaS Layer - Gmail Office365]
    apiA --> svcB[PaaS Layer - App Engine Beanstalk]
    apiA --> svcC[IaaS Layer - EC2 Compute VMs]

    svcC --> hyperA[Hypervisor Layer - KVM ESXi]
    svcB --> runA[Container Orchestration - Kubernetes Docker]
    svcA --> appA[Application Binaries]

    hyperA --> physA[Physical Hardware - Servers Storage Network]
    runA --> physA
    appA --> physA

    physA --> dcA[Data Center Facility - Power Cooling Security]
```

## 4.2 NIST Cloud Computing Model — Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACTORS[Cloud Computing Actors]
        cspA[Cloud Provider]
        cspB[Cloud Consumer]
        cspC[Cloud Broker]
        cspD[Cloud Auditor]
        cspE[Cloud Carrier]
    end

    subgraph ACTIVITIES[Cloud Activities]
        actA[Service Provisioning]
        actB[Service Orchestration]
        actC[Service Deployment]
        actD[Monitoring and Metering]
    end

    subgraph MODELS[Service and Deployment Models]
        mdlA[IaaS PaaS SaaS]
        mdlB[Public Private Hybrid Community]
    end

    cspA --> actA
    cspB --> actC
    cspC --> actB
    cspD --> actD
    cspE --> actA
    actA --> mdlA
    actC --> mdlB
```

## 4.3 Service Model Stack — Responsibility Layering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L6[Application - SaaS User]
    L5[Data]
    L4[Runtime - PaaS Developer]
    L3[Middleware]
    L2[Operating System - IaaS User]
    L1[Virtualization - Hypervisor]
    L0[Servers Storage Networking - Cloud Provider]

    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L0

    noteUser[User Manages Above] -.-> L6
    noteIaas[User Manages] -.-> L4
    noteIaas2[IaaS Boundary] -.-> L2
    notePaas[PaaS Boundary] -.-> L3
    noteProvider[Provider Manages Below] -.-> L0
```

## 4.4 Elastic Scalability — Horizontal vs Vertical

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VS[Vertical Scaling - Scale Up]
        v1[Small VM] --> v2[Medium VM] --> v3[Large VM]
    end

    subgraph HS[Horizontal Scaling - Scale Out]
        h1[VM 1]
        h2[VM 2]
        h3[VM 3]
        h4[Load Balancer]
        h4 --> h1
        h4 --> h2
        h4 --> h3
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Cloud providers prefer **horizontal scaling (scale-out)** because it is more fault-tolerant and elastic. Vertical scaling has a hard ceiling (largest instance size). AWS Auto Scaling Groups (ASG) and Kubernetes ReplicaSets implement horizontal scaling.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define cloud computing. List the five essential characteristics of cloud computing as per the NIST definition.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort. (1.5 Marks)

The five essential characteristics are: **(1) On-demand self-service, (2) Broad network access, (3) Resource pooling, (4) Rapid elasticity, and (5) Measured service.** (1.5 Marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Model | User Manages | Provider Manages | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| IaaS | OS, runtime, app, data | Hardware, virtualization, storage, network | Amazon EC2 |
| PaaS | Application and data | OS, middleware, runtime, hardware | Google App Engine |
| SaaS | Only user data | Entire stack | Microsoft 365 / Gmail |

(1 mark per row × 3 = 3 Marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Question (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the NIST reference architecture of cloud computing in detail. List the actors, activities, and service/deployment models involved. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture defines the **five major actors**, **four primary activities**, and categorizes the **three service models** with **four deployment models**.

**Actors (3 Marks):**
1. **Cloud Consumer** — The individual or organization that uses the cloud services.
2. **Cloud Provider** — The entity responsible for making services available (e.g., AWS, Azure, GCP).
3. **Cloud Broker** — An intermediary that manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services between providers and consumers.
4. **Cloud Auditor** — An independent entity that evaluates cloud services for security, performance, and compliance.
5. **Cloud Carrier** — The intermediary that provides connectivity and transport of cloud services (e.g., ISPs, telecom providers).

**Activities (2 Marks):**
- **Service Provisioning** — Acquiring and configuring cloud resources.
- **Service Orchestration** — Coordinating multiple resources to fulfill user requests.
- **Service Deployment** — Delivering services to consumers.
- **Monitoring & Metering** — Tracking usage, performance, and SLAs.

**Service and Deployment Models (2 Marks):**
- *Service Models:* IaaS, PaaS, SaaS.
- *Deployment Models:* Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.

---

**(b) A company wants to migrate its on-premise web application to AWS. They need 10 EC2 t3.medium instances (priced at \$0.0416/hour), 2 TB of EBS storage at \$0.10/GB-month, and 1 TB of outbound data transfer at \$0.05/GB. Calculate the monthly bill assuming the servers run 24/7 for 30 days. Also, identify the cloud deployment model that best fits a regulated bank. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Hours in a Month**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 24 \times 30 = 720 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{compute} &= T \times N \times P_{h} \\
&= 720 \times 10 \times 0.0416 \\
&= 7200 \times 0.0416 \\
&= \$299.52 \\[6pt]
C_{storage} &= S_{GB} \times P_{GB} \\
&= (2 \times 1024) \times 0.10 \\
&= 2048 \times 0.10 \\
&= \$204.80 \\[6pt]
C_{egress} &= D_{out} \times P_{e} \\
&= (1 \times 1024) \times 0.05 \\
&= 1024 \times 0.05 \\
&= \$51.20 \\[6pt]
C_{total} &= 299.52 + 204.80 + 51.20 \\
&= \$555.52
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Deployment Model for a Regulated Bank (1 Mark)**
A **Private Cloud** is best suited for a regulated bank because it provides **dedicated infrastructure, full control over data, and compliance with regulations** such as RBI guidelines, PCI-DSS, and data residency laws. Public cloud may be used in a **Hybrid** configuration for non-sensitive workloads.

**Total Monthly Bill: \$555.52**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) With neat diagrams, explain the three cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) and clearly indicate the responsibility boundary between the user and the provider. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **cloud service models** form a layered stack, often visualized as an "onion" or "pizza":

- **IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service):** The user provisions raw infrastructure — VMs, block storage, virtual networks. The user is responsible for the OS, middleware, runtime, and application. Examples: **Amazon EC2, Google Compute Engine.** (2.5 Marks)

- **PaaS (Platform as a Service):** The provider supplies a managed platform with OS, runtime, and middleware pre-installed. The user only deploys application code and data. Examples: **Heroku, Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk.** (2.5 Marks)

- **SaaS (Software as a Service):** The provider delivers a fully functional application. The user only manages their data and user access. Examples: **Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Dropbox.** (2 Marks)

**Responsibility Boundary:** The boundary shifts **upward** from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS. In IaaS, the user manages everything above the hypervisor; in PaaS, only the application and data; in SaaS, only the data and identity.

---

**(b) Compare and contrast Public, Private, Hybrid, and Community cloud deployment models. Identify two scenarios where hybrid cloud is the most appropriate choice. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud | Community Cloud |
