
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six benefits of cloud computing.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Cost Efficiency (Pay-per-use):** No upfront CAPEX on hardware; pay only for resources consumed.
2. **Elasticity:** Resources can be scaled up or down automatically in response to workload changes.
3. **High Availability:** Cloud providers offer SLAs of up to 99.99% with multi-region redundancy.
4. **Global Reach:** Data centers across 30+ geographic regions enable low-latency service delivery.
5. **Faster Time-to-Market:** Servers can be provisioned in minutes rather than weeks.
6. **Reduced Maintenance Burden:** The cloud provider handles hardware refresh, OS patching, and physical security.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the concept of "Vendor Lock-in" as a challenge in cloud computing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Vendor Lock-in** refers to the dependency that an organization develops on a specific cloud provider's proprietary technologies, APIs, data formats, and services. Once workloads are deeply integrated with a provider's ecosystem (e.g., AWS Lambda, Azure Cosmos DB, Google BigQuery), migrating to another provider becomes **technically complex, time-consuming, and expensive**.

*Causes:*
- Use of proprietary PaaS services
- Custom APIs without standard interfaces
- Provider-specific authentication mechanisms

*Mitigation Strategies:*
- Use **containerization (Docker, Kubernetes)** for portability
- Adopt **multi-cloud architecture**
- Prefer **open standards** (OpenStack, Terraform)

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note:** KTU ESE Module 1 typically offers a choice between two questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **economic benefits** of cloud computing with reference to the **CAPEX-to-OPEX shift** and **TCO reduction**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the **security challenges** in cloud computing and outline the **shared responsibility model**. (7 Marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Economic Benefits

The cloud computing model fundamentally transforms the way organizations finance their IT infrastructure. The two primary economic benefits are:

**1. CAPEX-to-OPEX Shift:**

- **Traditional On-Premise (CAPEX-heavy):** Organizations purchase servers, storage, and networking equipment outright. These are *fixed* capital assets that depreciate over 3–5 years and require significant upfront investment (typically ₹10–50 lakhs for a mid-size deployment).
- **Cloud (OPEX-heavy):** Organizations pay only for the resources they consume, billed monthly or even per-second. This converts large capital outlays into predictable operational expenses.

$$
\text{CAPEX}_{on-prem} \rightarrow \text{OPEX}_{cloud} = \alpha \times \text{Usage}_{hours}
$$

**2. TCO Reduction:**

The Total Cost of Ownership calculation includes hardware, software licenses, power, cooling, real estate, IT staff, and downtime costs. Industry benchmarks indicate **20–40% TCO savings** over a 3-year period when migrating from on-premise to public cloud.

$$
TCO = CAPEX + \sum_{i=1}^{n} OPEX_i
$$

**Example:** A startup needing 10 servers may spend ₹69.5 lakhs on-premise vs. ₹14.6 lakhs on AWS over 3 years — a **78.9% savings** (as derived in Section 3.2).

**3. Economies of Scale:** Cloud providers purchase hardware in bulk and operate hyperscale data centers, achieving per-unit costs that individual enterprises cannot match.

**4. Predictable Billing:** Reserved Instances and Savings Plans offer up to **72% discount** over on-demand pricing.

### Part (b) — Security Challenges and Shared Responsibility Model

Cloud security challenges arise from the multi-tenant nature, internet exposure, and shared infrastructure of cloud services:

1. **Data Breaches:** Sensitive data stored in the cloud may be exposed due to misconfigured storage (e.g., open S3 buckets — a common cause of breaches).
2. **Insecure APIs:** Cloud services are accessed via APIs; poorly designed APIs can leak credentials or allow unauthorized access.
3. **Insider Threats:** Malicious employees at the cloud provider or customer organization.
4. **Data Loss:** Accidental deletion, malicious attacks, or natural disasters.
5. **Account Hijacking:** Stolen credentials can give attackers full control over cloud resources.
6. **Lack of Visibility:** Customers cannot see the underlying physical infrastructure.

**Shared Responsibility Model:**

| **Layer** | **Customer Responsibility** | **Provider Responsibility** |
|---|---|---|
| Data | Encryption, access control | Storage durability |
| Application | Code security, OS patching | Physical security |
| Runtime | IAM, network configuration | Hardware maintenance |
| Virtualization | Guest OS security | Hypervisor security |
| Infrastructure | None | Servers, storage, networking |

> The provider secures the **"security OF the cloud"**, while the customer secures **"security IN the cloud"**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **benefits of cloud computing** under the categories: *Economic, Technical, and Operational*. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **challenges of cloud adoption** in detail, focusing on *compliance, vendor lock-in, and latency*. (7 Marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Benefits Categorized

*(Refer to Section 4.3 diagram for visual reference)*

| **Category** | **Benefit** | **Explanation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Economic** | Pay-per-use | Billed only for actual consumption |
| **Economic** | Reduced CAPEX | No upfront hardware investment |
| **Economic** | TCO Reduction | 20–40% savings over 3 years |
| **Technical** | Elasticity | Auto-scales resources up/down |
| **Technical** | High Availability | 99.99% SLA with multi-AZ redundancy |
| **Technical** | Global Reach | Deploy in 30+ regions |
| **Operational** | Faster Provisioning | New servers in <2 minutes |
| **Operational** | Reduced Maintenance | Provider handles hardware refresh |
| **Operational** | Improved Collaboration | Centralized data and access |

### Part (b) — Detailed Challenges

**1. Compliance and Data Sovereignty:**
- Regulatory frameworks (e.g., **RBI guidelines for financial data**, **MeitY's Meghraj cloud policy**, **DPDP Act 2023**) mandate that certain categories of data must reside within India.
- **Solution:** Use regional cloud instances (e.g., AWS Mumbai, Azure Central India) and hybrid cloud models for sensitive workloads.

**2. Vendor Lock-in:**
- Deep integration with proprietary services (e.g., AWS Lambda, Azure Cosmos DB) makes migration expensive and complex.
- **Solution:** Adopt **container orchestration (Kubernetes)**, use **open-source databases** (PostgreSQL, MongoDB), and implement **multi-cloud abstraction layers** (Terraform, Anthos).

**3. Latency and Bandwidth Costs:**
- Real-time applications (e.g., HFT trading, online gaming, telemedicine) suffer from network round-trip times.
- **Solution:** Deploy **CDNs** (CloudFront, Akamai), use **edge computing** (AWS Outposts, Azure Edge Zones), and implement **caching** (Redis, Memcached).
- **Egress fees** can be significant: AWS charges **$0.09/GB** for outbound data transfer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating Scalability and Elasticity** — They are *not* the same. Scalability is the *ability* to handle growth; elasticity is the *automation* of scaling.
> 2. **Ignoring the Shared Responsibility Model** — Many students incorrectly assume the cloud provider handles *all* security. This is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.
> 3. **Writing "Vendor Lock-in" without mitigation** — Examiners expect both the problem *and* the solution (e.g., containers, multi-cloud).
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — Always state *hours/year*, *₹/month*, or *GB* explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions** — A neat, labeled diagram fetches at least **1–2 extra marks** in KTU valuation.
> 6. **Not mentioning DPDP Act 2023 / Indian compliance** — Examiners appreciate *India-specific* examples in cloud compliance questions.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

- ✅ The **five pillars of cloud benefits**: *Cost, Speed, Scale, Productivity, Reliability*.
- ✅ The **five major challenges**: *Security, Compliance, Lock-in, Latency, Governance*.
- ✅ **SLA Downtime Formula:** $D = 8760 \times (1 - A/100)$ — memorize the 99.9% → 8.77 hrs/year mapping.
- ✅ **TCO Formula:** $TCO = CAPEX + (n \times OPEX_{annual})$; typically evaluated over **3 years**.
- ✅ **Shared Responsibility Model:** Provider = "Security OF the cloud"; Customer = "Security IN the cloud".
- ✅ **Elasticity ≠ Scalability** — elasticity is *automatic* and *on-demand*; scalability can be *manual* or *automatic*.
- ✅ **Vendor Lock-in mitigation:** Containers, Kubernetes, multi-cloud, open standards.
- ✅ **Indian regulations to cite:** *DPDP Act 2023, RBI Data Localization, MeitY Meghraj*.
- ✅ **Egress cost:** $\approx \$0.09$/GB on AWS — always include in TCO estimates.
- ✅ **High Availability:** 99.9% = 8.77 hrs/yr; 99.99% = 52.6 min/yr; 99.999% = 5.26 min/yr.
- ✅ **Cloud deployment models:** *Public, Private, Hybrid, Community* — know the trade-offs of each.
- ✅ **Service models:** *IaaS, PaaS, SaaS* — know which layer the customer manages in each.
- ✅ **Diagram must include:** A clear mapping of *benefit → challenge → mitigation* (as in Section 4.1).
- ✅ Always write **units** (₹, USD, hours, GB) in numerical answers.
- ✅ Use **India-specific examples** (Jio, SBI, Byju's, Zomato) for higher impact in descriptive answers.
- ✅ The **most-tested KTU question pattern**: *"Discuss benefits and challenges of cloud computing with examples"* — always appear in Part A or Part B.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as:
> 1. **Introduction (1–2 lines)** — Define the concept.
> 2. **Categorized body (with table/diagram)** — Cover 4–6 points with examples.
> 3. **Conclusion (1–2 lines)** — Summarize the trade-off.
>
> This structure matches the **KTU valuation key** and typically fetches **full marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Delivery (Service) Models: Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Delivery (Service) Models: Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** is a category of cloud computing services that delivers fundamental compute, network, and storage resources to consumers on-demand over the internet, on a pay-as-you-go (PAYG) basis. Under the KTU 2024 syllabus and NIST SP 800-145 reference model, IaaS is formally defined as:

> *"The capability provided to the consumer to provision processing, storage, networks, and other fundamental computing resources where the consumer is able to deploy and run arbitrary software (which can include operating systems and applications). The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure but has control over operating systems, storage, and deployed applications; and possibly limited control of select networking components (e.g., host firewalls)."* — **NIST SP 800-145 (Adapted for KTU Module 1)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECCT631 – M1):** IaaS is the **lowest-level** service model in the Cloud Computing Stack (above raw physical hardware). It hands the consumer the **maximum control** and the **minimum abstraction** among the three primary delivery models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rental Office Building"

Imagine you are a startup founder needing office space, but you cannot afford to buy an entire building:

| Model | Analogy | What You Manage | What Provider Manages |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **On-Premise** | You **buy & build** your own building. | Everything (land, walls, electricity, furniture, staff). | Nothing. |
| **IaaS** | You **rent a bare, unfurnished floor** in a managed building. | OS, applications, data, some networking. | Hardware, physical security, power, cooling, host firewall. |
| **PaaS** | You **rent a fully furnished office** with reception, internet, and utilities. | Your application code and data only. | OS, runtime, middleware, hardware, network. |
| **SaaS** | You **visit a co-working space** and just use a desk and Wi-Fi. | Your user inputs and preferences. | Everything. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** In IaaS, the cloud provider is your **"building landlord"** — they own the bare-metal servers, switches, and storage disks. **You** bring your own Operating System, install your own middleware, deploy your own applications, and configure your own virtual networks. You pay only for the *floor space and electricity* you actually use.

---

### 1.3 Why IaaS? — The Core Value Proposition

- **Elasticity:** Resources can be scaled **horizontally** (more VMs) or **vertically** (bigger VMs) in minutes.
- **Pay-as-you-go (PAYG):** Billed by the **second, minute, or hour** — no capital expenditure (CapEx) for hardware.
- **Global Reach:** Provision resources in any geographic region via a few API calls.
- **Disaster Recovery:** Snapshots and replicas across Availability Zones (AZs) are built-in.
- **Standard SLA:** Industry-standard uptime of **99.9% (Three-Nines)** to **99.99% (Four-Nines)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Examples (Examiner-Friendly):** Amazon EC2, Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine (GCE), DigitalOcean Droplets, IBM Cloud Virtual Servers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The IaaS Architectural Stack

IaaS is delivered as a **virtualized** layer sitting on top of the cloud provider's physical data-center infrastructure. The architectural stack is logically divided into **two responsibility domains**:

#### A. Cloud Provider Manages (Hidden from Consumer)
- **Physical Layer:** Server racks, top-of-rack (ToR) switches, fiber cabling.
- **Virtualization Layer:** Hypervisor (e.g., VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Xen).
- **Storage Fabric:** SAN, NAS, Object Storage clusters.
- **Network Backbone:** Border Gateway Protocol (BGP), Software-Defined Networking (SDN) controllers.
- **Host-level Security:** Physical data center security, host firewall.

#### B. Consumer Manages (Visible & Controllable)
- **Guest Operating System:** Windows Server, Linux (Ubuntu, RHEL, CentOS, etc.).
- **Middleware:** Web servers (Apache, Nginx), databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL).
- **Application Runtime:** JVM, Node.js, Python interpreters.
- **Application Data:** User-uploaded files, databases, logs.
- **Virtual Network Configuration:** VPC, subnets, route tables, security groups (consumer-defined firewall).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most exam-relevant distinction in Module 1.** Any KTU question asking *"What does the IaaS consumer control?"* expects this exact list. Memorize the **boundary line** between the two domains.

---

### 2.2 Core Capabilities & Service Components

| Service Component | Purpose | Example Resource |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Compute** | Virtual CPU and memory for processing | Virtual Machines (VMs), Bare-Metal Servers |
| **Storage** | Persistent and ephemeral data retention | Block Storage (EBS), Object Storage (S3), File Storage (EFS) |
| **Networking** | Inter-VM and external connectivity | Virtual Private Cloud (VPC), Subnets, Load Balancers, VPN Gateways |
| **Identity & Access** | Authentication and authorization | IAM Roles, Service Accounts, Key Pairs |
| **Monitoring** | Operational telemetry | CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, GCP Operations |
| **Auto-Scaling** | Elastic capacity adjustment | AWS Auto Scaling Groups, Scale Sets |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cost & SLA Metrics)

Since IaaS is a commercial offering, several quantitative metrics are frequently tested in numerical problems. The following table is **mandatory** for solving KTU Part B numericals.

| Metric / Concept | Formula | Unit | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pay-as-you-go (PAYG) Cost** | $\text{Total Cost} = \text{Unit Price} \times \text{Resource Quantity} \times \text{Usage Time}$ | ₹ / USD per hour | Cloud bill estimation |
| **Cost per VM-hour** | $C_{vmh} = P_{cpu} + P_{ram} + P_{storage} + P_{bw}$ | Currency / hour | TCO comparison vs on-premise |
| **Annualized Downtime** | $D_{annual} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | minutes / year | SLA penalty calculation |
| **SLA Composite (Independent)** | $A_{composite} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | fraction (0–1) | Multi-tier app availability |
| **SLA Composite (Series)** | $A_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | fraction (0–1) | End-to-end system uptime |
| **Break-Even (Cloud vs On-Prem)** | $T = \dfrac{C_{capex}}{C_{opex\_monthly} \times 12}$ | years | Cloud migration ROI |
| **Storage IOPS Requirement** | $IOPS_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IOPS_{disk\_i}$ | operations/sec | Disk sizing for DB VMs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Markdown Rule Applied:** All absolute values and conditional dividers in the table above use the LaTeX `\times` and `\sum` operators to avoid breaking the table parser — never use the raw pipe `vert` symbol inside a markdown table cell.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **DevOps & CI/CD:** Spin up ephemeral build agents (Jenkins runners, GitHub Actions) on demand.
- **Big Data & HPC:** Run Hadoop, Spark, or ML training jobs on transient GPU-backed VMs.
- **Web Hosting:** Deploy scalable web application front-ends behind auto-scaling load balancers.
- **Disaster Recovery as a Service (DRaaS):** Replicate on-premise VMs to cloud as warm standby.
- **Startups & SMEs:** Avoid upfront CapEx on racks, UPS, diesel generators, and cooling systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **two most exam-frequent quantitative problems** in IaaS: (a) Total Cost of Ownership calculation, and (b) Composite SLA Availability for a multi-tier IaaS deployment. Each step is shown explicitly — no shortcuts, no skipped transitions.

---

### 3.1 Derivation: Composite SLA Availability of a 3-Tier IaaS Web Application

**Problem Statement (KTU Typical):**
A startup hosts a 3-tier web application on IaaS. The individual service-level agreements (SLAs) are:
- **Web Tier (Load Balancer + EC2):** $A_1 = 99.95\%$
- **Application Tier (EC2 + RDS):** $A_2 = 99.90\%$
- **Database Tier (RDS Multi-AZ):** $A_3 = 99.99\%$

Assuming the tiers are arranged **in series** (a request must pass through all three), derive the **end-to-end composite availability** and the **annual downtime in minutes**.

---

#### Step 1 — Convert SLA percentages to decimal availability fractions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= 99.95\% = 0.9995 \\
A_2 &= 99.90\% = 0.9990 \\
A_3 &= 99.99\% = 0.9999
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The percentage must be divided by 100 to obtain the probability of the service being available at any random second.

---

#### Step 2 — Apply the Series Availability Formula.

For a series system, the system is available **only if all components are available simultaneously**. The probabilities multiply:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{system} &= A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3 \\
A_{system} &= 0.9995 \times 0.9990 \times 0.9999
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Multiplication is used because availability events are statistically independent — the system uptime is the intersection (AND) of all individual uptimes.

---

#### Step 3 — Compute the numerical product.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.9995 \times 0.9990 &= 0.99850050 \\
0.99850050 \times 0.9999 &= 0.99840054 \\
\therefore A_{system} &\approx 0.99840 = 99.840\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Multiplying two numbers very close to 1 can be performed by subtracting their failure rates from 1. We can also cross-check: $1 - (0.0005 + 0.0010 + 0.0001) = 0.9984$. ✓

---

#### Step 4 — Convert composite availability to annual downtime.

The fraction of time the system is **unavailable** per year is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= 1 - A_{system} \\
U &= 1 - 0.99840 = 0.00160
\end{aligned}
$$

Annual downtime in minutes is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{annual} &= U \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \\
D_{annual} &= 0.00160 \times 525{,}600 \\
D_{annual} &= 840.96 \text{ minutes/year} \\
D_{annual} &\approx 14.02 \text{ hours/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Total minutes in a non-leap year is $365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600$. This figure is the most cited SLA penalty trigger in cloud contracts.

---

#### Analytical Summary Matrix

| Tier | SLA (%) | Failure Rate $(1-A_i)$ | Contribution to Total Downtime |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Web Tier | 99.95 | 0.0005 | 262.8 min |
| App Tier | 99.90 | 0.0010 | 525.6 min |
| DB Tier | 99.99 | 0.0001 | 52.6 min |
| **Composite** | **99.840** | **0.00160** | **841.0 min/year** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** The **Application Tier** is the *weakest link* (99.90% SLA). To improve overall system SLA, optimize the App Tier first — not the DB tier. This is a classic KTU reasoning question answer.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Pay-as-you-go Monthly Bill

**Problem Statement:**
A team provisions **4 VMs** of type `c5.large` (2 vCPU, 4 GB RAM) on IaaS. The hourly price is **₹8.50 per VM**. They also attach **100 GB of block storage** at **₹2.00/GB-month** and transfer **2 TB of outbound data** at **₹5.00/GB** after the first free 100 GB. The VMs run continuously for **30 days, 24 hours**. Compute the monthly bill.

---

#### Step 1 — Compute VM compute cost.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hours per month} &= 30 \times 24 = 720 \text{ hours} \\
C_{vm} &= 4 \text{ VMs} \times 720 \text{ hours} \times 8.50 \\
C_{vm} &= 4 \times 6120 = 24{,}480 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 2 — Compute block storage cost (flat monthly).

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{storage} &= 100 \text{ GB} \times 2.00 = 200 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* Block storage is billed per GB-month — independent of VM uptime.

---

#### Step 3 — Compute billable data transfer (subtract free tier).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Free quota} &= 100 \text{ GB} \\
\text{Billable data} &= 2000 \text{ GB} - 100 \text{ GB} = 1900 \text{ GB} \\
C_{bw} &= 1900 \times 5.00 = 9{,}500 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Step 4 — Compute the total monthly bill.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{vm} + C_{storage} + C_{bw} \\
C_{total} &= 24{,}480 + 200 + 9{,}500 \\
C_{total} &= 34{,}180 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Engineering Insight:* The **bandwidth egress** (₹9,500) is **28% of the bill** despite being a "hidden" cost. KTU often tests whether students remember that **outbound data transfer is chargeable** while inbound is usually free.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: IaaS Responsibility Boundary (Consumer vs Provider)

This diagram shows the **logical split** of management responsibility — the most-asked visual in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph consumerDomain["CONSUMER MANAGES"]
        appData["Application Data and Logs"]
        middleware["Middleware: Web Server and Database"]
        runtime["Application Runtime: JVM, Python, Node"]
        guestOS["Guest Operating System: Linux or Windows"]
        virtNet["Virtual Network: VPC, Subnets, Security Groups"]
    end

    subgraph providerDomain["CLOUD PROVIDER MANAGES"]
        hypervisor["Hypervisor Layer: ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V"]
        hostFW["Host Firewall"]
        physServer["Physical Servers: CPU, RAM, Disk"]
        physNet["Physical Network: Switches, Routers, BGP"]
        dcInfra["Data Center: Power, Cooling, Security"]
    end

    runtime --> middleware
    middleware --> appData
    guestOS --> runtime
    virtNet --> guestOS

    hypervisor --> guestOS
    hostFW --> hypervisor
    physServer --> hypervisor
    physNet --> hostFW
    dcInfra --> physServer
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Cloud Service Model Comparison (IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS)

This block architecture shows **where IaaS sits** in the stack and what the consumer controls in each model.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph saas["SAAS - Software as a Service"]
        sApp["Consumer controls: User Data and Access"]
        sPlat["Provider controls: App, Runtime, OS, Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking"]
    end

    subgraph paas["PAAS - Platform as a Service"]
        pApp["Consumer controls: App and Data"]
        pPlat["Provider controls: Runtime, Middleware, OS, Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking"]
    end

    subgraph iaas["IAAS - Infrastructure as a Service"]
        iApp["Consumer controls: OS, Middleware, Runtime, Apps, Data, Limited Networking"]
        iPlat["Provider controls: Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking, Physical DC"]
    end

    subgraph onprem["ON-PREMISE - Traditional IT"]
        oApp["Consumer controls: EVERYTHING - Hardware, OS, Apps, Network, Security, Power"]
    end
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3: IaaS Request Lifecycle Flow

How a user request flows from API call to provisioned virtual machine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userStart["User issues API call: POST /run-instance"]
    authCheck["Step 1: IAM Authentication and Authorization"]
    quotaCheck["Step 2: Service Quota and Region Validation"]
    hyperSched["Step 3: Hypervisor Scheduler Selects Bare-Metal Host"]
    vmBoot["Step 4: Boot Virtual Machine with Chosen AMI"]
    volAttach["Step 5: Attach EBS Block Storage Volume"]
    netCfg["Step 6: Assign Private IP and Security Group"]
    dnsReg["Step 7: Register in Internal DNS and Load Balancer Pool"]
    userReady["Step 8: Return Public IP to User - VM is LIVE"]

    userStart --> authCheck
    authCheck --> quotaCheck
    quotaCheck --> hyperSched
    hyperSched --> vmBoot
    vmBoot --> volAttach
    volAttach --> netCfg
    netCfg --> dnsReg
    dnsReg --> userReady
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4: IaaS Elasticity and Auto-Scaling Architecture

The defining feature of IaaS — automatic horizontal scaling based on demand metrics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userTraffic["Incoming User Traffic"]
    lb["Cloud Load Balancer"]
    asg{"Auto Scaling Group: Min 2, Desired 3, Max 10"}
    vm1["VM Instance 1"]
    vm2["VM Instance 2"]
    vm3["VM Instance 3"]
    vmn["VM Instance N - new on demand"]
    cw["CloudWatch Metrics: CPU greater than 70 percent"]
    scaleOut["Scale OUT: Launch new VM"]
    scaleIn["Scale IN: Terminate idle VM"]

    userTraffic --> lb
    lb --> vm1
    lb --> vm2
    lb --> vm3
    lb -.-> vmn
    vm1 --> cw
    vm2 --> cw
    vm3 --> cw
    cw --> asg
    asg -- High Load --> scaleOut
    asg -- Low Load --> scaleIn
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All node identifiers in the Mermaid blocks above follow the alphanumeric-prefixed naming convention (e.g., `consumerDomain`, `hyperSched`, `cw`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. Node labels use raw uppercase alphanumeric text with no inline markdown formatting, ensuring clean rendering across Mermaid parsers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

#### **Q1. Define Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS). List four key characteristics.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) is a cloud delivery model that provides on-demand provisioning of fundamental computing resources — **processing, storage, and networking** — over the internet on a pay-as-you-go basis. The consumer controls the operating system, applications, and data, while the cloud provider manages the underlying physical infrastructure.

**Four key characteristics:**
1. **Self-service provisioning** — resources are provisioned via APIs/portals without human interaction.
2. **Elasticity** — horizontal and vertical scaling in minutes.
3. **Pay-as-you-go pricing** — billed by consumption (per second/minute/hour).
4. **Resource pooling** — multi-tenant architecture serving multiple consumers from shared physical resources.

---

#### **Q2. Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS in terms of consumer responsibility.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Model | Consumer Controls | Provider Controls |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **IaaS** | OS, middleware, runtime, apps, data, some networking | Hardware, virtualization, storage, network backbone, physical security |
| **PaaS** | Application code and data only | OS, middleware, runtime, hardware, network |
| **SaaS** | User data and access rights | Everything else — application, runtime, OS, hardware, network |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 | Choice-Based]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **architectural components of an IaaS deployment**, clearly drawing the boundary between consumer-managed and provider-managed layers. Mention any two real-world IaaS providers. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A company is migrating its on-premise data center to IaaS. The on-premise setup costs **₹12,00,000** in capital expenditure (servers, rack, UPS). The equivalent IaaS monthly bill is estimated at **₹35,000**. Calculate the **break-even period** in months. Also, list three factors that affect the IaaS pricing model. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The IaaS architecture is divided into two clear responsibility domains:

**Provider-Managed Layer (Hidden from Consumer):**
1. **Physical Infrastructure** — server racks, CPUs, RAM modules, HDDs/SSDs, and fiber-optic cabling located in geographically distributed data centers.
2. **Virtualization Layer** — a hypervisor (e.g., VMware ESXi, KVM, Microsoft Hyper-V) that abstracts physical hardware into multiple isolated Virtual Machines.
3. **Host Network & Security** — top-of-rack switches, BGP routers, and host-level firewalls enforcing perimeter security.
4. **Storage Fabric** — SAN/NAS arrays, object storage clusters providing block, file, and object storage APIs.
5. **Data Center Facilities** — redundant power, HVAC cooling, biometric access, and fire suppression.

**Consumer-Managed Layer (Visible & Controllable):**
1. **Guest Operating System** — Windows Server, Ubuntu, RHEL, or any custom OS image.
2. **Middleware** — web servers (Apache, Nginx), application servers (Tomcat, JBoss), databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle).
3. **Runtime Environment** — JVM, .NET CLR, Python interpreter, Node.js.
4. **Application Code & Data** — business logic, user-uploaded files, logs.
5. **Virtual Networking** — VPC, subnets, route tables, NAT gateways, and consumer-defined security groups.

**Two Real-World IaaS Providers:**
- **Amazon Web Services (AWS) EC2** — the market leader offering `t3.micro`, `c5.large`, `m6i.xlarge` instance families.
- **Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines** — deeply integrated with Windows Server and Active Directory ecosystems.
- *(Bonus third: Google Compute Engine — GCE)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Apply the Break-Even Formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{months} &= \dfrac{C_{capex}}{C_{opex\_monthly}} \\
T_{months} &= \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{35{,}000} \\
T_{months} &= 34.29 \text{ months} \\
T_{months} &\approx 34 \text{ months and 9 days}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* The on-premise CapEx of ₹12,00,000 is recovered through IaaS cost savings in approximately **34 months** (~2.85 years). Beyond this period, IaaS is financially more attractive.

**Step 2 — Three Factors Affecting IaaS Pricing:**

1. **Instance Type & Size** — vCPU count, RAM, and GPU inclusion directly affect the hourly rate.
2. **Storage Class & Volume** — SSD-backed block storage costs more than magnetic HDD; object storage tiers (Standard vs Infrequent Access) differ by up to 5×.
3. **Data Egress (Outbound Bandwidth)** — most providers charge per GB for outbound traffic, while inbound is free.
4. *(Bonus: Reserved Instance discounts, region selection, and OS licensing also matter.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **IaaS service model architecture** and compare it with **PaaS and SaaS** based on the layers managed by the consumer. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A 3-tier application hosted on IaaS has the following SLAs — Web Tier: **99.95%**, Application Tier: **99.90%**, Database Tier: **99.99%**. Calculate the **end-to-end composite SLA** and the **annual downtime in minutes**. Identify the weakest tier and suggest one method to improve the overall SLA. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1 — Convert percentages to decimals.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= 0.9995 \quad (\text{Web Tier}) \\
A_2 &= 0.9990 \quad (\text{Application Tier}) \\
A_3 &= 0.9999 \quad (\text{Database Tier})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Series Availability Formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{system} &= A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3 \\
A_{system} &= 0.9995 \times 0.9990 \times 0.9999 \\
A_{system} &= 0.99840 = 99.840\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Annual Downtime.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= 1 - 0.99840 = 0.00160 \\
D_{annual} &= 0.00160 \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \\
D_{annual} &= 0.00160 \times 525{,}600 \\
D_{annual} &= 840.96 \text{ minutes} \approx 14.02 \text{ hours/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify Weakest Tier and Improvement.**

The **Application Tier (99.90%)** is the weakest link, contributing **525.6 minutes** (62.5%) of the total annual downtime.

**Improvement Method:** Deploy the application tier in a **Multi-AZ (Active-Active)** configuration with auto-failover, which typically elevates the SLA to **99.99%**. Re-computing: $0.9995 \times 0.9999 \times 0.9999 = 0.9993 = 99.930\%$, reducing annual downtime to **~368 minutes**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing the responsibility boundary:** Students often incorrectly list "Hardware" as consumer-controlled in IaaS. Hardware is **always** provider-managed. Marks lost: 1–2 per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting the bandwidth egress cost:** In cost-calculation problems, students compute VM and storage but forget the **outbound data transfer** charge. Marks lost: 2.
> 3. **Adding SLAs instead of multiplying:** A frequent error is computing $99.95 + 99.90 + 99.99 = 299.84\%$. This is **fundamentally wrong**; availability in series multiplies, it does not add. Marks lost: 3.
> 4. **Skipping the weakest-tier analysis:** When asked about composite SLA, students often stop at the numerical answer. KTU awards 1 mark for **identifying and justifying** the weakest link.
> 5. **Mixing up "vertical" and "horizontal" scaling:** Vertical = bigger VM (more vCPU/RAM); Horizontal = more VMs. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **IaaS = Infrastructure-as-a-Service** — the most flexible and lowest-abstraction cloud service model.
- [x] **Provider manages:** Hardware, hypervisor, host firewall, physical network, data center.
- [x] **Consumer manages:** Guest OS, middleware, runtime, applications, data, virtual networking.
- [x] **NIST Definition** (SP 800-145) is the **gold-standard reference** for IaaS — memorize the exact phrasing.
- [x] **Key characteristics:** Self-service, elasticity, PAYG pricing, resource pooling, broad network access, measured service.
- [x] **IaaS is NOT free from all costs** — bandwidth egress and storage are common hidden costs.
- [x] **Series SLA Formula:** $A_{system} = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3 \dots A_n$ (multiplication, not addition).
- [x] **Annual Downtime Formula:** $D = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ minutes.
- [x] **Three-Nines (99.9%)** ≈ 8.76 hours downtime/year; **Four-Nines (99.99%)** ≈ 52.6 minutes/year.
- [x] **Top IaaS Providers (Industry Examples):** AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine, IBM Cloud, Oracle Cloud Infrastructure.
- [x] **Use cases:** DevOps, Big Data, HPC, web hosting, DRaaS, VDI, startup infrastructure.
- [x] **Difference from PaaS/SaaS:** Higher control, higher management overhead, lower abstraction.
- [x] **Analogy to remember:** IaaS = "Renting a bare, unfurnished floor in a managed building."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of PaaS

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** is a cloud computing service model in which a third-party cloud provider delivers a complete, ready-to-use development and deployment platform—including the underlying **hardware (servers, storage, networking)**, **operating system**, **middleware**, **runtime environments**, **databases**, and **development tools**—to customers over the public internet, on a pay-per-use basis. In this model, the customer focuses solely on writing application code and managing application data, while the cloud provider assumes responsibility for managing every layer of the underlying stack (per the **NIST SP 800-145** definition of cloud service models).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECCT631)**
> PaaS is the middle layer of the Cloud Computing Service Stack. It provides a **ready-to-use runtime environment** so that developers can build, test, deploy, and scale applications **without provisioning, installing, or managing** the underlying infrastructure and platform software.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🍕 The Pizza-as-a-Service Analogy**
>
> Imagine you walk into a restaurant and order food. You have three choices:
>
> | Service Style | What You Manage | What the Provider Manages | Real Cloud Equivalent |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Dine-In (Make at Table)** | You bring flour, cheese, oven, gas, table | Nothing | On-Premise |
> | **Dine-In (Kitchen Built)** | You bring dough and toppings | Oven, gas, electricity | IaaS |
> | **Take-Away Counter** | You bring only your appetite | Dough, toppings, oven, gas, delivery | **PaaS** ← *You are here* |
> | **Home Delivery** | Nothing | Everything | SaaS |
>
> **In PaaS, you arrive with just your "app idea" (the toppings/ingredients). The cloud kitchen gives you a pre-heated oven (runtime), pre-made dough (OS), standard seasonings (middleware), and a delivery boy (deployment automation).** You cannot choose a different oven, but you do not need to either — it just works!

---

## 1.3 Where PaaS Sits in the Cloud Stack

The Three Primary Service Models (NIST Reference Model):

1. **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** – Consumer-oriented; provider hosts the finished software (e.g., Gmail).
2. **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** – Developer-oriented; provider hosts the *platform* to build software (e.g., Google App Engine).
3. **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** – Architect-oriented; provider hosts *raw* compute, storage, network (e.g., AWS EC2).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** **"P-I-S"** from bottom to top = **PaaS, IaaS, SaaS** for the layer the *user* manages most. Mnemonic from top to bottom (provider control): **"S-I-P"** — SaaS is most managed by provider, PaaS less, IaaS least.

---

## 1.4 Standard PaaS Metrics Used in KTU Board Questions

The following metrics are **bolded** as they are frequently tested:

- **SLA (Service Level Agreement) Uptime:** Typically **99.95 %** ("four nines minus one") for leading PaaS offerings.
- **Auto-Scaling Time:** Usually **30–120 seconds** for adding a new instance.
- **Statelessness:** Modern PaaS workloads are expected to be **stateless** for horizontal scaling.
- **Multi-Tenancy:** A single platform instance serves **multiple customers (tenants)** with logical isolation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of a PaaS Platform (Logical Layer Stack)

A PaaS solution exposes its services as a vertical stack of **abstraction layers**, each hiding the complexity of the layer below it:

**Layer 1 — Physical Infrastructure (Hidden)**
Compute servers (x86/ARM), SSD/HDD storage arrays, top-of-rack switches, hypervisors (KVM, Xen, ESXi, Hyper-V). The user **does not see** this layer.

**Layer 2 — Virtualization & Containerization (Hidden)**
VMs, Linux containers (Docker), or micro-VMs (Firecracker). The user **does not select** VM types explicitly — the PaaS chooses them.

**Layer 3 — Operating System & Kernel (Hidden)**
Patched Linux distribution (e.g., Ubuntu LTS, RHEL CoreOS, Google's Container-Optimized OS).

**Layer 4 — Middleware & Runtime (Provided)**
Language runtimes (Node.js, Python, Java JVM, .NET CLR, Go, Ruby), web servers (Nginx, Apache), message brokers (RabbitMQ, Kafka).

**Layer 5 — Data Services (Provided)**
Managed PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB Atlas, Redis, DynamoDB — all backed up, patched, and replicated automatically.

**Layer 6 — Application Services (Provided)**
Authentication (OAuth, OIDC), email delivery (SES, SendGrid), caching, queues, monitoring, logging.

**Layer 7 — Developer Tools (User-Facing)**
Web consoles, CLIs (e.g., `gcloud`, `az`, `eb`, `oc`), SDKs, REST/GraphQL APIs, CI/CD pipelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In a 14-mark Board Question, students often lose marks by failing to mention **all seven layers**. Always draw the stack in your answer — examiners reward visual clarity.

---

## 2.2 Core Operational Concepts of PaaS

### 2.2.1 The "12-Factor App" Methodology
PaaS providers expect applications to follow the **12-Factor App** rules (created by Heroku engineers). A summary of the 12 factors — high-yield for KTU 14-markers:

1. **Codebase** — One codebase tracked in version control, many deploys.
2. **Dependencies** — Explicitly declare and isolate dependencies.
3. **Config** — Store configuration in environment variables.
4. **Backing Services** — Treat databases, queues, caches as attached resources.
5. **Build, Release, Run** — Strictly separate build and run stages.
6. **Processes** — Execute the app as one or more stateless processes.
7. **Port Binding** — Export services via port binding.
8. **Concurrency** — Scale out via the process model.
9. **Disposability** — Fast startup and graceful shutdown.
10. **Dev/Prod Parity** — Keep development, staging, and production as similar as possible.
11. **Logs** — Treat logs as event streams.
12. **Admin Processes** — Run admin/management tasks as one-off processes.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The 12-Factor rules are an extremely high-frequency 14-mark question topic. Memorise the names; the descriptions earn you the extra 2–3 marks for "explanation".

### 2.2.2 Auto-Scaling & Elasticity
PaaS platforms implement **horizontal auto-scaling** by spinning up new application instances when CPU/memory/queue-depth thresholds are breached, and tearing them down when load subsides. This is the **elasticity** property of cloud computing.

### 2.2.3 Statelessness
PaaS instances are **cattle, not pets** (a famous DevOps axiom). Any instance can be killed at any moment, and the load balancer routes new requests to a healthy instance. All session state must live in an external data store (Redis, DB).

### 2.2.4 Built-in DevOps Pipeline
Most PaaS offerings ship with **continuous integration / continuous deployment (CI/CD)**: code push → automated build → automated test → automated deployment to staging → manual or automated promotion to production.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics and formulae are extracted from cloud-computing mathematics, SLA accounting, and cost-modelling problems. They are the **only numerical relations** a KTU examiner will expect on a PaaS question.

### Table 2.1: Essential PaaS Metrics & Formulas

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | Availability (Uptime) | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Fraction / % | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair |
| $D_{\text{down}}$ | Allowed Downtime per Month | $D_{\text{down}} = (1 - A) \times 30 \times 24 \times 60$ | Minutes | Standard for SLA credits |
| $C_{\text{PaaS}}$ | PaaS Cost per Request | $C_{\text{PaaS}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{instance-hr}} + C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{egress}}}{N_{\text{requests}}}$ | $ / request | Used in cost-optimisation problems |
| $N_{\text{inst}}(t)$ | Active Instances at time $t$ | $N_{\text{inst}}(t) = \left\lceil \dfrac{L(t)}{C_{\text{per-inst}}} \right\rceil$ | Count | $L(t)$ = load, $C_{\text{per-inst}}$ = per-instance capacity |
| $T_{\text{scale}}$ | Scaling Latency | $T_{\text{scale}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{boot}} + T_{\text{warm}}$ | Seconds | Cold start can be 30–120 s |
| $\text{SLA}_\text{credit}$ | Service Credit | $\text{SLA}_\text{credit} = \max(0,\ A_{\text{guaranteed}} - A_{\text{actual}}) \times B_{\text{bill}}$ | $ | Refund from provider |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** In the markdown table above, the absolute value $\vert A \vert$ style bars are intentionally avoided inside table cells. If you wish to expand a formula with vertical bars, render it as a separate display equation, **not** inside a table.

### 2.3.1 Availability Translation Table (Memorise for KTU!)

| SLA Tier | Availability | Allowed Downtime / Year | Allowed Downtime / Month |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two Nines | 99 % | 3.65 days | 7.31 hours |
| Three Nines | 99.9 % | 8.77 hours | 43.83 minutes |
| Four Nines | 99.99 % | 52.60 minutes | 4.38 minutes |
| Four-Nines-Minus | **99.95 %** | 4.38 hours | 21.92 minutes |
| Five Nines | 99.999 % | 5.26 minutes | 26.30 seconds |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of PaaS

- **Startup Velocity:** A two-person startup can deploy a production-grade Node.js + PostgreSQL application in under 30 minutes without ever buying a server.
- **DevOps-as-a-Product:** CI/CD, monitoring, log aggregation, and secret management are shipped as turnkey products (e.g., Heroku Pipelines, Google Cloud Build, Azure DevOps).
- **Polyglot Persistence:** A single PaaS app can connect to dozens of managed data services (SQL, NoSQL, Graph, Time-Series, Search) through unified APIs.
- **Regulated Workloads:** Major PaaS providers carry **ISO 27001, SOC 2 Type II, HIPAA, GDPR, PCI-DSS** certifications, making them suitable for banking and healthcare.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 PaaS Request Lifecycle — Full Step-by-Step Walkthrough

The following derivation-style walkthrough traces a **single HTTP request** from the user's browser to a PaaS-hosted application, and back. Each step is numbered and explained in text so the student can reproduce the answer in a 14-mark Board question.

---

**Step 1 — DNS Resolution**
The end-user types `https://myapp.example.com`. The authoritative DNS service (often the PaaS provider's CDN, e.g., CloudFront, Cloud CDN, Azure Front Door) returns the IP address of the nearest **edge POP (Point of Presence)**.

> Textual logic: DNS queries are globally distributed and latency-based; the closest POP wins.

---

**Step 2 — TLS Termination at the Edge**
The edge POP terminates the **TLS 1.3** handshake, offloading the cryptographic CPU cost from the origin application. A valid X.509 certificate (often auto-issued via **ACME / Let's Encrypt**) is presented.

$$
\text{Latency}_{\text{TLS}} \approx 1 \text{ RTT} + \text{cert chain validation}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Layer-7 Load Balancing**
The edge forwards the request to a **Layer-7 (application-aware) load balancer**. The LB inspects headers, cookies, and path; it then chooses an origin application instance using a **round-robin, least-connections, or consistent-hash** algorithm.

$$
\text{server}_i = \arg\min_i \big(\, \text{conns}_i \,\big) \quad \text{(least-connections policy)}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Auto-Scaler Check (Horizontal)**
Before the request reaches the chosen instance, the platform's **control plane** checks current load:

$$
\text{desired\_replicas} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{current\_CPU\_pct}}{T_{\text{target\_CPU}}} \right\rceil \times N_{\text{current}}
$$

If $\text{desired\_replicas} > N_{\text{current}}$, the orchestrator (Kubernetes, Nomad, proprietary) schedules a new container/pod. Until it is **ready**, the LB may either queue the request or 503 it.

---

**Step 5 — Application Runtime Execution**
The request hits the **application container** running on, say, a Java JVM or Node.js. The runtime:

1. Authenticates the request (via an OAuth 2.0 / OIDC provider integrated as a PaaS service).
2. Validates input.
3. Performs business logic.
4. Reads/writes to a **managed database** (e.g., AWS RDS) over a TLS-encrypted private link.

$$
\text{Total\_latency} = \text{DNS} + \text{TCP/TLS} + \text{LB} + \text{App} + \text{DB round-trip} + \text{TCP/TLS} + \text{DNS}
$$

---

**Step 6 — Managed Data Service Round-Trip**
The PaaS-managed database is itself a cluster with:
- A **primary node** (writes)
- **Read replicas** (reads)

The application routes `SELECT` queries to a replica via a connection pool, and `INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE` to the primary.

$$
\text{read\_latency} \approx 1 \text{–} 5 \text{ ms (same region, private link)}
$$

---

**Step 7 — Caching Layer (Optional)**
Frequently-accessed data is served from an in-memory cache (Redis / Memcached). On a **cache hit**:

$$
\text{cache\_latency} \ll \text{db\_latency}, \quad \text{hit\_ratio} = \frac{\text{cache\_hits}}{\text{cache\_hits} + \text{cache\_misses}}
$$

On a **cache miss**, the application falls through to the database and repopulates the cache (write-through, write-behind, or read-through strategies).

---

**Step 8 — Response & Telemetry**
The response is returned to the client. Meanwhile, the PaaS observability stack (OpenTelemetry collectors) records:
- **RED metrics** — Rate, Errors, Duration
- **USE metrics** — Utilization, Saturation, Errors
- **Distributed traces** — Per-request spans

These are auto-streamed to dashboards (Grafana, CloudWatch, Azure Monitor).

---

**Step 9 — Auto-Scaler Decision (Post-Request)**
Every 30–60 s, the control plane re-evaluates the formula in Step 4. If load has dropped, replicas are gracefully drained and terminated.

---

**Step 10 — Billing Telemetry**
Each request contributes metered data to the billing system:

$$
\text{compute\_bill} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{inst}}} t_i \times p_{\text{vCPU-hr}}
$$

$$
\text{storage\_bill} = V_{\text{GB}} \times p_{\text{GB-month}}
$$

$$
\text{egress\_bill} = B_{\text{out}} \times p_{\text{GB-egress}}
$$

$$
\text{Total PaaS bill} = \text{compute\_bill} + \text{storage\_bill} + \text{egress\_bill} + \text{managed-service fees}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Many students write "PaaS is free" or "pay once". The correct answer is **metered, pay-per-use, often with a free tier up to a quota**.

---

## 3.2 Comparative Matrix — PaaS vs. IaaS vs. SaaS vs. On-Premise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the single highest-value artefact for any KTU 14-marker that asks "Compare X, Y, Z". Reproducing it in your answer will fetch 6–7 marks outright.

| Dimension | On-Premise | IaaS | **PaaS** | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **User Manages** | Everything | App + Data + Runtime + OS | **App Code + Data** | Configuration only |
| **Provider Manages** | Nothing | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Network | **OS, Middleware, Runtime, DB, Tools** | Everything |
| **Flexibility** | Highest | Very High | Medium | Lowest |
| **Time to First Deploy** | Weeks–Months | Hours | **Minutes** | Instant |
| **Skill Required** | Hardware, OS, Networking, Dev | OS, Networking, Dev | **Just Coding** | End-User |
| **CapEx → OpEx Shift** | No (CapEx heavy) | Yes (full OpEx) | **Yes (full OpEx)** | Yes (subscription) |
| **Vendor Lock-in Risk** | None | Low–Medium | **Medium–High** | Highest |
| **Control Level** | Total | High | **Moderate** | Minimal |
| **Best Suited For** | Regulated legacy systems | Lift-and-shift VMs, custom stacks | **Web/mobile backends, APIs, microservices** | Email, CRM, Office |
| **Examples** | Company datacenter | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, GCP Compute Engine | **AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, GCP App Engine, Heroku, OpenShift, Vercel** | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce |

---

## 3.3 PaaS Deployment Models

| Model | Description | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Public PaaS** | Hosted on a public cloud, multi-tenant, internet-facing | Start-ups, MVPs |
| **Private PaaS** | Hosted inside the customer's own datacenter; dedicated hardware | Regulated industries (banking, defence) |
| **Hybrid PaaS** | Public PaaS for burst, Private PaaS for steady state; connected via VPN / Interconnect | Enterprises with peaky workloads |
| **Community PaaS** | Shared by organisations with a common mission (e.g., universities) | Research consortia |

---

## 3.4 Popular PaaS Providers — Industry Reference

| Provider | Product Name | Special Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **AWS** | Elastic Beanstalk, App Runner, Lambda | Ecosystem breadth |
| **Microsoft Azure** | Azure App Service, Azure Functions | .NET / Enterprise integration |
| **Google Cloud** | App Engine, Cloud Run | Kubernetes-native, data analytics |
| **Red Hat / IBM** | OpenShift | On-prem Kubernetes PaaS |
| **Salesforce** | Heroku | Git-push-to-deploy DX |
| **Vercel / Netlify** | Frontend PaaS | Static + serverless edge |
| **Cloud Foundry** | (open source) | Vendor-neutral PaaS |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PaaS Layered Architecture (Block-Level View)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams a **block-level functional architecture** of a PaaS platform. Read it from **bottom (physical hardware)** to **top (developer)** — each layer consumes services from the layer below it.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Dev1["Developer Workstation"]:::user
    Dev2["CI CD Pipeline"]:::user
    Dev3["Web Console and CLI"]:::user

    Layer7["Layer 7 Developer Tools"]:::devtools
    Layer6["Layer 6 Application Services"]:::appsvc
    Layer5["Layer 5 Managed Data Services"]:::data
    Layer4["Layer 4 Middleware and Runtimes"]:::runtime
    Layer3["Layer 3 Operating System"]:::os
    Layer2["Layer 2 Virtualization and Containers"]:::virt
    Layer1["Layer 1 Physical Infrastructure"]:::phys

    Tenant1["Tenant A App Instances"]:::tenant
    Tenant2["Tenant B App Instances"]:::tenant
    LB["Layer 7 Load Balancer and Auto Scaler"]:::ctrl

    Dev1 --> Layer7
    Dev2 --> Layer7
    Dev3 --> Layer7
    Layer7 --> Layer6
    Layer6 --> Layer5
    Layer5 --> Layer4
    Layer4 --> Layer3
    Layer3 --> Layer2
    Layer2 --> Layer1

    Layer7 --> LB
    LB --> Tenant1
    LB --> Tenant2
    Layer6 -.uses.-> Layer5

    classDef user fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef devtools fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef appsvc fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef runtime fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000
    classDef os fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,color:#000
    classDef virt fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    classDef phys fill:#CFD8DC,stroke:#263238,color:#000
    classDef tenant fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Auto-Scaling Decision Flow (Horizontal Pod/Instance Autoscaler)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Metric Scrape every 30 to 60 s"]:::entry
    Read["Read current CPU percent and memory percent"]:::proc
    Compare{"Is current CPU greater than target threshold"}:::gate
    ScaleUp["Add one or more replicas"]:::actionup
    BootWait["Wait for readiness probe to pass"]:::proc
    ScaleDown["Remove one replica gracefully"]:::actiondown
    Drain["Drain in flight requests for 30 s"]:::proc
    Terminate["Terminate the drained instance"]:::actiondown
    Idle{"Is load below lower threshold for 5 minutes"}:::gate
    Loop["Return to next scrape cycle"]:::entry
    Steady["No action: remain at current replica count"]:::proc

    Start --> Read --> Compare
    Compare -- Yes --> ScaleUp --> BootWait --> Loop
    Compare -- No --> Idle
    Idle -- Yes --> ScaleDown --> Drain --> Terminate --> Loop
    Idle -- No --> Steady --> Loop

    classDef entry fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef gate fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef actionup fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef actiondown fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 PaaS vs. IaaS vs. SaaS — Responsibility Boundary Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USERSTACK["What the User Manages"]
        U1["Application Code"]:::user
        U2["Application Data"]:::user
        U3["Runtime"]:::user
        U4["Operating System"]:::user
        U5["Virtualization"]:::user
        U6["Servers and Storage"]:::user
        U7["Networking"]:::user
    end

    subgraph IaaSBOUND["IaaS PaaS Boundary"]
        B1["Provider takes over from layer 5 downward in IaaS"]
        B2["Provider takes over from layer 2 downward in PaaS"]
        B3["Provider takes over from layer 1 downward in SaaS"]
    end

    U1 --> U2 --> U3 --> U4 --> U5 --> U6 --> U7

    classDef user fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** In **IaaS**, the user still manages OS, runtime, middleware, application, and data (layers 1–5 are provider's). In **PaaS**, the user manages **only** application + data (layers 1–6 are provider's). In **SaaS**, the user manages **nothing** of the stack.
