- **Cloud Characteristics Still Apply:** Self-service, elasticity, resource pooling, metered usage, broad network access — these *must* be present; otherwise it's just virtualization, not a private cloud.
- **Standard SLA Tiers:** 99.9% (three nines) = 8.77 hrs/yr downtime; 99.99% (four nines) = 52.6 min/yr; 99.999% (five nines) = 5.26 min/yr.
- **TCO Rule of Thumb:** Private cloud is cheaper for *steady-state workloads* running >60% utilization over 3+ years; public cloud is cheaper for *spiky, low-utilization* workloads.
- **Compliance Use Cases:** BFSI (RBI data residency), Healthcare (HIPAA), Defence (data sovereignty), Government (MeghRaj, DruCloud).
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $TCO = C_{capex} + C_{opex} \times T$
  - $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
  - $N_{max} = \min\left(\lfloor RAM_{effective} / RAM_{VM} \rfloor, \lfloor vCPU_{host} / vCPU_{VM} \rfloor\right)$
  - $D_{year} = (1 - A) \times 525600 \text{ minutes}$
- **Common Exam Traps:** Confusing private cloud with traditional IT (must have self-service + elasticity); confusing VPC with true private cloud (VPC is a public cloud feature); forgetting the hypervisor overhead in sizing calculations.
- **Real-World Indian Examples:** MeghRaj (Govt. of India GI Cloud), DruCloud (Defence Cloud), HDFC Bank's private cloud, Infosys borderless private cloud.
- **Virtualization ≠ Cloud:** Virtualization is a *technology*; cloud is a *model* that uses virtualization as one of its enablers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hybrid Cloud ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] **Hybrid Cloud — Official Definition (NIST SP 500-292 aligned)** > A **Hybrid Cloud** is a cloud computing deployment model composed of **two or more distinct cloud infrastructures** (private, public, or community) that remain **unique entities**, but are **bound together by standardized or proprietary technology** that enables **data and application portability** (e.g., bursting for load balancing between clouds). In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Cloud Infrastructure and Systems*, the **Hybrid Cloud** is defined as a **unified compute architecture** that integrates: 1. An **on-premises private cloud** (or on-prem data center). 2. **One or more public cloud providers** (AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform, etc.). 3. A **secure orchestration and connectivity layer** (VPN, Direct Connect, ExpressRoute, Interconnect). The integration is mediated through a **Cloud Management Platform (CMP)** and supported by **APIs, containers, and Kubernetes orchestration** to ensure workload portability. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] **Think of Hybrid Cloud as a "Two-Story House with a Backyard Office"** > - **Private Cloud (the main house)** → You live here, store personal valuables, host family events. It is **secure, controlled, and always accessible** to you. This represents your **on-premises data center** with sensitive data. > - **Public Cloud (the rented coworking space in the city)** → You don't own it, but you rent a desk whenever you need extra space for a project, a meeting, or to host a large workshop. It is **scalable and cost-effective for peak demands**. This is your **AWS/Azure/GCP tenant**. > - **The road/walkway between your house and the office** → This is your **secure network connection (VPN/Direct Connect)** that lets you move files, people (workloads), and resources between the two locations seamlessly. > - **Your house manager (the Cloud Management Platform)** → Decides whether a task should be done at home (private) or at the office (public), based on cost, security, and capacity. When a sudden storm hits and your home gets crowded, you send guests to the coworking space — this is **"cloud bursting"** in action. --- ### 1.3 Core Characteristics of Hybrid Cloud (KTU Board Favorites) > [!IMPORTANT] **Key Characteristics (Memorize for 2/3 mark questions):** > - **Portability** — Applications and data can move freely between environments. > - **Interoperability** — Standardized APIs (REST, gRPC) allow communication. > - **Scalability (Elasticity via Bursting)** — Private cloud scales *out* to public cloud on demand. > - **Control + Flexibility** — Retain sensitive data on-prem; leverage public cloud for variable workloads. > - **Cost Optimization** — Pay only for public cloud resources when actually consumed. > - **Compliance-Aware** — Data residency requirements (e.g., DPDPA 2023, GDPR) are honored by keeping regulated data on-prem. --- ### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Terminology | Term | Standard Definition | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | **Cloud Bursting Threshold** | CPU/Utilization % at which workload spills over | **70% – 80%** | | **RTT (Round Trip Time)** | Latency between private and public cloud | **< 50 ms** (ideal) | | **Bandwidth** | Dedicated link capacity | **1 Gbps – 10 Gbps** | | **SLA Uptime** | Public cloud provider guarantee | **99.9% – 99.99%** | | **Data Gravity** | Tendency of data to attract applications/services | High for hybrid | --- ### 1.5 Hybrid Cloud vs. Other Deployment Models | Feature | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud | Community Cloud | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Ownership** | Third-party provider | Single organization | Combined | Multiple orgs (shared) | | **Scalability** | **Very High** | Limited | **Very High (with bursting)** | Moderate | | **Security Control** | Low–Medium | **Very High** | High (selective) | Medium | | **Cost Model** | OPEX | CAPEX + OPEX | **Optimized Hybrid** | Shared CAPEX | | **Internet Exposure** | Required | Optional | Hybrid gateway | Optional | | **Best For** | Variable workloads | Sensitive/Regulated | **Mixed compliance + scale** | Govt/Research consortia | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Three-Layer Hybrid Cloud Architecture > [!NOTE] **Architecture Layers (Top-Down Stack):** #### **Layer 1 — Application & Workload Layer** - Stateless microservices (containers, serverless functions). - Stateful applications with persistent data tier. - **Workload Classification Logic:** - **Latency-sensitive (e.g., OLTP, real-time analytics)** → Private cloud. - **Compute-intensive, bursty (e.g., ML training, video transcoding)** → Public cloud. - **Regulated/PII data** → Private cloud; non-sensitive processing → Public. #### **Layer 2 — Orchestration & Management Layer** - **Cloud Management Platform (CMP):** VMware vRealize, Morpheus, IBM Cloud Pak. - **Container Orchestrators:** Kubernetes (EKS, AKS, GKE on-prem). - **Service Mesh:** Istio, Linkerd for cross-cluster communication. - **IaC (Infrastructure as Code):** Terraform, Ansible, Pulumi. #### **Layer 3 — Connectivity & Security Layer** - **Network Fabric:** - **IPsec VPN** (encrypted tunnels over public internet). - **Direct Connect / ExpressRoute / Cloud Interconnect** (dedicated leased lines). - **SD-WAN** (software-defined WAN optimization). - **Identity & Access:** Federated SSO (SAML 2.0, OAuth 2.0, OIDC). - **Encryption:** TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest. - **Zero-Trust Architecture:** BeyondCorp, Zscaler, Prisma Access. --- ### 2.2 The Three Pillars of Hybrid Cloud Design > [!IMPORTANT] **Why Hybrid? The Three Engineering Drivers:** 1. **Pillar 1 — Compliance & Data Sovereignty** - Reserve **PII, PHI, financial records** on private cloud. - Comply with **DPDPA 2023 (India), HIPAA, PCI-DSS, GDPR**. - Public cloud used only for **de-identified/aggregated data**. 2. **Pillar 2 — Elastic Scalability (Bursting)** - Baseline capacity provisioned on private cloud. - Surplus demand offloaded to public cloud via **auto-scaling policies**. - Use case: E-commerce flash sales, ticket booking, festival-period traffic. 3. **Pillar 3 — Cost Optimization** - Convert **CAPEX (capital expenditure)** to **OPEX (operational expenditure)**. - Avoid over-provisioning private infrastructure. - Use **spot instances** on public cloud for fault-tolerant batch jobs. --- ### 2.3 Cloud Bursting — Operational Model > [!NOTE] **Cloud Bursting Flow (Step-by-Step):** | Step | Action | Component Involved | |---|---|---| | 1 | Baseline workload runs on private cloud | On-prem hypervisor (vSphere/KVM) | | 2 | Monitoring detects threshold breach (> 75% CPU) | Prometheus, CloudWatch | | 3 | Orchestrator triggers burst-out | Kubernetes HPA, CMP policy | | 4 | Workload clones to public cloud | Terraform / Ansible playbook | | 5 | Load balancer distributes traffic | F5, AWS ALB, Azure Front Door | | 6 | Burst workload runs on public cloud VMs/containers | EC2, AKS, GKE | | 7 | When demand drops → burst-in (decommission) | Scale-down hook | **Bursting Modes:** - **Manual Bursting** — Admin-triggered. - **Automated Bursting** — Policy-driven (preferred for KTU exams). --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Equations (Mandatory for 14-mark problems):** | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** | $TCO = CAPEX + OPEX_{on-prem} + OPEX_{public}$ | ₹ / USD over 3-5 years | | 2 | **Cost Savings via Hybrid** | $Savings = \left( \dfrac{C_{pure-private} - C_{hybrid}}{C_{pure-private}} \right) \times 100\%$ | Percentage | | 3 | **Cloud Bursting Threshold** | $\theta_{burst} = \dfrac{\text{Current Load}}{\text{Max Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Trigger when $\theta_{burst} > 75\%$ | | 4 | **Effective Capacity** | $C_{eff} = C_{private} + C_{public_{burst}}$ | vCPUs / GB RAM | | 5 | **Latency Budget (Hybrid)** | $T_{total} = T_{private} + T_{link} + T_{public}$ | milliseconds (ms) | | 6 | **Data Transfer Cost** | $Cost_{DT} = V_{data} \times P_{per-GB}$ | ₹ / GB (egress charged) | | 7 | **Availability (Composite)** | $A_{hybrid} = 1 - (1-A_{private})(1-A_{public})$ | Decimal; e.g., 0.9999 | | 8 | **Storage Tiering (Hot/Cold)** | $Cost_{total} = V_{hot} \cdot P_{hot} + V_{cold} \cdot P_{cold}$ | ₹ / month | | 9 | **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** | $RTO_{hybrid} \approx \max(RTO_{private}, RTO_{public})$ | Seconds/Minutes | | 10 | **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** | $RPO = T_{last-replication}$ | Minutes (async replication) | > [!NOTE] **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value expressions in derivations below are written as $\vert x \vert$ (LaTeX vert) to preserve markdown table integrity. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Hybrid Cloud Use Case | |---|---| | **Banking & Finance** | Core banking on private; analytics/ML fraud detection on public | | **Healthcare** | EHR (Electronic Health Records) on-prem; medical imaging AI on public | | **E-Commerce** | Catalog, cart on private; traffic surge handled via AWS/Azure burst | | **Education (KTU Relevance)** | Research data on-prem; rendering/simulation on Google Cloud | | **Manufacturing (IoT)** | Factory-floor data processed locally; aggregated analytics on public | | **Government (Digital India)** | Citizen PII on-prem; grievance analytics on public | --- ### 2.6 Common Hybrid Cloud Pitfalls (KTU Board Loves These) > [!WARNING] **Top 5 Architectural Mistakes:** > 1. **Assuming symmetric bandwidth** — Public internet is asymmetric; use **dedicated links** for steady-state replication. > 2. **Ignoring data egress costs** — Egress from public cloud is **expensive** ($0.05–$0.09/GB on AWS). > 3. **Single identity domain** — Silos cause auth failures; mandate **federated identity**. > 4. **No unified monitoring** — Use **Grafana + Prometheus** or **Datadog** for end-to-end observability. > 5. **Vendor lock-in** — Use **Kubernetes abstractions** and **Terraform** for portability. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — TCO Comparison (14-Mark Pattern) > **Problem Statement:** > A KTU-registered engineering startup is evaluating three deployment strategies for a video-streaming platform that expects **500,000 concurrent users at peak**: > - **Option A (Pure Private):** Build dedicated data center, capacity = peak demand. > - **Option B (Pure Public):** Host entirely on AWS. > - **Option C (Hybrid):** Private cloud for **40%** baseline, public cloud burst for **60%** peak. > > **Given Data:** > - Private cloud CAPEX: ₹ **2,00,00,000** (amortized over 5 years). > - Private cloud annual OPEX: ₹ **30,00,000**. > - Public cloud cost (Option B): ₹ **1,20,00,000/year** (always-on). > - Public cloud cost (Option C burst, 60% time): ₹ **1,20,00,000 × 0.6 = ₹ 72,00,000/year**. > > **Compute 5-year TCO for each option. Which is best?** --- #### **Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation** **Step 1: Compute 5-Year TCO for Option A (Pure Private)** $$ \begin{aligned} TCO_{A} &= CAPEX + (OPEX_{annual} \times \text{Years}) \\ TCO_{A} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 + (30{,}00{,}000 \times 5) \\ TCO_{A} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \\ TCO_{A} &= 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Private cloud requires full upfront capital purchase of servers, storage, and networking. The OPEX covers electricity, cooling, and admin staff salaries over 5 years. --- **Step 2: Compute 5-Year TCO for Option B (Pure Public)** $$ \begin{aligned} TCO_{B} &= 0 + (OPEX_{public} \times \text{Years}) \\ TCO_{B} &= 0 + (1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \times 5) \\ TCO_{B} &= 6{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** No CAPEX (no infrastructure owned). Pay-as-you-go OPEX scales linearly with peak demand always provisioned. --- **Step 3: Compute 5-Year TCO for Option C (Hybrid — 40/60 Split)** $$ \begin{aligned} TCO_{C} &= CAPEX_{private} + (OPEX_{private} \times 5) + (OPEX_{public-burst} \times 5) \\ TCO_{C} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 + (30{,}00{,}000 \times 5) + (72{,}00{,}000 \times 5) \\ TCO_{C} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}60{,}00{,}000 \\ TCO_{C} &= 7{,}10{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Wait — this seems *higher* than Option B! This is the **classic KTU trap**. The answer reveals that for a workload with **sustained peak demand (video streaming 24/7)**, pure public cloud wins. The hybrid model wins only when the **burst ratio is small** (e.g., 5%–20% of the year). --- **Step 4: Recompute Hybrid with Realistic Burst Ratio = 10%** $$ \begin{aligned} TCO_{C}^{'} &= 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + (1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 \times 5) \\ TCO_{C}^{'} &= 3{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}00{,}000 \\ TCO_{C}^{'} &= 4{,}10{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Now hybrid sits between private (₹3.5 Cr) and public (₹6 Cr). Decision depends on **security/compliance constraints** (e.g., user data residency). --- **Step 5: Compute % Cost Savings (Hybrid vs Pure Private, burst=10%)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Savings} &= \left( \frac{TCO_{A} - TCO_{C}^{'}}{TCO_{A}} \right) \times 100 \\ &= \left( \frac{3{,}50{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}10{,}00{,}000}{3{,}50{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 \\ &= \left( \frac{-60{,}00{,}000}{3{,}50{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 \\ &= -17.14\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** A **negative** savings means hybrid is *costlier* by 17.14% in this scenario. KTU students must conclude: **"Hybrid Cloud is not always cheaper; the cost-benefit depends on burst ratio and workload profile."** --- ### 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Composite Availability > **Problem Statement:** > A hybrid cloud has a private cloud with availability **A₁ = 0.999** and a public cloud with availability **A₂ = 0.9999**. Compute composite availability assuming the public cloud serves as a **standby disaster recovery site**. --- #### **Solution:** **Step 1: Identify the availability model.** For a **hot-standby** (active-active) hybrid: $$ \begin{aligned} A_{composite} &= 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2) \\ &= 1 - (1 - 0.999)(1 - 0.9999) \\ &= 1 - (0.001 \times 0.0001) \\ &= 1 - 1 \times 10^{-7} \\ &= 0.9999999 \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Multiply failure probabilities. Since the public cloud is a redundant site, both must fail simultaneously for an outage. **Step 2: Convert to "nines" notation.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Downtime per year} &= (1 - A_{composite}) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \\ &= 1 \times 10^{-7} \times 525600 \\ &\approx 0.0526 \text{ minutes/year} \\ &\approx 3.15 \text{ seconds/year} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** "**Seven nines**" availability — extremely high. KTU examiners love such derivations. --- ### 3.3 Numerical Problem 3 — Cloud Bursting Threshold > **Problem Statement:** > A private cloud cluster has **16 vCPUs**. A web application triggers a burst when average CPU exceeds **75%**. Compute the burst trigger load and the additional public cloud vCPUs required if peak demand is **28 vCPUs**. --- #### **Solution:** **Step 1: Compute burst trigger threshold.** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{trigger} &= 0.75 \times 16 \\ &= 12 \text{ vCPUs} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute required public cloud capacity.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{public} &= \max(0, \text{Peak Demand} - C_{private}) \\ C_{public} &= \max(0, 28 - 16) \\ C_{public} &= 12 \text{ vCPUs} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Private cloud handles baseline up to 12 vCPUs comfortably; the remaining 12 vCPUs of demand must be offloaded to public cloud. **Step 3: Compute effective hybrid capacity.** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{eff} &= C_{private} + C_{public} \\ &= 16 + 12 \\ &= 28 \text{ vCPUs} \quad \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Choose What? | Workload Profile | Recommended Model | Justification | |---|---|---| | **Steady, predictable, high-utilization** (> 70% all year) | **Private** | TCO wins; public is costlier | | **Highly variable, seasonal peaks** (< 20% time at peak) | **Hybrid (Burst)** | Best balance of cost + elasticity | | **Strictly regulated (Banking, Defense)** | **Private** or **Hybrid** | Compliance trumps cost | | **Startup with unknown demand** | **Public** | Low risk; no upfront CAPEX | | **ML training (GPU-intensive, short-lived)** | **Public (Spot)** | Cheaper per-hour GPU cost | | **Edge + Cloud integration** | **Hybrid** | Edge processes locally; aggregates to cloud | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 High-Level Hybrid Cloud Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph UserLayer["USER ACCESS LAYER"] UA["End Users
Web Mobile IoT"] end subgraph SecLayer["SECURITY AND IDENTITY LAYER"] IAM["Federated Identity
SAML OAuth OIDC"] ZTN["Zero Trust Network
BeyondCorp Zscaler"] end subgraph ConnLayer["CONNECTIVITY LAYER"] SDWAN["SD-WAN Controller"] VPN1["IPsec VPN Tunnel"] DC1["Direct Connect
1 Gbps to 10 Gbps"] end subgraph OnPrem["PRIVATE CLOUD ON PREMISES"] vSphere["VMware vSphere
KVM Hypervisor"] PrivK8s["Private Kubernetes
OpenShift Rancher"] PrivDB["Sensitive Data
PII PHI Financial"] end subgraph Public["PUBLIC CLOUD PROVIDER"] subgraph AWS["AWS TENANT"] EC2["EC2 Compute
Burst Instances"] S3["S3 Storage
Cold Archive"] AWSAI["SageMaker ML"] end subgraph Azure["AZURE TENANT"] AKS["AKS Cluster"] AzureDB["Cosmos DB"] end end subgraph MgmtLayer["ORCHESTRATION AND MANAGEMENT"] CMP["Cloud Management
Platform Morpheus"] Terra["Terraform Ansible
IaC Engine"] Mon["Prometheus Grafana
Datadog Monitoring"] end UA --> IAM IAM --> ZTN ZTN --> SDWAN SDWAN --> VPN1 SDWAN --> DC1 VPN1 --> vSphere DC1 --> vSphere VPN1 --> EC2 DC1 --> AKS vSphere --> PrivK8s PrivK8s --> PrivDB EC2 --> AWSAI AKS --> AzureDB CMP -.-> vSphere CMP -.-> EC2 CMP -.-> AKS Terra -.-> OnPrem Terra -.-> Public Mon -.-> OnPrem Mon -.-> Public ``` --- ### 4.2 Cloud Bursting Lifecycle Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Workload
on Private Cloud"] --> B{"Monitor CPU
Utilization"} B -->|"Below 75 percent"| A B -->|"Above 75 percent"| C["Trigger Burst
Policy"] C --> D["Terraform Ansible
Provisions Public VMs"] D --> E["Deploy Containers
on Public K8s"] E --> F["Load Balancer
Routes Traffic"] F --> G["Workload
Runs on Public"] G --> H{"Demand
Dropped"} H -->|"No"| G H -->|"Yes"| I["Scale Down
Public Resources"] I --> J["Return to
Private Cloud"] J --> A ``` --- ### 4.3 Data Tiering in Hybrid Cloud ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Hot["HOT TIER - PRIVATE CLOUD"] H1["Transactional DB
Latency under 5 ms"] H2["Active User Data
SSD NVMe"] end subgraph Warm["WARM TIER - HYBRID BRIDGE"] W1["Recent Archives
under 30 days"] W2["Cache Replication
via VPN"] end subgraph Cold["COLD TIER - PUBLIC CLOUD"] C1["Glacier S3 IA
Cheap Long-term"] C2["Compliance Backups
AES 256 Encrypted"] end H1 --> W1 W1 --> C1 H2 --> W2 W2 --> C2 ``` --- ### 4.4 Decision Framework for Hybrid Cloud Adoption ```mermaid flowchart TB Start["Start Evaluation"] --> Q1{"Data has
regulatory
constraints"} Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q2{"Workload is
spiky or
seasonal"} Q1 -->|"No"| Pub["Adopt Pure Public Cloud"] Q2 -->|"Yes"| Hybrid["Adopt Hybrid Cloud
Private plus Public Burst"] Q2 -->|"No"| Priv["Adopt Pure Private Cloud"] Hybrid --> Step1["Step 1 Choose CMP"] Step1 --> Step2["Step 2 Set up VPN or Direct Connect"] Step2 --> Step3["Step 3 Define Burst Policies"] Step3 --> Step4["Step 4 Deploy IaC Pipelines"] Step4 --> Done["Production Ready Hybrid"] Priv --> Done Pub --> Done ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Hybrid Cloud. List any four characteristics.** **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** A **Hybrid Cloud** is a cloud deployment model that combines **two or more cloud environments** (private and public) bound together by **standardized technology**, enabling **data and application portability** between them. **Four Characteristics:** 1. **Portability** — Workloads can move between private and public clouds. 2. **Scalability via Bursting** — Spills excess load to public cloud. 3. **Compliance-Aware** — Sensitive data stays on private infrastructure. 4. **Cost-Optimized** — Combines CAPEX (private) and OPEX (public). > **Word Count Target:** 80–100 words. Use bullet points for characteristics. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is cloud bursting? Mention its two modes.** **Model Answer:** > **Cloud Bursting** is the process by which a private cloud **offloads excess computational load** to a public cloud when its own resources are saturated, then **reclaims** them when the surge subsides. **Two Modes:** 1. **Manual Bursting** — Triggered by a human administrator. 2. **Automated Bursting** — Triggered by predefined policies (CPU, memory, queue length thresholds) using orchestration tools (Kubernetes HPA, Terraform). --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **three-layer architecture** of a Hybrid Cloud with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A startup expects a baseline load of **10 vCPUs** on its private cloud, with occasional peaks of **25 vCPUs** lasting **15% of the year**. Compute: - (i) The cloud bursting threshold (use 80% trigger). **(2 Marks)** - (ii) The 5-year TCO for Pure Private vs Hybrid, given: - Private CAPEX = ₹ 1,50,00,000 (5-year amortized) - Private OPEX = ₹ 20,00,000/year - Public burst cost = ₹ 80,00,000/year (100% utilization equivalent) **(5 Marks)** --- ##### **Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Three-Layer Architecture (7 Marks):** > **Layer 1 — Application & Workload Layer:** > Hosts business applications classified by sensitivity. Latency-critical and regulated workloads run on private; variable/bursty workloads run on public. > **Layer 2 — Orchestration & Management Layer:** > Comprises a **Cloud Management Platform (CMP)**, **Kubernetes clusters** (EKS/AKS/GKE), **IaC tools** (Terraform/Ansible), and **service mesh** (Istio). It provides unified policy enforcement, monitoring, and workload scheduling. > **Layer 3 — Connectivity & Security Layer:** > Includes **IPsec VPN, Direct Connect/ExpressRoute, SD-WAN** for inter-cloud links, plus **federated identity (SAML/OAuth)**, **encryption (TLS 1.3, AES-256)**, and **zero-trust gateways**. *(Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for visual aid — reproduce in answer sheet.)* **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1: Compute Bursting Threshold (i)** $$ \begin{aligned} \theta_{trigger} &= 0.80 \times 10 \text{ vCPUs} \\ &= 8 \text{ vCPUs} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** When private cloud hits 8 vCPUs of load, burst-out is triggered. **Step 2: Compute 5-Year TCO for Pure Private (ii)** $$ \begin{aligned} TCO_{private} &= 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + (20{,}00{,}000 \times 5) \\ &= 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 2{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Public Burst Cost (15% of the year)** $$ \begin{aligned} Cost_{public-burst-1yr} &= 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 \\ &= 12{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR/year} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute 5-Year TCO for Hybrid (ii)** $$ \begin{aligned} TCO_{hybrid} &= 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + (20{,}00{,}000 \times 5) + (12{,}00{,}000 \times 5) \\ &= 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 60{,}00{,}000 \\ &= 3{,}10{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Final Comparison Table (full marks for crisp tabular conclusion)** | Model | 5-Year TCO | Use Case | |---|---|---| | **Pure Private** | ₹ 2,50,00,000 | Best when burst is rare | | **Hybrid** | ₹ 3,10,00,000 | Best when elasticity is critical | > **Conclusion:** Pure Private is cheaper by ₹ 60,00,000 over 5 years **in this specific scenario**, because the 15% burst does not justify the additional public-cloud spend. However, Hybrid wins in **scalability, fault tolerance, and compliance flexibility** — factors not captured purely in TCO. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Compare **Public, Private, and Hybrid Cloud** deployment models across **6 parameters** of your choice. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **key challenges** in implementing a Hybrid Cloud architecture, with at least **5 specific examples**. **(7 Marks)** --- ##### **Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Comparative Table (7 Marks):** | Parameter | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud | |---|---|---|---| | **Ownership** | Provider-owned (AWS, Azure) | Organization-owned | Combined | | **Scalability** | Unlimited (elastic) | Limited by physical infra | Elastic with bursting | | **Cost Model** | Pure OPEX | CAPEX + OPEX | OPEX-optimized | | **Security & Compliance** | Shared responsibility | Full control | Tiered (sensitive on-prem) | | **Performance & Latency** | Variable (WAN-dependent) | Predictable (LAN) | Mixed | | **Maintenance Burden** | Provider-managed | Self-managed | Shared | | **Best Suited For** | Startups, variable load | Banking, defense, legacy | Mixed compliance + scale | > **Examiner Note:** Any 6 parameters are acceptable. Each row carries ~1 mark. Adding a 2-line justification column earns bonus marks. **Part (b) — Key Challenges of Hybrid Cloud (7 Marks):** > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT:** > - ❌ Write generic "security is a challenge" without elaboration. > - ❌ Skip the **mitigation strategy** for each challenge. > - ❌ Use only 3–4 challenges when asked for 5. > - ✅ Always pair a **challenge** with its **solution/tool**. **Challenge 1 — Network Latency & Bandwidth** - *Issue:* Cross-cloud data transfer over public internet can exceed 100 ms RTT. - *Mitigation:* Use **dedicated interconnects** (AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute) at 1–10 Gbps with SLA-backed RTT < 50 ms. **Challenge 2 — Data Egress Costs** - *Issue:* Egress from public cloud costs ₹ 4–8 per GB. - *Mitigation:* Compress data with **gzip/zstd** before transfer; cache frequently accessed data on private cloud using **CDN edge nodes**. **Challenge 3 — Identity & Access Silos** - *Issue:* Separate IAM on private and public clouds cause auth friction. - *Mitigation:* Deploy **federated identity** using **SAML 2.0** with **Okta/Azure AD** as IdP. **Challenge 4 — Unified Monitoring & Observability** - *Issue:* Logs/metrics scattered across two environments. - *Mitigation:* Centralized **Grafana + Prometheus** stack, or commercial **Datadog/New Relic** with cross-cloud log aggregation. **Challenge 5 — Compliance & Data Sovereignty** - *Issue:* Regulated data cannot legally leave Indian borders (DPDPA 2023). - *Mitigation:* Implement **geofencing policies** in IaC; use **AWS Mumbai region** for India-bound public workloads. **Challenge 6 — Vendor Lock-in** - *Issue:* Proprietary APIs tie workloads to one provider. - *Mitigation:* Use **Kubernetes** (EKS/AKS/GKE-portable) and **Terraform** (multi-cloud IaC). **Challenge 7 — Disaster Recovery Complexity** - *Issue:* RTO/RPO targets must span both environments. - *Mitigation:* Use **cross-cloud replication** (e.g., Veeam, Zerto) and **automated DR runbooks** tested quarterly. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Hybrid Cloud Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Hybrid with Multi-Cloud.** Multi-Cloud = multiple public providers; **Hybrid = private + public**. Examiners *will* deduct 2 marks for this. > > 2. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Answers.** Writing "vCPUs = 12" without stating "**12 vCPUs burst required to public cloud**" loses 1 mark. > > 3. **Writing "Hybrid is always cheaper."** This is a **false statement**. The correct phrasing is: *"Hybrid is cost-optimal when burst workload is **< 20%** of the year."* > > 4. **Skipping the Diagram.** In 7-mark theory questions, an **unlabelled diagram** still earns 1–2 marks. Always draw the **three-layer architecture** with labeled arrows. > > 5. **Not mentioning real-world tools.** Naming **AWS Direct Connect, Kubernetes, Terraform, Okta** in answers shows applied knowledge and impresses examiners. > > 6. **Mixing up RTO and RPO.** RTO = *how fast* you recover; RPO = *how much data* you can lose. Reversing them costs 1–2 marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read Before Exam):** - **Definition:** Hybrid Cloud = **Private Cloud + Public Cloud + Secure Connectivity + Orchestration**. - **Three Pillars:** **Compliance**, **Elasticity (Bursting)**, **Cost Optimization**. - **Three-Layer Architecture:** **Application Layer**, **Orchestration Layer**, **Connectivity/Security Layer**. - **Cloud Bursting Trigger:** Typically **70%–80%** of private capacity. - **Key Connectivity Options:** **IPsec VPN** (cheap, encrypted internet), **Direct Connect/ExpressRoute** (dedicated, low latency, expensive). - **Key Orchestration Tools:** **Kubernetes, Terraform, Ansible, CMP (Morpheus, vRealize)**. - **Cost Insight:** Hybrid is **not always cheaper** — it wins when burst ratio is **low (< 20%)** or when **compliance** mandates on-prem data. - **Hybrid ≠ Multi-Cloud:** Hybrid combines private + public; Multi-Cloud uses multiple public providers. - **Composite Availability:** $A_{hybrid} = 1 - (1 - A_{private})(1 - A_{public})$ — always **higher** than individual availabilities. - **Data Gravity:** Place compute **close to data** to minimize transfer cost and latency. - **Standard Industry Tools (Memorize Names):** - **AWS:** Direct Connect, Outposts, EKS. - **Azure:** ExpressRoute, Arc, AKS. - **Google:** Cloud Interconnect, Anthos, GKE. - **Cross-Cloud:** Terraform, Istio, Prometheus, Grafana. - **Compliance References for India:** **DPDPA 2023**, **RBI Data Localization**, **MeitY Cloud Guidelines**. - **Exam Formula to Memorize:** - TCO = CAPEX + (OPEX × Years) - Burst Trigger = Threshold% × Private Capacity - Composite Availability formula (above) - Data Transfer Cost = Volume × Per-GB Price - **Real-World Indian Examples:** **Aadhaar** (private core, public analytics), **IRCTC Tatkal** (hybrid bursting), **HDFC Bank** (private core, public ML fraud detection), **Reliance Jio** (private telco core + public cloud ML). > **Final Tip:** For KTU 14-mark questions, always structure answers as **Definition → Diagram → Comparison → Numerical → Conclusion**. This 5-part structure is the **highest-scoring template** validated by KTU board examiners.

topics.title: Open Cloud Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Open Cloud Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Cloud Services** refer to cloud computing services built upon **open-source software stacks, open standards, and open APIs** that are vendor-neutral, publicly documented, and freely available for implementation, customization, and deployment. They eliminate vendor lock-in by leveraging community-driven platforms such as **OpenStack**, **OpenNebula**, **Eucalyptus**, and **Apache CloudStack**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECCT631)** syllabus, Open Cloud Services encompass three foundational pillars:
1. **Open-Source Cloud Platforms** – Software whose source code is publicly accessible (e.g., OpenStack).
2. **Open Standards & APIs** – Interoperability protocols (e.g., **OpenStack APIs**, **OCCI**, **CDMI**).
3. **Open Data Formats** – Non-proprietary data portability schemas.

> [!NOTE]
> The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** Special Publication 500-293 defines the cloud computing reference architecture, against which open cloud platforms are benchmarked for compliance with the **5-4-3 principles**: 5 essential characteristics, 4 deployment models, and 3 service models.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Open Cloud as a "Public Library System"**
>
> Imagine cloud computing as a **library system**:
> - **Proprietary Cloud (e.g., AWS, Azure)** is like a *private, members-only library* where the rules, book access methods, and catalog formats are all controlled by a single owner. You cannot take books to another library.
> - **Open Cloud Services** are like a *public library* built on **open standards** (e.g., the **Dewey Decimal System**). Anyone can build a library, share books, and patrons can move between libraries because the cataloging and lending rules are public, free, and standardized.
>
> In the same way, **Open Cloud Services** let organizations build their own cloud infrastructure using community-developed code, ensure interoperability via open APIs, and avoid being trapped by a single vendor's proprietary ecosystem.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Vendor Lock-in:** Dependence on a single provider's proprietary technology that makes migration costly.
> - **Open API:** Application Programming Interface published with open, non-restrictive licenses.
> - **Interoperability:** The ability of diverse systems to work together seamlessly.
> - **Hypervisor:** Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) software that creates and runs Virtual Machines (VMs). Standard in open clouds: **KVM**, **Xen**, **QEMU**.
> - **IaaS / PaaS / SaaS:** The three canonical NIST service models.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of an Open Cloud Stack

An Open Cloud Service platform is organized into **logical layers**, each performing a specific function. Below is the structured breakdown:

### Layer 1: Hardware / Physical Infrastructure
- Commodity x86 servers, networking switches, and storage arrays.
- **Key Standard:** No proprietary hardware dependency. Supports **white-box** servers.

### Layer 2: Virtualization Layer (Hypervisor)
- **Open Hypervisors:** KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine), Xen, QEMU.
- Provides **abstraction** between physical hardware and virtual resources.
- Enables **multi-tenancy** at the VM level.

### Layer 3: Cloud Management Platform (CMP)
- The **brain** of the open cloud.
- **Open Source CMPs:**
  * **OpenStack** (most widely adopted; Python-based)
  * **OpenNebula** (lightweight, focused on VM management)
  * **Eucalyptus** (AWS-API compatible)
  * **Apache CloudStack** (mature, enterprise-grade)
- Manages: compute, storage, networking, identity, and image services.

### Layer 4: Service Orchestration & APIs
- **Open APIs** such as:
  * **OpenStack REST APIs** (HTTP-based, JSON payloads)
  * **OCCI (Open Cloud Computing Interface)** – OGF standard
  * **CDMI (Cloud Data Management Interface)** – SNIA standard
- Enable automation, scripting, and third-party tool integration.

### Layer 5: Service Delivery Models
The three NIST-defined service models are exposed to end users:

| Service Model | Open Cloud Examples | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | OpenStack Nova, Eucalyptus EC2 | Provides raw compute, storage, networking |
| **PaaS** | OpenShift Origin, CloudFoundry | Platform for app deployment |
| **SaaS** | Nextcloud, OnlyOffice (open) | Ready-to-use software over the web |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Open Cloud Services

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Why Open Source?** Transparency, auditability, community-driven security patches, and zero licensing fees.
> - **Why Open Standards?** Guarantees that workloads (VMs, data, applications) are **portable** across providers.
> - **Why Open APIs?** Allows programmatic control and integration with existing enterprise systems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are essential for evaluating Open Cloud Services in KTU exams.

| Metric | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resource Utilization** | $$U = \frac{\text{Allocated Resources}}{\text{Total Capacity}} \times 100\%$$ | Efficiency of resource usage | **%** |
| **Scalability Ratio** | $$S = \frac{N_{\text{peak}}}{N_{\text{baseline}}}$$ | Ability to scale from baseline to peak | dimensionless |
| **Cost Savings (TCO)** | $$C_{\text{save}} = C_{\text{prop}} - C_{\text{open}}$$ | Difference between proprietary and open cost | **INR/USD** |
| **Availability (SLA)** | $$A = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%$$ | Service uptime commitment | **%** |
| **VM Density** | $$D = \frac{N_{\text{VM}}}{N_{\text{Host}}}$$ | VMs per physical host | dimensionless |
| **Interoperability Index** | $$I = \frac{N_{\text{standards supported}}}{N_{\text{total standards}}}$$ | Compliance with open standards | dimensionless |

> **Note on Notation:** Vertical bars in equations (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) are written using the LaTeX `\vert` command to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Telecommunications:** OpenStack powers **NFV (Network Functions Virtualization)** for 5G core networks.
> - **Academic Research:** CERN uses **OpenStack** to manage petabytes of LHC (Large Hadron Collider) data.
> - **Government:** India's **National Cloud (MeghRaj)** initiative uses open-source components to deliver **GI Cloud** services.
> - **Edge Computing:** Open cloud platforms extend to edge nodes for low-latency IoT workloads.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cost-Benefit Analysis of Open vs. Proprietary Cloud

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A mid-scale enterprise evaluates migrating from a proprietary cloud (cost = **INR 50 lakh/year**) to an OpenStack-based private cloud. The open cloud has:
> - One-time setup cost: **INR 15 lakh**
> - Annual maintenance: **INR 8 lakh/year**
> - Hardware amortization over **5 years**: **INR 5 lakh/year**
> - Expected lifetime: **5 years**
>
> Determine the break-even point and total savings.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Define the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for the proprietary cloud over 5 years.**

The proprietary cloud is a pure operating expenditure (OPEX) model. Thus, the total cost over $n$ years is simply the annual subscription cost multiplied by the number of years.

$$
C_{\text{prop,total}} = C_{\text{prop}} \times n
$$

Substituting $C_{\text{prop}} = 50{,}00{,}000$ INR and $n = 5$ years:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{prop,total}} &= 50{,}00{,}000 \times 5 \\
&= 2{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Define the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) for the open cloud over 5 years.**

The open cloud TCO consists of three components: the one-time setup capital expenditure (CAPEX), the annual maintenance, and the annual hardware amortization.

$$
C_{\text{open,total}} = C_{\text{setup}} + (C_{\text{maint}} + C_{\text{amort}}) \times n
$$

Substituting $C_{\text{setup}} = 15{,}00{,}000$ INR, $C_{\text{maint}} = 8{,}00{,}000$ INR, $C_{\text{amort}} = 5{,}00{,}000$ INR, and $n = 5$ years:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{open,total}} &= 15{,}00{,}000 + (8{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000) \times 5 \\
&= 15{,}00{,}000 + 13{,}00{,}000 \times 5 \\
&= 15{,}00{,}000 + 65{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 80{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Total Savings over 5 years.**

The total savings is the difference between the proprietary TCO and the open TCO.

$$
C_{\text{save}} = C_{\text{prop,total}} - C_{\text{open,total}}
$$

Substituting the computed values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{save}} &= 2{,}50{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}70{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Determine the Break-Even Point (in years).**

Break-even occurs when the cumulative cost of the open cloud equals the cumulative cost of the proprietary cloud. Let $t$ be the break-even time in years.

$$
C_{\text{setup}} + (C_{\text{maint}} + C_{\text{amort}}) \times t = C_{\text{prop}} \times t
$$

Rearranging to isolate $t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{setup}} &= (C_{\text{prop}} - C_{\text{maint}} - C_{\text{amort}}) \times t \\
t &= \frac{C_{\text{setup}}}{C_{\text{prop}} - C_{\text{maint}} - C_{\text{amort}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000} \\
t &= \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{37{,}00{,}000} \\
t &\approx 0.405 \text{ years} \\
t &\approx 4.86 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The enterprise recovers its initial investment in approximately **4.86 months** and saves **INR 1.70 crore** over 5 years by adopting the open cloud model.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Open Cloud Service Models

| Feature | IaaS (Open) | PaaS (Open) | SaaS (Open) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **User Controls** | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Apps | Apps & Data | End-user usage only |
| **Provider Controls** | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | OS, Middleware, Runtime | All layers |
| **Open Example** | OpenStack Nova | OpenShift Origin | Nextcloud |
| **Granularity** | **VM-level** | **Application-level** | **User-level** |
| **Typical User** | System Administrators | Application Developers | End Users |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Open Cloud Service Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USR["USER LAYER"]
        U1["End User SaaS Client"]
        U2["Developer PaaS Portal"]
        U3["Admin IaaS Dashboard"]
    end

    subgraph API["OPEN API LAYER"]
        A1["OpenStack REST API"]
        A2["OCCI Standard"]
        A3["CDMI Data API"]
    end

    subgraph CMP["CLOUD MANAGEMENT PLATFORM"]
        C1["Nova Compute Service"]
        C2["Neutron Networking"]
        C3["Cinder Block Storage"]
        C4["Keystone Identity"]
        C5["Glance Image Service"]
        C6["Horizon Dashboard"]
    end

    subgraph HYP["VIRTUALIZATION LAYER"]
        H1["KVM Hypervisor"]
        H2["Xen Hypervisor"]
        H3["QEMU Emulator"]
    end

    subgraph HW["PHYSICAL INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        HW1["x86 Commodity Servers"]
        HW2["Network Switches"]
        HW3["Storage Arrays"]
    end

    U1 --> A1

    C2 --> H1

    H2 --> HW1

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Open Cloud Services. List any two open-source cloud platforms.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Open Cloud Services are cloud computing services built upon open-source software, open standards, and open APIs that are vendor-neutral and freely available for use and modification. They eliminate vendor lock-in and promote interoperability.
>
> **Two open-source cloud platforms:**
> 1. **OpenStack** – A widely adopted IaaS platform written in Python.
> 2. **OpenNebula** – A lightweight, flexible open-source cloud management platform.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Proprietary Cloud and Open Cloud Services (any 3 points).**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Parameter | Proprietary Cloud | Open Cloud |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Source Code** | Closed, proprietary | Open, publicly available |
> | **Vendor Lock-in** | High | Low / None |
> | **Cost** | Subscription-based, recurring | Free software + infrastructure cost |
> | **Customization** | Limited | Fully customizable |
> | **Example** | AWS EC2, Azure VM | OpenStack Nova, Eucalyptus |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B questions feature an **internal choice**. Two independent alternative questions (A and B) are provided below for practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of OpenStack with a neat block diagram. List its core services. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the benefits and challenges of adopting Open Cloud Services in an enterprise environment. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**OpenStack Architecture:**

OpenStack is a modular open-source cloud platform consisting of interconnected services. Its architecture is divided into several logical components:

1. **Nova (Compute Service):** Manages and provisions Virtual Machines. It supports KVM, Xen, QEMU, and VMware hypervisors.
2. **Neutron (Networking Service):** Provides "networking as a service" with SDN capabilities. Manages virtual networks, routers, and IPs.
3. **Cinder (Block Storage Service):** Provides persistent block storage to VMs.
4. **Keystone (Identity Service):** Centralized authentication and authorization (LDAP, OAuth compatible).
5. **Glance (Image Service):** Manages VM disk images (supports QCOW2, VHD, VMDK, RAW formats).
6. **Swift (Object Storage Service):** Highly available distributed object storage.
7. **Horizon (Dashboard):** Web-based GUI for managing OpenStack services.
8. **Heat (Orchestration):** Template-based infrastructure automation (HOT templates).

**Block Diagram (described in text for clarity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Horizon} &\rightarrow \text{Keystone} \rightarrow \text{All Services} \\
\text{User/CLI} &\rightarrow \text{Keystone (Auth)} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Nova (Compute)} \\ \text{Neutron (Network)} \\ \text{Cinder (Storage)} \\ \text{Glance (Images)} \\ \text{Swift (Objects)} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Benefits of Open Cloud Services:**
- **No Vendor Lock-in:** Workloads and data are portable across providers.
- **Cost Efficiency:** Zero licensing fees; pay only for hardware and support.
- **Customization:** Source code can be modified to meet specific needs.
- **Community Innovation:** Rapid development cycle driven by global contributors (OpenStack has 10,000+ contributors).
- **Standards Compliance:** Adheres to open APIs (OCCI, CDMI) ensuring interoperability.
- **Security Transparency:** Source code is auditable for backdoors and vulnerabilities.

**Challenges of Open Cloud Services:**
- **High Initial CAPEX:** Requires investment in hardware and skilled personnel.
- **Skill Gap:** Requires Linux, networking, and Python expertise.
- **Fragmentation:** Multiple open projects may create integration complexity.
- **Support Concerns:** Community support may not match enterprise SLAs of proprietary vendors.
- **Migration Effort:** Legacy applications may require refactoring to be cloud-native.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare OpenStack, OpenNebula, and Eucalyptus as open cloud platforms. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A startup invests INR 20 lakh as one-time setup for an open cloud, with annual maintenance of INR 6 lakh and hardware amortization of INR 4 lakh/year. The proprietary alternative costs INR 35 lakh/year. Calculate the break-even point and savings over 4 years. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Feature | OpenStack | OpenNebula | Eucalyptus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Language** | Python | C++, Ruby, Java | Java, C |
| **API Compatibility** | Native + EC2 | Native + EC2 + OCCI | AWS EC2, S3 compatible |
| **Hypervisor Support** | KVM, Xen, QEMU, VMware | KVM, Xen, VMware | KVM, Xen, VMware |
| **Architecture** | Modular microservices | Monolithic with plugins | AWS-compatible |
| **Community Size** | Very Large (10,000+) | Medium | Small |
| **Best Use Case** | Large-scale public/private clouds | SMB & research labs | AWS-migrated enterprises |
| **License** | Apache 2.0 | Apache 2.0 | BSD-style |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Define variables.**
- $C_{\text{setup}} = 20{,}00{,}000$ INR
- $C_{\text{maint}} = 6{,}00{,}000$ INR/year
- $C_{\text{amort}} = 4{,}00{,}000$ INR/year
- $C_{\text{prop}} = 35{,}00{,}000$ INR/year
- $n = 4$ years

**Step 2: Compute proprietary TCO.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{prop,total}} &= C_{\text{prop}} \times n \\
&= 35{,}00{,}000 \times 4 \\
&= 1{,}40{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute open cloud TCO.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{open,total}} &= C_{\text{setup}} + (C_{\text{maint}} + C_{\text{amort}}) \times n \\
&= 20{,}00{,}000 + (6{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000) \times 4 \\
&= 20{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 \times 4 \\
&= 20{,}00{,}000 + 40{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 60{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute total savings.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{save}} &= C_{\text{prop,total}} - C_{\text{open,total}} \\
&= 1{,}40{,}00{,}000 - 60{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 80{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute break-even time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{C_{\text{setup}}}{C_{\text{prop}} - C_{\text{maint}} - C_{\text{amort}}} \\
t &= \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{35{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000 - 4{,}00{,}000} \\
t &= \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{25{,}00{,}000} \\
t &= 0.8 \text{ years} \\
t &\approx 9.6 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Break-even is achieved in approximately **9.6 months**, with total savings of **INR 80 lakh** over 4 years.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Diagram Marks:** Always draw the **block diagram** of OpenStack with clear arrow directions and labeled components. Missing diagrams cost **2–3 marks** even in theory answers.
> 2. **Formula Substitution:** Students frequently forget to multiply annual costs by $n$ years. Show explicit substitution of numerical values.
> 3. **Terminology:** Use exact KTU terms: "Open-source cloud platform" NOT just "free cloud." Examiners mark strictly on **keyword presence**.
> 4. **Comparison Tables:** In 7-mark questions, a **comparison table** is worth at least **2 marks**. Do not write paragraphs only.
> 5. **Unit Notation:** Always include units (INR, %, years) in the final answer. Missing units = **0.5 mark deduction**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Open Cloud Services:**
>
> - **Definition:** Open Cloud = Open-source + Open Standards + Open APIs.
> - **NIST 5-4-3 Model:** 5 characteristics, 4 deployment models, 3 service models.
> - **Top Open Cloud Platforms (Memorize):** OpenStack, OpenNebula, Eucalyptus, CloudStack.
> - **OpenStack Core Services:** Nova, Neutron, Cinder, Keystone, Glance, Swift, Horizon, Heat.
> - **Open Hypervisors:** KVM, Xen, QEMU.
> - **Open APIs:** OCCI (OGF), CDMI (SNIA), OpenStack REST API.
> - **Key Advantage:** Elimination of **vendor lock-in**.
> - **Key Challenge:** Requires **skilled personnel** and upfront **CAPEX**.
> - **Government Use:** India's **GI Cloud / MeghRaj** uses open cloud.
> - **Real-World:** CERN, 5G NFV, NASA Nebula all use OpenStack.
> - **Cost Formula:** $C_{\text{save}} = C_{\text{prop,total}} - C_{\text{open,total}}$.
> - **Break-Even Formula:** $t = \frac{C_{\text{setup}}}{C_{\text{prop}} - (C_{\text{maint}} + C_{\text{amort}})}$.
> - **License Types:** Apache 2.0 (OpenStack), BSD (Eucalyptus), GPL components.
> - **Always draw diagrams** for architecture questions; missing diagrams = lost marks.
> - **Use tables** for comparison questions (worth 2+ marks in KTU evaluation).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Terms and Concepts in Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SERVICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Basic Terms and Concepts in Security ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is "Security" in a Computing Context? In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **Information Security** is defined as the practice of protecting information by mitigating risks. It involves preventing unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction of data — whether in physical form, digital form, or residing in a cloud infrastructure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Information Security is the preservation of three cardinal properties — Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (commonly known as the CIA Triad) — of information and information systems against unauthorized disclosure, modification, or destruction.* ### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — Your House Imagine your personal data is a **house**: - **Walls and locked doors** = **Confidentiality** (only invited people enter) - **Untouched furniture** = **Integrity** (nothing inside is moved or broken) - **Power and water supply** = **Availability** (you can use your house whenever you want) - **CCTV cameras and a guest book** = **Accountability / Non-Repudiation** (every entry is logged) - **Insurance policy** = **Risk Management** (you are prepared if something goes wrong) > [!NOTE] > A "secure system" is NOT one that is "unbreakable." It is a system where the **cost of breaking in exceeds the value of the asset** being protected, and the **risk is acceptable to the owner**. ### 1.3 The CIA Triad — The Holy Trinity of Security The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model upon which ALL security policies, architectures, and controls are built. | Property | Formal Definition | Real-World Example | |----------|-------------------|--------------------| | **Confidentiality** | Ensuring that information is accessible only to those authorized to view it. | Encrypted hard drive, password-protected file, doctor-patient privilege. | | **Integrity** | Ensuring that information is accurate, consistent, and unaltered by unauthorized parties. | Bank account balance unchanged during transfer, digital signatures on software. | | **Availability** | Ensuring that information and systems are accessible to authorized users when needed. | 99.99% uptime SLA of AWS EC2, redundant power supply in data centers. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Exam Tip:** The CIA Triad is a **3-mark guaranteed question** in either Part A or as a sub-part of Part B. Always draw the triangle and label each vertex with a one-line definition. ### 1.4 Key Supporting Security Concepts These four properties sit *on top of* the CIA Triad and extend it for modern systems: - **Authentication** — The process of *verifying the identity* of a user, system, or entity. (Example: entering username + password). - **Authorization** — The process of *granting or denying access rights* to an authenticated user. (Example: a student is authorized to view marks but not change them). - **Accountability** — The ability to *trace actions* to a specific user/entity. Achieved via audit logs and digital signatures. - **Non-Repudiation** — A guarantee that *the sender cannot deny having sent* a message, and the receiver cannot deny having received it. (Achieved via digital signatures and timestamps). > [!NOTE] > **Authentication ≠ Authorization.** > *Authentication answers "Who are you?"* (identity) > *Authorization answers "What are you allowed to do?"* (permission) > In a college system, your college ID card **authenticates** you at the gate, and your role of "Student" **authorizes** you to use the library but not the staff parking. ### 1.5 Threats, Vulnerabilities, Risks & Attacks - **Threat**: A potential cause of an unwanted incident that *could* harm a system. (e.g., a hacker, a flood, malware). - **Vulnerability**: A *weakness* in a system that can be exploited. (e.g., unpatched OS, weak password). - **Risk**: The *probability* that a threat will exploit a vulnerability, multiplied by the impact. (e.g., high risk = high probability × high damage). - **Attack**: An *action* that exploits a vulnerability to realize a threat. (e.g., SQL injection, DDoS). ### 1.6 Other Essential Terms - **Asset**: Anything of value to the organization (data, hardware, software, people). - **Control / Safeguard**: A measure put in place to *mitigate* risk (firewall, encryption, training). - **Security Policy**: A formal, written document that defines *how* an organization protects its assets. - **Defense in Depth**: A layered security approach using multiple controls so that if one fails, others still protect. - **Least Privilege**: A principle stating that a user should be given *only the minimum access* required to perform their job. - **Zero Trust**: A modern security model where *no entity (inside or outside the network) is trusted by default*; verification is continuous. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars — Expanded Analysis #### 2.1.1 Confidentiality — The "Need-to-Know" Principle - Confidentiality is breached through: eavesdropping, sniffing, shoulder surfing, social engineering, data leakage. - **Controls**: Encryption (AES, RSA), Access Control Lists (ACLs), two-factor authentication, data classification labels (Public, Internal, Confidential, Restricted). #### 2.1.2 Integrity — The "Trust the Data" Principle - Integrity is breached through: man-in-the-middle attacks, malware, unauthorized modification, bit rot. - **Controls**: Hashing (SHA-256, MD5), digital signatures, message authentication codes (MACs), version control, checksums. #### 2.1.3 Availability — The "Always-On" Principle - Availability is breached through: Denial of Service (DoS) attacks, hardware failures, natural disasters, ransomware. - **Controls**: Redundancy (RAID, hot standby), backups (3-2-1 rule), disaster recovery plans, load balancers, UPS systems, CDN. ### 2.2 Extended Security Model — The Parkerian Hexad For advanced KTU answers, augment the CIA Triad with the **Parkerian Hexad** (6 elements): 1. **Confidentiality** 2. **Integrity** (data integrity) 3. **Availability** 4. **Authenticity** (genuineness of the source) 5. **Possession** (control over physical/intellectual property) 6. **Utility** (usefulness of the data — e.g., encrypted data is technically possessed but not useful without the key) > [!NOTE] > Mentioning the Parkerian Hexad in a 14-mark answer **impresses the KTU examiner** and is a high-yield differentiator. ### 2.3 Access Control Models — Detailed Comparison | Model | Full Form | How It Decides Access | KTU Use Case | |-------|-----------|----------------------|--------------| | **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | Owner of the resource decides who gets access. | Personal Google Drive sharing. | | **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | System-enforced labels (Top Secret, Secret, Confidential) — user cannot change. | Military, government classified systems. | | **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Access is granted based on the user's *role* in the organization. | College ERP — students, faculty, admin get different menus. | | **ABAC** | Attribute-Based Access Control | Access based on attributes (user, resource, environment, action). | AWS IAM policies with conditions like `MFA=true AND region=us-east-1`. | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | |---------|----------------|-------------| | **Risk Magnitude** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | Risk = Threat probability × Vulnerability score × Impact value. | | **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | $SLE$ = Single Loss Expectancy (cost of one incident). $ARO$ = Annualized Rate of Occurrence. | | **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | Exposure Factor is the % of asset lost in one incident. | | **Net Savings of Control** | $NS = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost\ of\ Control$ | Positive NS = control is worth implementing. | | **Availability (Uptime)** | $Availability = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair. | | **Nines of Uptime** | $\text{Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | A "five-nines" ($99.999\%$) system has $\approx 5.26$ min downtime/year. | | **Information Entropy (Shannon)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measures uncertainty/randomness of data — foundation of cryptography. | | **Kerckhoffs's Principle** | *Security must reside in the key, not in the secrecy of the algorithm.* | Holds for AES, RSA, etc. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Note:** The ALE formula $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ is a **direct question favorite** in Cloud Infrastructure courses. Memorize it as a 7-mark numerical. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cloud Security (AWS/Azure/GCP)**: AWS IAM implements **RBAC + ABAC**. Availability is contractual via **SLAs** (e.g., EC2 = 99.99%). - **Healthcare (HIPAA)**: Enforces **Confidentiality** through encryption-at-rest and audit logs. - **Banking (PCI-DSS)**: Enforces **Integrity** through transaction hashing and **Non-Repudiation** via digital signatures. - **Defense (Military)**: Enforces **MAC** with clearance levels. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Computing ALE and Net Savings > **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 7-Mark Question):** > An e-commerce server hosts an asset worth **₹10,00,000**. The Exposure Factor (EF) for a ransomware attack is **0.4**, and such an attack is expected to occur **3 times per year**. The company's proposed control (advanced endpoint detection) costs **₹2,00,000 per year** and is expected to reduce the ARO from 3 to 0.5 and the EF from 0.4 to 0.1. Determine: > (a) The SLE and ALE *before* implementing the control. > (b) The SLE and ALE *after* implementing the control. > (c) Whether the control is worth its cost (compute Net Savings). #### Step 1 — Identify the Given Parameters We extract the numerical inputs as a clean variable set: $$Asset\ Value = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$$ $$EF_{before} = 0.4$$ $$ARO_{before} = 3$$ $$EF_{after} = 0.1$$ $$ARO_{after} = 0.5$$ $$Cost\ of\ Control = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000$$ #### Step 2 — Compute SLE Before Control Using the formula: $$SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$$ Substituting the values for the "before" scenario: $$SLE_{before} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.4$$ $$SLE_{before} = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000$$ **Interpretation:** A single ransomware attack would cause a loss of ₹4,00,000. #### Step 3 — Compute ALE Before Control Using the formula: $$ALE = SLE \times ARO$$ Substituting the "before" values: $$ALE_{before} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 3$$ $$ALE_{before} = \text{₹}12{,}00{,}000$$ **Interpretation:** Without any control, the company expects to lose ₹12,00,000 per year. #### Step 4 — Compute SLE After Control Re-applying the SLE formula with the reduced EF: $$SLE_{after} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1$$ $$SLE_{after} = \text{₹}1{,}00{,}000$$ **Interpretation:** With the control, even a successful attack causes only ₹1,00,000 in damage (because the EDR limits encryption spread). #### Step 5 — Compute ALE After Control Re-applying the ALE formula with the reduced ARO: $$ALE_{after} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5$$ $$ALE_{after} = \text{₹}50{,}000$$ **Interpretation:** With the control, the expected annual loss drops to ₹50,000. #### Step 6 — Compute Net Savings (NS) The Net Savings formula is: $$NS = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost\ of\ Control$$ Substituting the computed values: $$NS = 12{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000$$ $$NS = 12{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}50{,}000$$ $$NS = \text{₹}9{,}50{,}000$$ **Conclusion:** The control yields a **positive Net Savings of ₹9,50,000**, so it is **financially justified and should be implemented**. > [!NOTE] > **Board Tip:** Always show the substitution step separately for *before* and *after* — KTU examiners give partial marks for each correct intermediate value. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Concepts to Cloud Services | Security Concept | AWS Counterpart | Azure Counterpart | Google Cloud Counterpart | |------------------|-----------------|-------------------|--------------------------| | Confidentiality (encryption at rest) | AWS KMS, S3 SSE | Azure Key Vault, Storage SSE | Cloud KMS, CMEK | | Integrity (object immutability) | S3 Object Lock | Blob Immutable Storage | Object Versioning + Retention | | Availability (multi-region) | S3 Cross-Region Replication | Azure Geo-Redundant Storage (GRS) | Multi-Region Storage Buckets | | Authentication | AWS IAM Users, Cognito | Azure Active Directory | Cloud IAM, Identity Platform | | Authorization | IAM Policies | Azure RBAC | IAM Roles + Conditions | | Accountability | AWS CloudTrail | Azure Activity Log | Cloud Audit Logs | | Non-Repudiation | AWS CloudTrail + S3 Object Lock | Azure Immutable Blob Logs | Cloud Audit Logs + Signed URLs | ### 3.3 Decision Tree — Choosing an Access Control Model The following pseudocode-style logic is what an Information Security Architect uses: ``` IF organization is MILITARY / GOVERNMENT then USE Mandatory Access Control (MAC) ELSE IF organization has CLEAR JOB ROLES then USE Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) ELSE IF rules depend on USER + RESOURCE + CONTEXT attributes then USE Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) ELSE USE Discretionary Access Control (DAC) END IF ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This decision tree, when written in a KTU exam, **earns full method marks** even if the final answer is debated. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The CIA Triad (Visual Foundation) ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef ciaNode fill:#1a73e8,stroke:#0b3d91,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold classDef attrNode fill:#fbbc04,stroke:#b06000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 C[CONFIDENTIALITY
Authorized disclosure only]:::ciaNode I[INTEGRITY
Accurate and unaltered]:::ciaNode A[AVAILABILITY
Accessible on demand]:::ciaNode A1[Authentication]:::attrNode A2[Authorization]:::attrNode A3[Accountability]:::attrNode A4[Non Repudiation]:::attrNode C --- A1 I --- A2 A --- A3 C --- A4 A1 -.- A2 A2 -.- A3 A3 -.- A4 ``` ### 4.2 Layered "Defense in Depth" Architecture for Cloud ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef layer1 fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#7a0e0e,color:#ffffff classDef layer2 fill:#ffa94d,stroke:#7a3e00,color:#ffffff classDef layer3 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#7a6700,color:#000000 classDef layer4 fill:#69db7c,stroke:#0e5a1d,color:#ffffff classDef layer5 fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#0b3d91,color:#ffffff classDef layer6 fill:#b197fc,stroke:#3a1f8c,color:#ffffff classDef dataNode fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 L1[LAYER 1 PERIMETER
WAF DDoS Protection]:::layer1 L2[LAYER 2 NETWORK
VPC Security Groups NACL]:::layer2 L3[LAYER 3 HOST
OS Patching EDR Antivirus]:::layer3 L4[LAYER 4 APPLICATION
Input Validation AuthN]:::layer4 L5[LAYER 5 DATA
Encryption At Rest And In Transit]:::layer5 L6[LAYER 6 MONITORING
SIEM Logging Alerting]:::layer6 D[DATA AND ASSETS
Crown Jewels]:::dataNode L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 L5 --> L6 L6 --> D ``` ### 4.3 Risk Management Lifecycle (NIST-Aligned) ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef phase fill:#e7f5ff,stroke:#1971c2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef dec fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#b08900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 P1[ASSET IDENTIFICATION]:::phase P2[THREAT IDENTIFICATION]:::phase P3[VULNERABILITY ASSESSMENT]:::phase P4[RISK ANALYSIS
R = T x V x I]:::dec P5[RISK TREATMENT
Accept Mitigate Transfer Avoid]:::phase P6[MONITOR AND REVIEW]:::phase P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 P6 -.feedback.-> P1 ``` ### 4.4 Access Control Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef ok fill:#b2f2bb,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000 classDef deny fill:#ffc9c9,stroke:#7a0e0e,color:#000000 classDef q fill:#d0bfff,stroke:#3a1f8c,color:#000000 Q1{Who is the user?}:::q Q2{Is the user authenticated?}:::q Q3{Is the user authorized for this resource?}:::q Q4{Is the request anomalous or risky?}:::q OK[ALLOW ACCESS
Log to Audit Trail]:::ok D1[DENY UNAUTHENTICATED]:::deny D2[DENY UNAUTHORIZED]:::deny D3[STEP UP AUTH
Require MFA]:::ok Q1 --> Q2 Q2 -- NO --> D1 Q2 -- YES --> Q3 Q3 -- NO --> D2 Q3 -- YES --> Q4 Q4 -- YES --> D3 Q4 -- NO --> OK D3 --> OK ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Tip for KTU Answer Sheet:** Even a hand-drawn *Defense in Depth onion* with 5 concentric circles is worth **2-3 extra marks** in a 14-mark answer on cloud security. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]`** *Define the CIA Triad. Why is it considered the foundation of information security?* **Model Answer (≈ 60 words):** The CIA Triad is a foundational model in information security consisting of three principles: - **Confidentiality** — ensuring information is accessible only to authorized entities. - **Integrity** — ensuring information is accurate and unaltered. - **Availability** — ensuring information and systems are accessible when needed. It is foundational because every security policy, control, or technology in cloud or traditional systems is designed to protect at least one of these three properties. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Set B]`** *Differentiate between Authentication and Authorization with a suitable example.* **Model Answer (≈ 70 words):** | Aspect | Authentication | Authorization | |--------|---------------|---------------| | Question answered | "Who are you?" | "What can you do?" | | Mechanism | Passwords, biometrics, OTP, MFA | RBAC roles, IAM policies, ACLs | | Order | Happens *first* | Happens *after* authentication | | Example | Entering username + password on KTU portal | A "Student" role can view marks but cannot edit them | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define the terms *threat, vulnerability, risk*, and *attack*. Explain with a cloud-based example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An organization has a database server worth **₹20,00,000**. The Exposure Factor for a SQL injection attack is **0.5**, and the Annualized Rate of Occurrence is **2 per year**. A Web Application Firewall (WAF) costing **₹3,00,000/year** can reduce the EF to **0.1** and ARO to **0.25**. Compute the **SLE, ALE (before and after)**, and the **Net Savings** of the control. Recommend whether the control should be adopted. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Definitions + Cloud Example:** | Term | Definition | Cloud Example | |------|-----------|--------------| | **Threat** | A potential cause of an unwanted incident. | A hacker using a botnet to attack an EC2 instance. | | **Vulnerability** | A weakness in a system. | An unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability in an AWS-hosted Tomcat server. | | **Risk** | Probability × Impact of a threat exploiting a vulnerability. | High — the EC2 instance holds customer PII and is publicly accessible. | | **Attack** | The actual action that exploits a vulnerability. | SQL injection against the RDS database to dump user credentials. | The relationship: a **vulnerability** (unpatched software) is exploited by a **threat** (botnet) to carry out an **attack** (data exfiltration), resulting in **risk** (loss of customer trust and GDPR fines). **(b) Numerical Solution:** *Step 1 — Compute SLE Before:* $$SLE_{before} = Asset\ Value \times EF_{before} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$$ *Step 2 — Compute ALE Before:* $$ALE_{before} = SLE_{before} \times ARO_{before} = 10{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = \text{₹}20{,}00{,}000$$ *Step 3 — Compute SLE After:* $$SLE_{after} = 20{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1 = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000$$ *Step 4 — Compute ALE After:* $$ALE_{after} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 0.25 = \text{₹}50{,}000$$ *Step 5 — Compute Net Savings:* $$NS = ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Cost\ of\ Control$$ $$NS = 20{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}16{,}50{,}000$$ **Conclusion:** Net Savings is **positive (+₹16,50,000)**, so the WAF is **strongly recommended** for adoption. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **(a)** Explain the four access control models — **DAC, MAC, RBAC, and ABAC** — with one real-world example each. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Defense in Depth** security model. List and briefly describe **any four layers** of this model as applied to a **cloud data center**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Access Control Models:** - **DAC (Discretionary Access Control):** The data owner decides who can access. Example — A Google Drive owner can share a file with "Anyone with the link." - **MAC (Mandatory Access Control):** The system enforces access via sensitivity labels. Example — Military networks use "Top Secret," "Secret," and "Unclassified" labels; a user with "Secret" clearance cannot read a "Top Secret" file. - **RBAC (Role-Based Access Control):** Access is granted by job role. Example — In a college ERP, "Faculty" role can upload marks, "Student" role can view them, "HOD" role can approve them. - **ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control):** Access depends on attributes (user, resource, environment). Example — AWS IAM policy allowing `s3:GetObject` *only* if `aws:MultiFactorAuthPresent=true` AND `aws:RequestedRegion=us-east-1`. **(b) Defense in Depth Diagram + Cloud Layers:** The model uses **multiple overlapping security layers** so that if one layer fails, the next still protects. Applied to a cloud data center: 1. **Perimeter Layer** — Web Application Firewall (WAF), AWS Shield for DDoS protection at the edge. 2. **Network Layer** — VPC Security Groups, Network ACLs, private subnets, VPC Flow Logs. 3. **Host / Compute Layer** — Patched AMIs, EC2 Inspector, EDR (CrowdStrike/SentinelOne), IMDSv2. 4. **Application Layer** — Input validation, OAuth 2.0 authentication, parameterized SQL queries. 5. **Data Layer** — Encryption at rest (KMS, S3 SSE), encryption in transit (TLS 1.3), S3 Object Lock. 6. **Monitoring Layer** — CloudTrail, GuardDuty, Security Hub, SIEM, automated incident response. If an attacker bypasses the WAF, the Security Group still blocks them. If they break the SG, the host EDR still catches them. If they compromise the host, encryption still protects the data. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse Authentication with Authorization.** Examiners deduct **1 full mark** for this mix-up, and it is the most common mistake. > 2. **For numerical questions**, always state the *formula first* → *substitute values* → *final answer with units (₹)*. Skipping the formula means you lose **2 marks** out of 7 even if your final number is correct. > 3. **Do NOT list "Antivirus" and "Firewall" as layers of CIA Triad.** The CIA Triad has three properties; controls like firewalls *protect* those properties — they are not properties themselves. > 4. **For Defense in Depth**, you must draw the diagram AND name the layers. A diagram without layer names = 3 marks lost. Layers without diagram = 2 marks lost. > 5. **In ALE/ARO questions**, if the computed Net Savings is **negative**, the correct answer is *"Control should NOT be adopted"* — do not write "yes, adopt" just because the control is good in theory. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality + Integrity + Availability. **Always** draw the triangle. - **Authentication** = "Who are you?"; **Authorization** = "What can you do?"; **Accountability** = "Who did it?"; **Non-Repudiation** = "Sender can't deny." - **Threat ≠ Vulnerability ≠ Risk ≠ Attack.** Threat is the *cause*, vulnerability is the *weakness*, risk is the *likelihood × impact*, attack is the *action*. - **Defense in Depth** = multiple overlapping security layers — security in depth, not security in one place. - **Least Privilege** = give minimum access needed; **Zero Trust** = verify everything, trust nothing by default. - **Access Control Models**: DAC (owner decides), MAC (system labels), RBAC (role-based), ABAC (attribute-based). - **ALE Formula** = $SLE \times ARO$; **SLE** = $Asset\ Value \times EF$; **Net Savings** = $ALE_{before} - ALE_{after} - Control\ Cost$. - **Positive Net Savings** → adopt the control; **Negative** → reject. - **Parkerian Hexad** = CIA + Authenticity + Possession + Utility (use this to impress the examiner). - **Kerckhoffs's Principle** = security rests in the *key*, not in keeping the algorithm secret. - **CIA Triad ↔ Cloud Mapping**: AWS KMS = Confidentiality; S3 Object Lock = Integrity; Multi-Region = Availability; CloudTrail = Accountability; IAM = AuthN/AuthZ. - **Common KTU 3-mark Qs**: Define CIA, list 4 access control models, differentiate AuthN vs AuthZ, define risk, state Kerckhoffs's principle. - **Common KTU 7-mark Qs**: Solve an ALE numerical; draw and explain Defense in Depth; explain access control models with examples; explain the risk management lifecycle.

topics.title: Threat Agents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Threat Agents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of cloud infrastructure and systems security, a **Threat Agent** is formally defined as any internal or external entity, circumstance, or condition that possesses the **capability**, **intent**, and **opportunity** to exploit a vulnerability within a cloud system, thereby causing adverse effects to the confidentiality, integrity, availability, or non-repudiation of cloud-hosted assets.

According to the NIST SP 800-30 framework (adapted to KTU 2024 cloud security curriculum), a threat agent is characterized by three orthogonal vectors: **Agent Class** (who/what), **Capability Level** (skill/resources), and **Intent** (motivational profile).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Threat Agents form the *first pillar* of the classic CIA (Confidentiality-Integrity-Availability) Threat Model. Without identifying the threat agent, no risk assessment, penetration test, or security control implementation in a cloud environment can be considered complete.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **high-security bank vault**. The vault itself is the *cloud infrastructure*. The locked door, CCTV, and motion sensors are the *security controls*. The thieves, rogue employees, natural disasters (floods/earthquakes), and even careless cleaners are the **threat agents**.
