> **Examiner's Trick:** Notice that the *same* attack is intercepted at *multiple* layers. This is the essence of **Defense in Depth** — even if Control at Layer N fails, Control at Layer N+1 should catch it.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CSA Cloud Security Reference Model — Layered Stack Architecture

The diagram below renders the nine-layer stack with the five cross-cutting concerns attached as orthogonal overlays. Each layer box carries a one-word token (no markdown inside quotes) to remain Mermaid-compliant.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[LAYER 9 APPLICATION]
    B[LAYER 8 DATA]
    C[LAYER 7 RUNTIME]
    D[LAYER 6 MIDDLEWARE]
    E[LAYER 5 OPERATING SYSTEM]
    F[LAYER 4 VIRTUALIZATION]
    G[LAYER 3 COMPUTE]
    H[LAYER 2 STORAGE]
    I[LAYER 1 NETWORK]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I

    CC1[IAM IDENTITY AND ACCESS]
    CC2[AUDIT AND LOGGING]
    CC3[COMPLIANCE AND GOVERNANCE]
    CC4[DATA PRIVACY AND SOVEREIGNTY]
    CC5[BC AND DR CONTINUITY]

    CC1 -.-> A
    CC1 -.-> B
    CC1 -.-> C
    CC1 -.-> D
    CC1 -.-> E
    CC1 -.-> F
    CC1 -.-> G
    CC1 -.-> H
    CC1 -.-> I

    CC2 -.-> A
    CC3 -.-> B
    CC4 -.-> H
    CC5 -.-> G
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The vertical chain (A→I) is the *cloud stack* (top = customer-facing, bottom = CSP-controlled). The dotted lines from CC1–CC5 are the *cross-cutting concerns* that pierce every layer.

### 4.2 Shared Responsibility Model — Service Model Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IAAS[IaaS RESPONSIBILITY SPLIT]
        I1[Customer Secures Application Data Runtime Middleware OS]
        I2[CSP Secures Virtualization Compute Storage Network]
    end

    subgraph PAAS[PaaS RESPONSIBILITY SPLIT]
        P1[Customer Secures Application Data]
        P2[CSP Secures Runtime Middleware OS Virtualization Compute Storage Network]
    end

    subgraph SAAS[SaaS RESPONSIBILITY SPLIT]
        S1[Customer Secures Access and Data Usage]
        S2[CSP Secures Application Runtime Middleware OS Virtualization Compute Storage Network]
    end

    IAAS --> PAAS
    PAAS --> SAAS

    S1 --> NOTE[Customer Burden DECREASES as you move IaaS to SaaS]
    S2 --> NOTE2[CSP Burden INCREASES as you move IaaS to SaaS]
```

### 4.3 Defense-in-Depth — Attack Path Tracing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    STEP1[Attacker sends phishing email]
    STEP2[Network Layer EMAIL GATEWAY filters]
    STEP3[Application Layer WAF blocks malicious URL]
    STEP4[OS Layer APPARMOR restricts process]
    STEP5[Data Layer DLP blocks exfiltration]
    STEP6[Audit Layer SIEM raises alert]
    STEP7[IAM Layer MFA challenge issued]

    STEP1 --> STEP2
    STEP2 --> STEP3
    STEP3 --> STEP4
    STEP4 --> STEP5
    STEP5 --> STEP6
    STEP6 --> STEP7
    STEP7 --> FINAL[INCIDENT CONTAINED]

    DEC1{Layer 2 fail}
    DEC2{Layer 4 fail}
    DEC3{Layer 6 fail}

    STEP2 -.->|YES| DEC1
    DEC1 -.->|Layer 3 still works| STEP3
    STEP4 -.->|YES| DEC2
    DEC2 -.->|Layer 5 still works| STEP5
    STEP6 -.->|YES| DEC3
    DEC3 -.->|Layer 7 still works| STEP7
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each diamond represents a *control failure* at a particular layer. The model demonstrates that even when one layer's control is bypassed, the *next* layer's control intercepts the attack — this is **resilience through layering**.

### 4.4 NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture — Actor View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CSC[Cloud Service Consumer]
    CSP[Cloud Service Provider]
    CSA[Cloud Service Auditor]
    CB[Cloud Broker]
    CC[Cloud Carrier]

    CSC -->|uses services| CSP
    CSC -->|audits| CSA
    CSA -->|audits| CSP
    CB -->|aggregates and arbitrates| CSP
    CC -->|provides connectivity| CSP
    CC -->|provides connectivity| CSC
    CSC -->|uses connectivity| CC
```

> **Mapping to KTU syllabus:** NIST SP 500-292 defines **five primary actors**. The CSA reference model is layered *vertically*; the NIST reference architecture is layered *horizontally* by actor roles. The two are complementary, not redundant.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q1a]`
> **Define the Cloud Security Reference Model. List any four abstraction layers defined by the CSA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for layers):**

The **Cloud Security Reference Model** is a conceptual, layered framework that maps security risks, threats, and corresponding controls to each architectural layer of a cloud computing stack. It provides a standardized taxonomy for understanding *what* must be secured at each level of abstraction and *who* bears the responsibility.

Four abstraction layers defined by the CSA (top to bottom):

1. **Application Layer** — SaaS applications, web apps, and APIs.
2. **Data Layer** — Customer data, databases, and object stores.
3. **Runtime Layer** — Execution environments (JVM, CLR, container runtimes).
4. **Network Layer** — Switches, routers, SDN fabric, and physical connectivity.

*(Any four accepted; full credit for correct identification.)*

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q1b]`
> **Differentiate between the responsibilities of a Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and Cloud Service Consumer (CSC) in an IaaS deployment. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | IaaS — CSP Responsibility | IaaS — CSC Responsibility |
|--------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Hardware | Physical servers, storage, networking | None |
| Virtualization | Hypervisor, VM isolation | Choose VM size/OS image |
| Operating System | None | Patching, hardening of guest OS |
| Middleware | None | Install and configure |
| Runtime | None | Configure execution environment |
| Application & Data | None | Develop, deploy, secure |
| Identity & Access | IAM for cloud account | IAM for their own resources |

**Key insight (1 mark):** In IaaS, the CSC has the **maximum** security responsibility — everything *above* and *including* the guest OS.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

> **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q2a]`
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **CSA Cloud Security Reference Model** and enumerate the **cross-cutting security concerns**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Explain the **Shared Responsibility Model** for IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. Which service model places the **least** security burden on the consumer? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CSA Cloud Security Reference Model (7 marks)**

The CSA Cloud Security Reference Model, derived from CSA Guidance v4.0, is a layered conceptual framework that decomposes the cloud environment into **nine vertical layers** and overlays **five horizontal cross-cutting concerns**.

**The Nine Vertical Layers (3 marks):**

1. **Application** — SaaS applications, web apps, APIs (e.g., Salesforce, Office 365).
2. **Data** — Customer data, databases, object storage (e.g., S3, BigQuery).
3. **Runtime** — Execution engines like JVM, CLR, Python interpreter.
4. **Middleware** — API gateways, message queues, ESBs.
5. **Operating System** — Guest OS of VMs, container host OS.
6. **Virtualization** — Hypervisors (KVM, ESXi), container engines (Docker, containerd).
7. **Compute** — Physical CPUs, memory, server hardware.
8. **Storage** — Block, file, and object storage subsystems.
9. **Network** — Switches, routers, SDN controllers, physical cabling.

**Cross-Cutting Concerns (3 marks):**

1. **Identity & Access Management (IAM)** — Authentication (AuthN), Authorization (AuthZ), federation, MFA, RBAC.
2. **Audit & Logging** — Immutable, tamper-evident logs forwarded to SIEM.
3. **Compliance & Governance** — GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOC 2, ISO 27001.
4. **Data Privacy & Sovereignty** — Data residency, encryption key management (BYOK, HYOK).
5. **Business Continuity & Disaster Recovery (BC/DR)** — RTO, RPO, multi-region failover.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes. (Mention the vertical stack with horizontal cross-cutting arrows.)

**(b) Shared Responsibility Model (7 marks)**

| Layer | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|-------|------|------|------|
| Application | CSC | CSC | CSP |
| Data | CSC | CSC | CSC |
| Runtime | CSC | CSP | CSP |
| Middleware | CSC | CSP | CSP |
| OS | CSC | CSP | CSP |
| Virtualization | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| Compute / Storage / Network | CSP | CSP | CSP |

**Analysis (3 marks):**

- In **IaaS**, the customer secures from the OS upward — maximum burden.
- In **PaaS**, the customer secures only the application and data — medium burden.
- In **SaaS**, the customer secures only access, identity, and their own data usage — **minimum burden**.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The **SaaS** service model places the **least security burden** on the consumer because the CSP manages almost the entire stack — from physical infrastructure through the application layer — leaving the consumer responsible only for user access control and the data they upload.

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Q2b]`
> **(a)** What is **Defense in Depth**? Describe how a single attack traverses multiple layers of the cloud security reference model. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A cloud-hosted e-commerce application has an asset value of ₹80,00,000. A SQL injection attack has an exposure factor of 0.60 and is expected to occur **twice per year**. The company is evaluating a **Web Application Firewall (WAF)** that costs ₹3,00,000 per year and reduces the exposure factor to **0.15**. Calculate the **inherent ALE**, the **residual ALE with WAF**, and the **ROSI** of deploying the WAF. Recommend whether the WAF should be deployed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Defense in Depth (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Defense in Depth (DiD) is a security strategy that employs **multiple, overlapping layers of controls** so that the failure of one control does not lead to a complete compromise. Each layer is designed to detect, deter, delay, or respond to attacks that bypassed the previous layer.

**Attack Traversal (5 marks):**
Consider a **SQL injection attack** against a cloud-hosted web application:

| Step | Layer | What Happens | Control Engaged |
|------|-------|--------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Network | Attacker's HTTP request enters the VPC via the load balancer. | NACL, Security Group |
| 2 | Application | Malicious SQL reaches the web app. | WAF signature matching |
| 3 | Runtime | Database query interpreter executes the payload. | Input sanitization, parameterized queries |
| 4 | Data | Unauthorized rows are returned to attacker. | Row-Level Security (RLS), DLP |
| 5 | Audit | Anomalous query volume is logged. | SIEM alerting |
| 6 | IAM | Attacker's session is invalidated. | Session timeout, MFA re-challenge |

**Insight:** Even if the WAF misses the payload, the application's parameterized-query defense stops the injection. If that fails, RLS limits the data returned. The attacker must defeat *all* layers sequentially — exponentially increasing difficulty.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Asset Value = ₹80,00,000
- Exposure Factor (inherent) = 0.60
- ARO = 2 attacks/year
- WAF cost = ₹3,00,000/year
- Exposure Factor (residual, with WAF) = 0.15

**Step 1 — Inherent SLE:**
$$
SLE_{inherent} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.60 = ₹48{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Inherent ALE:**
$$
ALE_{inherent} = 48{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = ₹96{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Residual SLE (with WAF):**
$$
SLE_{WAF} = 80{,}00{,}000 \times 0.15 = ₹12{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Residual ALE (with WAF):**
$$
ALE_{WAF} = 12{,}00{,}000 \times 2 = ₹24{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 5 — Risk Reduction (Annual Savings):**
$$
\Delta ALE = 96{,}00{,}000 - 24{,}00{,}000 = ₹72{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 6 — ROSI Calculation:**
$$
ROSI = \dfrac{\Delta ALE - Cost\_of\_Control}{Cost\_of\_Control} \times 100
$$

$$
ROSI = \dfrac{72{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
ROSI = \dfrac{69{,}00{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 2300\%
$$

**Step 7 — Recommendation (1 mark):**
Since $ROSI = 2300\%$, the WAF yields a **23-fold return** on its cost. **The WAF must be deployed.** Beyond pure economics, the WAF also reduces breach probability, regulatory exposure, and reputational damage — none of which are captured in the ALE model.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps Reported by KTU Valuators:**
> 1. **Confusing layers and cross-cutting concerns** — Many students list the nine layers but forget the five horizontal concerns (or vice versa). Examiners explicitly ask for both — losing 2–3 marks here is common.
> 2. **Saying "AWS is responsible for everything"** — This violates the Shared Responsibility Model. Even in AWS EC2 (IaaS), the customer must patch the guest OS. Generic answers are penalized.
> 3. **Skipping the ARO unit** — ARO is dimensionless (*breaches per year*), not "per month." Converting units correctly earns full marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert ALE to per-year** — If ARO is "once every 5 years," then $ARO = 0.20$, not "5."
> 5. **Treating the CSA model and NIST model as the same** — They are complementary. CSA is *layer-centric*; NIST is *actor-centric*. Examiners reward students who articulate the distinction.
> 6. **Skipping the recommendation step** in ROSI problems — The numerical answer is not enough; the examiner expects a clear *verdict* with economic justification.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cloud Security Reference Model**

- ✅ **CSA Reference Model** has **9 vertical layers** and **5 cross-cutting concerns** — both are required for full marks.
- ✅ Layer order top→bottom: **Application → Data → Runtime → Middleware → OS → Virtualization → Compute → Storage → Network**.
- ✅ Cross-cutting concerns: **IAM, Audit, Compliance, Data Privacy, BC/DR**.
- ✅ **Shared Responsibility Model**: IaaS = customer owns OS-up; PaaS = customer owns app+data; SaaS = customer owns access+data only.
- ✅ **SaaS = least customer burden**; **IaaS = most customer burden**.
- ✅ Risk formula: $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ where $SLE = Asset\_Value \times Exposure\_Factor$.
- ✅ ROSI formula: $ROSI = \dfrac{\Delta ALE - Cost\_of\_Control}{Cost\_of\_Control} \times 100\%$. **Positive ROSI → deploy the control.**
- ✅ Availability: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$. SLA of "three nines" (99.9%) permits ~8.77 hours/year downtime.
- ✅ **Defense in Depth** = multiple overlapping controls across layers; failure of one layer should not compromise the system.
- ✅ **NIST Reference Architecture** identifies **5 actors**: Consumer, Provider, Auditor, Broker, Carrier — *complementary* to the CSA layered view.
- ✅ **CSA Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM) v4** has **197 controls across 17 domains** — referenced in real-world audits.
- ✅ **Standards to memorize by acronym**: ISO 27017 (cloud security), ISO 27018 (cloud privacy), SOC 2 (service organization controls), PCI-DSS (payment cards), HIPAA (health data), GDPR (EU data protection).
- ✅ **Exam rule of thumb**: If a question mentions "who secures what," draw a **layer-vs-service-model table** for guaranteed full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examining Cloud Security Against Traditional Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Examining Cloud Security Against Traditional Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cloud Security** is the discipline and set of technologies, policies, controls, and services that protect cloud computing environments, applications, data, and infrastructure from internal and external threats. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, examining cloud security against traditional computing involves a comparative analysis of the security postures, threat landscapes, trust boundaries, and defense mechanisms that differentiate the on-premise (traditional) data center model from the public, private, hybrid, and community cloud delivery models.

**Traditional Computing Security** (also termed *on-premise security* or *legacy IT security*) refers to the protection of computing assets, data, and network resources housed within an organization's physically owned and controlled infrastructure, where the organization assumes full ownership of every layer of the security stack — from the physical perimeter to the application logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The central pivot of this topic is the **Shared Responsibility Model (SRM)** — the conceptual framework that delineates *who is responsible for what* between the Cloud Service Provider (CSP) and the cloud consumer. Mastering this boundary is a guaranteed high-yield area for Part A (3-mark) questions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The House vs. The Apartment (with a Shared Security Guard):**

Imagine you own a **detached house** (Traditional IT). You build your own boundary wall, install your own CCTV, hire your own security guard, maintain your own locks, and repair your own roof. You are the **sole custodian** of every security concern. If a thief enters, the blame rests entirely with you because the entire perimeter is yours to defend.

Now imagine you move into a **high-rise serviced apartment** (Cloud Computing). The building management provides structural walls, fire alarms, perimeter security, and elevator access control. However, **inside your flat, you must still lock your own door, install your own safe, and protect your personal belongings**. The building management guarantees structural safety; you guarantee personal safety. The boundary shifts — and so does the **liability**. This boundary is exactly what the *Shared Responsibility Model* formalizes in cloud computing.

### 1.3 Key Distinguishing Metrics

The following are standard industry metrics that quantify the security posture shift:

- **Perimeter Surface Area:** Traditional = $\approx N$ (defined perimeter); Cloud = $\approx N \times k$ where $k$ is the elasticity factor due to dynamic resource provisioning.
- **Mean Time to Patch (MTTP):** Traditional = weeks to months; Cloud = hours to days.
- **Shared Responsibility SLA Uptime:** Typically **99.9% (Three 9s)**, **99.99% (Four 9s)**, or **99.999% (Five 9s)** depending on the service tier.
- **Attack Vector Count:** Cloud introduces **3–7 new attack vectors** per NIST SP 800-144, including side-channel, hypervisor escape, and API abuse.

### 1.4 Module Positioning

This topic serves as the **conceptual bridge** between Module 1 (Cloud Fundamentals) and the remaining modules of PECCT631 that deal with virtualization hardening, identity federation, and cloud-native security services.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Trust Boundary Comparison

In **Traditional Computing**, the security perimeter is well-defined, static, and fully owned by the enterprise. In **Cloud Computing**, the perimeter is fluid, dynamic, and partially delegated to a third party. The following bulleted logic breaks down the operational reality of each model.

#### 2.1.1 Traditional Computing — Operational Security Stack

- **Layer 1 — Physical Security:** Fenced data centers, biometric access, mantraps, CCTV, and on-site security guards. All assets are under one corporate roof.
- **Layer 2 — Network Perimeter:** Hardened firewalls, DMZ (Demilitarized Zone) segmentation, IDS/IPS appliances, and dedicated MPLS links.
- **Layer 3 — Host & OS Security:** Hardened OS images, antivirus, HIDS (Host-based Intrusion Detection Systems), local patch management cycles.
- **Layer 4 — Application & Data:** In-house developed applications secured via WAF (Web Application Firewalls), database encryption at rest, and TLS 1.2/1.3 in transit.
- **Layer 5 — Identity & Access:** On-premise Active Directory (AD), LDAP, RBAC (Role-Based Access Control), and manually managed user lifecycles.

> The enterprise has **full-stack control** but must **bear 100% of the cost, risk, and operational burden**.

#### 2.1.2 Cloud Computing — Operational Security Stack

- **Layer 1 — Physical Security (CSP-owned):** Hyperscale data centers with biometric access, geo-redundancy, and 24/7 SOC (Security Operations Center). Consumers do not own this.
- **Layer 2 — Network & Virtualization (Shared):** Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) isolation, Security Groups, Network ACLs, micro-segmentation, and provider-managed DDoS protection.
- **Layer 3 — Compute & Storage (Shared):** Hypervisor hardening, confidential computing enclaves (e.g., Intel SGX, AMD SEV), customer-managed encryption keys (CMK) via KMS/HSM.
- **Layer 4 — Application (Consumer-owned):** SAST/DAST scanning, API gateways, OAuth 2.1, OpenID Connect, secure SDLC practices.
- **Layer 5 — Data & Identity (Consumer-owned):** Cloud IAM, Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA), customer-managed keys, data classification, and DLP (Data Loss Prevention) policies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently frame a 7-mark question around drawing and explaining this comparative stack. A clean labeled diagram with clear "Customer vs. Provider" color bands is a guaranteed differentiator in the answer script.

### 2.2 The Shared Responsibility Model (SRM) — The Core Theorem

The SRM is best expressed as a **responsibility allocation function** $R(s, l)$ where $s$ is the service model and $l$ is the architectural layer:

$$
R(s, l) =
\begin{cases}
\text{CSP}, & \text{if } l \in L_{\text{provider}}(s) \\
\text{Customer}, & \text{if } l \in L_{\text{consumer}}(s) \\
\text{Shared}, & \text{if } l \in L_{\text{joint}}(s)
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $s \in \{\text{IaaS, PaaS, SaaS}\}$ — the cloud service model.
- $L_{\text{provider}}(s)$ — the set of layers abstracted away from the consumer as $s$ increases (moving from IaaS toward SaaS).
- $L_{\text{consumer}}(s)$ — the layers the customer must always defend (data, identity, access).
- $L_{\text{joint}}(s)$ — the overlapping operational responsibilities (patching, configuration, monitoring).

**Key Insight:** As $s$ moves from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS, $L_{\text{provider}}$ **grows monotonically**, and $L_{\text{consumer}}$ **shrinks monotonically**, but **never reaches zero**. The customer always retains data and identity sovereignty.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Traditional Computing | Cloud Computing |
|---|---|---|
| **Perimeter** | Static, well-defined | Dynamic, software-defined, ephemeral |
| **Control** | $100\%$ enterprise-owned | Distributed via SRM |
| **Cost Model** | CapEx (Capital Expenditure) | OpEx (Operational Expenditure) |
| **Patch Latency** | Weeks to months | Hours to days (CSP automated) |
| **Scalability of Defense** | Linear with hardware | Elastic, on-demand |
| **Data Location** | Known, fixed | Multi-region, often unknown to user |
| **Compliance Scope** | Internal audit only | Multi-jurisdictional (GDPR, DPDPA, HIPAA) |
| **Identity Boundary** | Single AD forest | Federated (SAML, OIDC, OAuth 2.1) |
| **Encryption Key Custody** | Internal HSM | Customer or CSP-managed (KMS/HSM) |
| **DDoS Resilience** | Limited by pipe size | Provider-scale scrubbing (e.g., AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection) |
| **Logging Telemetry** | Siloed SIEM | Centralized, API-driven (CloudTrail, Azure Monitor) |
| **Disaster Recovery RTO** | Hours to days | Seconds to minutes (multi-AZ failover) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Tip:** When asked to "compare," always present a **two-column table** with **at least 6 rows**. KTU evaluators award extra marks for structured, visual comparisons over paragraph-style answers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cloud security principles underpin:
- **Financial Services:** PCI-DSS 4.0 compliance in the cloud, tokenization of cardholder data.
- **Healthcare:** HIPAA-compliant PHI storage using BAA-backed cloud services.
- **Government:** FedRAMP-authorized cloud deployments for sovereign workloads.
- **DevSecOps:** Shift-left security via SAST/DAST in CI/CD pipelines — a capability nearly impossible to retrofit in traditional environments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Security Surface Area Equation

Let us mathematically formalize the **attack surface expansion** that occurs when an enterprise migrates from traditional to cloud computing.

**Step 1 — Define the Traditional Attack Surface.**

In traditional computing, every exposed service endpoint is a discrete attack vector. Let the enterprise have $N$ discrete assets, each with a vulnerability exposure $v_i \in [0, 1]$.

$$
S_{\text{trad}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Cloud Multiplier.**

When workloads are migrated to the cloud, the elasticity factor $k$ (where $k \geq 1$) captures the additional vectors introduced by:
- Public APIs
- Ephemeral container instances
- Serverless function triggers
- Cross-region replication channels
- Identity federation trust links

$$
k = 1 + \alpha \cdot N_{\text{api}} + \beta \cdot N_{\text{ephem}} + \gamma \cdot N_{\text{feder}}
$$

Where:
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are empirically derived weighting coefficients with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma \leq 1$.
- $N_{\text{api}}$ = number of public-facing API endpoints.
- $N_{\text{ephem}}$ = number of ephemeral compute instances.
- $N_{\text{feder}}$ = number of federated identity trust links.

**Step 3 — Express the Cloud Attack Surface.**

$$
S_{\text{cloud}} = k \cdot S_{\text{trad}} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} v_j^{\text{new}}
$$

Where $M$ is the count of *new* cloud-native attack vectors (e.g., misconfigured S3 buckets, exposed Kubernetes dashboards) and $v_j^{\text{new}}$ is their respective exposure score.

**Step 4 — Net Security Posture Comparison.**

The *effective* security posture is the attack surface scaled inversely by the defense density $D$:

$$
P_{\text{eff}} = \frac{S}{D}
$$

For traditional systems, $D_{\text{trad}}$ is bounded by the organization's static headcount and budget. For cloud, $D_{\text{cloud}}$ scales with the CSP's global SOC and automated remediation. Thus:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}^{\text{cloud}} = \frac{k \cdot S_{\text{trad}} + \sum v_j^{\text{new}}}{D_{\text{trad}} + D_{\text{csp}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion of Derivation.**

The cloud model is *only* more secure than the traditional model when the following inequality holds:

$$
D_{\text{csp}} > (k - 1) \cdot S_{\text{trad}} + \sum_{j=1}^{M} v_j^{\text{new}} - D_{\text{trad}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This inequality is the **theoretical justification** for why a small-to-mid enterprise can achieve stronger security in the cloud than on-premise — the provider's $D_{\text{csp}}$ typically overwhelms any surface expansion $k$ the customer introduces, *provided* the customer avoids configuration drift.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Threat Category Mapping

The following matrix aligns traditional threats with their cloud analogues, mapping the **OWASP Cloud Top 10** and **CSA Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM v4)** controls.

| Threat Category | Traditional Manifestation | Cloud Manifestation | Mitigation Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Breach** | SQL injection on internal DB | Misconfigured object storage (S3, Blob) leaking to public internet | Bucket policies, Block Public Access (BPA), KMS encryption |
| **Identity Theft** | Credential dumping from AD | Stolen IAM keys, OAuth token theft | MFA, short-lived STS tokens, IAM Access Analyzer |
| **Insider Threat** | Privileged local admin | Cross-account role assumption, exfiltration via CLI | Privileged Access Management (PAM), Just-in-Time access |
| **Man-in-the-Middle** | ARP poisoning on LAN | TLS stripping on misconfigured API endpoints | TLS 1.3 enforcement, certificate pinning, mTLS |
| **Denial of Service** | UDP flood on-prem | Volumetric L3/L7 DDoS at provider edge | AWS Shield Advanced, Azure DDoS Protection, Cloudflare |
| **VM Escape** | N/A in physical | Hypervisor vulnerability (e.g., Venom, Cloudburst) | Confidential Computing, hypervisor patching cadence |
| **API Abuse** | Limited legacy SOAP | BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization) per OWASP API Top 10 | API Gateway throttling, OAuth scopes, rate limiting |
| **Supply Chain** | Vendor hardware implant | Dependency confusion in container images | SBOM, signed images (Cosign), SLSA framework |
| **Compliance Drift** | Annual audit | Continuous compliance monitoring | AWS Audit Manager, Azure Policy, CIS Benchmarks |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step — Mapping SRM Across Service Models

**Step 1:** For **IaaS** (e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VM), the customer owns the Guest OS, middleware, runtime, application, data, and identity. The CSP owns the physical host, network, hypervisor, and storage fabric.

**Step 2:** For **PaaS** (e.g., AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service), the customer owns the application and data. The CSP owns everything below, including the OS and middleware.

**Step 3:** For **SaaS** (e.g., Microsoft 365, Google Workspace), the customer owns only the data and identity configuration (users, groups, policies). The CSP owns the entire stack.

**Step 4:** A useful mnemonic — **"In PaaS you Patch; in SaaS you Police"** — meaning even in SaaS, the customer must police user activity, data sharing, and access policies.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Shared Responsibility Model Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph csplayer["CSP OWNED SECURITY"]
        phys["Physical Security"]
        net["Network Infrastructure"]
        hv["Hypervisor and Virtualization"]
        provOS["Provisioning and Orchestration"]
    end
    subgraph sharedlayer["SHARED RESPONSIBILITY"]
        patchOS["OS Patching and Hardening"]
        cfg["Configuration Management"]
        mon["Monitoring and Logging"]
    end
    subgraph custlayer["CUSTOMER OWNED SECURITY"]
        id["Identity and Access Management IAM"]
        data["Data Encryption and Classification"]
        app["Application Logic and API Security"]
    end
    csplayer --> sharedlayer
    sharedlayer --> custlayer
    phys --> net
    net --> hv
    hv --> provOS
    patchOS --> cfg
    cfg --> mon
    mon --> id
    id --> data
    data --> app
```

### 4.2 Traditional vs Cloud Security Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph trad["TRADITIONAL ON PREMISE"]
        tradUser["User"] --> tradFW["Perimeter Firewall"]
        tradFW --> tradDMZ["DMZ Web Tier"]
        tradDMZ --> tradApp["Internal Application"]
        tradApp --> tradDB["On Premise Database"]
        tradDB --> tradAudit["Internal SOC and SIEM"]
    end
    subgraph cloud["CLOUD ENVIRONMENT"]
        cloudUser["User"] --> cloudEdge["Cloud Edge and WAF"]
        cloudEdge --> cloudVPC["VPC with Microsegmentation"]
        cloudVPC --> cloudApp["Containerized Microservice"]
        cloudApp --> cloudStore["Encrypted Object Storage"]
        cloudStore --> cloudSIEM["Centralized Cloud SIEM"]
        cloudSIEM --> cloudSOC["CSP and Customer Joint SOC"]
    end
    tradAudit -. "Migration Path" .- cloudEdge
```

### 4.3 Cloud Threat Vector Taxonomy (Block-Level Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph attack["ATTACK VECTORS IN CLOUD"]
        vec1["API Misconfiguration"]
        vec2["Credential Compromise"]
        vec3["Data Exfiltration via Object Storage"]
        vec4["Hypervisor Side Channel"]
        vec5["Container Escape"]
        vec6["Federated Identity Trust Abuse"]
    end
    subgraph control["DEFENSE IN DEPTH CONTROLS"]
        ctrl1["Zero Trust Architecture ZTA"]
        ctrl2["Encryption At Rest and In Transit"]
        ctrl3["CSPM Cloud Security Posture Management"]
        ctrl4["CWPP Cloud Workload Protection Platform"]
        ctrl5["CIEM Cloud Infrastructure Entitlement Management"]
        ctrl6["CASB Cloud Access Security Broker"]
    end
    vec1 --> ctrl3
    vec2 --> ctrl1
    vec2 --> ctrl5
    vec3 --> ctrl2
    vec3 --> ctrl6
    vec4 --> ctrl4
    vec5 --> ctrl4
    vec6 --> ctrl1
    vec6 --> ctrl5
```

### 4.4 Defense in Depth Layered Model (5-Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["Layer 1 Perimeter WAF and DDoS Protection"] --> L2["Layer 2 Network VPC Isolation and NACL"]
    L2 --> L3["Layer 3 Host HIDS Hardened OS Confidential Computing"]
    L3 --> L4["Layer 4 Application SAST DAST API Gateway"]
    L4 --> L5["Layer 5 Data Encryption Tokenization DLP and IAM"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Key:** Each box is a **functional security zone**, not a physical device. In cloud environments, these layers are delivered as managed services (e.g., AWS Shield = L1, Security Groups = L2, Inspector = L3, WAF = L4, KMS + Macie = L5).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**  
*Define the Shared Responsibility Model (SRM) in cloud computing. Why is it critical for an enterprise to clearly understand SRM boundaries?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Shared Responsibility Model (SRM)** is a cloud security framework that defines the division of security obligations between the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** and the **cloud customer** across all layers of the cloud stack (physical, network, compute, application, data, and identity).

It is critical because:
1. **Prevents security gaps:** Without clear ownership, both parties may assume the other has secured a layer, leaving vulnerabilities (e.g., the 2019 Capital One breach).
2. **Drives compliance:** Regulations like GDPR and DPDPA require demonstrable data stewardship, which SRM audits confirm.
3. **Enables accurate risk assessment:** Security budgets and controls must be allocated based on which party owns each risk vector.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**  
*List any three fundamental differences between traditional (on-premise) security and cloud security.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Perimeter Definition:** Traditional security relies on a *static, well-defined physical perimeter*; cloud security uses a *dynamic, software-defined perimeter* that shifts with workload mobility.
2. **Control Ownership:** Traditional = $100\%$ enterprise-owned; Cloud = *distributed* per the Shared Responsibility Model.
3. **Scalability of Defense:** Traditional defenses scale linearly with hardware investment; cloud defenses scale *elastically* with the provider's global infrastructure.
4. (Optional) **Compliance Scope:** Traditional = single jurisdiction; Cloud = multi-jurisdictional, requiring sovereign cloud or data residency controls.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing CSP vs. Customer responsibility** in the SRM table — examiners deduct 1–2 marks instantly for every misclassified layer.
> 2. **Omitting diagrams** in 14-mark answers — always include a **labeled block diagram**; it is worth 2–3 marks even if the surrounding text is weak.
> 3. **Writing paragraphs instead of tables** for comparative questions — KTU evaluators award 1–2 bonus marks for tabular presentation.
> 4. **Failing to cite NIST/CSA frameworks** (e.g., NIST SP 800-144, CSA CCM v4) — adds academic weight and signals depth.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Attempt Either A or B*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labeled diagram, explain the **Shared Responsibility Model** across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS service models. Clearly distinguish the responsibilities of the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** and the **cloud customer** at each layer. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Shared Responsibility Model (SRM)** defines the security ownership boundary between the CSP and the cloud consumer. As the service model abstraction increases from IaaS to PaaS to SaaS, the CSP takes on more responsibility, and the customer's burden reduces — but **never disappears**; data and identity sovereignty always remain with the customer.

**IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) — e.g., AWS EC2, Azure VM, GCP Compute Engine:**
- **CSP Owns:** Physical data center, server hardware, host OS, hypervisor, storage fabric, and network infrastructure.
- **Customer Owns:** Guest OS, middleware, runtime, application code, data, and identity/access management.
- **Shared:** Configuration of firewalls/Security Groups, patch management of guest OS, and logging.

**PaaS (Platform as a Service) — e.g., AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Azure App Service, Google App Engine:**
- **CSP Owns:** Everything from the physical layer up to the OS and middleware.
- **Customer Owns:** Application code, data, and identity configuration.
- **Shared:** Application-level patching and network ACLs.

**SaaS (Software as a Service) — e.g., Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Google Workspace:**
- **CSP Owns:** The entire stack including application logic and runtime.
- **Customer Owns:** Data, user access policies, and identity governance.
- **Shared:** User activity monitoring and data loss prevention policies.

A clean 3-column diagram showing "Layer | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS" with color-coded CSP/Customer cells is the ideal answer format.

---

**(b)** Examine **cloud security against traditional computing** with respect to: (i) attack surface, (ii) identity and access management, (iii) data protection, and (iv) compliance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Attack Surface:**
- *Traditional:* Static, well-defined, limited to corporate LAN/WAN endpoints.
- *Cloud:* Dynamic, elastic, expanded by public APIs, ephemeral containers, and federated identities. **However**, CSP-managed DDoS protection (e.g., AWS Shield, Azure Front Door) provides defense at a scale unattainable in traditional setups.

**(ii) Identity and Access Management:**
- *Traditional:* Centralized AD/LDAP, manual provisioning, perimeter-based trust.
- *Cloud:* Federated IAM with SAML 2.0, OIDC, OAuth 2.1, short-lived STS tokens, and Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) replacing VPN-based trust.

**(iii) Data Protection:**
- *Traditional:* AES-256 at rest managed by internal HSMs; TLS 1.2/1.3 in transit; data resides in known physical locations.
- *Cloud:* Customer-managed keys (CMK) via KMS/HSM, BYOK (Bring Your Own Key) and HYOK (Hold Your Own Key) options, server-side encryption default, but data residency across multiple geographic regions introduces sovereignty challenges.

**(iv) Compliance:**
- *Traditional:* Single-jurisdictional audits (e.g., internal ISO 27001).
- *Cloud:* Multi-jurisdictional compliance (GDPR, DPDPA, HIPAA, PCI-DSS 4.0) requiring continuous compliance tools like AWS Audit Manager, Azure Policy, and CSA STAR certifications. Hyperscale CSPs often *exceed* the baseline compliance posture achievable by mid-sized enterprises on-premise.

**Conclusion:** Cloud is not inherently more or less secure than traditional computing — it is **differently secure**. The security posture depends entirely on the customer's understanding of SRM and the maturity of their cloud configuration governance (CSPM, IaC scanning).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the major **security challenges unique to cloud computing** as compared to traditional on-premise environments. Categorize them under technical, operational, and governance dimensions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Technical Challenges:**
1. **Multi-tenancy and VM Escape:** Hypervisor vulnerabilities can allow one tenant to access another's data (e.g., Venom CVE-2015-3456).
2. **Data Residency Uncertainty:** Data may be replicated across continents, conflicting with sovereign data laws.
3. **API Exposure:** Every cloud service is API-driven; OWASP API Top 10 vulnerabilities (BOLA, BOPLA) are amplified.
4. **Encryption Key Management:** Mismanagement of KMS keys can lead to total data inaccessibility or exposure.

**Operational Challenges:**
1. **Configuration Drift:** Manual console changes lead to misconfigured S3 buckets, open security groups — the #1 cause of cloud breaches (per Gartner).
2. **Lack of Visibility:** Shadow IT and unsanctioned SaaS usage create blind spots.
3. **Incident Response Complexity:** Forensic data is distributed across multi-region logs requiring specialized cloud IR playbooks.
4. **Vendor Lock-in:** Proprietary CSP security tooling (e.g., AWS Macie, Azure Defender) hinders portability.

**Governance Challenges:**
1. **Shared Responsibility Ambiguity:** Customers assume CSP handles "everything cloud-related," creating gaps.
2. **Compliance Complexity:** Multi-jurisdictional regulations (GDPR, DPDPA 2023, CCPA) require continuous monitoring.
3. **Audit Rights:** Customers may have limited physical/logical access to CSP infrastructure for audits.
4. **Contractual SLA Gaps:** Standard CSP SLAs cover availability, *not* data integrity or breach liability at desired levels.

---

**(b)** Explain the **Defense-in-Depth strategy** in cloud environments. How does it differ from the perimeter-centric model used in traditional data centers? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Defense-in-Depth in Cloud Computing** is a layered security strategy where multiple independent controls are deployed across the stack so that the failure of one control does not result in a complete compromise.

**The 5 Layers in Cloud Defense-in-Depth:**

1. **Layer 1 — Perimeter:** WAF, DDoS protection (AWS Shield, Cloudflare), edge TLS termination.
2. **Layer 2 — Network:** VPC isolation, Security Groups, Network ACLs, micro-segmentation, service mesh mTLS.
3. **Layer 3 — Host/Compute:** HIDS, hardened AMIs, confidential computing enclaves (Intel SGX, AMD SEV), vulnerability scanning (Amazon Inspector).
4. **Layer 4 — Application:** SAST/DAST, API Gateway throttling, OAuth 2.1, secure SDLC.
5. **Layer 5 — Data:** KMS encryption, tokenization, DLP, customer-managed HSM, immutable backups.

**Comparison with Traditional Perimeter-Centric Model:**

| Aspect | Traditional Perimeter Model | Cloud Defense-in-Depth |
|---|---|---|
| **Trust Philosophy** | "Trust everything inside the firewall" (castle-and-moat) | "Never trust, always verify" (Zero Trust) |
| **Control Density** | Concentrated at perimeter, sparse internally | Uniformly distributed across all 5 layers |
| **Failure Tolerance** | Single perimeter breach = total compromise | Compromise of one layer ≠ total failure |
| **Identity Model** | Network location = trust | Identity + context = trust (ZTNA) |
| **Detection Capability** | Signature-based, perimeter IDS | Behavioral, ML-driven, API-native (GuardDuty, Sentinel) |

**Conclusion:** Traditional perimeter-centric security is *reactive and brittle*; cloud defense-in-depth is *proactive, distributed, and assumes breach* — aligning with modern Zero Trust Architecture (NIST SP 800-207).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- ✅ **SRM Boundary** — Customer **always** owns data and identity; CSP always owns the physical layer.
- ✅ **IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS** — As abstraction rises, customer responsibility **decreases monotonically but never to zero**.
- ✅ **Cloud ≠ Insecure** — Cloud is *differently* secure; security outcome depends on customer configuration maturity.
- ✅ **Top 3 Cloud Breaches Cause:** Misconfiguration > Credential Compromise > Insider Threat (Gartner 2023).
- ✅ **Zero Trust (NIST SP 800-207)** supersedes the legacy perimeter model in cloud-native architectures.
- ✅ **Encryption Standards:** AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, customer-managed KMS keys preferred over CSP-default.
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth = 5 layers:** Perimeter → Network → Host → Application → Data.
- ✅ **CASB, CSPM, CWPP, CIEM** are the four pillars of cloud-native security tooling (Gartner CNAPP framework).
- ✅ **Frameworks to cite:** NIST SP 800-144, NIST SP 800-207, CSA CCM v4, ISO 27017/27018, OWASP Cloud Top 10.
- ✅ **Exam Formula:** Always present comparative answers as a **two-column markdown table** with a supporting block diagram.
- ✅ **Mnemonic — "In PaaS you Patch, in SaaS you Police."**
- ✅ **Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM)** tools continuously detect misconfigurations against CIS Benchmarks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to AWS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to AWS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Introduction to AWS (Amazon Web Services)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** is a comprehensive, evolving cloud computing platform provided by Amazon.com that offers a mixture of Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS), Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS), and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) offerings. AWS provides on-demand cloud computing platforms and APIs to individuals, companies, and governments, on a metered pay-as-you-go basis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> In Module 3, the focus is on understanding the *global infrastructure model* of AWS, its *core service taxonomy*, the *shared responsibility paradigm*, and the *economic models* (pricing). Students must grasp how AWS abstracts physical data center operations into programmable, region-aware, fault-tolerant cloud primitives.

### Conceptual Analogy: AWS as an "Electricity Grid for Compute"

Imagine you are setting up a small business in 1995. To power your office, you would have to:
1. Buy a diesel generator (buy a physical server).
2. Hire an electrician to install it (set up a data center).
3. Pay for fuel even when lights are off (pay for idle hardware).
4. Maintain it yourself (patch, upgrade, replace).

Now imagine you could simply **plug into a wall socket** and pay only for the electricity you consume. If you need more power, you flip a switch. If you need less, you turn off the lights. The grid operator handles generation, distribution, redundancy, and maintenance.

**AWS is exactly that — but instead of electricity, you are plugging into *compute, storage, databases, networking, and AI services*.** You do not buy servers; you rent capacity by the second, minute, or hour. AWS handles the physical infrastructure, security of the data center, cooling, power, and hardware refreshes.

### Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Region** | A physical geographic location around the world where AWS clusters data centers. |
| **Availability Zone (AZ)** | One or more discrete data centers within a Region, each with redundant power, networking, and connectivity. |
| **Edge Location** | A site that AWS uses to cache content closer to users for lower latency (part of CloudFront CDN). |
| **Local Zone** | A deployment of AWS infrastructure closer to large population centers, for low-latency requirements. |
| **Wavelength** | AWS infrastructure embedded within 5G networks for ultra-low-latency edge computing. |
| **Outpost** | A fully managed hardware rack installed on-premises that runs AWS services locally. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard AWS Global Infrastructure Metrics (as of 2024–2025):**
> - **33+ Launched Regions**
> - **100+ Availability Zones**
> - **600+ CloudFront Edge Locations**
> - **13 Regional Edge Caches**
> - **30+ Local Zones**
> - **30+ Wavelength Zones**

### Why AWS? The "Five Pillars" of Value Proposition

1. **Elasticity** – Resources scale up or down automatically based on demand.
2. **Pay-as-you-go Pricing** – No upfront capital expenditure; operational expenditure only.
3. **Global Reach** – Deploy in any region worldwide in minutes.
4. **Security** – Industry-leading compliance, encryption, and identity management.
5. **Breadth of Services** – 200+ services spanning compute, storage, ML, IoT, blockchain, quantum, and more.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The AWS Global Infrastructure — Operational Logic

The AWS infrastructure is structured in a strict hierarchy. Understanding this hierarchy is **critical for KTU questions on fault tolerance, latency, and disaster recovery**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1: The Planet (Global Infrastructure Layer)**
- AWS operates data centers on **multiple continents**.
- The fundamental unit is the **Region**.

**Step 2: The Region**
- A Region is a *named* geographic area (e.g., `us-east-1`, `ap-south-1`).
- Each Region is **completely independent** — services in `us-east-1` do not share data or resources with `ap-south-1` by default.
- Regions are chosen to be **isolated from each other** (separated by large geographic distances) so that a natural disaster in one does not affect another.

**Step 3: The Availability Zone (AZ)**
- Each Region contains **2 or more AZs** (typically 3).
- An AZ is **one or more physically separate, independent data centers** within a Region.
- AZs within a Region are connected by **low-latency, high-bandwidth private fiber-optic links** (typically < 2 ms latency).
- AZs are designed to be **tolerant of single-AZ failures**.

**Step 4: The Edge Layer**
- Beyond the core data center fabric, AWS maintains **Edge Locations** worldwide.
- These do not host full AWS services; they cache content (CloudFront CDN) and run Lambda@Edge functions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU "Why does this matter?" Question:** Why are AZs inside a Region but Regions are not inside each other?
> **Answer:** AZs are deliberately placed close (typically within 100 km / 60 miles) to provide *low-latency synchronous replication* (e.g., database multi-AZ). Regions are deliberately far apart to provide *asynchronous disaster recovery* and *geopolitical/jurisdictional isolation*.

## 2.2 AWS Shared Responsibility Model — The Most-Tested KTU Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AWS operates under a **Shared Responsibility Model**. This is one of the *highest-weightage* conceptual questions in KTU exams.

| Layer | AWS is Responsible For ("Security OF the Cloud") | Customer is Responsible For ("Security IN the Cloud") |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Data Center** | Building security, cooling, power, hardware disposal | None |
| **Network Infrastructure** | Global backbone, AZ-to-AZ links, DDoS protection at the edge | VPC configuration, Security Groups, NACLs |
| **Compute (EC2)** | Hypervisor patching, host OS security | Guest OS patching, application security, IAM roles |
| **Storage (S3)** | Infrastructure durability (11 9s), replication | Bucket policies, encryption keys, access logging |
| **Database (RDS)** | Engine patching, automated backups, replication | Database-level access, query tuning, encryption at rest |
| **Identity & Data** | None | IAM user/role management, data encryption, MFA setup |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often say "AWS handles all security." This is **wrong**. AWS secures the *infrastructure*; the customer secures *what they put in the cloud* (configuration, data, access). This distinction is worth **7 marks alone** in KTU 14-mark questions.

## 2.3 AWS Service Taxonomy (High-Yield Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most important table for KTU Module 3**. Memorize the service-to-category mapping.

| Category | Core Services | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Compute** | EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, Fargate, Lightsail | Run applications and workloads |
| **Storage** | S3, EBS, EFS, FSx, S3 Glacier | Object, block, and file storage |
| **Database** | RDS, DynamoDB, Aurora, Redshift, Neptune | Managed relational, NoSQL, and analytics DBs |
| **Networking** | VPC, Route 53, CloudFront, ELB, API Gateway | Isolated networks, DNS, CDN, load balancing |
| **Security & Identity** | IAM, KMS, Cognito, WAF, Shield | Authentication, encryption, threat protection |
| **Management & Governance** | CloudWatch, CloudTrail, Config, Trusted Advisor | Monitoring, auditing, compliance |
| **DevOps & Developer Tools** | CodeCommit, CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, CodePipeline | CI/CD pipelines |
| **Analytics** | Athena, EMR, Kinesis, Glue | Big data processing |
| **AI/ML** | SageMaker, Rekognition, Comprehend, Bedrock | Machine learning services |

## 2.4 AWS Pricing Models — Economic Foundations

AWS offers several pricing models designed to optimize cost based on workload predictability:

| Pricing Model | Best For | Discount vs. On-Demand |
|---|---|---|
| **On-Demand** | Short-term, unpredictable workloads, testing | Baseline (0%) |
| **Reserved Instances (1/3 yr)** | Steady-state workloads (e.g., databases) | Up to **75%** |
| **Savings Plans (1/3 yr)** | Flexible commitment to compute usage | Up to **72%** |
| **Spot Instances** | Fault-tolerant, interruptible workloads (batch, CI) | Up to **90%** |
| **Dedicated Hosts** | Compliance / licensing-bound workloads | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pricing Dimensions** (the "3 V's" of AWS Billing):
> 1. **Compute** — charged per hour/second of usage.
> 2. **Storage** — charged per GB-month stored.
> 3. **Data Transfer OUT** — charged per GB (data IN to AWS is free in most cases).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Equations

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **exam-grade formulas** for AWS cost and availability calculations.

### Formula 1: Total Monthly Compute Cost (On-Demand EC2)

$$C_{\text{compute}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times H_{\text{month}} \times P_{\text{hour}}$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{compute}}$ = Total monthly compute cost (in USD)
- $N_{\text{inst}}$ = Number of running instances
- $H_{\text{month}}$ = Total hours of usage per month (730 hours ≈ 1 month)
- $P_{\text{hour}}$ = On-demand per-hour price of the instance type

### Formula 2: Total Storage Cost (S3 Standard)

$$C_{\text{storage}} = S_{\text{GB}} \times R_{\text{GB-month}}$$

Where:
- $S_{\text{GB}}$ = Storage size in GB
- $R_{\text{GB-month}}$ = Per-GB-month rate for the chosen storage class

### Formula 3: Effective Cost with Reserved Instance Discount

$$P_{\text{effective}} = P_{\text{on-demand}} \times (1 - D_{\text{RI}})$$

Where:
- $D_{\text{RI}}$ = Discount fraction (e.g., 0.75 for 75% discount)

### Formula 4: Multi-AZ Availability Calculation

For $n$ independent AZs each with availability $A_{AZ}$:

$$A_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{AZ}})^n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** This formula is used in *fault-tolerant system design* — engineers can compute the probability that at least one AZ survives to keep the system online. If $A_{AZ} = 0.999$ (three 9s) and $n = 3$ AZs, then $A_{\text{system}} = 1 - (0.001)^3 = 0.999999999$, i.e., **nine 9s of availability**.

### Formula 5: Data Egress Cost

$$C_{\text{egress}} = D_{\text{out\_GB}} \times R_{\text{egress}}$$

Where:
- $D_{\text{out\_GB}}$ = Data transferred OUT of AWS in GB
- $R_{\text{egress}}$ = Egress rate (typically $0.09/GB for first 10 TB, tiered downward)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Multi-AZ System Availability

**Problem Statement:**
A KTU question gives: "An application is deployed across 3 Availability Zones. Each AZ has an independent availability of 99.9%. Calculate the overall system availability assuming the application can run on any surviving AZ."

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let us model the system as running successfully if **at least one** of the $n = 3$ AZs is online.

We first compute the probability of a **single AZ failure**:

$$p_{\text{fail}} = 1 - A_{\text{AZ}} = 1 - 0.999 = 0.001$$

The probability that **all** 3 AZs fail simultaneously (a complete system outage):

$$P_{\text{all\_fail}} = p_{\text{fail}}^n = (0.001)^3$$

Expanding the cube:

$$P_{\text{all\_fail}} = 0.001 \times 0.001 \times 0.001$$

$$P_{\text{all\_fail}} = 10^{-3} \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-9}$$

Therefore, the **overall system availability** is:

$$A_{\text{system}} = 1 - P_{\text{all\_fail}} = 1 - 10^{-9}$$

$$A_{\text{system}} = 0.999999999$$

Expressed as a "number of nines":

$$A_{\text{system}} = 99.9999999\% \quad \text{(9 nines of availability)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why AWS multi-AZ architectures are considered *enterprise-grade*. A single AZ with 99.9% availability allows ~43.8 minutes of downtime per month. With 3 AZs, downtime drops to ~0.26 seconds per month.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: EC2 Cost Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A startup runs 5 `t3.medium` On-Demand EC2 instances 24/7. The hourly on-demand price is $0.0416/hour. Calculate the monthly bill. If they move to a 1-year Reserved Instance with a 40% discount, what is the new monthly cost?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute total hours per month (assuming 30 days):

$$H_{\text{month}} = 24 \text{ hours/day} \times 30 \text{ days} = 720 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the compute cost formula:

$$C_{\text{compute}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times H_{\text{month}} \times P_{\text{hour}}$$

Substituting values:

$$C_{\text{compute}} = 5 \times 720 \times 0.0416$$

Computing intermediate product:

$$5 \times 720 = 3600 \text{ instance-hours}$$

$$C_{\text{compute}} = 3600 \times 0.0416 = 149.76 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Reserved Instance discount ($D_{\text{RI}} = 0.40$):

$$P_{\text{effective}} = 0.0416 \times (1 - 0.40) = 0.0416 \times 0.60 = 0.02496 \text{ USD/hour}$$

**Step 4:** Recompute monthly cost:

$$C_{\text{RI}} = 5 \times 720 \times 0.02496 = 3600 \times 0.02496 = 89.856 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the savings:

$$\text{Savings} = 149.76 - 89.856 = 59.904 \text{ USD/month} \approx 718.85 \text{ USD/year}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Answer Format Tip:** Always show units in every step. Marks are awarded for *intermediate values* and *unit consistency*, not just the final answer.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: S3 Storage Tier Cost Comparison

**Problem Statement:**
A media company stores 5 TB of archive data on AWS. Compare the monthly cost between **S3 Standard** and **S3 Glacier Deep Archive**. Assume S3 Standard = $0.023/GB-month and S3 Glacier Deep Archive = $0.00099/GB-month.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert TB to GB:

$$S_{\text{GB}} = 5 \text{ TB} \times 1024 \text{ GB/TB} = 5120 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 2:** Compute S3 Standard cost:

$$C_{\text{Standard}} = 5120 \times 0.023$$

$$C_{\text{Standard}} = 117.76 \text{ USD/month}$$

**Step 3:** Compute S3 Glacier Deep Archive cost:

$$C_{\text{Glacier}} = 5120 \times 0.00099$$

$$C_{\text{Glacier}} = 5.0688 \text{ USD/month}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the cost reduction:

$$\text{Reduction} = 117.76 - 5.0688 = 112.69 \text{ USD/month}$$

$$\text{Reduction \%} = \frac{112.69}{117.76} \times 100 \approx 95.7\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do *not* suggest blindly putting all data in Glacier. Retrieval times differ drastically (Glacier Deep Archive: 12 hours; S3 Standard: milliseconds). Always tie storage class choice to *access frequency*.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Comparison

**Problem Statement:**
Compare the **3-year TCO** of running a workload on AWS (On-Demand) vs. a traditional on-premises data center. The on-premises setup costs $50,000 upfront, $1,000/month in power/cooling, and $500/month in admin. The AWS equivalent costs $800/month in EC2 + S3, with zero upfront.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** On-premises TCO over 3 years (36 months):

$$C_{\text{on-prem}} = C_{\text{upfront}} + C_{\text{op}} \times T_{\text{months}}$$

$$C_{\text{on-prem}} = 50{,}000 + (1000 + 500) \times 36$$

$$C_{\text{on-prem}} = 50{,}000 + 1500 \times 36 = 50{,}000 + 54{,}000 = 104{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 2:** AWS TCO over 3 years:

$$C_{\text{AWS}} = 0 + 800 \times 36 = 28{,}800 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 3:** Compute savings:

$$\text{Savings} = 104{,}000 - 28{,}800 = 75{,}200 \text{ USD over 3 years}$$

**Step 4:** Savings percentage:

$$\text{Savings \%} = \frac{75{,}200}{104{,}000} \times 100 \approx 72.3\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This is precisely the **CapEx-to-OpEx shift** that defines cloud economics. The customer converts a large fixed capital cost into a smaller, variable, predictable operating expense.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AWS Global Infrastructure Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[AWS Global Infrastructure] --> B[Region]
    A --> C[Edge Network]
    B --> D[Availability Zone 1]
    B --> E[Availability Zone 2]
    B --> F[Availability Zone 3]
    D --> G[Data Center 1A]
    D --> H[Data Center 1B]
    E --> I[Data Center 2A]
    E --> J[Data Center 2B]
    F --> K[Data Center 3A]
    F --> L[Data Center 3B]
    C --> M[CloudFront Edge Location]
    C --> N[Regional Edge Cache]
    C --> O[Wavelength Zone 5G]
    C --> P[Local Zone]
    C --> Q[Outpost OnPremises]
    D -.Low-latency fiber.-> E
    E -.Low-latency fiber.-> F
    D -.Low-latency fiber.-> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows show *containment* (a Region contains AZs; an AZ contains data centers). The *dotted arrows* between AZs denote the **low-latency private fiber links** that enable synchronous replication. Edge components are siblings of Regions — they do not contain AZs.

## 4.2 AWS Shared Responsibility Model (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph AWSResponsibility[AWS Responsibility Security OF the Cloud]
        A1[Physical Data Centers]
        A2[Network Infrastructure]
        A3[Hypervisor and Host OS]
        A4[Managed Service Internals]
        A5[Regional Availability]
    end
    subgraph CustomerResponsibility[Customer Responsibility Security IN the Cloud]
        B1[Identity and Access Management IAM]
        B2[Guest OS Patching for EC2]
        B3[Data Encryption at Rest and In Transit]
        B4[Network Firewall Rules Security Groups]
        B5[Application Level Security]
    end
    subgraph SharedLayer[Shared Controls]
        C1[Patch Management]
        C2[Configuration Management]
        C3[Awareness and Training]
    end
    AWSResponsibility --- SharedLayer
    CustomerResponsibility --- SharedLayer
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When drawing this in exams, always use **two distinct boxes** (AWS vs. Customer) with a clear overlap region in the middle for *shared* controls (e.g., patch management). A one-sided answer loses marks.

## 4.3 AWS Service Taxonomy Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    Root[AWS Service Catalog] --> C1[Compute]
    Root --> C2[Storage]
    Root --> C3[Database]
    Root --> C4[Networking]
    Root --> C5[Security]
    Root --> C6[Analytics]
    Root --> C7[AI and ML]
    Root --> C8[DevOps]
    C1 --> S1[EC2]
    C1 --> S2[Lambda]
    C1 --> S3[ECS EKS Fargate]
    C2 --> S4[S3 Object Storage]
    C2 --> S5[EBS Block Storage]
    C2 --> S6[EFS FSx File Storage]
    C2 --> S7[S3 Glacier Archive]
    C3 --> S8[RDS Aurora]
    C3 --> S9[DynamoDB]
    C3 --> S10[Redshift]
    C4 --> S11[VPC]
    C4 --> S12[Route 53]
    C4 --> S13[CloudFront]
    C5 --> S14[IAM KMS WAF Shield]
    C6 --> S15[Athena EMR Kinesis]
    C7 --> S16[SageMaker Bedrock Rekognition]
    C8 --> S17[CodePipeline CodeBuild CodeDeploy]
```

## 4.4 AWS Pricing Model Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Choose EC2 Pricing Model] --> Q1{Is workload predictable and steady state?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Commitment of 1 or 3 years acceptable?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| RI[Reserved Instances or Savings Plans up to 75 percent off]
    Q2 -->|No| OD[On Demand Baseline 0 percent off]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Can the workload tolerate interruption?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Spot[Spot Instances up to 90 percent off]
    Q3 -->|No| OD2[On Demand Instances for safety]
    Q1 -->|Compliance or licensing needs| DH[Dedicated Hosts]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define AWS and list its three primary cloud service models.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Amazon Web Services (AWS)** is a secure cloud platform that offers a broad set of global compute, storage, database, analytics, application, and deployment services. It delivers these capabilities via three service models:
1. **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** — e.g., Amazon EC2, where the customer rents virtualized hardware.
2. **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)** — e.g., AWS Elastic Beanstalk, where AWS manages the platform stack.
3. **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** — e.g., Amazon Chime, where the entire application is consumed as a service.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between an AWS Region and an Availability Zone.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Region | Availability Zone |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A named geographic area containing multiple data centers | One or more discrete data centers within a Region |
| **Scope** | Country/continent level (e.g., `ap-south-1`) | Within a Region, e.g., `ap-south-1a` |
| **Distance** | Geographically far from other Regions | Typically < 100 km apart within a Region |
| **Latency Between Units** | High (inter-region) | Low (< 2 ms, private fiber) |
| **Purpose** | Geographic isolation, disaster recovery | High availability, fault tolerance |

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the AWS Global Infrastructure in detail. Describe Regions, Availability Zones, Edge Locations, and Local Zones with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The AWS Global Infrastructure is the physical foundation of all AWS services. It is organized hierarchically:

**1. Regions (2 marks):**
A *Region* is a physical geographic location in the world where AWS clusters data centers. Each Region is named (e.g., `us-east-1`, `eu-west-2`, `ap-south-1`). AWS Regions are deliberately isolated from one another to ensure that a regional disaster (earthquake, flood, power grid failure) does not impact customers in another Region. Customers can choose any Region to deploy workloads, often to minimize latency or meet data residency requirements.

**2. Availability Zones (2 marks):**
Each Region consists of **2 or more Availability Zones (AZs)**, typically 3. An AZ is one or more discrete data centers, each with redundant power, networking, and connectivity, housed in separate facilities. AZs within a Region are connected via low-latency, high-throughput private fiber-optic links. This allows customers to deploy applications across multiple AZs to achieve high availability. If one AZ fails, the application continues to run from another AZ.

**3. Edge Locations (1.5 marks):**
Edge Locations are AWS data endpoints deployed in metropolitan cities worldwide. They are used by **Amazon CloudFront** (the AWS Content Delivery Network) to cache copies of content close to end users, reducing latency. Edge Locations also run **Lambda@Edge** functions for serverless code execution near the user.

**4. Local Zones and Wavelength (1.5 marks):**
- **Local Zones** place AWS compute, storage, and database services closer to large population centers where no full Region exists, enabling low-latency applications like real-time gaming or video production.
- **Wavelength Zones** embed AWS infrastructure inside 5G telecom networks for ultra-low-latency mobile edge applications.
- **AWS Outposts** extend AWS into customer-owned on-premises data centers for hybrid deployments.

**Diagram (Already in Section 4.1):** Refer to the Mermaid hierarchy diagram above for the visual representation.

---

**(b)** Explain the AWS Shared Responsibility Model with examples. Why is it critical for cloud security? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **AWS Shared Responsibility Model** defines the division of security obligations between AWS and the customer.

**1. AWS Responsibility — "Security OF the Cloud" (3 marks):**
AWS is responsible for protecting the infrastructure that runs all of the AWS services offered in the AWS Cloud. This includes:
- **Physical security** of data centers (guards, cameras, biometric access).
- **Hardware** procurement, maintenance, and disposal.
- **Network infrastructure** — routers, switches, global backbone.
- **Hypervisor** and host operating system patches.
- **Managed service internals** (e.g., AWS automatically patches the RDS engine).

*Example:* When a customer uses Amazon S3, AWS ensures the underlying disks are durable (11 nines) and that the storage infrastructure is replicated. AWS secures the *data center* and the *S3 engine*.

**2. Customer Responsibility — "Security IN the Cloud" (3 marks):**
The customer is responsible for anything they *put into* the cloud or *configure*:
- **Identity & Access Management (IAM)** — creating users, roles, policies.
- **Data encryption** — both at rest (S3, EBS) and in transit (TLS).
- **Guest OS patching** (for EC2 — not for RDS, which is AWS-managed).
- **Network configuration** — Security Groups, NACLs, route tables.
- **Application security** — code-level vulnerabilities.

*Example:* If a customer creates an S3 bucket and leaves it public, AWS is *not* responsible for the data leak. The customer failed to set a proper bucket policy.

**3. Shared Controls (1 mark):**
Some responsibilities are shared — e.g., patch management (AWS patches the *managed service*, customer patches the *guest OS*), configuration management, and security awareness training.

**Why is it critical?** (additional 0 marks as it ties the answer together)
Understanding this model prevents *mis-attribution of blame* during incidents and forces customers to adopt a **defense-in-depth** posture. It is the cornerstone of any AWS compliance audit (SOC 2, ISO 27001, HIPAA).

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

**(a)** Describe the different AWS pricing models for EC2 instances. Compute the monthly cost of running 4 `m5.large` instances continuously for 30 days under (i) On-Demand, and (ii) 1-year Reserved Instance with 40% discount. Assume On-Demand rate = $0.096/hour. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. AWS EC2 Pricing Models (4 marks):**

| Model | Description | Discount |
|---|---|---|
| **On-Demand** | Pay per hour/second with no commitment. Best for short-term, unpredictable workloads. | 0% |
| **Reserved Instances (RI)** | 1-year or 3-year commitment for a specific instance type. Best for steady-state workloads. | Up to 75% |
| **Savings Plans** | Flexible 1- or 3-year commitment to a consistent amount of compute usage (USD/hour). | Up to 72% |
| **Spot Instances** | Bid for unused EC2 capacity; can be interrupted with 2-min warning. Best for fault-tolerant batch jobs. | Up to 90% |
| **Dedicated Hosts** | Physical server dedicated to your use. Required for compliance or BYOL (Bring Your Own License). | Variable |

**2. Cost Computation (3 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{inst}} = 4$ instances
- $H_{\text{month}} = 24 \times 30 = 720$ hours
- $P_{\text{hour}} = \$0.096/\text{hour}$

**(i) On-Demand Monthly Cost:**

$$C_{\text{OD}} = 4 \times 720 \times 0.096$$

$$C_{\text{OD}} = 2880 \times 0.096 = \$276.48/\text{month}$$

**(ii) 1-Year Reserved Instance (40% discount):**

$$P_{\text{effective}} = 0.096 \times (1 - 0.40) = 0.096 \times 0.60 = \$0.0576/\text{hour}$$

$$C_{\text{RI}} = 4 \times 720 \times 0.0576 = 2880 \times 0.0576 = \$165.888/\text{month}$$

**Savings:**

$$\text{Savings} = 276.48 - 165.888 = \$110.592/\text{month} \approx \$1327/\text{year}$$

---

**(b)** An application is deployed across 3 Availability Zones. Each AZ has an availability of 99.5%. Calculate the overall system availability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Compute the failure probability of a single AZ:

$$p_{\text{fail}} = 1 - A_{\text{AZ}} = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005$$

**Step 2:** Compute the probability that all 3 AZs fail simultaneously (system outage):

$$P_{\text{all\_fail}} = (p_{\text{fail}})^n = (0.005)^3$$

Expanding:

$$P_{\text{all\_fail}} = 0.005 \times 0.005 \times 0.005$$

$$P_{\text{all\_fail}} = 25 \times 10^{-9} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 3:** Compute overall system availability:

$$A_{\text{system}} = 1 - P_{\text{all\_fail}}$$

$$A_{\text{system}} = 1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-8} = 0.999999975$$

**Step 4:** Express as percentage:

$$A_{\text{system}} \approx 99.9999975\% \quad \text{(about 7 nines)}$$

**Step 5 (Bonus 0 marks, but impressive):** Translate to downtime per month:

$$\text{Downtime/month} = (1 - A_{\text{system}}) \times 720 \text{ hours} \times 3600 \text{ s/hour}$$

$$\text{Downtime/month} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \times 2{,}592{,}000 \approx 0.065 \text{ seconds/month}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not write "availability is 99.5% because that's the AZ availability"** — that is worth 0 marks. The question asks for *system* availability, which requires the multi-AZ formula.
> 2. **Do not add AZ availabilities** (i.e., do not write 3 × 99.5% = 298.5%). Availabilities are *not* additive.
> 3. **Do not forget to compute the *complement*** of availability. Many students write $A_{\text{system}} = 0.995^3 = 0.985$ — this is **incorrect** because $0.995^3$ is the probability that *all* AZs are up, not the system availability. The correct formula subtracts the probability of *all failing*.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Introduction to AWS**

- ✅ **AWS = Pay-as-you-go cloud platform** with 200+ services, 33+ Regions, 100+ AZs.
- ✅ **Region** = named geographic area; **AZ** = one or more data centers within a Region; **Edge** = CloudFront CDN endpoints.
- ✅ **Shared Responsibility Model:** AWS secures the *cloud infrastructure*; customer secures *their data, configurations, and guest OS*.
- ✅ **EC2 Pricing Models:** On-Demand, Reserved (up to 75% off), Savings Plans (up to 72% off), Spot (up to 90% off), Dedicated Hosts.
- ✅ **S3 Storage Classes:** S3 Standard, S3 IA, S3 Glacier, S3 Glacier Deep Archive — cost vs. retrieval time trade-off.
- ✅ **Multi-AZ Availability Formula:** $A_{\text{system}} = 1 - (1 - A_{\text{AZ}})^n$. Always compute the complement of failure.
- ✅ **CapEx → OpEx shift:** Cloud converts large upfront capital costs to small, predictable monthly operating costs.
- ✅ **Three billing dimensions:** Compute, Storage, Data Transfer OUT (egress).
- ✅ **Always show units** in KTU calculations and intermediate steps — marks are awarded step-wise.
- ✅ **Diagram must include two distinct zones** (AWS vs. Customer) in Shared Responsibility answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra for AWS Module:**
> *"Region contains AZs; AZs contain data centers; AWS secures the cloud, you secure in the cloud; multi-AZ availability uses the complement-of-failure formula."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AWS History
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** AWS History

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Amazon Web Services (AWS)** is a comprehensive, evolving cloud computing platform provided by Amazon.com, which offers a mixture of Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS), Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS), and Software-as-a-Service (SaaS) offerings. AWS history chronicles the strategic, technical, and business evolution of Amazon's cloud division from a 2002 internal infrastructure experiment into the world's largest public cloud provider, commanding roughly **31% of the global cloud infrastructure market share (IaaS) as of 2024**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Within the KTU 2024 *Cloud Infrastructure and Systems* course, AWS History establishes the foundational context for understanding *why* the cloud paradigm shift occurred, *how* economies of scale enabled pay-as-you-go computing, and *what* architectural lessons (e.g., service-oriented design, virtualization, multi-tenancy) shaped modern cloud design.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a massive **electricity power grid** in its early 1900s. Back then, every factory had to build its own generator. Then, a central utility appeared and let factories plug into the grid, paying only for the electricity they used. AWS did the *exact same thing* for computing power. Before AWS, companies had to buy, install, and maintain their own physical servers (their own "generators"). After AWS launched its first public services in **2006**, anyone with a credit card could "plug in" and rent servers, storage, and databases on demand, paying only for what they consumed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The "history of AWS" is essentially the *history of cloud computing going mainstream* — they are practically synonymous. Understanding AWS's chronological evolution is the most direct way to understand the timeline of cloud adoption worldwide.

### Origin Point — The "Internal Spark"
The story of AWS begins not with a cloud strategy, but with an **internal infrastructure crisis at Amazon.com** in the early 2000s:

- In **2000–2002**, Amazon's retail website was experiencing severe scalability bottlenecks. Engineers were spending up to **70% of their time** on infrastructure plumbing (provisioning servers, configuring databases, managing storage) rather than on features that directly served customers.
- In **2003**, Amazon's then-CTO **Benjamin Black** and a small team — including **Chris Pinkham** and **Willem van Biljon** — drafted a now-legendary internal memo proposing a **service-oriented architecture (SOA)** for Amazon's own infrastructure.
- The visionary idea: expose Amazon's internal infrastructure as a set of **reusable, standardized APIs** that any developer (internal or external) could consume on demand.
- The decision-maker: Amazon founder **Jeff Bezos** issued the famous **API Mandate (2002)**, requiring all internal teams to expose their data and functionality exclusively through service interfaces. This mandate effectively forced the internal infrastructure to be *cloud-ready* before the public cloud even existed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (bolded for exam):** AWS officially launched in **March 2006** with the public release of **Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service)**, followed by **Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud)** in **August 2006**. These two services remain the cornerstones of AWS's IaaS portfolio to this day.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Timeline

### The "Why" and "How" Behind AWS's Evolution

The history of AWS is best understood as a **five-phase evolution**, each phase answering a critical business and engineering question:

#### **Phase 1: The Internal Genesis (2000–2003)**
- **Why:** Amazon's retail site was buckling under its own success; developer productivity was collapsing.
