- **How:** Internal teams were re-architecting around the API Mandate; infrastructure was being decomposed into discrete, callable services (storage, compute, queueing, database, payment, etc.).
- **Outcome:** A robust internal "service fabric" existed — but only Amazon engineers could use it.

#### **Phase 2: Commercialization (2003–2006)**
- **Why:** Bezos and leadership realized that if *Amazon* needed this infrastructure, *every other business* on the planet probably did too.
- **How:** A new entity was formed — the **Amazon Web Services team** — under Pinkham and van Biljon, tasked with *externalizing* the internal services.
- **Outcome:** AWS was officially registered as a brand in **2003**; the public S3 beta launched in **March 2006**, EC2 in **August 2006**.

#### **Phase 3: The IaaS Consolidation (2006–2010)**
- **Why:** Customers needed more than just storage and VMs — they needed a *complete* infrastructure toolkit.
- **How:** Rapid service launches including SQS (2004 beta, 2006 GA), SimpleDB (2007), EBS (2008), RDS (2009), VPC (2009), Route 53 (2010).
- **Outcome:** AWS became a credible alternative to on-premise data centers. The first **AWS re:Invent conference** was held in **2012**, formalizing the developer community.

#### **Phase 4: Platform & Specialization (2010–2015)**
- **Why:** Customers wanted to *build applications*, not just run servers.
- **How:** Introduction of PaaS offerings — Elastic Beanstalk (2011), DynamoDB (2012), Redshift (2012), Lambda (2014 — a *paradigm-shifting* serverless launch).
- **Outcome:** AWS transitioned from "renting hardware" to "renting outcomes."

#### **Phase 5: AI/ML & Ecosystem Dominance (2015–Present)**
- **Why:** The next frontier is intelligent workloads, vertical specialization, and global reach.
- **How:** SageMaker (2017), Lambda@Edge (2017), Ground Station (2018), Graviton ARM processors (2018), Inferentia/Trainium chips (2019/2020), Bedrock GenAI (2023), Q generative assistant (2023).
- **Outcome:** AWS holds the largest share of the public cloud market; spans **33+ geographic regions** and **100+ Availability Zones** globally as of 2024.

### KTU High-Yield Timeline Cheat Sheet

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| **2000–2002** | Internal SOA redesign at Amazon | Birth of service-oriented infrastructure |
| **2002** | Bezos API Mandate | Forced decomposition into discrete services |
| **2003** | AWS brand registered; team formed | Commercialization begins |
| **2004** | SQS launched (beta) | First public AWS service — message queuing |
| **March 2006** | S3 GA | Object storage for the world |
| **August 2006** | EC2 GA | Resizable compute capacity in the cloud |
| **2007** | SimpleDB | First managed NoSQL database |
| **2009** | RDS, VPC | Managed relational DB; private networks |
| **2012** | DynamoDB, Redshift, re:Invent | NoSQL scale; data warehousing; community |
| **2014** | AWS Lambda | Birth of *serverless computing* |
| **2016** | 1 million active enterprise customers | Mass enterprise adoption confirmed |
| **2017** | SageMaker | Democratized machine learning |
| **2018** | AWS Graviton (ARM) | Custom silicon era begins |
| **2019–2020** | Inferentia, Trainium | ML accelerator chips |
| **2023** | Bedrock, Amazon Q | Generative AI platform |
| **2024** | 100+ AZs, 33+ Regions | Global infrastructure dominance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility for Engineers:** Studying AWS history is not merely "dates and facts." It teaches engineering students the *patterns* behind scaling: API-first design, horizontal scaling primitives, decoupling via queues, eventual consistency, and the move toward managed services. These patterns reappear in *every* modern distributed system — Kubernetes, Azure, GCP, and on-premise cloud-native stacks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: The Evolution of AWS Service Categories

Because "AWS History" is a chronological-conceptual topic rather than a numerical one, the analytical matrix below maps the *causal chain* of decisions, technical enablers, and resulting service launches. Each row is a logical step in the AWS evolution narrative that KTU examiners expect students to articulate.

### Analytical Matrix — The Causal Chain of AWS Evolution

| Step | Trigger / Business Need | Technical Enabler | Resulting AWS Service / Event | Strategic Outcome |
|------|------------------------|-------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|
| **1** | Retail site scalability crisis (2000) | Decomposition of monolith | Internal SOA redesign | Foundation laid |
| **2** | API Mandate (2002) | Standardized service interfaces | Internal services like storage/queue become callable | Infrastructure becomes "API-ready" |
| **3** | Recognition of commercial potential (2003) | Reusable internal platform | AWS team formed; brand registered | Cloud becomes a business line |
| **4** | Developers need messaging glue (2004) | Distributed systems patterns | **SQS** released (beta 2004, GA 2006) | First public cloud service |
| **5** | Need for durable object storage (2006) | Custom storage fabric built for Amazon.com | **Amazon S3** | Pay-per-GB storage model born |
| **6** | Need for elastic compute (2006) | Xen hypervisor + custom provisioning | **Amazon EC2** | "Rent a server by the hour" |
| **7** | Need for managed NoSQL (2007) | Eventually consistent key-value design | **SimpleDB** → later **DynamoDB** (2012) | NoSQL in the cloud mainstream |
| **8** | Customers want isolated networks (2009) | Software-defined networking | **Amazon VPC** | Enterprise security compliance met |
| **9** | Customers want managed relational DBs (2009) | Automation of patching/replication | **Amazon RDS** | DBAs freed from ops work |
| **10** | Data warehousing demand (2012) | Columnar storage, MPP architecture | **Amazon Redshift** | Cloud analytics at petabyte scale |
| **11** | Event-driven computing demand (2014) | Container isolation, fine-grained billing | **AWS Lambda** | Serverless paradigm born |
| **12** | Mainstream enterprise adoption (2016) | Compliance, hybrid capabilities | AWS Outposts, GovCloud, multiple regions | Trust barrier broken |
| **13** | Machine learning democratization (2017) | Managed Jupyter, optimized frameworks | **Amazon SageMaker** | ML accessible to non-experts |
| **14** | Price/performance pressure (2018) | Custom ARM silicon | **AWS Graviton** | Cloud-native hardware era |
| **15** | Generative AI wave (2023) | Foundation models via API | **Amazon Bedrock**, **Amazon Q** | AWS enters GenAI platform wars |
| **16** | Global scale (2024) | Region/AZ engineering | **33+ Regions, 100+ AZs** | Largest cloud footprint on Earth |

### KTU Exam Logic — How to Structure a 14-Mark Answer on AWS History

When asked "Describe the history and evolution of AWS" in a KTU exam, examiners expect students to follow this **logical chain** (worth full marks):

$$\text{Internal Crisis (2000)} \rightarrow \text{API Mandate (2002)} \rightarrow \text{Service Decomposition} \rightarrow \text{Commercial Spin-off (2003)} \rightarrow \text{Public Launch (S3 + EC2, 2006)} \rightarrow \text{Service Proliferation (2007–2014)} \rightarrow \text{Serverless + ML (2014–2017)} \rightarrow \text{Custom Silicon + GenAI (2018–2024)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this chain matters for marks:** KTU valuation key patterns reward *causal reasoning* — i.e., explaining *why* each step happened, not just *what* happened. A student who merely lists dates earns ~5/14; a student who explains the *strategic motivation* behind each phase earns 12–14.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: AWS Evolution Timeline (Mermaid Timeline)

```mermaid
timeline
    title AWS History and Evolution Timeline
    2000-2002 : Internal SOA redesign at Amazon.com
              : Bezos API Mandate issued
    2003-2005 : AWS team formed and branded
              : SQS released as beta
    2006      : Amazon S3 launched (Object Storage)
              : Amazon EC2 launched (Elastic Compute)
    2007-2009 : SimpleDB released
              : Amazon RDS launched
              : Amazon VPC launched
    2010-2012 : Route 53 launched
              : DynamoDB released
              : Redshift released
              : First re:Invent conference
    2013-2014 : Kinesis, Lambda preview
              : Lambda GA - serverless era begins
    2015-2017 : Re:Invent becomes global
              : SageMaker launched
              : Lambda@Edge launched
    2018-2020 : AWS Graviton ARM processor
              : Ground Station launched
              : Inferentia and Trainium chips
    2021-2022 : Continued global expansion
              : Industry-specific solutions
    2023-2024 : Amazon Bedrock for GenAI
              : Amazon Q assistant
              : 100 plus Availability Zones
```

### Diagram 2: AWS Service Category Block Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Amazon Internal Infrastructure Crisis 2000] --> B[API Mandate 2002]
    B --> C[Service Oriented Architecture]
    C --> D[AWS Brand Established 2003]
    D --> E[Public Cloud Launch 2006]
    E --> F[IaaS Foundation Layer]
    F --> F1[Amazon S3 - Storage]
    F --> F2[Amazon EC2 - Compute]
    F --> F3[Amazon VPC - Networking]
    E --> G[PaaS Expansion Layer]
    G --> G1[Amazon RDS - Managed DB]
    G --> G2[Amazon DynamoDB - NoSQL]
    G --> G3[AWS Lambda - Serverless]
    E --> H[SaaS and AI Layer]
    H --> H1[Amazon SageMaker - ML Platform]
    H --> H2[Amazon Bedrock - GenAI]
    H --> H3[Amazon Q - AI Assistant]
    E --> I[Global Infrastructure]
    I --> I1[33 plus Regions]
    I --> I2[100 plus Availability Zones]
    I --> I3[400 plus Edge Locations]
```

### Diagram 3: Phase-wise Strategic Evolution

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Phase 1 Internal Genesis 2000-2003] --> P2[Phase 2 Commercialization 2003-2006]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 IaaS Consolidation 2006-2010]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Platform and Specialization 2010-2015]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5 AI/ML and Dominance 2015-Present]
    P1 -- API Mandate --> P2
    P2 -- S3 and EC2 --> P3
    P3 -- RDS and VPC --> P4
    P4 -- Lambda and SageMaker --> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All three diagrams together give a *holistic* view of AWS history — the **timeline** captures chronological milestones, the **block architecture** shows the *layered service growth* from IaaS to AI services, and the **phase diagram** illustrates the *strategic causal chain*. KTU examiners often award bonus marks to students who include even a simple visual representation in descriptive answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. List the first two public services launched by AWS and state the year of their release.**

**Model Answer:**
The first two public services launched by AWS were:
1. **Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service)** — launched in **March 2006**, providing object storage with a pay-per-GB model.
2. **Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud)** — launched in **August 2006**, providing resizable virtual machine compute capacity billed per hour.

Together, S3 and EC2 established the **IaaS foundation** upon which the entire AWS portfolio was built. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. What was the "API Mandate" issued by Jeff Bezos in 2002, and why was it critical to AWS's origin?**

**Model Answer:**
The **API Mandate (2002)** was an executive directive from Jeff Bezos requiring all Amazon.com internal teams to expose their data and functionality exclusively through **service interfaces (APIs)**. Its significance to AWS history is threefold:
- It **forced the internal decomposition** of Amazon's monolithic retail platform into discrete, reusable services (storage, queueing, payments, etc.).
- It created a **cloud-ready infrastructure** long before the public cloud existed.
- It established the **service-first design philosophy** that became the architectural blueprint for AWS's external offerings, allowing Amazon to commercialize its internal services starting in 2006. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks): Describe the origin and evolution of AWS. Highlight the key milestones from 2000 to 2010 in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Origin Story and the Internal Spark (7 marks)**

The history of AWS begins inside Amazon.com itself. In the late 1990s and early 2000s, Amazon's retail website experienced explosive growth that exposed critical limitations of its monolithic architecture. Engineers reported spending up to **70% of their time** on infrastructure plumbing — provisioning servers, configuring databases, managing storage — rather than building customer-facing features. This bottleneck triggered a strategic response.

In **2002**, Amazon's founder **Jeff Bezos** issued what is now known as the **API Mandate**: every internal team *must* expose its data and functionality through well-defined service interfaces. No team could communicate with another team's data structures, databases, or backend logic directly — only through APIs. This mandate forced Amazon's architects to decompose the monolithic retail application into a **service-oriented architecture (SOA)** with discrete services for storage, queueing, payments, recommendations, and more.

By 2003, the internal infrastructure had matured into a sophisticated "service fabric." Engineers like **Benjamin Black, Chris Pinkham, and Willem van Biljon** recognized that this infrastructure could solve the same problems for *every other business* in the world. Bezos approved the creation of a dedicated **Amazon Web Services** team that year, and the AWS brand was officially registered. However, the team was given an explicit constraint: it would only expose *existing* internal services, not build new ones, ensuring that every external offering was battle-tested at Amazon's scale.

**(b) Key Milestones from 2003 to 2010 (7 marks)**

The public AWS journey unfolded as follows:
- **2004 — Amazon SQS (Simple Queue Service)** released in limited beta; the very first publicly accessible AWS service, derived from Amazon's internal message queuing fabric.
- **March 2006 — Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service)** launched generally available, offering virtually unlimited object storage billed per gigabyte-month. S3 became the de facto standard for cloud object storage.
- **August 2006 — Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud)** launched generally available, enabling developers to launch on-demand virtual machines (instances) with hourly billing, based on the Xen hypervisor.
- **2007 — Amazon SimpleDB**, a managed NoSQL database service, extending AWS's data portfolio beyond simple key-value queues and blob storage.
- **2008 — Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store)** provided persistent block storage for EC2 instances, addressing one of EC2's early criticisms.
- **2009 — Amazon RDS (Relational Database Service)** automated the deployment, patching, and backup of relational databases, freeing customers from operational overhead.
- **2009 — Amazon VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)** gave customers logically isolated network environments within AWS, unlocking enterprise and regulated-industry adoption.

By the end of 2010, AWS had transformed from a single-service beta into a **full-featured IaaS platform**, and the era of "renting servers by the hour" had become a mainstream architectural pattern. **(14 marks total)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks): Explain the evolution of AWS from an IaaS provider to a comprehensive cloud platform (2010–2024). Discuss the strategic significance of serverless computing, custom silicon, and generative AI in this evolution.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Transition from IaaS to PaaS and SaaS (7 marks)**

After 2010, AWS faced a strategic question: should it remain a pure Infrastructure-as-a-Service provider, or should it abstract infrastructure further to let customers focus on *outcomes* rather than servers? AWS chose the latter, marking a multi-year pivot from IaaS to PaaS, SaaS, and specialized services.

Key milestones include:
- **2011 — AWS Elastic Beanstalk** offered a Platform-as-a-Service layer for deploying applications without managing the underlying EC2 fleet.
- **2012 — Amazon DynamoDB**, a fully managed NoSQL database with single-digit-millisecond latency at any scale, became the modern successor to SimpleDB and powered workloads from gaming to IoT to financial services.
- **2012 — Amazon Redshift** brought petabyte-scale data warehousing to the cloud, disrupting the on-premise data warehouse industry.
- **2012 — First AWS re:Invent Conference** in Las Vegas, formalizing the AWS developer and partner ecosystem.
- **2013 — Amazon Kinesis** enabled real-time streaming data ingestion, opening the door to big-data and analytics workloads.
- **November 2014 — AWS Lambda** was announced at re:Invent, introducing the **serverless computing paradigm**. With Lambda, developers could upload code that runs *only* in response to events, with zero server management and millisecond-billed execution. This was arguably the most architecturally significant launch in AWS's post-2010 history.
- **2015–2017 — Service Proliferation:** API Gateway, Step Functions, Fargate, ECS/EKS (Kubernetes), and dozens of analytics and IoT services broadened the platform.

**(b) Strategic Significance of Serverless, Custom Silicon, and Generative AI (7 marks)**

- **Serverless Computing (Lambda, 2014):** Lambda's significance lies in *decoupling* application logic from infrastructure management. It proved that the cloud could be *finer-grained* than "rent a server" — customers could rent a *function execution*. This influenced the entire industry (Azure Functions, Google Cloud Functions followed). It also enabled event-driven architectures at massive scale, reducing cost since customers paid only for actual execution time measured in milliseconds.

- **Custom Silicon (Graviton, Inferentia, Trainium, 2018+):** AWS realized that off-the-shelf Intel/AMD processors left performance and cost optimizations on the table. **AWS Graviton (2018)** was the company's first ARM-based server processor, offering up to 40% better price-performance for general workloads. **AWS Inferentia (2019)** and **AWS Trainium (2020)** were purpose-built AI accelerators for inference and training of machine learning models. By designing its own chips, AWS reduced its dependence on third-party vendors, lowered costs for customers, and created a sustainable competitive moat.

- **Generative AI (Bedrock, Q, 2023+):** Recognizing the industry-wide shift triggered by large language models, AWS launched **Amazon Bedrock** in 2023 — a managed service providing access to multiple foundation models (Anthropic Claude, Meta Llama, Mistral, Amazon Titan) through a unified API. **Amazon Q**, also launched in 2023, is a generative AI-powered assistant for business users and developers. These launches signaled AWS's commitment to making generative AI *enterprise-grade*, *secure*, and *integrated* with the rest of the AWS portfolio.

**Conclusion:** From 2010 to 2024, AWS evolved from a 5-service IaaS pioneer into a **2,000+ service platform** spanning compute, storage, networking, databases, analytics, ML, IoT, robotics, quantum computing, and generative AI. The strategic narrative is one of continuous *abstraction* — moving customers closer to "rent the outcome" rather than "rent the hardware." **(14 marks total)**

---

### > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
> **Common mistakes students make in AWS History answers:**
> 1. **Listing without explaining (–3 to –5 marks):** Memorizing dates without explaining *why* they matter. Always tie each milestone to a *strategic motivation* (e.g., "Lambda was launched because customers needed event-driven, granular billing").
> 2. **Skipping the pre-2006 origin (–2 marks):** Many students jump directly to 2006, missing the API Mandate and the internal SOA redesign. These are *frequently asked* in KTU exams and are easy scoring opportunities.
> 3. **Confusing SQS and S3 launch dates (–1 mark):** SQS was the *first* (beta 2004), S3 was the first *GA blockbuster* (2006). Get this order right.
> 4. **Ignoring custom silicon and GenAI (–2 marks):** Recent KTU papers (2023, 2024) emphasize post-2018 evolution. Students who stop at 2014 lose marks.
> 5. **No diagram (–1 to –2 marks):** A simple timeline or block diagram can earn 1–2 extra marks in 14-mark descriptive questions. Always include one.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **AWS = Amazon Web Services**, launched commercially in **2006**, but its roots trace to Amazon's **2002 API Mandate**.
- **First public service:** Amazon SQS (beta 2004, GA 2006). **First blockbuster:** Amazon S3 (March 2006).
- **First compute service:** Amazon EC2 (August 2006).
- **First managed relational DB:** Amazon RDS (2009). **First managed NoSQL:** SimpleDB (2007) → DynamoDB (2012).
- **First serverless platform:** AWS Lambda (November 2014) — a paradigm shift.
- **First machine learning platform:** Amazon SageMaker (2017).
- **First custom AWS processor:** AWS Graviton (2018) — ARM-based, ~40% better price-performance.
- **First dedicated AI accelerators:** Inferentia (2019) for inference, Trainium (2020) for training.
- **Generative AI offerings:** Amazon Bedrock, Amazon Q (both 2023).
- **Global scale (2024):** 33+ regions, 100+ Availability Zones, 400+ edge locations, 200+ services.
- **Market position:** ~31% global IaaS market share, the **largest public cloud provider**.
- **Key strategic pattern:** Continuously move up the abstraction stack — IaaS → PaaS → SaaS → AI-as-a-Service.
- **Architectural DNA:** API-first, service-oriented, pay-per-use, globally distributed, multi-tenanted.
- **Founders/Key figures:** Jeff Bezos (API Mandate), Benjamin Black, Chris Pinkham, Willem van Biljon.
- **Remember the "Five Phases" for KTU answers:** Internal Genesis → Commercialization → IaaS Consolidation → Platform & Specialization → AI/ML & Dominance.
- **Causal reasoning always beats memorization:** *Why* a service launched is more important than *when* — examiners reward the "why."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AWS Global Infrastructure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTURE AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** AWS Global Infrastructure # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **AWS Global Infrastructure** is the physical and virtual foundation of Amazon Web Services, encompassing the global network of **data centers**, **Availability Zones (AZs)**, **Regions**, **Edge Locations**, and **Local Zones** that Amazon has provisioned across the world to deliver cloud computing services with high availability, fault tolerance, low latency, and scalability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** AWS Global Infrastructure refers to the geographically distributed collection of Regions, Availability Zones, Edge Locations, and Points of Presence (PoPs) interconnected via a private global network backbone, engineered to support redundant, secure, and high-performance cloud workloads for customers worldwide. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **🏢 The "Real-World" Analogy — The Global Power Grid:** Imagine Amazon is the world's largest **electricity provider**: - A **Region** is like an entire country-level power grid (say, the Kerala State Electricity Board or the entire US Eastern Grid). - An **Availability Zone (AZ)** is one independent power plant within that country — it has its own generators, its own fuel supply, and its own control systems. If one plant fails, the others keep the lights on. - A **Data Center** is one specific building housing the actual turbines and transformers (servers, storage, networking). - An **Edge Location** is like a small sub-station near your home — it doesn't generate power but caches frequently needed content (electricity, in this case, is "content") close to you for instant delivery. - The **private global network** is the high-voltage transmission line connecting all these countries together. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** Just as you wouldn't put all your eggs (data) in one country (Region) or one basket (data center), AWS Global Infrastructure is designed so you can **spread your workload across multiple independent failure domains** to guarantee uptime. --- ## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Terminology (KTU High-Yield) The following metrics and standard terms are **explicitly tested** in KTU 2024 ESE papers: | Term | Standard Value / Definition | |---|---| | **Region** | A separate geographic area (e.g., `ap-south-1` for Mumbai) | | **Availability Zone (AZ)** | One or more discrete data centers within a Region, each with redundant power, networking, and connectivity | | **Latency between AZs** | Typically **< 10 ms** (round-trip) within a single Region | | **Number of AZs per Region** | Minimum **2**, maximum **6** (varies by Region) | | **Distance between AZs** | Tens of miles apart (close enough for low latency, far enough for isolation) | | **Edge Location (PoP)** | 600+ globally; used by Amazon CloudFront & Route 53 | | **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** | EC2 multi-AZ SLA = **99.99%** (Four 9s) | | **AWS Backbone** | Private fiber-optic network, NOT the public internet | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bolded Constant for Exam Recall:** The standard AWS SLA for compute services (EC2, RDS multi-AZ) is **99.99%** availability, which translates to a maximum of **52.56 minutes of downtime per year**. --- ## 1.4 Why AWS Global Infrastructure Matters (The "Why" for KTU) 1. **Fault Isolation:** A flood, earthquake, or power grid failure in one AZ does not affect another AZ. 2. **Latency Optimization:** Placing compute near the user reduces application response time. 3. **Regulatory Compliance:** Data residency laws (e.g., GDPR, DPDP Act 2023 in India) require data to stay within specific geographic boundaries. 4. **Disaster Recovery (DR):** Multi-Region architectures survive even the loss of an entire geographic area. 5. **Scalability:** Each Region operates independently, allowing customers to scale out without contention. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hierarchy of AWS Global Infrastructure (Bottom-Up) AWS Global Infrastructure is best understood as a **4-tier hierarchical model**: ### Tier 1 — Data Center (The Physical Foundation) - A secure building housing: - **Servers** (compute, with x86_64 / ARM Graviton processors) - **Storage arrays** (EBS, S3 storage nodes) - **Networking gear** (Top-of-Rack switches, aggregation routers) - Power: redundant utility feeds + UPS + diesel generators - Cooling: N+1 redundant HVAC systems - Security: 24×7 armed guards, biometric access, CCTV > [!NOTE] > AWS does not publicly disclose the exact number of data centers, but industry estimates place it at **over 1000 data centers globally** (as of 2024–2025). ### Tier 2 — Availability Zone (AZ) - A **logical construct** comprising **one or more physically separate data centers** - Each AZ has: - Independent power infrastructure - Independent cooling infrastructure - **Direct, low-latency private fiber** links to other AZs in the same Region - AZs within a Region are typically within **~100 km** of each other **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The most commonly asked question is *"What is the difference between a Region and an AZ?"* — answer in 1 line each, always emphasize **geography** (Region = area) and **isolation** (AZ = isolated failure domain). ### Tier 3 — Region - A **named geographic area** (e.g., `us-east-1` = N. Virginia, `ap-south-1` = Mumbai) - Contains **≥ 2 AZs** - Each Region is **completely independent** of every other Region (no shared infrastructure, separate AWS account teams, separate fault isolation) - Services are deployed **per-Region** (i.e., when you launch an EC2, you launch it into a specific Region) ### Tier 4 — Edge Locations & Points of Presence (PoPs) - Used **only for content delivery** (CloudFront) and **DNS resolution** (Route 53) - **Do NOT host compute or storage** services - Connected to the AWS backbone via high-speed links - Number: **600+ Edge Locations** and **13 Regional Edge Caches** (as of 2024) --- ## 2.2 Additional Edge Constructs (Frequently Asked in KTU) | Construct | Purpose | KTU Memory Hook | |---|---|---| | **AWS Local Zones** | Brings AWS compute closer to metropolitan areas (e.g., Las Vegas, Boston) for **single-digit millisecond latency** | "Local Zone = mini-Region in your city" | | **AWS Wavelength** | Embeds AWS compute **inside 5G carrier networks** (Verizon, Vodafone) for ultra-low latency mobile/IoT | "Wavelength = AWS inside telecom towers" | | **AWS Outposts** | AWS-managed hardware **inside your on-premises data center** | "Outpost = AWS rack in your building" | | **AWS GovCloud (US)** | Isolated US Region for US government agencies, ITAR-compliant | "GovCloud = government-only fortress" | | **AWS China Regions** | Operated by **Sinnet (Beijing)** and **NWCD (Ningxia)** under Chinese law | "China = separate, partner-operated" | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet While AWS Global Infrastructure is not a numerical subject, KTU examiners often test **availability and latency calculations**. Master these formulas: | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Constant | |---|---|---| | **Maximum downtime per year (SLA)** | $T_{down} = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | Minutes per year | | **Composite SLA (parallel services)** | $A_{total} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | | **Composite SLA (series services)** | $A_{total} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | | **EC2 single-AZ SLA** | $A = 0.9999$ | 99.99% | | **RDS Multi-AZ SLA** | $A = 0.9999$ | 99.99% | | **CloudFront SLA** | $A = 0.9999$ | 99.99% | | **Inter-AZ latency target** | $\tau \leq 10$ ms | Milliseconds | | **Inter-Region latency** | $\tau \geq 30$ ms (varies) | Milliseconds | | **Round-trip time budget for two AZs** | $RTT \approx 2 \times \tau_{link} + T_{processing}$ | Milliseconds | | **Number of nines conversion** | $A_n = 1 - 10^{-n}$ | e.g., $A_3 = 0.999$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Rule for KTU:** When two services are invoked **in series** (one after the other), you **multiply** their availabilities. When they are invoked **in parallel** (redundant paths), you multiply their **failure probabilities** and subtract from 1. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Banking & Finance:** Multi-Region active-active setup to survive a regional AWS outage. 2. **Healthcare (HIPAA):** Deploying workloads in US Regions to satisfy data residency. 3. **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Using CloudFront Edge Locations to serve product images with **< 50 ms** latency globally. 4. **Gaming & IoT:** AWS Wavelength for sub-10 ms latency between 5G towers and end devices. 5. **Government (India):** Using the Mumbai (`ap-south-1`) Region to keep citizen data within Indian borders, complying with the **Digital Personal Data Protection (DPDP) Act 2023**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Worked Examples > [!NOTE] > AWS Global Infrastructure is largely architectural. Hence, the "derivations" below are **analytical walkthroughs** of availability, latency, and architectural design — exactly the type of 7 or 14-mark questions KTU examiners ask. --- ## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough #1: Composite SLA Calculation (Active-Active Multi-Region) ### Problem (Sample KTU Style — 7 Marks) A startup deploys its application on **EC2 instances across two AWS Regions** (each Region has its own load balancer and database). The SLA of EC2 in a single Region is **99.95%**, and the load balancer's SLA is **99.99%**. If the application invokes EC2 → Load Balancer **in series** within each Region, and the two Regions act as **redundant parallel paths**, calculate the **overall composite availability**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Compute the per-Region availability (series path EC2 → Load Balancer).** For series components, the formula is: $$A_{path} = A_{EC2} \times A_{LB}$$ Substituting the values: $$A_{path} = 0.9995 \times 0.9999$$ $$A_{path} = 0.9994$$ **Step 2: Compute the failure probability of one path.** $$P_{fail} = 1 - A_{path} = 1 - 0.9994 = 0.0006$$ **Step 3: For two parallel paths, the system fails ONLY if both paths fail simultaneously.** For parallel components: $$A_{total} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{fail,i}$$ $$A_{total} = 1 - (0.0006 \times 0.0006)$$ $$A_{total} = 1 - 0.00000036$$ $$A_{total} = 0.99999964$$ **Step 4: Convert to "nines" notation for engineering interpretation.** $$A_{total} \approx 0.99999964 \approx 99.99996\% \approx \text{Six 9s}$$ **Step 5: Annual downtime.** $$T_{down} = (1 - 0.99999964) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$$ $$T_{down} = 0.00000036 \times 525600$$ $$T_{down} \approx 0.189 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 11.3 \text{ seconds/year}$$ ### Final Answer The composite system availability is **99.99996% (six 9s)**, translating to approximately **11.3 seconds of allowable downtime per year**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough #2: Why Multi-AZ Beats Single-AZ (Availability Proof) ### Problem (Sample KTU Style — 7 Marks) Compare the **availability of a single-AZ deployment** versus a **multi-AZ active-passive deployment** for an RDS database. Given: - Single-AZ RDS SLA = 99.95% - Multi-AZ RDS SLA = 99.99% How much additional uptime does multi-AZ provide annually? ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Single-AZ annual downtime.** $$T_{down, single} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525600 \text{ minutes}$$ $$T_{down, single} = 0.0005 \times 525600$$ $$T_{down, single} = 262.8 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours/year}$$ **Step 2: Multi-AZ annual downtime.** $$T_{down, multi} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 525600$$ $$T_{down, multi} = 0.0001 \times 525600$$ $$T_{down, multi} = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 0.876 \text{ hours/year}$$ **Step 3: Improvement gained.** $$\Delta T = 262.8 - 52.56 = 210.24 \text{ minutes/year}$$ $$\Delta T = 3.504 \text{ hours/year}$$ ### Final Answer Multi-AZ deployment provides an additional **210.24 minutes (≈ 3.5 hours)** of uptime per year compared to single-AZ, justifying its slight cost premium for production workloads. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough #3: Latency Budget for Inter-AZ vs Inter-Region Communication ### Problem (Sample KTU Style — 7 Marks) A microservices-based application runs across **two AZs in the Mumbai Region**. Each request involves: - 1 hop to DynamoDB in AZ-A: latency = **3 ms** - Cross-AZ synchronous RPC: latency = **8 ms** - API response to AZ-B: latency = **2 ms** Calculate the **total user-perceived latency** and determine if it meets the SLA of **≤ 20 ms**. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Identify the sequential (series) latency components.** $$L_{total} = L_{DB} + L_{cross\_AZ} + L_{API}$$ **Step 2: Substitute values.** $$L_{total} = 3 \text{ ms} + 8 \text{ ms} + 2 \text{ ms}$$ $$L_{total} = 13 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3: Compare with the SLA budget.** $$L_{total} = 13 \text{ ms} \leq 20 \text{ ms (SLA)}$$ ### Final Answer The total latency is **13 ms**, which satisfies the **20 ms SLA target** with a **7 ms headroom**. The system is well within acceptable performance bounds because the **inter-AZ latency of 8 ms** is within the AWS-guaranteed **< 10 ms** envelope. > [!NOTE] > **Design Insight:** Had the components been distributed **across Regions** (e.g., Mumbai → Singapore), the cross-Region latency alone would have been **≥ 60 ms**, violating the SLA. This is why **latency-sensitive workloads are kept within a single Region** but spread across multiple AZs for fault tolerance. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram #1: Hierarchical Architecture of AWS Global Infrastructure ```mermaid flowchart TB EdgeLayer["EDGE LAYER
600+ Edge Locations and PoPs
Used for CloudFront and Route 53"] RegionLayer["REGION LAYER
Geographic Areas like us-east-1, ap-south-1
Each Region is independent"] AZLayer["AVAILABILITY ZONE LAYER
Minimum 2 AZs per Region
Connected via private fiber"] DCLayer["DATA CENTER LAYER
Physical buildings with servers, storage, networking
Redundant power and cooling"] GovCloud["GOVCLOUD AND CHINA REGIONS
Isolated for regulatory compliance"] LocalZone["AWS LOCAL ZONES
Mini regions inside metro cities"] Wavelength["AWS WAVELENGTH
Compute inside 5G carrier networks"] Outposts["AWS OUTPOSTS
AWS hardware inside customer data center"] Backbone["AWS PRIVATE GLOBAL BACKBONE
High speed fiber interconnecting all layers"] EdgeLayer --> RegionLayer RegionLayer --> AZLayer AZLayer --> DCLayer RegionLayer -.optional.-> GovCloud RegionLayer -.optional.-> LocalZone RegionLayer -.optional.-> Wavelength RegionLayer -.optional.-> Outposts Backbone -.connects.-> EdgeLayer Backbone -.connects.-> RegionLayer ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram in an exam:** Start from the **top (Edge Layer)** and explain that AWS serves content to end users from the **closest edge location**, while **compute and storage workloads** live in the lower layers (Region → AZ → Data Center). The optional constructs (GovCloud, Local Zones, Wavelength, Outposts) are **special-purpose extensions** of the Region layer for specific compliance, latency, or hybrid needs. --- ## 4.2 Diagram #2: Multi-AZ High Availability Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR User["END USER
Browser or Mobile App"] R53["AMAZON ROUTE 53
DNS Health Check
and Traffic Routing"] ELB["APPLICATION LOAD BALANCER
Distributes traffic across AZs"] AZ1Node["AZ-A: EC2 INSTANCE 1
Active compute node"] AZ2Node["AZ-B: EC2 INSTANCE 2
Active compute node"] DB1["AZ-A: RDS PRIMARY
Read and Write"] DB2["AZ-B: RDS STANDBY
Synchronous replication"] S3["S3 BUCKET
Cross Region replicated object store"] CloudWatch["CLOUDWATCH
Health monitoring and alarms"] CWARN["ALARM TRIGGERED
Failover initiated"] User --> R53 R53 --> ELB ELB --> AZ1Node ELB --> AZ2Node AZ1Node --> DB1 AZ2Node --> DB2 DB1 -.synchronous replication.-> DB2 AZ1Node --> S3 AZ2Node --> S3 AZ1Node --> CloudWatch AZ2Node --> CloudWatch DB1 --> CloudWatch DB2 --> CloudWatch CloudWatch --> CWARN ``` > [!NOTE] > **Exam-Ready Interpretation:** The **Load Balancer** is the entry point that distributes requests between two **independent AZs**. If AZ-A fails, AZ-B continues serving. The **RDS standby** in AZ-B receives **synchronous replication** (within a Region), ensuring **zero data loss (RPO = 0)**. **CloudWatch** triggers alarms and initiates automated failover. This block diagram is a high-yield KTU 2024 answer for "Explain how AWS Global Infrastructure enables high availability" questions. --- ## 4.3 Diagram #3: AWS Global Infrastructure — Geographic Distribution Map (Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NA["NORTH AMERICA"] USE1["us-east-1 N. Virginia
6 AZs"] USW2["us-west-2 Oregon
4 AZs"] CAC1["ca-central-1 Canada
3 AZs"] end subgraph EU["EUROPE"] EUW1["eu-west-1 Ireland
3 AZs"] EUC1["eu-central-1 Frankfurt
3 AZs"] EUN1["eu-north-1 Stockholm
3 AZs"] end subgraph APAC["ASIA PACIFIC"] APS1["ap-south-1 Mumbai
3 AZs"] APSE1["ap-southeast-1 Singapore
3 AZs"] APNE1["ap-northeast-1 Tokyo
4 AZs"] APSY1["ap-sydney-1 Sydney
3 AZs"] end subgraph SA["SOUTH AMERICA"] SAE1["sa-east-1 Sao Paulo
3 AZs"] end Backbone2["AWS PRIVATE GLOBAL BACKBONE
Connects all Regions via dedicated fiber"] NA --- Backbone2 EU --- Backbone2 APAC --- Backbone2 SA --- Backbone2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Tip:** AWS Global Infrastructure has **30+ Regions** and **600+ Edge Locations** (as of 2024). You do **not** need to memorize all Region codes, but you **must know** `us-east-1` (N. Virginia — the oldest/largest), `ap-south-1` (Mumbai — closest to India), and `eu-west-1` (Ireland). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the terms Region, Availability Zone, and Edge Location as used in AWS Global Infrastructure. Mention any one distinguishing feature of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Region:** A Region is a **separate geographic area** containing multiple Availability Zones. Each Region is **completely independent** in terms of infrastructure, services, and fault isolation, ensuring that failures or events affecting one Region do not impact another. *(1 mark)* - **Availability Zone (AZ):** An AZ is a **logical data center group** within a Region, comprising one or more physically separate data centers with **independent power, cooling, and networking**. AZs within a Region are interconnected via **low-latency (< 10 ms) private fiber links**. *(1 mark)* - **Edge Location:** An Edge Location (Point of Presence) is a **smaller AWS site** used by services like **Amazon CloudFront** (CDN) and **Route 53** (DNS) to **cache content close to end users**, reducing latency. Edge Locations **do not host compute or storage** workloads. *(1 mark)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]` **What is the difference between AWS Outposts and AWS Wavelength? State the use case for each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **AWS Outposts** are **fully managed AWS hardware racks** that are physically installed **inside the customer's own on-premises data center**, providing a **truly hybrid cloud** experience where AWS manages the infrastructure but it resides on the customer's site. Use case: running **latency-sensitive applications** that must remain on-premises due to data residency laws or extreme low-latency requirements (e.g., manufacturing floor control systems). *(1.5 marks)* - **AWS Wavelength** embeds AWS compute and storage **inside 5G carrier networks** (e.g., Verizon, Vodafone, KDDI), bringing AWS infrastructure to the **edge of the telecom network** to deliver **single-digit millisecond latency** for mobile and IoT applications. Use case: **real-time gaming, autonomous vehicles, AR/VR, and smart city applications** that require ultra-low latency between the device and the cloud. *(1.5 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each module offers an internal choice between two 14-mark questions, each split into two 7-mark sub-parts. ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]`** **(a)** Explain in detail the **hierarchical architecture of AWS Global Infrastructure** (Data Center → AZ → Region → Edge Location). Describe the connectivity, fault isolation, and latency characteristics of each tier. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A healthcare application must achieve **99.99% availability** and comply with the **DPDP Act 2023** (data must remain in India). Design a **multi-AZ, multi-Region disaster recovery architecture** using AWS services. Calculate the composite availability and justify the choice of Region(s). *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Answer for Q-A(a): Hierarchical Architecture (7 Marks) The AWS Global Infrastructure is organized in **four hierarchical tiers**, each providing specific guarantees in terms of fault isolation, connectivity, and latency: 1. **Data Center Tier (Lowest Level):** A data center is a **physically secure facility** housing servers, storage systems, and networking equipment. AWS operates **1000+ data centers** globally, with **N+1 redundancy** in power (utility + UPS + diesel generators), cooling (water and air-cooled HVAC), and connectivity (multiple Tier-1 ISP links). Data centers are protected by **24×7 security, biometric access, and CCTV surveillance**. *(2 marks)* 2. **Availability Zone (AZ) Tier:** An AZ is a **logical grouping of one or more physically separated data centers** within a Region. Key properties: - AZs are **independent failure domains** — power, cooling, and network failures in one AZ do not affect another. - AZs are interconnected via **dedicated, redundant, low-latency private fiber links** with typical round-trip latency **< 10 ms**. - Each Region has **at least 2 AZs** (most have 3–6). - AZs are typically **within 100 km** of each other to balance latency and isolation. *(2 marks)* 3. **Region Tier:** A Region is a **named, independent geographic area** (e.g., `ap-south-1` for Mumbai). Each Region: - Has **≥ 2 AZs** and operates as a **completely independent fault domain**. - Houses the **full catalog of AWS services** (EC2, S3, RDS, Lambda, etc.). - Customers **select a Region** at the time of resource creation, and data **does not leave the Region** unless explicitly replicated. - Region-to-Region communication occurs over the **AWS private global backbone**, not the public internet. *(1.5 marks)* 4. **Edge Location Tier (Highest Level):** Edge Locations (Points of Presence or PoPs) are **600+ smaller sites globally** that host only **content caching** (CloudFront) and **DNS resolution** (Route 53) services. They do **not run EC2, S3, or RDS**. The purpose is to **reduce latency** by serving cached content (e.g., images, videos, API responses) from the **geographically closest location** to the user. Additionally, **Regional Edge Caches** sit between CloudFront Edge Locations and the origin (e.g., S3) to reduce repeated origin fetches. *(1.5 marks)* --- #### Model Answer for Q-A(b): Healthcare Application Design (7 Marks) **1. Region Selection (DPDP Compliance):** The application must keep patient data in India → select the **Mumbai Region (`ap-south-1`)** as the **primary Region**. AWS also has the **Hyderabad Region (`ap-south-2`)** as a secondary in-India option. *(1 mark)* **2. Multi-AZ Architecture (High Availability within Primary Region):** - Deploy the application across **3 AZs** in `ap-south-1` (e.g., `ap-south-1a`, `ap-south-1b`, `ap-south-1c`). - Use an **Application Load Balancer (ALB)** to distribute traffic across EC2 instances in each AZ. - Use **RDS Multi-AZ deployment** with one primary in AZ-A and synchronous standby replicas in AZ-B and AZ-C → **RPO = 0** (zero data loss). - Use **ElastiCache Redis** for session management in Multi-AZ mode. - Enable **Amazon S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR)** to a second Region in India for DR. *(2 marks)* **3. Multi-Region Disaster Recovery:** - Use **Route 53** with health checks and **latency-based or failover routing policies**. - Deploy a **"warm standby"** in `ap-south-2` (Hyderabad) with a minimal fleet of EC2 instances and **RDS Read Replicas** that are promoted to primary during failover. - Schedule **automated, daily cross-Region snapshots** of EBS volumes and push to S3 in the secondary Region. *(1.5 marks)* **4. Composite Availability Calculation:** Assuming per-Region availability of 99.99% (multi-AZ EC2 + ALB + RDS): $$A_{primary} = 0.9999$$ With a warm standby Region: $$A_{system} = 1 - (1 - 0.9999) \times (1 - 0.9999)$$ $$A_{system} = 1 - 0.0001 \times 0.0001$$ $$A_{system} = 1 - 0.00000001 = 0.99999999 \text{ (Eight 9s)}$$ $$T_{down} \approx 0.005 \text{ minutes/year} \approx 0.3 \text{ seconds/year}$$ *(1.5 marks)* **5. Compliance Justification:** - Data resides in Indian Regions only → satisfies **DPDP Act 2023 Section 8 (data localization)**. - All data encrypted **at rest** (KMS) and **in transit** (TLS 1.2+). - AWS services are **HIPAA-eligible** in `ap-south-1`, satisfying medical data regulations. - Use **AWS CloudTrail + AWS Config** for audit logging. *(1 mark)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 (Expected Pattern)]`** **(a)** Compare **AWS Local Zones, AWS Wavelength, and AWS Outposts** in terms of purpose, deployment location, latency characteristics, and target use cases. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A global e-commerce company wants to serve its product images and videos to users in 50+ countries with **< 50 ms latency**. Design the **content delivery architecture** using AWS Global Infrastructure. Explain the role of Edge Locations, Regional Edge Caches, and the AWS backbone. *(7 marks)* --- #### Model Answer for Q-B(a): Comparison of Edge Constructs (7 Marks) | Feature | **AWS Local Zones** | **AWS Wavelength** | **AWS Outposts** | |---|---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Brings AWS compute closer to **metro areas** | Embeds AWS inside **5G carrier networks** | Places AWS hardware **inside customer data centers** | | **Deployment Location** | In major cities (e.g., Las Vegas, Boston, Delhi) | Inside telecom operator's central office (e.g., Verizon, Vodafone) | Customer's on-premises data center | | **Latency** | **Single-digit ms** to nearby end users | **Sub-10 ms** to 5G devices | **Near-zero** for on-premises workloads | | **Compute Services** | EC2, EBS, VPC, ECS, EKS | EC2, EBS (limited set) | Full AWS service catalog (locally) | | **Connectivity** | Back to parent Region via AWS backbone | Back to parent Region via carrier network | Back to chosen AWS Region via private link | | **Target Use Cases** | Gaming, video rendering, ML inference in metros | Real-time mobile gaming, AR/VR, autonomous vehicles, smart cities | Low-latency on-prem apps, hybrid workloads, regulatory data residency | | **Management** | Fully AWS-managed | Fully AWS-managed (with carrier) | AWS-managed hardware; customer-managed in terms of placement | | **KTU Mnemonic** | "**Local** = city-level" | "**Wave**length = inside 5G" | "**Out**posts = in your building" | *(7 marks distributed across the comparison table with 1 mark for each key differentiator explained)* --- #### Model Answer for Q-B(b): Global Content Delivery Architecture (7 Marks) **1. Problem Statement:** A global e-commerce company needs to serve **product images, videos, and static assets** to users across 50+ countries with a **latency budget of < 50 ms**, without overloading the origin servers. *(0.5 mark)* **2. Solution Architecture:** - **Step 1: Origin Storage in AWS S3** — Store all product images and videos in an **S3 bucket** in a primary Region (e.g., `us-east-1`). Enable **S3 Transfer Acceleration** for global uploads. *(1 mark)* - **Step 2: Distribute via Amazon CloudFront** — Create a **CloudFront distribution** with the S3 bucket as the **origin**. CloudFront is AWS's **Content Delivery Network (CDN)**, which **caches content at 600+ Edge Locations worldwide**. When a user in Tokyo requests a product image, CloudFront serves it from the **nearest Edge Location** (e.g., Tokyo PoP). *(2 marks)* - **Step 3: Leverage Regional Edge Caches** — CloudFront uses **Regional Edge Caches** (13 globally) as an intermediate layer between the Edge Locations and the origin. If the asset is **not** in the local Edge Location, CloudFront fetches it from the **Regional Edge Cache**, which has a **higher cache-hit ratio** and reduces **repeated origin fetches**, offloading traffic from S3. *(1.5 marks)* - **Step 4: Use the AWS Backbone for Origin Fetches** — When CloudFront must fetch the asset from the origin (S3 in `us-east-1`), the request travels over the **AWS private global backbone**, **not the public internet**. This guarantees **consistent, secure, low-latency origin fetches** even across continents. *(1 mark)* - **Step 5: DNS Routing via Route 53** — Use **Amazon Route 53** with **latency-based routing** to direct user requests to the **best CloudFront edge** for their location. *(0.5 mark)* - **Step 6: Latency Estimation:** $$L_{user} = L_{user \rightarrow EdgePoP} + L_{cache\_miss \rightarrow regional\_cache \rightarrow origin}$$ For a cached hit: $$L_{user} \approx L_{user \rightarrow EdgePoP} \approx 5 \text{ to } 20 \text{ ms}$$ This is **well within the 50 ms budget** for the majority of users. *(0.5 mark)* > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Edge Locations with AZs/Regions:** Edge Locations do **NOT** host EC2, S3, or RDS — they are only for **caching (CloudFront) and DNS (Route 53)**. Marks are deducted if students claim Edge Locations run compute. > 2. **Forgetting AWS GovCloud and China Regions are special:** These are **isolated** from the standard AWS partition and require **separate accounts**. Many students miss this. > 3. **Not specifying "private backbone" for Region-to-Region communication:** AWS does **not** use the public internet for inter-Region traffic — it uses its **private fiber-optic global network**. This is a high-yield fact. > 4. **Confusing SLA "nines" with downtime conversion:** When asked *"What does 99.99% SLA mean?"*, you MUST give **both** the percentage and the **downtime per year (52.56 minutes)**. > 5. **Mixing up Local Zones, Wavelength, and Outposts:** These are often asked in "compare and contrast" questions. Memorize the table in Q-B(a) above. > 6. **Ignoring the AZ naming convention:** AZs are named **Region-code + letter** (e.g., `ap-south-1a`). The letters are mapped **per account** to physical AZs (a feature called **AZ mapping**), so `ap-south-1a` in one account may correspond to a different physical AZ than in another account. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Memorize This Block** - ✅ **AWS Global Infrastructure = Data Centers + AZs + Regions + Edge Locations**, all connected via the **private AWS backbone**. - ✅ **Region = geographic area** with **≥ 2 AZs**; **completely isolated** from other Regions. - ✅ **Availability Zone (AZ) = isolated failure domain** with **< 10 ms inter-AZ latency**; contains 1+ data centers. - ✅ **Data Center = physical building** with N+1 power, cooling, and security. - ✅ **Edge Location = caching layer** for **CloudFront + Route 53 only**; does NOT run EC2/S3/RDS. - ✅ **600+ Edge Locations**, **30+ Regions**, **1000+ data centers** (as of 2024). - ✅ **EC2/RDS Multi-AZ SLA = 99.99%** → max downtime = **52.56 minutes/year**. - ✅ **AWS Private Global Backbone** is used for inter-Region and CloudFront origin fetches — NOT the public internet. - ✅ **AWS Local Zones = city-level** compute; **AWS Wavelength = 5G-embedded** compute; **AWS Outposts = on-premises AWS rack**. - ✅ **AWS GovCloud (US)** is **ITAR-compliant**; **AWS China Regions** are operated by **Sinnet and NWCD** (separate from global AWS). - ✅ **AZ Naming:** `Region-code + letter` (e.g., `us-east-1a`), with **per-account AZ mapping** for redundancy distribution. - ✅ **Composite Availability Formulas:** - Series: $A_{total} = \prod A_i$ - Parallel: $A_{total} = 1 - \prod (1 - A_i)$ - ✅ **DPDP Act 2023 (India) compliance** → use `ap-south-1` (Mumbai) or `ap-south-2` (Hyderabad) for data localization. - ✅ **Cross-AZ traffic is FREE in AWS**; **cross-Region traffic is billable** (per GB egress). - ✅ **Regional Edge Caches** sit **between Edge Locations and origin** to improve cache hit ratio and reduce origin load. - ✅ **Route 53 + Health Checks** enable **automatic DNS failover** between Regions. --- > [!NOTE] > **End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes for Module 3 — AWS Global Infrastructure.** > *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (L1–L5: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze, Evaluate).*

topics.title: AWS Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** AWS Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# AWS Services — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Amazon Web Services (AWS)** is a comprehensive, evolving cloud computing platform provided by Amazon that offers a mixture of infrastructure-as-a-service (IaaS), platform-as-a-service (PaaS), and packaged software-as-a-service (SaaS) offerings. AWS services are on-demand, pay-as-you-go, and provide **global compute, storage, database, networking, analytics, machine learning, security, and DevOps** capabilities delivered over the internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Verbatim):** *"AWS Services are a set of over 200 fully featured, ready-to-use cloud computing services offered by Amazon Web Services, categorized into domains such as Compute, Storage, Database, Networking, Security, Analytics, and Management, designed to reduce operational overhead, scale elastically, and provide high availability across global regions and availability zones."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlights (Module 3):**
> - Service categorization across **7 core pillars**
> - Deployment models (Region, Availability Zone, Edge Location)
> - Pricing models (On-Demand, Reserved, Spot, Savings Plans)
> - Service Level Agreements (SLAs) and shared responsibility model
> - Service selection for specific workloads

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The "Cloud Supermarket" Analogy:**

Imagine AWS as a **giant 24×7 self-service supermarket** that never closes, never runs out of stock, and bills you *only* for what you take out of the freezer.

- 🏪 **AWS = The Supermarket Building** — the entire global cloud infrastructure.
- 🛒 **Each Service = A Specialized Aisle** — Compute aisle, Storage aisle, Database aisle, etc.
- 🧊 **Elasticity = Freezer that expands** — when you need more space, the freezer magically grows; when you don't, you pay less.
- 💳 **Pay-as-you-go = Barcode scanner billing** — every item scanned is billed per-gram or per-minute.
- 🌍 **Regions = Branches of the supermarket in different cities** — pick the branch closest to your customers for minimum delivery time.
- 🏗️ **Shared Responsibility Model = You cook the food, the supermarket keeps the kitchen clean** — AWS secures the *cloud*, you secure what you put *in the cloud*.

> [!TIP]
> **For First-Time Learners:** Whenever you hear a new AWS service name, ask:
> 1. **Which aisle (category)?**
> 2. **What problem does it solve?**
> 3. **Does it replace an on-premise tool, or enable something new?**
> 
> This 3-question filter will help you slot every service into your mental model.

---

## 1.3 The 7 Pillars of AWS Services

| # | Pillar | What It Does | One-Line Memory Cue |
|---|--------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Compute** | Runs your code and applications | "The Engine Room" |
| 2 | **Storage** | Holds your files, blocks, archives | "The Warehouse" |
| 3 | **Database** | Structured & NoSQL data engines | "The Filing Cabinet" |
| 4 | **Networking** | Routes, delivers, balances traffic | "The Highway System" |
| 5 | **Security & Identity** | Controls *who* can do *what* | "The Bouncers" |
| 6 | **Management & Monitoring** | Observes, logs, audits | "The CCTV Room" |
| 7 | **Analytics & ML / Messaging** | Processes streams, decouples apps | "The Insight Lab" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** AWS currently operates in **32+ geographic Regions**, **100+ Availability Zones**, and **600+ Edge Locations / Points of Presence** (as of 2024 KTU syllabus update). Each Region contains **≥ 2 Availability Zones (AZs)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The AWS Service Taxonomy — Structured Breakdown

Below is the **exam-ready breakdown** of the major AWS services organized by category. This is the single most high-yield table for KTU Board Exams.

### 2.1.1 🖥️ Compute Services

| Service | Full Form | Service Model | Core Use Case | Pricing Unit |
|---------|-----------|---------------|---------------|--------------|
| **EC2** | Elastic Compute Cloud | IaaS | Virtual servers in the cloud | Per hour/second |
| **Lambda** | AWS Lambda | FaaS / Serverless | Run code without servers | Per request & GB-s |
| **ECS** | Elastic Container Service | Containers (PaaS) | Run Docker containers | Per hour per task |
| **EKS** | Elastic Kubernetes Service | Containers | Managed Kubernetes clusters | Per hour per cluster |
| **Fargate** | AWS Fargate | Serverless Compute | Serverless containers (no EC2 mgmt) | Per vCPU & GB-hr |
| **Lightsail** | Amazon Lightsail | IaaS (simplified) | Small projects, predictable pricing | Per month flat |
| **Batch** | AWS Batch | Batch Computing | Run hundreds of batch jobs | Per vCPU/GB-hr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EC2 = IaaS (you manage OS, runtime, app)** vs **Lambda = Serverless (AWS manages everything except your code)**. This distinction is a **3-mark favourite** in KTU exams.

---

### 2.1.2 💾 Storage Services

| Service | Full Form | Data Type | Access Pattern | Durability |
|---------|-----------|-----------|----------------|------------|
| **S3** | Simple Storage Service | Objects (files) | HTTP/REST API | **99.999999999% (11 9s)** |
| **EBS** | Elastic Block Store | Block volumes | Attached to 1 EC2 | 99.8%–99.9% |
| **EFS** | Elastic File System | File system (NFS) | Shared across many EC2 | 99.999999999% |
| **FSx** | FSx for Lustre/Windows | High-perf file | HPC, Windows workloads | Varies |
| **Glacier** | Amazon Glacier | Cold archive | Rarely accessed (mins–hrs retrieval) | 99.999999999% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Block vs File vs Object — The KTU Hot Question:**
> - **EBS** = Hard drive attached to one server (Block).
> - **EFS** = Network shared folder for many servers (File).
> - **S3** = Internet-accessible bucket of files identified by URL (Object).

---

### 2.1.3 🗄️ Database Services

| Service | Type | Engine | Best For |
|---------|------|--------|----------|
| **RDS** | Relational (SQL) | MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server, MariaDB | Traditional OLTP apps |
| **Aurora** | Relational (Cloud-native SQL) | MySQL/PostgreSQL compatible | High-performance relational, 5× MySQL throughput |
| **DynamoDB** | NoSQL Key-Value/Document | Serverless | Millisecond latency at any scale |
| **Redshift** | Data Warehouse (Columnar) | PostgreSQL-based | OLAP, BI, analytics queries |
| **ElastiCache** | In-Memory | Redis or Memcached | Sub-millisecond caching, session store |
| **Neptune** | Graph Database | Apache TinkerPop | Social networks, fraud graphs |
| **DocumentDB** | Document (JSON) | MongoDB-compatible | Semi-structured data |
| **Timestream** | Time-Series | Purpose-built | IoT, DevOps metrics |
| **QLDB** | Ledger Database | Immutable, cryptographically verifiable | Audit trails, financial records |

---

### 2.1.4 🌐 Networking & Content Delivery Services

| Service | Function | Analogy |
|---------|----------|---------|
| **VPC** | Virtual Private Cloud — isolated network | Your private plot in the cloud |
| **Subnet** | Subdivision of a VPC (public/private) | Rooms inside your private plot |
| **Route 53** | DNS & Domain Registration | The phone book of the internet |
| **CloudFront** | Content Delivery Network (CDN) | Pizza delivery — local ovens for fast delivery |
| **ELB** | Elastic Load Balancing | Traffic policeman distributing cars |
| **API Gateway** | Front-door for REST/WebSocket APIs | Reception desk for your APIs |
| **Direct Connect** | Dedicated private line to AWS | Fiber cable from your office to AWS |
| **Transit Gateway** | Hub connecting many VPCs | Central railway station |
| **Global Accelerator** | Optimizes global TCP/UDP routing | Fast lane for global traffic |

---

### 2.1.5 🔐 Security, Identity & Compliance

| Service | Function | KTU Memory Cue |
|---------|----------|----------------|
| **IAM** | Identity & Access Management (users, roles, policies) | "Who can touch what" |
| **Cognito** | User sign-up/sign-in for apps | "Login for mobile/web apps" |
| **KMS** | Key Management Service | "Vault for encryption keys" |
| **Secrets Manager** | Rotate & retrieve secrets (DB passwords, API keys) | "Password manager for apps" |
| **WAF** | Web Application Firewall | "Filter for malicious HTTP requests" |
| **Shield** | DDoS protection (Standard = free, Advanced = paid) | "Anti-flood wall" |
| **GuardDuty** | Threat detection service | "Security camera + alarm" |
| **Inspector** | Vulnerability scanner for EC2/containers | "Health checkup for VMs" |
| **Macie** | Discovers & protects sensitive data in S3 | "Privacy scanner" |
| **Artifact** | On-demand access to compliance reports | "Certificate cupboard" |

---

### 2.1.6 📊 Management, Monitoring & Governance

| Service | Function | Tag Line |
|---------|----------|----------|
| **CloudWatch** | Metrics, logs, alarms, dashboards | "Telemetry dashboard" |
| **CloudTrail** | API call audit log | "Who did what, when" |
| **AWS Config** | Resource inventory & change history | "Config drift detector" |
| **Trusted Advisor** | Cost, security, performance recommendations | "Cloud consultant" |
| **Systems Manager (SSM)** | Operational management of EC2 fleets | "Remote admin toolkit" |
| **CloudFormation** | Infrastructure-as-Code (JSON/YAML templates) | "Blueprint architect" |
| **CDK** | Cloud Development Kit (code-defined infra) | "Infra as code in TypeScript/Python" |
| **Organizations** | Multi-account governance | "HQ for AWS accounts" |
| **Control Tower** | Landing zone for multi-account setup | "Standardized multi-account factory" |

---

### 2.1.7 📬 Application Integration, Analytics & ML

| Service | Category | Function |
|---------|----------|----------|
| **SQS** | Messaging | Queue (decoupling) — pull-based |
| **SNS** | Messaging | Pub/Sub fanout (push) |
| **EventBridge** | Event Bus | Serverless event routing |
| **Step Functions** | Orchestration | Coordinate distributed workflows |
| **Kinesis** | Streaming | Real-time data streams (Data Streams, Firehose, Analytics) |
| **Athena** | Analytics | SQL on S3 data (pay per TB scanned) |
| **EMR** | Analytics | Managed Hadoop/Spark |
| **Glue** | Analytics | ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) |
| **QuickSight** | Analytics | BI dashboards |
| **SageMaker** | ML | Build, train, deploy ML models |
| **Rekognition** | ML | Image/video analysis |
| **Comprehend** | ML | NLP (sentiment, entity extraction) |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas appear in KTU numerical/short-answer questions (Module 3).**

### 2.2.1 EC2 Pricing Formula

$$
\text{Monthly\ EC2\ Cost} = \text{Instance\ Hourly\ Rate} \times 24 \times 30 \times \text{Number\ of\ Instances}
$$

### 2.2.2 Lambda Pricing Formula

$$
\text{Lambda\ Cost} = (\text{Requests} \times \$0.20 / 1{,}000{,}000) + (\text{GB\text{-}seconds} \times \$0.0000166667)
$$

### 2.2.3 S3 Pricing Formula

$$
\text{S3\ Cost} = (\text{Storage\ GB} \times \text{Per\text{-}GB\ Rate}) + (\text{Requests} \times \text{Per\text{-}Request\ Rate}) + \text{Data\ Transfer\ Out}
$$

### 2.2.4 S3 Data Transfer Pricing

$$
\text{Transfer\ Out\ Cost} = \text{GB\ transferred\ out} \times \$0.09\ \text{(first\ 10\ TB,\ varies\ by\ region)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **S3 has NO charge for data transfer IN to the bucket.**

### 2.2.5 Availability Formula (for redundant architecture)

$$
\text{System\ Availability} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)
$$

Where $A_i$ is the availability of each component.

### 2.2.6 RDS Pricing (rough)

$$
\text{RDS\ Cost} = \text{Instance\ hr} \times \text{Rate} + \text{Storage\ GB} \times \text{Storage\ Rate} + \text{IOPS\ provisioned} \times \text{IOPS\ Rate}
$$

### 2.2.7 KTU Quick-Reference Cheat Table

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Common Trap |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| EC2 reserved vs on-demand | Reserved = **up to 75%** discount | Min 1-year commitment |
| S3 Standard IA retrieval fee | Charged per GB retrieved | "IA = Infrequent Access" ≠ free retrieval |
| Glacier retrieval | Standard = 3–5 hrs; Expedited = 1–5 min | Cost differs by retrieval speed |
| Data transfer in | **FREE** | Always free in AWS |
| Data transfer out to internet | Tiered: first 100 GB/month free | Lower in, higher out |
| Spot Instance max discount | **Up to 90%** off on-demand | Can be terminated with 2-min notice |
| EBS snapshot cost | Per GB-month | Snapshots in different region cost more |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by Cloud Architects to perform **TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) modelling**, **FinOps budgeting**, and **capacity planning**. The ability to compute monthly spend from configuration parameters is a core FinOps skill demanded in industry roles.

---

## 2.3 Shared Responsibility Model — The "Who Secures What" Boundary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a guaranteed 3-mark question in KTU exams.**

| Layer | AWS is Responsible For | Customer is Responsible For |
|-------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| Infrastructure (Hardware, Regions) | ✅ | ❌ |
| Compute (EC2 host, hypervisor) | ✅ | ❌ |
| Storage (EBS, S3 underlying) | ✅ | ❌ |
| Networking (VPC plumbing) | ✅ | ❌ |
| Operating System (EC2) | ❌ | ✅ |
| Application Code | ❌ | ✅ |
| Data Encryption (client-side) | ❌ | ✅ |
| IAM Users / Roles / Policies | ❌ | ✅ |
| Network Firewall Rules (SG, NACL) | ❌ | ✅ |
| Patches on EC2 OS | ❌ | ✅ |

> **Memory Cue:** *"AWS secures the cloud, you secure what's in the cloud."*

---

## 2.4 Global Infrastructure — Region, AZ, Edge

- **Region** = A physical geographic location (e.g., `ap-south-1` = Mumbai). Each Region is **completely independent** (no data sharing across regions by default).
- **Availability Zone (AZ)** = One or more **discrete data centers** within a Region, with redundant power, networking, and connectivity. Each Region has **≥ 2 AZs** (most have 3+).
- **Edge Location / PoP** = A site used by **CloudFront** and **Route 53** to cache content close to end-users for low latency.

$$
\text{Region-to-Region\ Latency} \approx 60\text{–}250\ \text{ms (typical intercontinental)}
$$

$$
\text{AZ-to-AZ\ Latency} < 2\ \text{ms (within a Region)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating Monthly EC2 Cost

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A company runs 4 `t3.medium` EC2 instances 24×7 in the `ap-south-1` (Mumbai) region. The on-demand hourly rate is ₹6.37/hour. Reserved instance rate is ₹4.10/hour with a 1-year commitment. Calculate the **annual savings** using Reserved over On-Demand.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute On-Demand Monthly Cost per Instance**

$$
C_{\text{OD\ monthly}} = \text{Rate}_{\text{OD}} \times 24 \times 30
$$

Substitute values:

$$
C_{\text{OD\ monthly}} = 6.37 \times 24 \times 30
$$

$$
C_{\text{OD\ monthly}} = 6.37 \times 720 = ₹4{,}586.40\ \text{per instance}
$$

**Step 2 — Scale to 4 Instances**

$$
C_{\text{OD\ 4\ inst\ monthly}} = 4{,}586.40 \times 4 = ₹18{,}345.60
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Annual On-Demand Cost**

$$
C_{\text{OD\ annual}} = 18{,}345.60 \times 12 = ₹2{,}20{,}147.20
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Reserved Monthly Cost per Instance**

$$
C_{\text{RI\ monthly}} = 4.10 \times 24 \times 30 = 4.10 \times 720 = ₹2{,}952
$$

**Step 5 — Scale and Annualize for Reserved**

$$
C_{\text{RI\ 4\ inst\ annual}} = 2{,}952 \times 4 \times 12 = ₹1{,}41{,}696
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Annual Savings**

$$
\text{Savings} = C_{\text{OD\ annual}} - C_{\text{RI\ annual}}
$$

$$
\text{Savings} = 2{,}20{,}147.20 - 1{,}41{,}696 = ₹78{,}451.20
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Savings Percentage**

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \frac{78{,}451.20}{2{,}20{,}147.20} \times 100 \approx 35.6\%
$$

> **Result:** The company saves **₹78,451.20 per year (~35.6%)** by switching to 1-year Reserved Instances.

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Logic Explained:** EC2 bills by the hour, so 1 day = 24 hours, 1 month ≈ 30 days (730 hours/month is AWS's official convention, but KTU accepts 720 for simplification).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lambda Cost Calculation

> **Problem:** A Lambda function is invoked **5 million times/month**, runs for an **average of 200 ms**, and allocates **512 MB** of memory. Compute the monthly Lambda bill.
> (Free tier: 1M requests + 400,000 GB-seconds/month.)

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Billable Requests**

$$
R_{\text{billable}} = 5{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute GB-Seconds per Invocation**

$$
\text{GB-s} = 0.512\ \text{GB} \times 0.2\ \text{s} = 0.1024\ \text{GB-s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Monthly GB-Seconds**

$$
\text{Total GB-s} = 0.1024 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = 512{,}000\ \text{GB-s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Billable GB-Seconds (after free tier)**

$$
\text{Billable GB-s} = 512{,}000 - 400{,}000 = 112{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Request Cost**

$$
C_{\text{req}} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times \$0.20 = \$0.80
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Compute Cost**

$$
C_{\text{comp}} = 112{,}000 \times \$0.0000166667 = \$1.87
$$

**Step 7 — Total Cost**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 0.80 + 1.87 = \$2.67\ \text{per month}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: System Availability — Single EC2 vs Multi-AZ

> **Problem:** A web app runs on **1 EC2 instance in 1 AZ** (monthly availability = 99.5%). If you deploy the same app across **2 AZs** with a load balancer (each AZ independent), compute the new availability.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert 99.5% to Probability of Failure**

$$
P_{\text{fail\ single}} = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005
$$

**Step 2 — For 2 Independent AZs, Both Must Fail for Outage**

$$
P_{\text{both\ fail}} = 0.005 \times 0.005 = 0.000025
$$

**Step 3 — Compute New Availability**

$$
A_{\text{new}} = 1 - 0.000025 = 0.999975 = 99.9975\%
$$

**Step 4 — Nines Notation Improvement**

$$
\text{Single\ AZ} = 2\text{ nines }(99.5\%)\ \longrightarrow\ \text{Multi-AZ} = 4\text{ nines }(99.9975\%)
$$

> **Result:** Multi-AZ deployment improved availability from 99.5% to 99.9975% — downtime reduced from ~3.6 hours/month to **~13 seconds/month**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Service Selection by Workload

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the highest-yield visual for KTU 2024. Memorize it.**

| Workload Requirement | Recommended AWS Service | Reason |
|----------------------|--------------------------|--------|
| Run a Windows server with full admin | **EC2** | IaaS gives OS-level control |
| Run code only when an S3 upload happens | **Lambda** | Event-driven, no server to manage |
| Run 100 Docker containers in production | **ECS / EKS / Fargate** | Purpose-built container orchestration |
| Store user-uploaded images | **S3** | Object storage, cheap, durable, CDN-friendly |
| Block storage for a database on EC2 | **EBS** | Low-latency block device |
| Shared file system for 50 EC2 instances | **EFS** | NFS, scales to thousands of nodes |
| Backups that are kept 7 years for legal | **S3 Glacier Deep Archive** | Cheapest storage tier |
| 10,000 reads/writes per second, single-digit ms | **DynamoDB** | Serverless NoSQL at any scale |
| Complex SQL joins on 1 TB of data | **RDS (Aurora)** | Managed relational DB |
| Analytics on petabytes | **Redshift** | Columnar warehouse |
| Real-time fraud detection on streams | **Kinesis + Lambda** | Streaming + serverless processing |
| Send SMS to 10 million users | **SNS** | Pub/sub fan-out |
| Coordinate a 5-step order workflow | **Step Functions** | Visual workflow orchestrator |
| Front a REST API with auth + throttling | **API Gateway + Lambda** | Serverless API |
| Global static website with low latency | **S3 + CloudFront** | S3 origin, CDN edge cache |
| Centralized user login for mobile app | **Cognito** | Managed user pool / identity pool |
| Manage encryption keys for HIPAA data | **KMS** | FIPS 140-2 validated HSM |
| Detect unusual API calls | **GuardDuty** | ML-based threat detection |
| Audit who deleted an S3 bucket last week | **CloudTrail** | Immutable API audit log |
| Provision 50 identical VPCs in 5 minutes | **CloudFormation** | IaC templating |

---

## 3.5 SLA Comparison Matrix — KTU Favourite

| Service | SLA Uptime | Downtime/Month (approx) |
|---------|------------|--------------------------|
| EC2 (Single-AZ) | 99.99% | 4.3 min |
| EC2 (Multi-AZ) | 99.99% | 4.3 min |
| RDS (Multi-AZ) | 99.95% | 21.9 min |
| S3 | 99.9% | 43.8 min |
| DynamoDB (Global Tables) | 99.999% | 2.6 sec |
| Lambda | 99.95% | 21.9 min |
| CloudFront | 99.9% | 43.8 min |
| API Gateway | 99.95% | 21.9 min |
| Route 53 | 100% (with health checks) | 0 sec |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Cue for KTU:** *"DynamoDB Global Tables = 5 nines, S3 = 3 nines, RDS Multi-AZ = 3.5 nines."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
