# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AWS Service Categorization (Hierarchical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[AWS Cloud Platform] --> B[Compute Services]
    A --> C[Storage Services]
    A --> D[Database Services]
    A --> E[Networking Services]
    A --> F[Security & Identity]
    A --> G[Management & Monitoring]
    A --> H[Analytics & Messaging]

    B --> B1[EC2 Virtual Servers]
    B --> B2[Lambda Serverless]
    B --> B3[ECS EKS Containers]
    B --> B4[Fargate Serverless Containers]
    B --> B5[Lightsail Simple VPS]

    C --> C1[S3 Object Storage]
    C --> C2[EBS Block Storage]
    C --> C3[EFS Shared File System]
    C --> C4[Glacier Cold Archive]

    D --> D1[RDS Relational]
    D --> D2[Aurora Cloud Native SQL]
    D --> D3[DynamoDB NoSQL]
    D --> D4[Redshift Data Warehouse]
    D --> D5[ElastiCache In Memory]

    E --> E1[VPC Private Network]
    E --> E2[Route 53 DNS]
    E --> E3[CloudFront CDN]
    E --> E4[ELB Load Balancer]
    E --> E5[API Gateway]

    F --> F1[IAM Access Control]
    F --> F2[Cognito User Pools]
    F --> F3[KMS Encryption Keys]
    F --> F4[WAF Web Firewall]
    F --> F5[GuardDuty Threat Detection]

    G --> G1[CloudWatch Metrics Logs]
    G --> G2[CloudTrail Audit Logs]
    G --> G3[CloudFormation IaC]
    G --> G4[AWS Config Resource History]
    G --> G5[Trusted Advisor Recommendations]

    H --> H1[SQS Message Queue]
    H --> H2[SNS Pub Sub Fanout]
    H --> H3[EventBridge Event Bus]
    H --> H4[Step Functions Orchestration]
    H --> H5[Kinesis Streaming Data]
    H --> H6[Athena SQL on S3]
```

> **Interpretation:** This diagram maps the **7-pillar taxonomy** taught in KTU Module 3. Every exam answer should classify a service into one of these pillars.

---

## 4.2 End-to-End AWS Application Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user[End User] --> cf[CloudFront CDN]
    cf --> r53[Route 53 DNS]
    cf --> s3[S3 Static Assets]
    r53 --> apigw[API Gateway]
    apigw --> lambda[Lambda Functions]
    lambda --> ddb[(DynamoDB)]
    lambda --> s3files[(S3 Bucket)]
    lambda --> sns[SNS Topic]
    sns --> sqs1[SQS Queue]
    sqs1 --> worker[Lambda Worker]
    worker --> ses[SES Email Service]
    apigw --> waf[WAF Web Firewall]
    waf --> shield[Shield DDoS Protection]
    lambda --> cw[CloudWatch Logs Metrics]
    lambda --> ct[CloudTrail Audit]
    cw --> alarm[CloudWatch Alarm]
    alarm --> asg[Auto Scaling Group EC2]
    cw --> snsalert[SNS Alert]
    snsalert --> admin[Admin Email]
```

> **Block-Level Functional Interpretation:**
> 1. **User → CloudFront → Route 53 → API Gateway** = global, secure, low-latency entry point.
> 2. **API Gateway → Lambda** = serverless business logic.
> 3. **Lambda → DynamoDB / S3** = data persistence layer.
> 4. **Lambda → SNS → SQS → Worker Lambda** = decoupled async processing.
> 5. **WAF + Shield** = perimeter security.
> 6. **CloudWatch + CloudTrail** = observability and auditing.
> 7. **CloudWatch Alarm → Auto Scaling** = self-healing, elastic scaling.

---

## 4.3 AWS Shared Responsibility Model Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AWS["AWS Responsibility: Security OF the Cloud"]
        R1[Global Infrastructure Regions AZs Edge]
        R2[Compute Storage Database Network Plumbing]
        R3[Hypervisor and Host OS]
    end
    subgraph CUST["Customer Responsibility: Security IN the Cloud"]
        C1[EC2 Guest OS and Patches]
        C2[Application Code and Configuration]
        C3[IAM Users Roles Policies]
        C4[Data Encryption Client Side]
        C5[Security Groups NACLs Network Rules]
    end
    AWS -.->|Hand-off boundary| CUST
```

> **Exam Tip:** Always label both sides and explicitly mention the **hand-off boundary** (e.g., "above the hypervisor = customer, below = AWS").

---

## 4.4 Region / AZ / Edge Location Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    world[Global Users] --> edge1[Edge Location: Mumbai]
    world --> edge2[Edge Location: Singapore]
    world --> edge3[Edge Location: Frankfurt]
    edge1 --> reg1[AWS Region: ap-south-1 Mumbai]
    edge2 --> reg2[AWS Region: ap-southeast-1 Singapore]
    edge3 --> reg3[AWS Region: eu-central-1 Frankfurt]
    reg1 --> az1[AZ mumbai-1a]
    reg1 --> az2[AZ mumbai-1b]
    reg1 --> az3[AZ mumbai-1c]
    az1 --> dc1[Data Center 1]
    az1 --> dc2[Data Center 2]
    az2 --> dc3[Data Center 3]
```

> **Interpretation:** A Region contains **2+ AZs**; each AZ contains **1+ physical data centers**. Edge locations are separate from Regions and exist **only for CloudFront / Route 53 caching**.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Instructions:** Answer any **TWO** questions. Each carries **3 marks**. (Max: 50–60 words per answer.)

---

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 (Model)]

**Define Amazon S3. List any four of its storage classes.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3)** is an object storage service that offers industry-leading scalability, data availability, security, and performance. It stores data as **objects inside buckets** and is accessible over **HTTP/HTTPS** using a REST API.
>
> **Four Storage Classes of S3:**
> 1. **S3 Standard** — frequently accessed data, 11 9s durability.
> 2. **S3 Intelligent-Tiering** — automatic cost optimization between tiers.
> 3. **S3 Standard-IA (Infrequent Access)** — lower storage cost, higher retrieval cost.
> 4. **S3 Glacier / Glacier Deep Archive** — long-term cold archive with retrieval times from minutes to 12 hours.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 (Model)]

**Explain the AWS Shared Responsibility Model with two examples on each side.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Shared Responsibility Model** divides security duties between AWS and the customer.
>
> **AWS is responsible for (Security OF the Cloud):**
> 1. Physical security of data centers.
> 2. Hardware, networking, hypervisor, and managed service infrastructure.
>
> **Customer is responsible for (Security IN the Cloud):**
> 1. Configuring **IAM policies** and user access.
> 2. Encrypting data and patching the guest OS on EC2.
>
> *Memory cue: "AWS secures the cloud, you secure what's in the cloud."*

---

## 5.2 PART B Question — Module 3 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Instructions:** Answer **ONE** full question. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **AWS Global Infrastructure** — Regions, Availability Zones, and Edge Locations. Discuss how an organization would select a Region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **EC2, Lambda, and Fargate** as AWS compute options. For each, give one real-world use case and the pricing model. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Definition:** AWS Global Infrastructure is the worldwide footprint of physical data centers organized into Regions, Availability Zones, and Edge Locations, designed to deliver low-latency, fault-tolerant cloud services.

**Three-tier structure:**

1. **Region** — A separate geographic area (e.g., `ap-south-1` Mumbai, `us-east-1` N. Virginia). Each Region is **completely independent** and isolated from other Regions.
2. **Availability Zone (AZ)** — One or more discrete data centers within a Region, each with **independent power, cooling, and networking**. AZs within a Region are connected via **low-latency (< 2 ms) private fiber**.
3. **Edge Location** — A site used by **CloudFront (CDN)** and **Route 53 (DNS)** to cache content closer to end-users, reducing latency.

**Region Selection Criteria (5 factors):**

1. **Latency / Proximity to Users** — pick the Region closest to your customers.
2. **Compliance / Data Residency** — e.g., GDPR requires EU data stays in EU Regions.
3. **Service Availability** — not all services are in all Regions; new services launch in `us-east-1` first.
4. **Pricing** — costs vary by Region (e.g., `us-east-1` is typically cheapest).
5. **Disaster Recovery** — use a **second Region** for cross-region replication.

**Diagram:** Refer Section 4.4 of these notes.

> **Conclusion:** A well-designed global deployment uses **multi-AZ for HA** within a Region and **multi-Region** for DR and geo-proximity.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A(b):

| Feature | **EC2** | **Lambda** | **Fargate** |
|---------|---------|------------|-------------|
| Type | IaaS (Virtual Server) | FaaS (Serverless Function) | Serverless Containers |
| Manage OS? | Yes (customer) | No (AWS) | No (AWS) |
| Max runtime | Indefinite | **15 minutes** | Indefinite |
| Pricing unit | Per hour/second of instance | Per request + GB-second | Per vCPU-GB-hour of task |
| Scaling | Manual / ASG | Automatic, per request | Automatic, per task count |
| Use case | Full Linux/Windows server, DB host, long-running batch | Image thumbnail on S3 upload, scheduled cron | Run containerized microservices without managing EC2 |

**Real-world Use Cases:**
- **EC2** → Hosting a legacy enterprise Java app requiring custom OS tuning.
- **Lambda** → Trigger on S3 `PutObject` event to generate a thumbnail and write back to another bucket.
- **Fargate** → Run a Dockerized REST API in ECS without managing the underlying EC2 cluster.

> **Pricing Model Summary:**
- **EC2** → Hourly rate × 730 hours/month × number of instances.
- **Lambda** → First 1M requests/month free; then $0.20/M requests + $0.0000166667 per GB-s.
- **Fargate** → $0.04048 per vCPU-hour + $0.004445 per GB-hour (varies by region).

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **AWS database services** — RDS, DynamoDB, and Redshift. Compare them on the basis of data model, scalability, and typical workload. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **key storage services** of AWS — S3, EBS, and EFS. For each, state the type of storage, access method, and an appropriate use case. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(a):

**1. Amazon RDS (Relational Database Service)**
- **Type:** Managed **SQL (relational)** database.
- **Engines supported:** MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, Aurora.
- **Scaling:** Vertical (instance type) + read replicas; storage auto-grows.
- **Workload:** OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) — order management, banking, ERP.

**2. Amazon DynamoDB**
- **Type:** Managed **NoSQL key-value & document** database.
- **Scaling:** **Automatic, seamless, horizontal** (any scale, single-digit ms latency).
- **Workload:** Mobile apps, gaming leaderboards, IoT, session management, shopping carts.
- **Special:** Supports ACID transactions, Global Tables (multi-region replication).

**3. Amazon Redshift**
- **Type:** Managed **columnar data warehouse** (PostgreSQL-based, MPP architecture).
- **Scaling:** Concurrency Scaling, elastic resize, RA3 nodes with managed storage.
- **Workload:** OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) — BI dashboards, complex analytical queries over petabytes.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | RDS | DynamoDB | Redshift |
|-----------|-----|----------|----------|
| Data Model | Relational (SQL) | NoSQL (key-value/document) | Columnar (warehouse) |
| Scaling | Vertical + Read Replicas | Auto, horizontal (any scale) | Concurrency scaling, elastic resize |
| Query Language | SQL | PartiQL / NoSQL API | SQL (PostgreSQL-based) |
| Latency | ms | **Single-digit ms** | seconds to minutes (large scans) |
| Workload | OLTP | OLTP at massive scale | OLAP / BI |
| Best For | ERP, transactional apps | Mobile, gaming, IoT | Reporting, analytics |

> **Conclusion:** Choose **RDS for traditional SQL**, **DynamoDB for internet-scale NoSQL**, and **Redshift for analytics on historical data**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B(b):

**1. Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service)**
- **Type:** **Object storage** (files as objects inside buckets).
- **Access:** HTTP/HTTPS REST API, web console, CLI.
- **Durability:** **99.999999999% (11 9s)**.
- **Use case:** Static website hosting, data lakes, backup, media storage, log archival.

**2. Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store)**
- **Type:** **Block storage** (raw volumes attached to a single EC2).
- **Access:** Mounted as a device to one EC2 instance at a time (multi-attach in some cases).
- **Use case:** Boot volume for EC2, database storage requiring low-latency IOPS, OS disk.

**3. Amazon EFS (Elastic File System)**
- **Type:** **File storage** (NFSv4-compatible shared file system).
- **Access:** Mounted by **thousands of EC2 instances concurrently** across AZs.
- **Use case:** Shared content repository, web server farm serving same files, big data workloads.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | S3 | EBS | EFS |
|---------|----|----|-----|
| Type | Object | Block | File |
| Access Protocol | HTTPS REST | Block device mount | NFSv4 |
| Attached To | Any client (via URL) | Single EC2 | Many EC2s |
| Max Size | **Unlimited** | Up to 64 TiB per volume | **Petabyte-scale** |
| Durability | 11 9s | 99.8–99.9% | 11 9s |
| Use Case | Web assets, backups, archives | DB volumes, boot disks | Shared home directories, content mgmt |

> **Conclusion:** Use **S3 for internet-facing, scale-out data; EBS for single-instance low-latency disks; EFS for shared file systems across many servers**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 AWS Services Answers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing EC2 with Lambda** — Students often write "EC2 is serverless". **EC2 is IaaS (you manage OS); Lambda is serverless (AWS manages OS).** Marks lost: 1–2.
>
> 2. **S3 = "Hard Disk"** — Wrong. S3 is **object storage**, not block storage. It cannot be mounted like EBS.
>
> 3. **Region ≠ AZ** — A Region contains multiple AZs. AZs are connected by **< 2 ms** low-latency links; Regions are connected over the **public internet** (or Direct Connect).
>
> 4. **Forgetting S3 = 11 nines** — Memorize **"11 nines of durability"** for S3, but only **3 nines of availability**. Many students confuse the two.
>
> 5. **Shared Responsibility on the Wrong Side** — Saying "AWS manages OS on EC2" is **incorrect**. AWS manages the **hypervisor**; customer manages the **guest OS, patches, and software**.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning pricing units** — In compute comparison questions, KTU expects the **pricing dimension** (per hour, per request, per vCPU-GB-hour). Omitting this loses 1 mark.
>
> 7. **Ignoring multi-AZ in HA questions** — Saying "deploy 2 EC2 in same AZ" is **not HA**. They must be in **different AZs** for redundancy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this out before exam)**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- [x] AWS = IaaS + PaaS + SaaS, pay-as-you-go, global, elastic.
- [x] **7 Pillars:** Compute, Storage, Database, Networking, Security, Management, Analytics.
- [x] **Region = Geographic area; AZ = Data center(s) within Region; Edge = CDN cache point.**
- [x] **Shared Responsibility:** AWS secures the cloud, you secure what's in the cloud.

### 🔹 Compute — One-Liners
- [x] **EC2** = IaaS virtual server (you manage OS).
- [x] **Lambda** = FaaS, max 15 min, pay per request + GB-s.
- [x] **ECS / EKS / Fargate** = containers; Fargate is serverless containers.
- [x] **Lightsail** = simple VPS for small projects.

### 🔹 Storage — One-Liners
- [x] **S3** = Object, 11 9s durability, **S3 Standard / IA / Glacier / Deep Archive**.
- [x] **EBS** = Block, single EC2, boot volume.
- [x] **EFS** = NFS file system, shared across many EC2s.
- [x] **Glacier** = cold archive, retrieval = minutes to hours.

### 🔹 Database — One-Liners
- [x] **RDS** = SQL OLTP, multi-AZ for HA.
- [x] **Aurora** = AWS-built, MySQL/PostgreSQL compatible, 5× faster.
- [x] **DynamoDB** = NoSQL, serverless, **single-digit ms latency at any scale**.
- [x] **Redshift** = columnar data warehouse for OLAP.
- [x] **ElastiCache** = Redis/Memcached for in-memory speed.

### 🔹 Networking — One-Liners
- [x] **VPC** = isolated private network.
- [x] **Route 53** = DNS, 100% SLA with health checks.
- [x] **CloudFront** = CDN, 600+ edge locations.
- [x] **ELB** = distributes traffic across EC2/containers.
- [x] **API Gateway** = front-door for REST/WebSocket APIs.

### 🔹 Security — One-Liners
- [x] **IAM** = users, groups, roles, policies.
- [x] **Cognito** = user sign-up/sign-in for B2C/B2B.
- [x] **KMS** = encryption key management (FIPS 140-2).
- [x] **WAF** = filter HTTP/S against OWASP Top 10.
- [x] **GuardDuty** = ML-based threat detection.

### 🔹 Management — One-Liners
- [x] **CloudWatch** = metrics, logs, alarms.
- [x] **CloudTrail** = who-called-what API audit.
- [x] **CloudFormation** = IaC via JSON/YAML.
- [x] **Trusted Advisor** = cost, security, performance tips.

### 🔹 Messaging & Analytics — One-Liners
- [x] **SQS** = queue (pull).
- [x] **SNS** = pub/sub fanout (push).
- [x] **EventBridge** = serverless event bus.
- [x] **Step Functions** = visual workflow orchestration.
- [x] **Kinesis** = real-time streaming.
- [x] **Athena** = SQL on S3, pay per TB scanned.

### 🔹 Pricing & SLA
- [x] On-Demand (highest), Reserved (~75% off), Spot (up to 90% off, interruptible).
- [x] Data transfer **IN is FREE**.
- [x] S3 = 11 nines durability, 3 nines availability.
- [x] DynamoDB Global Tables = 5 nines.
- [x] Lambda = 99.95%; API Gateway = 99.95%; Route 53 = 100% (with health checks).

### 🔹 Formulas to Memorize
- [x] EC2 monthly = rate × 24 × 30 × N.
- [x] Lambda = (requests × $0.20/M) + (GB-s × $0.0000166667).
- [x] System availability (independent components) = $1 - \prod(1 - A_i)$.

---

> ✅ **Final Exam Tip:** In any KTU Module 3 answer on AWS Services, always structure your answer as:
> 1. **Definition (1–2 lines)**
> 2. **Category / Classification (1 line)**
> 3. **Key Features (3–4 bullet points)**
> 4. **Use Case (1 real-world example)**
> 5. **Comparison (if asked) — use a table**
> 6. **Diagram (if question is for 7+ marks)**
>
> This 6-step template **guarantees full marks** under KTU's 2024 outcome-based evaluation pattern.

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — AWS Ecosystem

The **Amazon Web Services (AWS) Ecosystem** is a comprehensive, evolving cloud computing platform provided by Amazon, comprising more than **240+ fully featured services** delivered from a global network of data centers. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Cloud Infrastructure and Systems*, the AWS ecosystem is understood as an integrated set of **on-demand compute, storage, database, networking, analytics, machine learning, security, and developer tools** that operate over a shared, elastic, and metered infrastructure.

In formal cloud-engineering terms, AWS implements the **NIST SP 500-292 Reference Architecture** of cloud computing — providing IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS service models across a globally distributed, virtualized resource pool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Board-ready):** AWS is a hyperscale public cloud platform that offers secure, resizable compute capacity, services, and APIs over the open Internet, billed on a pay-per-use metered model governed by Service Level Agreements (SLAs).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The AWS Power Grid"

Imagine AWS as the **national electricity power grid** of a country:
- The **power stations** spread across the country are the **AWS Regions** — each independently operational and geographically isolated.
- Each power station contains **multiple sub-stations** — these are the **Availability Zones (AZs)**, providing redundancy within a region.
- **Local transformers** in towns correspond to **Edge Locations / CloudFront Points of Presence (PoPs)**, delivering content close to consumers.
- **Customers (you)** simply plug in devices (EC2 instances, S3 buckets) and pay only for the electricity (compute/storage) consumed.
- The **AWS Management Console** is the smart meter + remote control you use to switch appliances on/off, monitor consumption, and scale usage.

Just as you do not worry about *how* the grid generates electricity, AWS abstracts away physical infrastructure — you only consume resources through APIs/Console.

### 1.3 The Six Pillars of the AWS Ecosystem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **AWS Well-Architected Framework** which organizes services into six pillars:

| Pillar | Functional Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Operational Excellence** | Run & monitor systems to deliver business value (CloudWatch, CloudTrail) |
| **Security** | Protect information, systems, assets (IAM, KMS, GuardDuty) |
| **Reliability** | Recover from failures, meet demand (Multi-AZ, Auto Scaling) |
| **Performance Efficiency** | Use IT resources efficiently (Right-sizing, Serverless) |
| **Cost Optimization** | Deliver value at lowest price (Spot, Reserved, Savings Plans) |
| **Sustainability** | Minimize environmental impact (Region selection, efficient code) |

### 1.4 Service Categories at a Glance

AWS services are classified into major domains:

- **Compute:** EC2, Lambda, ECS, EKS, Fargate, Lightsail
- **Storage:** S3, EBS, EFS, FSx, S3 Glacier
- **Database:** RDS, DynamoDB, Aurora, Redshift, ElastiCache
- **Networking:** VPC, Route 53, CloudFront, Direct Connect, ELB
- **Security, Identity & Compliance:** IAM, Cognito, KMS, WAF, Shield
- **Management & Governance:** CloudWatch, CloudTrail, Config, Trusted Advisor
- **Analytics & AI/ML:** Athena, EMR, SageMaker, Rekognition

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The AWS Global Infrastructure — Layered Architecture

The AWS infrastructure is **hierarchically organized** from broad to granular:

**Layer 1 — AWS Regions**
A **Region** is a physical geographical location in the world where AWS clusters data centers. Each Region is a completely independent, isolated geographic area.
- Currently **32+ launched Regions** worldwide (e.g., `ap-south-1` for Mumbai, `us-east-1` for N. Virginia).
- Each Region has a unique code following the pattern `<area>-<direction>-<number>`.
- Services are **region-scoped** by default; data does not leave a region unless explicitly configured.

**Layer 2 — Availability Zones (AZs)**
Each Region contains **multiple Availability Zones (typically 3, minimum 2, maximum 6)**. An AZ is one or more **discrete data centers** with redundant power, networking, and connectivity, housed in separate facilities.
- AZs are physically separated by **tens of kilometers** (≈ 100 km) to avoid correlated failures.
- They are interconnected with **low-latency, high-bandwidth, redundant fiber-optic private links**.
- AZ designator examples: `ap-south-1a`, `ap-south-1b`, `ap-south-1c`.

**Layer 3 — Edge Locations (PoPs)**
**Points of Presence (PoPs)** are AWS data centers located in hundreds of cities globally, used exclusively for content delivery via **Amazon CloudFront** (CDN) and DNS via **Route 53**. They are smaller than AZs and exist to **minimize latency** to end users.

**Layer 4 — Local Zones & Wavelength**
- **Local Zones:** Bring AWS compute, storage, database closer to large population centers (e.g., Las Vegas, Boston).
- **Wavelength:** Embeds AWS infrastructure at telecom 5G carrier edge for ultra-low-latency mobile apps.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory hook:** *Region → AZ → Edge Location* = *Country → City → Local Outlet*

### 2.2 The AWS Service Model Mapping (NIST Reference)

| Service Model | AWS Equivalent | Student-Useful Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | EC2, EBS, VPC | Renting an empty flat — you bring your own furniture (OS, software) |
| **PaaS** | Elastic Beanstalk, RDS, Lambda | Renting a furnished flat — you only move in |
| **SaaS** | WorkMail, Chime, Amazon Connect | Staying in a hotel — everything is managed |
| **FaaS (Serverless)** | AWS Lambda | Pay-per-burn gas stove — you pay only when you cook |

### 2.3 AWS Shared Responsibility Model — *Crucial KTU Question*

AWS operates under a **shared responsibility model** that divides security duties:

$$ \text{Total Security} = \text{AWS Responsibility} + \text{Customer Responsibility} $$

- **AWS is responsible for "Security OF the Cloud"**: physical data centers, hardware, networking, hypervisor, managed-service internals.
- **Customer is responsible for "Security IN the Cloud"**: IAM policies, data encryption, OS patching (for EC2), network ACLs, security groups.

### 2.4 AWS Pricing Models (Cost Pillar)

AWS offers multiple pricing models to optimize costs:

$$\text{Total Cost} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Resource}_i \times \text{Unit Price}_i \times \text{Usage Duration}_i)$$

- **On-Demand:** Pay by the second/hour, no commitment. Highest cost, maximum flexibility.
- **Reserved Instances (RI):** 1- or 3-year commitment, up to **72% discount**.
- **Savings Plans:** Flexible pricing model with 1-3 year commitment, up to **66% discount**.
- **Spot Instances:** Bid for spare capacity, up to **90% discount**, but can be terminated.
- **Dedicated Hosts:** Physical EC2 server dedicated to your use (compliance).

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Term | Boundary / Limit | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Region Count | **32+** launched regions globally | Each region is independent | Geographic isolation |
| AZs per Region | Min **2**, Max **6** (typical 3) | AZs in same region: < 10 ms latency | High availability design |
| S3 Object Limit | Up to **5 TB** per object | Multipart upload needed > 5 GB | Large file storage |
| S3 Durability | **99.999999999%** (11 9's) | Cross-region replication for DR | Backup & archival |
| EC2 Instance Types | 5 families (General, Compute, Memory, Storage, Accelerated) | Each family has multiple sizes | Workload matching |
| Lambda Timeout | Max **15 minutes** | Max memory: 10,240 MB | Short-lived tasks |
| RDS Multi-AZ | **2 AZs** synchronous replication | Read replicas can span regions | Database HA |
| DynamoDB Item Size | Max **400 KB** per item | Larger items via S3 + pointer | NoSQL design |
| CloudFront Edge | **600+** PoPs across 90+ cities | TTL-driven caching | CDN |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory hook for AZ count:** *Always design for at least 2 AZs, but 3 AZs is the AWS recommendation.* This is a recurring KTU question.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The AWS ecosystem is used in:
- **Netflix:** Streams video globally using S3, CloudFront, Lambda.
- **NASA:** Processes Mars Rover imagery using S3, EC2, Lambda.
- **Airbnb:** Uses DynamoDB, RDS, Redshift for booking infrastructure.
- **Aadhaar (UIDAI):** Leverages AWS for biometric data processing at scale.

This shows AWS is not a tool — it is a **digital operating system for the modern internet**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Architecture Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem — Cost Calculation for an EC2 Workload

> **Problem (KTU pattern):** A KTU student runs an **EC2 On-Demand m5.large instance** for **730 hours/month** in the `ap-south-1` (Mumbai) region. The hourly rate is **₹6.37/hour**. Calculate the monthly bill. If the student switches to a **1-year No-Upfront Reserved Instance** at **₹3.50/hour**, what is the **monthly savings in INR and percentage**?

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters given:**

$$\text{Rate}_{\text{OnDemand}} = ₹6.37/\text{hour}$$
$$\text{Rate}_{\text{Reserved}} = ₹3.50/\text{hour}$$
$$\text{Hours per month} = 730 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate On-Demand Monthly Cost:**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{OnDemand}} = \text{Rate}_{\text{OnDemand}} \times \text{Hours}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{OnDemand}} = 6.37 \times 730$$

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{OnDemand}} = ₹4,650.10$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Reserved Instance Monthly Cost:**

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{Reserved}} = \text{Rate}_{\text{Reserved}} \times \text{Hours}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{Reserved}} = 3.50 \times 730$$

$$\text{Cost}_{\text{Reserved}} = ₹2,555.00$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Absolute Savings:**

$$\text{Savings}_{₹} = \text{Cost}_{\text{OnDemand}} - \text{Cost}_{\text{Reserved}}$$

$$\text{Savings}_{₹} = 4650.10 - 2555.00 = ₹2,095.10$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Percentage Savings:**

$$\text{Savings}_{\%} = \left( \frac{\text{Savings}_{₹}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{OnDemand}}} \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{Savings}_{\%} = \left( \frac{2095.10}{4650.10} \right) \times 100 \approx 45.05\%$$

**Final Result:** Monthly savings = **₹2,095.10**, which is **45.05%** reduction.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU marker value: Always show units (₹, hours) and write the formula before substitution. This is a non-negotiable examiner expectation.

### 3.2 Analytical Problem — S3 Storage Class Cost Optimization

> **Problem:** A startup stores **10 TB of data** in S3 Standard. The first 50 TB in `ap-south-1` costs **₹1.50/GB/month**. After 90 days, the data is rarely accessed. Recommend transitioning to **S3 Standard-IA** at **₹0.95/GB/month**. Calculate:
> (a) Monthly cost on S3 Standard
> (b) Monthly cost on S3 Standard-IA
> (c) Annual savings

**Step 1 — Convert TB to GB:**

$$10 \text{ TB} = 10 \times 1024 \text{ GB} = 10{,}240 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 2 — S3 Standard monthly cost:**

$$\text{Cost}_{S3} = 10240 \text{ GB} \times ₹1.50/\text{GB}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{S3} = ₹15,360.00 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 3 — S3 Standard-IA monthly cost:**

$$\text{Cost}_{IA} = 10240 \text{ GB} \times ₹0.95/\text{GB}$$

$$\text{Cost}_{IA} = ₹9,728.00 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 4 — Annual savings calculation:**

$$\text{Annual Savings} = (\text{Cost}_{S3} - \text{Cost}_{IA}) \times 12$$

$$\text{Annual Savings} = (15360 - 9728) \times 12$$

$$\text{Annual Savings} = 5632 \times 12 = ₹67,584.00$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — AWS Service Selection Decision Tree

| Requirement | Service to Select | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Lift-and-shift traditional app to cloud | **EC2** | Full OS control → IaaS model |
| Stateless event-driven function (< 15 min) | **Lambda** | Serverless, pay-per-invocation |
| Object storage for images, videos, backups | **S3** | 11 9's durability, unlimited objects |
| Block storage attached to single EC2 | **EBS** | Low-latency block device |
| Shared file system across multiple EC2 (Linux) | **EFS** | NFS-based, multi-AZ |
| Relational DB with auto-failover | **RDS Multi-AZ** | Synchronous replication |
| Millisecond latency, key-value, NoSQL | **DynamoDB** | Single-digit-ms performance |
| Global content delivery (low latency video) | **CloudFront + S3** | CDN edge caching |
| DNS resolution and health checks | **Route 53** | Authoritative DNS service |
| Centralized user authentication | **AWS IAM + Cognito** | Identity federation |
| Auto-scaling group based on CPU | **EC2 + Auto Scaling + CloudWatch** | Reactive elasticity |
| Data warehouse analytics (petabyte scale) | **Redshift** | Columnar OLAP |
| Serverless real-time stream processing | **Kinesis Data Streams + Lambda** | Real-time analytics |
| Container orchestration | **EKS (Kubernetes) or ECS** | Managed container service |

### 3.4 Architectural Reasoning — Why Multiple AZs?

**Mathematical justification for Multi-AZ deployment:**

If the failure probability of a single AZ per year is $P_{AZ}$ and AZs are independent:

$$P_{\text{all AZs fail}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{AZ_i} = (P_{AZ})^n$$

For $n = 3$ AZs and $P_{AZ} = 0.01$ (1%):

$$P_{\text{all three fail}} = (0.01)^3 = 0.000001 = 10^{-6}$$

This means **1 in 1,000,000 years** the system fails — a **six-nine (99.9999%)** reliability level, which is acceptable for mission-critical systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **probability multiplication principle** is often asked in KTU as a numerical question. Memorize the logic, not just the number.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
> **Q1.** Define an AWS Region and an Availability Zone. Why are AZs interconnected with low-latency links?

**Model Answer (3 marks — for 60-80 words):**
An **AWS Region** is a separate geographic area containing multiple data centers, while an **Availability Zone (AZ)** is one or more discrete data centers within a Region, with independent power and networking. AZs are interconnected through **low-latency, high-bandwidth, redundant fiber-optic private links** to enable **synchronous replication, multi-AZ high availability, and fault-isolated deployments**. (3 marks: 1 def Region, 1 def AZ, 1 reason)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between AWS IAM, Cognito, and KMS with one-line functions.

**Model Answer (3 marks — for 60-80 words):**
- **IAM (Identity and Access Management):** Manages **users, groups, roles, and policies** for AWS resource access. (1 mark)
- **Cognito:** Provides **user sign-up, sign-in, and identity federation** for mobile and web applications. (1 mark)
- **KMS (Key Management Service):** Manages **encryption keys** for protecting data at rest and in transit. (1 mark)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Cloud Architecture Focus

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern]**
> **Q(A)(a)** [7 marks] — Explain the AWS Shared Responsibility Model with a clear diagram of responsibilities between AWS and the customer. Why is understanding this model critical for compliance?
>
> **Q(A)(b)** [7 marks] — A retail application requires 99.99% uptime. Design a **Multi-AZ deployment architecture** on AWS using EC2, RDS, and ELB. Justify each component's placement and explain how the system meets the SLA.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Shared Responsibility Model — 7 marks:**

| Layer | AWS Responsibility | Customer Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Data center security, hardware | — |
| **Network** | Routers, switches, fiber, hypervisor | VPC design, Security Groups, NACLs |
| **Compute** | Hypervisor patching | EC2 OS patching, AMI hardening |
| **Storage** | Hardware disk redundancy | Encryption (KMS), S3 bucket policy |
| **Database (RDS)** | Engine patching, Multi-AZ replication | DB credentials, parameter group tuning |
| **Application** | — | Code, dependency updates, WAF rules |
| **Identity** | IAM infrastructure | IAM user creation, MFA, password policy |
| **Data** | — | Customer-side encryption, backups |

It is **critical for compliance** because regulators (GDPR, HIPAA, RBI) require clear demarcation of duties. A customer assuming AWS patches their EC2 OS has a **compliance gap** — this is a common audit failure. (7 marks: 5 for table, 2 for compliance reasoning)

**(b) Multi-AZ Architecture — 7 marks:**

- **Region Selection:** `ap-south-1` (Mumbai) — 3 AZs available.
- **AZ Placement:**
  - EC2 instances (Web Tier) distributed across **AZ-a, AZ-b, AZ-c** behind an **Application Load Balancer (ALB)**.
  - RDS PostgreSQL deployed in **Multi-AZ mode** with a **standby replica in a different AZ** (synchronous replication).
  - **ElastiCache** cluster with replicas across AZs for session storage.
- **Auto Scaling Group** with min=2, max=10, desired=4 instances, scaling on **CPU > 60%**.
- **S3 + CloudFront** for static assets and global caching.

**SLA Justification:** EC2 SLA is 99.99%, RDS Multi-AZ SLA is 99.95%, ALB SLA is 99.99%. Combined architecture achieves **> 99.99%** (multiplied availability). (7 marks: 2 placement, 2 components, 1 auto-scaling, 2 SLA calculation)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Multi-AZ ≠ Cross-Region.** Many students confuse Multi-AZ (same Region, different AZ) with **Disaster Recovery** (different Region). Multi-AZ tolerates data-center failure; Cross-Region tolerates city/natural disaster. Failing to mention this distinction costs 2-3 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Cost & Service Selection Focus

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern]**
> **Q(B)(a)** [7 marks] — Compare **EC2, Lambda, and Elastic Beanstalk** in terms of service model (IaaS/PaaS/FaaS), scalability, billing unit, and a suitable use case.
>
> **Q(B)(b)** [7 marks] — An organization runs a workload 24/7 for 3 years. The on-demand EC2 rate is **$0.10/hour**, a 3-year Reserved Instance (All Upfront) is **$0.04/hour**, and Savings Plan is **$0.06/hour**. Calculate the total cost in each model and recommend the optimal choice.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison — 7 marks:**

| Feature | EC2 | Lambda | Elastic Beanstalk |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Service Model** | IaaS | FaaS (Serverless) | PaaS |
| **Scalability** | Manual / Auto Scaling group | Automatic, concurrent invocations | Automatic via configuration |
| **Billing Unit** | Per-hour/second running | Per-100ms execution + invocations | Pay for underlying AWS resources |
| **Max Duration** | Unlimited | 15 minutes | Unlimited (EC2-based) |
| **Use Case** | Long-running web server, batch jobs | Image resizing, API backends, event handlers | Quick deploy of web apps without infra mgmt |
| **Management Overhead** | High (OS, patches) | Zero | Low (platform-managed) |

(7 marks: 1.5 each row × 4 rows, 1 mark synthesis)

**(b) Cost Calculation — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute total hours for 3 years:**

$$T = 3 \text{ years} \times 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours} = 26{,}280 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2 — On-Demand Cost:**

$$C_{OD} = 0.10 \times 26{,}280 = \$2{,}628.00$$

**Step 3 — 3-Year Reserved Instance Cost:**

$$C_{RI} = 0.04 \times 26{,}280 = \$1{,}051.20$$

**Step 4 — Savings Plan Cost:**

$$C_{SP} = 0.06 \times 26{,}280 = \$1{,}576.80$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and Recommendation:**

$$\text{Savings of RI over OD} = 2628 - 1051.20 = \$1{,}576.80 \quad (\approx 60\% \text{ savings})$$
$$\text{Savings of SP over OD} = 2628 - 1576.80 = \$1{,}051.20 \quad (\approx 40\% \text{ savings})$$

**Recommendation:** Since the workload is **predictable 24/7 for 3 years**, the **3-year All-Upfront Reserved Instance** offers the **maximum savings (60%)** and is the optimal choice. (7 marks: 1 step1, 1.5 step2, 1.5 step3, 1.5 step4, 1.5 step5)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Units & Time Conversions.** Many students forget to convert **years → days → hours** and directly multiply the rate by 3. This single error leads to a **3-mark deduction**. Always state: *"3 years = 3 × 365 × 24 = 26,280 hours"* explicitly.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for the AWS Ecosystem:**

- ☑ **Region** = geographical area; **AZ** = one or more data centers inside a Region; **Edge Location** = CDN/CloudFront PoP.
- ☑ AWS has **32+ Regions** and **600+ Edge Locations**; each Region has **2-6 AZs**.
- ☑ Region codes follow: `<area>-<direction>-<number>` (e.g., `ap-south-1`).
- ☑ **Shared Responsibility:** AWS = Security OF the Cloud; Customer = Security IN the Cloud.
- ☑ **Well-Architected Framework:** Six Pillars — Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability.
- ☑ **EC2** = IaaS compute; **Lambda** = FaaS (max 15 min); **Elastic Beanstalk** = PaaS.
- ☑ **S3** = object storage, **11 9's durability**, max **5 TB per object**.
- ☑ **EBS** = block storage for single EC2; **EFS** = shared file system for multiple EC2 (Linux).
- ☑ **RDS Multi-AZ** = synchronous replication, automatic failover; **Read Replicas** = asynchronous, used for read scaling.
- ☑ **DynamoDB** = NoSQL, single-digit-ms latency, max **400 KB per item**.
- ☑ **VPC** = private network; **Security Group** = stateful instance firewall; **NACL** = stateless subnet firewall.
- ☑ **Route 53** = DNS + health checks; **CloudFront** = CDN with edge caching.
- ☑ **IAM** = identity for AWS users/services; **Cognito** = identity for end users of apps; **KMS** = encryption key management.
- ☑ **Pricing Models:** On-Demand (highest, flexible), Reserved (up to 72% off, commit), Savings Plans (up to 66% off, flexible commit), Spot (up to 90% off, interruptible).
- ☑ **Availability Multiplication Rule:** Independent AZs multiply reliability: $(P_{AZ})^n$.
- ☑ **CloudWatch** = monitoring; **CloudTrail** = API audit log; **Trusted Advisor** = cost/security optimization.
- ☑ **VPC Peering** = two VPCs in same or different regions; **Transit Gateway** = hub for many VPCs.
- ☑ **WAF** = Layer-7 firewall; **Shield** = DDoS protection (Standard free, Advanced paid).
- ☑ **Container Services:** **ECS** (AWS-native), **EKS** (managed Kubernetes), **Fargate** (serverless containers).
- ☑ **Athena** = serverless SQL on S3; **EMR** = managed Hadoop/Spark; **Redshift** = data warehouse; **Kinesis** = real-time streaming.
- ☑ **One-line definitions are mandatory** for Part A — write definition, expand abbreviation, give example in a single line.

> **End of AWS Ecosystem Module 3 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of AWS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTURE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison of AWS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cloud Service Providers (CSPs)** are organizations that offer on-demand, scalable computing resources—including servers, storage, databases, networking, software, analytics, and intelligence—over the public internet (the "cloud") on a pay-as-you-go basis. The comparison of cloud providers involves a systematic evaluation of their service portfolios, pricing models, global infrastructure footprint, security frameworks, SLAs (Service Level Agreements), and ecosystem maturity.

The "Big Three" hyperscale cloud providers dominating the global market are:
1. **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** — Pioneered IaaS in **2006** with the launch of S3 and EC2.
2. **Microsoft Azure** — Launched in **2010** as "Windows Azure", rebranded to "Microsoft Azure" in **2014**.
3. **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** — Officially launched in **2011**, evolving from internal Google infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 / 2024 scheme, students are expected to develop the ability to **analyze, compare, and select** an appropriate cloud platform for given workload requirements. This comparison topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO3** of PECCT631: *"Compare and contrast public cloud platforms and choose appropriate services."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Power Utilities"

Imagine three electricity utilities supplying power to a vast industrial city:

| Provider | Analogy |
|---|---|
| **AWS** | The **oldest, largest power grid** — wires everywhere, the widest variety of plugs, sockets, and appliances. Slightly more expensive, but unrivaled in coverage and ecosystem depth. |
| **Microsoft Azure** | The power grid **naturally integrated with corporate offices and Windows-based factories**. Best when your business already runs on Microsoft software. |
| **GCP** | The **newest, smartest grid** — built by data scientists for data scientists. The cheapest per unit and best at big-data and AI workloads. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** No single provider is "the best". The choice depends on the workload type, existing technology stack, budget, geographic presence, and compliance requirements of the organization. This is a **strategic architectural decision**, not a technical one alone.

## 1.3 Key Comparison Dimensions

For a rigorous KTU-level comparison, we evaluate providers across **six canonical dimensions**:

1. **Compute Services** (Virtual Machines, Containers, Serverless)
2. **Storage Services** (Object, Block, File, Archival)
3. **Database Services** (Relational, NoSQL, Data Warehouse)
4. **Networking Services** (Virtual Private Cloud, Load Balancing, CDN)
5. **AI/ML & Analytics Services**
6. **Pricing, SLA, and Global Footprint**

The standard service metric used to compare uptime guarantees is the **SLA percentage**, which we denote mathematically as:

$$S_{annual\_downtime} = (1 - SLA) \times 365 \times 24 \quad \text{[hours per year]}$$

For example, an SLA of **99.99%** ("four nines") yields:

$$S_{annual\_downtime} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 8760 = 0.876 \text{ hours/year} \approx 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Cloud Provider Comparison

Cloud computing operates on a **shared responsibility model**. The provider secures the infrastructure ("of the cloud"), while the customer secures their data and configurations ("in the cloud"). Comparing providers therefore requires examining both layers.

From a KTU examination perspective, comparison questions test the student's ability to:
- **Map equivalent services** across providers (e.g., AWS EC2 ↔ Azure VMs ↔ GCP Compute Engine).
- **Justify selection** based on quantitative metrics (SLA, pricing, latency).
- **Identify trade-offs** (vendor lock-in, ecosystem maturity, cost optimization).

## 2.2 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all the **mathematically verifiable metrics** that KTU examiners expect students to memorize for cloud comparison problems. Pay special attention to SLA → downtime conversions.

| # | Metric / Formula | Definition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Annual Downtime} = (1 - SLA) \times 8760$ | Converts SLA % to hours/year of permissible downtime | SLA comparison |
| 2 | $\text{Effective Cost} = P \times t \times u$ | $P$ = price/hr, $t$ = hours, $u$ = utilization (0 to 1) | TCO calculation |
| 3 | $\text{Storage Cost} = S \times c \times 30$ | $S$ = GB stored, $c$ = cost/GB/month, 30 = days | Monthly bill estimation |
| 4 | $\text{Egress Cost} = D_{out} \times c_{out}$ | $D_{out}$ = GB transferred out, $c_{out}$ = $/GB | Data transfer pricing |
| 5 | $\text{Availability} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Mean Time Between Failures / Mean Time To Repair | Reliability engineering |
| 6 | $\text{Regions} \times \text{AZs} = \text{Global Footprint}$ | Global infrastructure resilience | Disaster recovery |
| 7 | $\text{Reserved Discount} = 1 - \frac{P_{RI}}{P_{OD}}$ | Savings ratio for reserved vs on-demand | Cost optimization |

## 2.3 Breakdown of the Six Comparison Dimensions

### Dimension 1: Compute Services
- **AWS**: **EC2** (Elastic Compute Cloud) — broadest instance variety, **750+** instance types.
- **Azure**: **Virtual Machines** — deep integration with **Active Directory** and **Windows Server**.
- **GCP**: **Compute Engine** — pioneered **per-second billing** and **custom machine types**.

### Dimension 2: Storage Services
- **AWS**: **S3** (Simple Storage Service) — the de-facto industry standard for **object storage** with **99.999999999%** (eleven 9s) durability.
- **Azure**: **Blob Storage** — three tiers: Hot, Cool, Archive.
- **GCP**: **Cloud Storage** — four classes: Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive.

### Dimension 3: Database Services
- **AWS**: **RDS**, **DynamoDB**, **Aurora**, **Redshift**.
- **Azure**: **Azure SQL**, **Cosmos DB** (globally distributed multi-model).
- **GCP**: **Cloud SQL**, **Firestore**, **Spanner** (globally distributed, strongly consistent).

### Dimension 4: Networking
- **AWS**: **VPC**, **Route 53**, **CloudFront**, **Direct Connect**.
- **Azure**: **Virtual Network (VNet)**, **Azure DNS**, **Azure CDN**, **ExpressRoute**.
- **GCP**: **VPC**, **Cloud DNS**, **Cloud CDN**, **Cloud Interconnect**.

### Dimension 5: AI/ML & Analytics
- **AWS**: **SageMaker**, **Rekognition**, **Comprehend**, **Redshift ML**.
- **Azure**: **Azure Machine Learning**, **Cognitive Services**, **Synapse Analytics**.
- **GCP**: **Vertex AI**, **BigQuery** (serverless data warehouse), **AutoML**.

### Dimension 6: Pricing, SLA, and Global Footprint

| Metric | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Market Share (Q4 2024, Synergy Research)** | ~31% | ~24% | ~11% |
| **Number of Regions** | **33+** | **60+** | **40+** |
| **Availability Zones** | **100+** | **120+** | **120+** |
| **Compute SLA** | 99.99% | 99.99% (VMs w/ Availability Sets) | 99.99% |
| **Object Storage SLA** | 99.9% (S3 Standard) | 99.9% (Hot) | 99.9% (Standard) |
| **Billing Granularity** | Per-second (Linux) | Per-minute | **Per-second** |
| **Free Tier** | 12 months + Always Free | 12 months + Always Free | $300 credit (90 days) + Always Free |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this comparison matters in industry:** In a multi-cloud or hybrid-cloud deployment (a common KTU viva question), an architect may select **AWS S3** for primary storage (mature, 11 nines durability), **GCP BigQuery** for analytics (serverless, SQL-native), and **Azure AD** for identity (if the enterprise is Microsoft-centric). This is called a **"best-of-breed"** strategy, and it requires deep comparison knowledge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Comparison & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Master Service Mapping Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most exam-relevant** table for KTU. Memorize the row-by-row service equivalence. Part A questions (3 marks) frequently ask: *"What is the Azure equivalent of AWS EC2?"* or *"Compare S3 and Blob Storage."*

| Service Category | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compute (IaaS)** | EC2 | Virtual Machines | Compute Engine |
| **Container Orchestration** | ECS / EKS | AKS | GKE |
| **Serverless Functions** | Lambda | Azure Functions | Cloud Functions |
| **Object Storage** | **S3** | Blob Storage | Cloud Storage |
| **Block Storage** | EBS | Managed Disks | Persistent Disk |
| **Archival Storage** | Glacier | Archive Blob | Archive Storage |
| **Relational DB** | RDS / Aurora | Azure SQL | Cloud SQL / Spanner |
| **NoSQL DB** | DynamoDB | Cosmos DB | Firestore / Bigtable |
| **Data Warehouse** | Redshift | Synapse Analytics | **BigQuery** |
| **Virtual Network** | VPC | VNet | VPC |
| **DNS** | Route 53 | Azure DNS | Cloud DNS |
| **CDN** | CloudFront | Azure CDN | Cloud CDN |
| **Identity (IAM)** | IAM | Active Directory / Entra ID | Cloud IAM |
| **Auto Scaling** | Auto Scaling Groups | VM Scale Sets | Managed Instance Groups |
| **Load Balancer** | ELB (ALB/NLB/GLB) | Load Balancer | Cloud Load Balancing |
| **Monitoring** | CloudWatch | Azure Monitor | Cloud Monitoring (formerly Stackdriver) |
| **IaC Tool** | CloudFormation | ARM Templates / Bicep | Deployment Manager / Terraform |
| **Notification** | SNS / SQS | Service Bus / Event Grid | Pub/Sub |
| **Key Management** | KMS | Key Vault | Cloud KMS |
| **DNS & Traffic** | Route 53 + CloudFront | Traffic Manager + Front Door | Cloud DNS + Cloud CDN |

## 3.2 Mathematical Worked Example — SLA Downtime Calculation

This is a classic **14-mark Part B** sub-question type. Let us solve it exhaustively, step by step.

**Problem:** Compare the annual permissible downtime of an **AWS EC2** instance (SLA = **99.99%**) with an **Azure VM** deployed in an **Availability Set** (SLA = **99.99%**) and a **GCP Compute Engine** instance (SLA = **99.99%**). Which provider offers the strictest guarantee?

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

We use the canonical SLA-to-downtime formula:

$$T_{down} = (1 - \text{SLA}) \times 365 \times 24 \quad \text{[hours per year]}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute for AWS EC2.**

$$T_{AWS} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 365 \times 24$$

Calculate $(1 - 0.9999)$:

$$1 - 0.9999 = 0.0001$$

Calculate $365 \times 24$:

$$365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}$$

Therefore:

$$T_{AWS} = 0.0001 \times 8760 = 0.876 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to minutes.**

$$0.876 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ minutes/hour} = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute for Azure VM (Availability Set).**

$$T_{Azure} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 8760 = 0.876 \text{ hours/year} = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute for GCP Compute Engine.**

$$T_{GCP} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 8760 = 0.876 \text{ hours/year} = 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

**Step 6 — Compare and conclude.**

All three providers offer an **identical SLA of 99.99%**, resulting in a permissible downtime of **52.56 minutes per year**. None is strictly stricter than the others at the *compute* level. However, at the **object storage** level, the comparison diverges significantly (e.g., S3 offers **11 nines** of *durability*, which is a different metric from availability).

## 3.3 Exhaustive Service-Level Comparison

### 3.3.1 Compute: EC2 vs Azure VMs vs Compute Engine

| Sub-attribute | AWS EC2 | Azure VM | GCP Compute Engine |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Instance Types** | 750+ (T, M, C, R, P, G, etc.) | 600+ (B, D, E, F, M, etc.) | 400+ (E2, N2, M2, C2, etc.) |
| **Billing Granularity** | Per-second (after first minute) | Per-minute | **Per-second** (minimum 1 min) |
| **Live Migration** | Yes (for hardware maintenance) | Yes (with maintenance config) | Yes (automatic) |
| **Spot/Preemptible** | Spot Instances (~90% discount) | Spot VMs | Spot VMs (up to 91% discount) |
| **Bare Metal** | Yes (i3.metal, m5.metal, etc.) | Yes (M-series) | Yes |
| **Custom Machine Types** | No (use closest fit) | No (use closest fit) | **Yes** (custom vCPU & RAM) |

### 3.3.2 Object Storage: S3 vs Blob Storage vs Cloud Storage

| Sub-attribute | AWS S3 | Azure Blob | GCP Cloud Storage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Classes** | Standard, IA, One Zone-IA, Glacier IR, Glacier, Glacier Deep Archive | Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive | Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive |
| **Durability** | **99.999999999% (11 nines)** | 99.999999999% (11 nines) | 99.999999999% (11 nines) |
| **Availability** | 99.9% (Standard) | 99.9% (Hot) | 99.9% (Standard) |
| **Max Object Size** | **5 TB** | 4.75 TB (block blob) | 5 TB |
| **Egress Cost** | $0.09/GB (first 10 TB) | $0.08/GB | **$0.12/GB** (highest) |
| **Free Egress** | 100 GB/month | 100 GB/month | 100 GB/month |

### 3.3.3 Database: RDS/Aurora vs Azure SQL/Cosmos DB vs Cloud SQL/Spanner

| Sub-attribute | AWS RDS / Aurora | Azure SQL / Cosmos DB | GCP Cloud SQL / Spanner |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Managed RDBMS** | Aurora (MySQL/PostgreSQL) | Azure SQL (MSSQL-compatible) | Cloud SQL (MySQL/PostgreSQL) |
| **Global Distribution** | Aurora Global Database | **Cosmos DB** (multi-master, 5 consistency levels) | **Spanner** (globally distributed, strongly consistent) |
| **Consistency Model** | Eventual (default) | Tunable (5 levels) | **Strong** (external consistency) |
| **Serverless Option** | Aurora Serverless v2 | Azure SQL DB Serverless | Cloud SQL (limited) |
| **Multi-region Writes** | Aurora Global (async) | Cosmos DB (sync) | Spanner (sync, TrueTime API) |

### 3.3.4 Networking and CDN

| Sub-attribute | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Private Network** | VPC (with subnets, route tables, NACLs) | VNet (with subnets, NSGs, ASGs) | VPC (with subnets, firewall rules) |
| **Cross-region Peering** | VPC Peering / Transit Gateway | VNet Peering / Virtual WAN | VPC Peering / Network Connectivity Center |
| **CDN** | CloudFront (300+ PoPs) | Azure CDN / Front Door | Cloud CDN (100+ PoPs) |
| **Hybrid Connectivity** | Direct Connect | ExpressRoute | Cloud Interconnect |
| **DNS** | Route 53 (100% SLA) | Azure DNS | Cloud DNS (100% SLA) |

### 3.3.5 AI/ML and Analytics

| Sub-attribute | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Managed ML Platform** | SageMaker | Azure Machine Learning | **Vertex AI** |
| **AutoML** | SageMaker Autopilot | Azure AutoML | Vertex AI AutoML |
| **Data Warehouse** | Redshift | Synapse Analytics | **BigQuery** (serverless, columnar) |
| **Streaming Analytics** | Kinesis | Stream Analytics | Dataflow (Apache Beam) |
| **Big Data Processing** | EMR (Hadoop/Spark) | HDInsight | Dataproc |

### 3.3.6 Pricing Model Comparison

| Pricing Concept | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **On-Demand** | Per-second/hour | Per-minute/hour | Per-second/hour |
| **Reserved (1/3 yr)** | Up to 75% discount | Up to 72% discount | Up to 70% discount |
| **Spot/Preemptible** | Up to 90% off | Up to 90% off | Up to 91% off |
| **Sustained Use Discount** | No (use RIs) | **Yes** (automatic) | **Yes** (automatic) |
| **Free Tier** | 12-month + Always Free | 12-month + Always Free | $300 credit (90 days) |
| **Marketplace** | AWS Marketplace (largest) | Azure Marketplace | GCP Marketplace (smallest) |

## 3.4 Market Share and Adoption (as of Q4 2024)

According to **Synergy Research Group** and **Canalys** quarterly reports:

$$M_{share} = \frac{R_{provider}}{R_{global}} \times 100\%$$

where $R_{provider}$ is the provider's quarterly cloud infrastructure revenue and $R_{global}$ is the total global cloud infrastructure revenue.

| Rank | Provider | Market Share | Q4 2024 Revenue (approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **AWS** | **~31%** | ~$24 billion |
| 2 | **Azure** | **~24%** | ~$19 billion |
| 3 | **GCP** | **~11%** | ~$9 billion |
| 4 | Others (Alibaba, IBM, Oracle) | ~34% | ~$26 billion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Note:** AWS leads in market share and ecosystem breadth, Azure leads in enterprise integration, and GCP leads in data analytics and AI/ML innovation. This is a **frequently asked 3-mark conceptual question** in KTU exams.

## 3.5 Decision Framework: How to Choose?

For KTU viva and exam questions that ask *"Which cloud provider should an organization choose?"*, use this **decision tree logic**:

1. **If existing infrastructure is Microsoft-centric** → Choose **Azure** (native AD, Exchange, Teams integration).
2. **If the workload is big-data / AI / ML heavy** → Choose **GCP** (BigQuery, Vertex AI, TensorFlow origins).
3. **If the organization needs the broadest service catalog, mature ecosystem, and largest community** → Choose **AWS** (default safe choice).
4. **If cost optimization is the primary concern** → Compare **GCP's** automatic sustained-use discounts and per-second billing.
5. **If the organization requires multi-cloud / hybrid** → Use **Terraform** (provider-agnostic IaC) and **Kubernetes** (portable containers).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cloud Provider Comparison Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram presents a **block-level functional architecture** showing the three major cloud providers and their core service pillars, drawn from a uniform, top-down comparison perspective.

```mermaid
graph TD
    UserApp["Enterprise Application Workload"]
    UserApp --> DecisionNode{"Selection Criteria"}
    
    DecisionNode -->|Microsoft Ecosystem| AzureStack["Microsoft Azure"]
    DecisionNode -->|Data & AI Workload| GCPStack["Google Cloud Platform"]
    DecisionNode -->|Broadest Catalog| AWSStack["Amazon Web Services"]
    
    AzureStack --> AzCompute["Compute: Virtual Machines, AKS, Azure Functions"]
    AzureStack --> AzStorage["Storage: Blob, Disk, Files"]
    AzureStack --> AzDB["Database: Azure SQL, Cosmos DB"]
    AzureStack --> AzNetwork["Networking: VNet, ExpressRoute, Front Door"]
    AzureStack --> AzAI["AI/ML: Azure ML, Cognitive Services"]
    
    GCPStack --> GCPCompute["Compute: Compute Engine, GKE, Cloud Functions"]
    GCPStack --> GCPStorage["Storage: Cloud Storage, Persistent Disk"]
    GCPStack --> GCPDB["Database: Cloud SQL, Spanner, Firestore"]
    GCPStack --> GCPNetwork["Networking: VPC, Cloud Interconnect, Cloud CDN"]
    GCPStack --> GCPAI["AI/ML: Vertex AI, BigQuery, AutoML"]
    
    AWSStack --> AWSCompute["Compute: EC2, ECS/EKS, Lambda"]

    AWSStack --> AWSAI["AI/ML: SageMaker, Rekognition, Redshift ML"]
    
    AzCompute -.Equivalents.- AWSCompute
```

## 4.2 Service Equivalence Mapping (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid

    subgraph AWS["AWS Service Family"]
        A1["EC2"]
        A2["S3"]

    end
```

## 4.3 Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid

    Start([New Cloud Workload]) --> Q1{Existing Tech Stack?}
    
    Q1 -->|Microsoft Centric| Q2{Primary Need?}
    Q1 -->|Open Source / Linux| Q3{Workload Type?}
    Q1 -->|No Existing Stack| Q4{Priority?}
    
    Q2 -->|AD, Exchange, Teams| AzureRec["Choose Azure - Native Integration"]
    Q3 -->|Big Data / AI / ML| GCPRec["Choose GCP - BigQuery & Vertex AI"]

    Q3 -->|Hybrid / Multi-Cloud| K8sRec["Choose K8s on any provider, prefer AWS or GCP"]
```
