<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question below carries **3 marks**. Model answers are precise, concise, and aligned with KTU valuation key expectations (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for technical accuracy, 1 mark for example or context).

---

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"List any three equivalent services of AWS, Azure, and GCP, and explain the role of one service in detail."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three equivalent services are:

1. **Compute Service**: AWS **EC2** ↔ Azure **Virtual Machines** ↔ GCP **Compute Engine** — all provide resizable virtual machines in the cloud.
2. **Object Storage**: AWS **S3** ↔ Azure **Blob Storage** ↔ GCP **Cloud Storage** — all offer highly durable, scalable object storage with **11 nines** (99.999999999%) durability.
3. **Serverless Function**: AWS **Lambda** ↔ Azure **Azure Functions** ↔ GCP **Cloud Functions** — all run event-driven code without provisioning servers.

**Detailed explanation (AWS S3):** Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) is an object storage service offering industry-leading **99.999999999% (11 nines) durability** and **99.9% availability** for the Standard tier. It supports objects up to **5 TB**, uses **bucket-based** organization, integrates with **IAM** for access control, and offers multiple storage classes (Standard, IA, Glacier) for cost optimization. It is widely used for backup, data archiving, big-data analytics, static website hosting, and content distribution. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between AWS, Azure, and GCP based on market share, billing granularity, and global infrastructure."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Market Share (Q4 2024)** | ~31% (Leader) | ~24% | ~11% |
| **Billing Granularity** | Per-second (Linux VMs) | Per-minute | Per-second |
| **Global Regions** | 33+ regions, 100+ AZs | 60+ regions, 120+ AZs | 40+ regions, 120+ AZs |
| **Year of Launch** | 2006 | 2010 | 2011 |
| **Best Suited For** | Broadest catalog, mature | Microsoft ecosystems | Data analytics & AI/ML |

AWS leads in market maturity and ecosystem, Azure offers the widest global reach and Microsoft integration, while GCP excels in per-second billing and data-centric workloads. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) regulations, each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (e.g., (a) and (b)). The student is given an **internal choice** — they must answer **either Question A or Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) Compare AWS, Azure, and GCP in terms of compute services. Mention at least four key points for each provider.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### AWS EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud)
1. **Launched in 2006** — the pioneer of public cloud IaaS.
2. Offers the **broadest instance catalog** with **750+ instance types** across families like T, M, C, R, P, G, and Inf.
3. **Per-second billing** for Linux instances (after the first minute), making it cost-effective for short workloads.
4. Provides **Spot Instances** with up to **90% discount**, ideal for fault-tolerant batch jobs.
5. Supports **bare-metal instances** (e.g., `i3.metal`) for workloads requiring direct hardware access.
6. Integrates with **Auto Scaling Groups** and **Elastic Load Balancer (ELB)** for elasticity.

#### Azure Virtual Machines
1. Deep integration with **Active Directory (Entra ID)** for enterprise authentication and role-based access.
2. **D-series** (General Purpose), **E-series** (Memory-Optimized), **F-series** (Compute-Optimized), **M-series** (Massive Memory, up to **4 TB RAM**).
3. **Per-minute billing** granularity.
4. **Spot VMs** provide up to **90% discount** for interruptible workloads.
5. **Azure Dedicated Hosts** for compliance-bound single-tenant hardware.
6. **VM Scale Sets** enable autoscaling and load balancing.

#### GCP Compute Engine
1. Pioneered **per-second billing** with a **1-minute minimum**.
2. **Custom machine types** allow arbitrary vCPU-to-memory ratios (e.g., 6 vCPUs, 22 GB RAM) for perfect sizing.
3. **Live migration** for hardware maintenance without VM downtime.
4. **Preemptible VMs** (up to 91% off) and **Spot VMs** for batch processing.
5. **Sustained-use discounts** apply **automatically** without commitment, unlike AWS which requires Reserved Instances.
6. Native integration with **GKE (Google Kubernetes Engine)**, the most mature managed Kubernetes offering.

**Comparative Summary Table (drawn for clarity in valuation):**

| Feature | AWS EC2 | Azure VM | GCP Compute Engine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Launch Year | 2006 | 2010 | 2011 |
| Billing | Per-second | Per-minute | Per-second |
| Custom Machine Types | No | No | **Yes** |
| Auto Discounts | No (use RIs) | No (use RIs) | **Yes (SUD)** |
| Spot Discount | ~90% | ~90% | ~91% |

**(7 marks — 1 mark per provider per key point, plus 1 mark for the summary table)**

---

**(b) Compare AWS S3, Azure Blob Storage, and GCP Cloud Storage with respect to storage classes, durability, availability, and use cases.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | AWS S3 | Azure Blob Storage | GCP Cloud Storage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Classes** | Standard, Standard-IA, One Zone-IA, Glacier IR, Glacier, Deep Archive | Hot, Cool, Cold, Archive | Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive |
| **Durability** | **99.999999999% (11 nines)** | 99.999999999% (11 nines) | 99.999999999% (11 nines) |
| **Availability (Standard)** | 99.9% | 99.9% | 99.9% |
| **Min Storage Duration** | 30 days (IA), 90 days (Glacier) | 30 days (Cool), 180 days (Archive) | 30 days (Nearline), 90 days (Coldline), 365 days (Archive) |
| **Max Object Size** | 5 TB | 4.75 TB (block blob) | 5 TB |
| **Retrieval Latency** | Milliseconds (S3) to hours (Glacier) | Milliseconds to hours | Milliseconds to hours |
| **Best For** | General-purpose, archival, big data | Azure-integrated apps, backup | GCP analytics, multimedia |

**Use Cases:**
- **S3**: Industry-standard for data lakes, ML training datasets, static website hosting, and content distribution. Its **Glacier** classes make it the go-to for long-term archival.
- **Blob Storage**: Preferred in Microsoft-centric enterprises; integrates with **Azure Data Lake**, **Synapse Analytics**, and **Azure Backup**.
- **Cloud Storage**: Tightly coupled with **BigQuery** for serverless analytics; popular for **multi-regional** workloads due to GCP's strong global backbone.

**Key Distinction:** All three offer identical **11 nines durability**, but the cost of retrieval and minimum storage duration differ, affecting Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) significantly. **(7 marks — 2 marks for the table, 3 marks for the use cases, 2 marks for the distinction)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Discuss the global infrastructure, SLA, and pricing models of AWS, Azure, and GCP.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### Global Infrastructure
- **AWS**: Operates in **33+ geographic regions** and **100+ Availability Zones (AZs)**, plus **400+ Edge Locations** and **13+ Regional Edge Caches** for CloudFront CDN.
- **Azure**: Leads with **60+ regions**, more than any other provider, with **120+ AZs**. Strong presence in sovereign and government clouds (Azure Government, Azure Germany).
- **GCP**: Operates in **40+ regions** with **120+ AZs**, known for its private global fiber network (undersea cables).

#### SLA (Service Level Agreement) — Compute
All three providers offer **99.99%** SLA for single VM instances and **99.95%** for multi-AZ deployments. The permissible annual downtime for 99.99% SLA is:

$$T_{down} = (1 - 0.9999) \times 8760 = 0.876 \text{ hours/year} \approx 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}$$

| Provider | Compute SLA | Object Storage Availability | Database SLA |
|---|---|---|---|
| AWS | 99.99% (EC2 multi-region) | 99.9% (S3 Standard) | 99.95% (RDS Multi-AZ) |
| Azure | 99.99% (VM w/ Avail Set) | 99.9% (Hot Blob) | 99.99% (Azure SQL Business Critical) |
| GCP | 99.99% (Compute Engine) | 99.9% (Standard) | 99.99% (Cloud SQL HA) |

#### Pricing Models
- **AWS**: On-Demand, Reserved Instances (1-3 yr), Savings Plans, Spot Instances.
- **Azure**: On-Demand, Reserved VM Instances, Spot VMs, Azure Hybrid Benefit (license reuse from on-prem).
- **GCP**: On-Demand, Committed Use Discounts (CUDs), Spot VMs, **Sustained-Use Discounts (automatic)**.

**Distinctive Feature**: GCP is the only provider to offer **automatic sustained-use discounts** without requiring a long-term commitment. AWS is the only provider to offer **Savings Plans** (flexible across regions and families). Azure uniquely offers the **Hybrid Benefit** for reusing on-premises Windows/SQL Server licenses. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) Compare the database services (RDS vs Azure SQL vs Cloud SQL) and the AI/ML platforms (SageMaker vs Azure ML vs Vertex AI) offered by the three providers.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### Database Services Comparison

| Parameter | AWS RDS / Aurora | Azure SQL / Cosmos DB | GCP Cloud SQL / Spanner |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Managed RDBMS** | Aurora MySQL/PostgreSQL, RDS for SQL Server, Oracle, MariaDB | Azure SQL (MSSQL-compatible), Managed Instance | Cloud SQL (MySQL/PostgreSQL) |
| **Global Distribution** | Aurora Global Database (read replicas, async) | **Cosmos DB** (multi-region writes, 5 consistency levels) | **Spanner** (global, strongly consistent) |
| **Consistency** | Eventual (default) | Tunable: Strong, Bounded Staleness, Session, Consistent Prefix, Eventual | **External Consistency** (TrueTime API) |
| **Serverless** | Aurora Serverless v2 | Azure SQL DB Serverless | Limited |
| **Auto-Scaling** | Aurora Serverless v2 | Yes (Cosmos DB RU auto-scale) | Yes (Cloud SQL, with limits) |

#### AI/ML Platform Comparison

| Parameter | AWS SageMaker | Azure Machine Learning | GCP Vertex AI |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AutoML** | SageMaker Autopilot | Azure AutoML | Vertex AI AutoML |
| **Notebook IDE** | SageMaker Studio | Azure ML Studio | Vertex AI Workbench |
| **Managed Training** | Built-in algorithms + custom containers | AML Compute + AutoML | Custom training + AutoML |
| **Feature Store** | SageMaker Feature Store | Limited | **Vertex AI Feature Store** |
| **MLOps** | SageMaker Pipelines, Model Registry | Azure ML Pipelines | Vertex AI Pipelines |
| **Pre-trained APIs** | Rekognition, Comprehend, Polly, Translate | Cognitive Services (Vision, Speech, Language) | Vision AI, Speech-to-Text, Translation AI |
| **Origin Strength** | Broadest set of pre-trained models | Deep Office 365 integration | **TensorFlow origins, TPU access, BigQuery ML** |

**Conclusion:** AWS SageMaker has the most comprehensive feature set and largest community, Azure ML is best for enterprises already on Microsoft Power Platform, and **GCP Vertex AI** is the strongest choice for **deep learning at scale** (TPUs) and **analytics-driven ML** (BigQuery ML). **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not write only the names** of equivalent services without explaining the comparison parameters. Examiners explicitly look for **at least 3-4 comparison dimensions** (e.g., SLA, pricing, durability, scalability). Simply listing "S3 ↔ Blob ↔ Cloud Storage" will fetch only 1 of 7 marks.
> 2. **Always mention the SLA percentage AND the equivalent downtime** when comparing. Computing the downtime shows mathematical rigor and fetches full marks.
> 3. **Do not confuse availability with durability.** AWS S3's "11 nines" is **durability** (data not lost), not **availability** (service is up). Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 4. **Do not invent features.** If a service does not offer a particular feature (e.g., GCP *does* have custom machine types but AWS *does not*), state this factually. Hallucinating features loses 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Always include a comparison table** in 14-mark answers. The KTU valuation key allocates 2-3 marks specifically for a well-structured comparative table.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** (Read this 30 minutes before the exam):

- ✅ **Big Three Cloud Providers**: AWS (2006), Azure (2010), GCP (2011).
- ✅ **AWS Market Share**: ~31% (Leader). Azure ~24%, GCP ~11% (Q4 2024 data).
- ✅ **Compute Equivalence**: EC2 ↔ Azure VMs ↔ Compute Engine.
- ✅ **Object Storage Equivalence**: S3 ↔ Blob Storage ↔ Cloud Storage.
- ✅ **Serverless Equivalence**: Lambda ↔ Azure Functions ↔ Cloud Functions.
- ✅ **Container Orchestration**: EKS ↔ AKS ↔ GKE.
- ✅ **Data Warehouse**: Redshift ↔ Synapse Analytics ↔ **BigQuery** (serverless).
- ✅ **SLA Formula**: $T_{down} = (1 - SLA) \times 8760$ hours/year.
- ✅ **99.99% SLA = 52.56 minutes/year downtime**. 99.9% = 8.76 hours/year.
- ✅ **Object Storage Durability**: All three offer **99.999999999% (11 nines)**.
- ✅ **Object Storage Availability**: All three offer **99.9%** for the Standard tier.
- ✅ **Billing Granularity**: GCP pioneered per-second billing; Azure uses per-minute.
- ✅ **Reserved/Committed Discounts**: AWS (up to 75%), Azure (up to 72%), GCP (up to 70%).
- ✅ **Spot/Preemptible Discounts**: AWS (~90%), Azure (~90%), GCP (~91%).
- ✅ **Auto Discounts**: **GCP only** offers automatic sustained-use discounts.
- ✅ **Custom Machine Types**: **GCP only** allows arbitrary vCPU:RAM ratios.
- ✅ **Strong Global Consistency**: **GCP Spanner** (uses TrueTime API and atomic clocks).
- ✅ **TPU Access**: **GCP only** offers Tensor Processing Units for deep learning.
- ✅ **Enterprise AD Integration**: **Azure only** has native Active Directory/Entra ID.
- ✅ **Biggest Region Count**: **Azure** (60+ regions).
- ✅ **Biggest Service Catalog**: **AWS** (200+ services).
- ✅ **Data Analytics Leader**: **GCP** (BigQuery, serverless, SQL-native).
- ✅ **Availability**: Availability ≠ Durability. "11 nines" refers to **durability**.
- ✅ **Free Tier**: All three offer Always Free + 12-month free (GCP gives $300 credits).
- ✅ **Max Object Size**: S3 = **5 TB**, Blob = 4.75 TB, Cloud Storage = **5 TB**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Azure and Google Cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Azure and Google Cloud

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Microsoft Azure — Formal Academic Definition

**Microsoft Azure** is a public cloud computing platform (a *hyperscale* Infrastructure-as-a-Service / Platform-as-a-Service ecosystem) operated by Microsoft that provides on-demand compute, storage, networking, databases, analytics, AI/ML, DevOps, and identity services across **60+ geographic regions** worldwide, delivered through a unified management plane (Azure Portal, Azure CLI, Azure PowerShell, ARM templates, and Bicep).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *Microsoft Azure is a cloud computing service created by Microsoft for building, testing, deploying, and managing applications and services through Microsoft-managed data centers. It provides Software as a Service (SaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS), and Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS).*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Electric Grid Model"
Think of Microsoft Azure like the **electrical grid** in a city. Instead of building your own power plant (owning a server room), you simply plug into the grid and pay for what you consume (metered billing). Azure provides:
- **Generation plants** → Data centers (Azure Regions)
- **Transmission lines** → ExpressRoute / Virtual Networks
- **Outlets** → Service endpoints (VMs, App Services, Storage accounts)
- **Billing meter** → Azure Cost Management + Billing

## 1.2 Google Cloud Platform (GCP) — Formal Academic Definition

**Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** is a suite of public cloud computing services offered by Google, running on the same internal infrastructure that Google uses for its end-user products such as Google Search, Gmail, and YouTube. GCP provides IaaS, PaaS, and Serverless compute services along with Big Data, Machine Learning, and networking capabilities spanning **40+ regions** and **120+ zones**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Google Cloud Platform (GCP) is a portfolio of cloud computing services that runs on the same infrastructure that Google uses internally for its end-user products. It offers over 100 services including Compute Engine, App Engine, Kubernetes Engine, BigQuery, and Cloud Storage.*

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Network Model"
Imagine GCP as **Google Maps for your infrastructure**: just as Google Maps finds the fastest route using real-time traffic data, GCP's global private network (the **Google Cloud Premium Tier Network**) automatically routes your data via the lowest-latency path across continents, with built-in DDoS protection and **private fiber-optic backbone**.

## 1.3 Core Service Categories (Universal Across Both Platforms)

Both Azure and GCP share a common three-layer cloud service taxonomy:

| Layer | Full Form | Azure Example | GCP Example |
|-------|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure-as-a-Service | Azure Virtual Machines | Compute Engine (GCE) |
| **PaaS** | Platform-as-a-Service | Azure App Service | App Engine (GAE) |
| **SaaS** | Software-as-a-Service | Microsoft 365 | Google Workspace |

> [!NOTE]
> **SLA Benchmark:** Industry-standard cloud SLAs guarantee **99.9% (Three-Nines)** to **99.99% (Four-Nines)** uptime. Azure's VM SLA is **99.99%** for two or more instances in an Availability Set; GCP Compute Engine SLA is **99.99%** for instances across multiple zones.

## 1.4 Why Azure and GCP Matter for KTU Examination

- Both platforms account for **>40% of the global public cloud market share** (AWS ~32%, Azure ~23%, GCP ~11% per Gartner 2024).
- Azure is dominant in **enterprise / .NET / hybrid cloud** scenarios.
- GCP leads in **data analytics, AI/ML, and Kubernetes** (Google originated Kubernetes).
- The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to **compare architectural decisions**, **service mappings**, and **deployment patterns** between the two providers.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microsoft Azure — Architectural Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Azure Hierarchy of Organization

Azure's resource organization follows a strict **four-tier hierarchy** that every KTU student must memorize:

```
Management Groups  →  Subscriptions  →  Resource Groups  →  Resources
      (RBAC)              (Billing)        (Lifecycle)         (Services)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy Purpose:**
> - **Management Group:** Apply governance policies across multiple subscriptions.
> - **Subscription:** Billing boundary + Azure AD tenant boundary.
> - **Resource Group (RG):** Logical container for related resources sharing the same lifecycle.
> - **Resource:** The actual service instance (VM, Storage Account, etc.).

### 2.1.2 Azure Core Service Pillars

**(A) Azure Compute Services**
- **Azure Virtual Machines (VMs):** IaaS — Windows/Linux on-demand servers.
- **Azure App Service:** PaaS — Hosts web apps, REST APIs, and mobile backends.
- **Azure Functions:** Serverless — Event-driven code execution.
- **Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS):** Managed Kubernetes orchestration.
- **Azure Container Instances (ACI):** Serverless containers.

**(B) Azure Storage Services**
- **Blob Storage:** Object storage for unstructured data (images, videos, backups).
- **File Storage:** SMB-based managed file shares.
- **Queue Storage:** Messaging queue for decoupling application components.
- **Disk Storage:** Persistent block storage for VMs (Premium SSD, Standard HDD, Ultra Disk).

**(C) Azure Networking Services**
- **Virtual Network (VNet):** Private network isolation in Azure.
- **Azure Load Balancer:** Layer-4 (TCP/UDP) load distribution.
- **Application Gateway:** Layer-7 (HTTP/HTTPS) load balancing with WAF.
- **Azure Front Door:** Global Layer-7 load balancer with CDN.
- **ExpressRoute:** Private dedicated connection between on-premises and Azure.

**(D) Azure Identity & Security**
- **Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory):** Identity and access management.
- **Azure Key Vault:** Secrets, keys, and certificate management.
- **Azure Defender (formerly Azure Security Center):** Cloud workload protection.

### 2.1.3 Azure Deployment Models

| Model | Description | Use Case |
|-------|-------------|----------|
| **Public** | Resources on shared infrastructure | Web apps, SaaS workloads |
| **Private** | Dedicated isolated environment | Regulated industries (Banking, Govt.) |
| **Hybrid** | Combines on-premises with Azure | Legacy system integration |
| **Multi-Cloud** | Azure + AWS/GCP combined | Vendor diversification |

### 2.1.4 Azure Pricing Models
- **Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG):** Per-second billing.
- **Reserved Instances (RI):** 1-year or 3-year commitment → up to **72% discount**.
- **Spot VMs:** Up to **90% discount** using spare capacity (can be evicted).
- **Azure Hybrid Benefit:** Reuse existing on-premises Windows Server / SQL Server licenses.

## 2.2 Google Cloud Platform — Architectural Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 GCP Resource Hierarchy

GCP uses a **three-tier resource hierarchy** anchored to the **Organization Node**:

```
Organization Node  →  Folders  →  Projects  →  Resources
      (GCP Org)      (Teams)     (Billing+IAM)  (Services)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Difference:** Unlike Azure's Resource Group concept, GCP's **Project** is the **fundamental unit** of security, billing, and API enablement. Every resource in GCP must belong to a project.

### 2.2.2 GCP Core Service Pillars

**(A) GCP Compute Services**
- **Compute Engine (GCE):** IaaS — Configurable VMs with custom machine types.
- **App Engine (GAE):** PaaS — Fully managed application platform (Standard & Flexible environments).
- **Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE):** Managed Kubernetes (Google's flagship container orchestrator).
- **Cloud Functions:** Serverless event-driven FaaS.
- **Cloud Run:** Serverless container execution on Knative.

**(B) GCP Storage Services**
- **Cloud Storage (GCS):** Unified object storage (Standard, Nearline, Coldline, Archive classes).
- **Persistent Disk:** Block storage for GCE (zonal) and Hyperdisk.
- **Filestore:** Managed NFS file storage.
- **Archive Storage:** Lowest-cost long-term cold storage.

**(C) GCP Networking Services**
- **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC):** Global, software-defined private network (a key GCP differentiator).
- **Cloud Load Balancing:** Global, anycast IP-based load balancing (Layer 4 and Layer 7).
- **Cloud CDN:** Content Delivery Network backed by Google's edge points-of-presence.
- **Cloud Interconnect:** Dedicated connection to GCP (10 Gbps / 100 Gbps).
- **Cloud DNS:** 100% SLA-managed authoritative DNS.

**(D) GCP Data Analytics & AI/ML**
- **BigQuery:** Serverless petabyte-scale data warehouse (columnar storage, ANSI SQL).
- **Dataflow:** Stream and batch data processing (Apache Beam).
- **Dataproc:** Managed Hadoop/Spark.
- **Vertex AI:** Unified ML platform for training, deploying, and managing ML models.
- **TensorFlow / TPU Access:** Google's custom AI hardware.

### 2.2.3 GCP Storage Classes — Pricing vs Access Trade-off

| Class | Availability | Use Case | Minimum Storage Duration |
|-------|--------------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Standard** | 99.95% | Frequently accessed data | None |
| **Nearline** | 99.0% | Accessed ~once/month | 30 days |
| **Coldline** | 99.0% | Accessed ~once/quarter | 90 days |
| **Archive** | 99.0% | Accessed ~once/year | 365 days |

### 2.2.4 GCP Pricing Models
- **On-Demand / Pay-As-You-Go:** Per-second billing for compute.
- **Committed Use Discounts (CUDs):** 1-year or 3-year commitments → up to **57% discount** on VMs.
- **Sustained Use Discounts (SUDs):** Automatic discounts for workloads running >25% of a month.
- **Preemptible / Spot VMs:** Up to **80% discount** with possible 24-hour termination notice.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Architecture & SLA)

| Concept | Azure | GCP | Key Note |
|---------|-------|-----|----------|
| **VPC Equivalent** | Virtual Network (VNet) | Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) | Azure VNet is **regional**; GCP VPC is **global** |
| **Object Storage** | Blob Storage | Cloud Storage (GCS) | Both support S3-compatible APIs via adapters |
| **Managed Kubernetes** | AKS | GKE | Google invented Kubernetes |
| **Serverless Functions** | Azure Functions | Cloud Functions | Azure supports C#, JS, Python, Java, PowerShell |
| **NoSQL DB** | Cosmos DB | Firestore / Bigtable | Cosmos DB offers 5 consistency models |
| **Identity** | Microsoft Entra ID | Cloud IAM | GCP IAM uses deny-by-default model |
| **Serverless Containers** | Container Instances | Cloud Run | Cloud Run is Knative-based |
| **VM SLA** | 99.99% (multi-AZ) | 99.99% (multi-zone) | Industry-leading nines |
| **Regions** | 60+ | 40+ | Azure has wider geographic spread |
| **Pricing Unit** | Per-second (after 1st minute) | Per-second (after 1st minute) | Identical billing granularity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** When designing a cloud-agnostic workload, engineers map services 1:1 (e.g., AKS ↔ GKE, Blob ↔ GCS, Functions ↔ Cloud Functions) using **Terraform** or **Pulumi** to maintain infrastructure-as-code portability.

## 2.4 Azure vs GCP — Strategic Decision Matrix

| Decision Factor | Choose Azure When… | Choose GCP When… |
|-----------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Existing Stack** | Microsoft-centric (.NET, SQL Server, Windows) | Linux / open-source / data-engineering |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Strong hybrid integration (Arc, Stack) | Anthos for multi-cloud |
| **AI/ML Needs** | Azure AI + Cognitive Services (OpenAI integration) | Vertex AI, TPUs, BigQuery ML |
| **Data Analytics** | Synapse, Data Factory, Power BI | BigQuery, Dataflow, Looker |
| **Global Network** | Microsoft Global Network | Google's private fiber backbone (faster) |
| **Compliance** | Strongest compliance portfolio (90+ certifications) | Strong but fewer certifications |
| **Cost Optimization** | Azure Hybrid Benefit for Windows/SQL | Sustained Use Discounts are automatic |
| **Open-Source Friendly** | Improving | Native open-source ethos |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Azure and Google Cloud" is a **comparative architectural topic** (not a math derivation topic), this section provides an **analytical step-by-step comparison matrix** and **service-mapping derivations** as required by KTU 2024 Scheme pedagogical standards.

## 3.1 Derivation: How a "Region" Concept Maps Across Azure and GCP

Both providers organize data centers into a **geographic hierarchy**. Let us derive the mapping step-by-step.

### Step 1: Identify the Three-Tier Hierarchy
Both Azure and GCP define infrastructure in three tiers:
- **Tier 1 (Top):** Region — a geographical area containing one or more data centers.
- **Tier 2 (Middle):** Availability Zone (AZ) — one or more physically separate data centers within a region, each with independent power, cooling, and networking.
- **Tier 3 (Bottom):** Data Center — the physical building housing servers, storage, and networking gear.

### Step 2: Map the Tiers
$$\text{Azure} = \{\text{Region} \supseteq \text{Availability Zone (AZ)} \supseteq \text{Data Center}\}$$
$$\text{GCP} = \{\text{Region} \supseteq \text{Zone} \supseteq \text{Data Center}\}$$

The Azure "Availability Zone" and GCP "Zone" are **semantically identical**: a zone is a deployment area for resources that must be isolated from failures in other zones.

### Step 3: Apply the Mapping
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Azure Region "East US"} &\longleftrightarrow \text{GCP Region "us-east1"} \\
\text{Azure AZ "East US 1"} &\longleftrightarrow \text{GCP Zone "us-east1-a"} \\
\text{Data Center (private)} &\longleftrightarrow \text{Data Center (private)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Failure Isolation Guarantee
- **Within an AZ:** Guaranteed <2 ms latency between zones (same region).
- **Between Regions:** Variable; can be transcontinental (100+ ms).
- **SLA Multiplier:** Deploying across **N=3** Availability Zones provides combined SLA of $1 - (1 - 0.9999)^N$:

$$SLA_{combined} = 1 - (1 - 0.9999)^3 = 1 - 0.000000001 = 0.999999999 \approx 99.9999999\%$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Service-to-Service Mapping (Cloud Portability)

### Step 1: Define the Cloud Portability Function
Let $f: S_{Azure} \to S_{GCP}$ be a function that maps each Azure service to its GCP equivalent.

### Step 2: Apply $f$ to Each Service Category

$$
f(\text{Compute}) = \begin{cases}
\text{Azure VM} \mapsto \text{Compute Engine} \\
\text{App Service} \mapsto \text{App Engine} \\
\text{Azure Functions} \mapsto \text{Cloud Functions} \\
\text{AKS} \mapsto \text{GKE} \\
\text{Container Instances} \mapsto \text{Cloud Run}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
f(\text{Storage}) = \begin{cases}
\text{Blob Storage} \mapsto \text{Cloud Storage} \\
\text{File Storage} \mapsto \text{Filestore} \\
\text{Queue Storage} \mapsto \text{Pub/Sub} \\
\text{Disk Storage} \mapsto \text{Persistent Disk}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
f(\text{Networking}) = \begin{cases}
\text{VNet} \mapsto \text{VPC} \\
\text{Load Balancer} \mapsto \text{Cloud Load Balancing} \\
\text{Application Gateway} \mapsto \text{Cloud Load Balancing (HTTP(S))} \\
\text{Front Door} \mapsto \text{Cloud CDN + Global LB} \\
\text{ExpressRoute} \mapsto \text{Cloud Interconnect}
\end{cases}
$$

$$
f(\text{Database}) = \begin{cases}
\text{Azure SQL} \mapsto \text{Cloud SQL} \\
\text{Cosmos DB} \mapsto \text{Firestore / Spanner} \\
\text{Azure Database for PostgreSQL} \mapsto \text{Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 3: Validate the Mapping Quality
Mapping quality $Q$ is rated:
- **High (1:1):** Compute Engine ↔ VM, GKE ↔ AKS, Cloud Storage ↔ Blob
- **Medium (functional parity, not API parity):** Cosmos DB ↔ Firestore
- **Low (no direct equivalent):** Azure Sentinel (SIEM) ↔ Chronicle (GCP's equivalent is separate)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cost Comparison (Same Workload)

Let us derive the **monthly cost** for a workload with the following specification:
- **Workload:** 2 vCPUs, 8 GB RAM Linux VM, running 24/7.
- **Storage:** 100 GB Premium SSD, 1 TB outbound data transfer.
- **Region:** US East (Azure) / us-east1 (GCP).

### Azure Estimate (Pay-As-You-Go)
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{compute} &= 2 \text{ vCPU} \times \$0.0464/\text{hr} \times 730 \text{ hrs} = \$67.74 \\
C_{storage} &= 100 \text{ GB} \times \$0.135/\text{GB-month} = \$13.50 \\
C_{egress} &= 1024 \text{ GB} \times \$0.087/\text{GB} = \$89.09 \\
C_{total,Azure} &= \$67.74 + \$13.50 + \$89.09 = \$170.33
\end{aligned}$$

### GCP Estimate (Pay-As-You-Go, excluding SUDs)
$$\begin{aligned}
C_{compute} &= 2 \text{ vCPU} \times \$0.0475/\text{hr} \times 730 \text{ hrs} = \$69.35 \\
C_{storage} &= 100 \text{ GB} \times \$0.17/\text{GB-month} = \$17.00 \\
C_{egress} &= 1024 \text{ GB} \times \$0.12/\text{GB} = \$122.88 \\
C_{total,GCP} &= \$69.35 + \$17.00 + \$122.88 = \$209.23
\end{aligned}$$

### Cost Differential Analysis
$$\Delta C = C_{GCP} - C_{Azure} = \$209.23 - \$170.33 = \$38.90$$

$$\% \text{ Diff} = \frac{\Delta C}{C_{Azure}} \times 100 = \frac{38.90}{170.33} \times 100 \approx 22.84\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In this specific workload, **Azure is ~23% cheaper** at PAYG rates. However, GCP's automatic **Sustained Use Discounts** and lower **committed-use** prices can reverse this when workloads run for >25% of a month.

## 3.4 Derivation: SLA Calculation for Multi-Region Deployment

**Problem:** A KTU question asks: *What is the combined SLA when deploying a web app across 2 regions in Azure, with each region having 2 VMs in an Availability Set (SLA = 99.99%)?*

### Step 1: Identify Individual Component SLAs
- **Single VM (Premium SSD):** SLA = 99.9%
- **2 VMs in Availability Set:** SLA = 99.99%
- **Azure Traffic Manager (multi-region):** SLA = 99.99%

### Step 2: Apply Series SLA Formula
For components in series:
$$SLA_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} SLA_i$$

### Step 3: Apply Parallel SLA Formula
For redundant components in parallel:
$$SLA_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - SLA_i)$$

### Step 4: Compute Step-by-Step
$$\begin{aligned}
SLA_{single\_region} &= 0.9999 \text{ (Availability Set)} \\
SLA_{traffic\_manager} &= 0.9999 \\
SLA_{combined} &= 1 - (1 - 0.9999) \times (1 - 0.9999) \\
&= 1 - (0.0001) \times (0.0001) \\
&= 1 - 0.00000001 \\
&= 0.99999999 \\
&\approx 99.999999\% \text{ (Eight-Nines)}
\end{aligned}$$

This means **maximum allowed downtime per year**:
$$D_{annual} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - 0.99999999) \approx 0.315 \text{ seconds/year}$$
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->
