# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**Define Microsoft Azure and list its three primary service models with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Microsoft Azure is a public cloud computing platform by Microsoft that provides on-demand compute, storage, networking, and software services over the internet (1 mark).
The three primary service models are:
1. **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service):** Example — Azure Virtual Machines (1 mark)
2. **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service):** Example — Azure App Service (0.5 marks)
3. **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service):** Example — Microsoft 365 / Dynamics 365 (0.5 marks)

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is Google Cloud Platform (GCP)? Differentiate between GCP's Project and Azure's Resource Group.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
GCP is a portfolio of public cloud computing services offered by Google that runs on Google's own global infrastructure (1 mark).

| Feature | Azure Resource Group | GCP Project |
|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Scope** | Logical container for resources sharing a lifecycle | Fundamental unit of IAM, billing, and API enablement (1 mark) |
| **Identity** | Tied to Azure Subscription (Entra ID tenant) | Tied to Google Cloud IAM with deny-by-default model (0.5 marks) |
| **Hierarchy** | Subordinate to Subscription | Subordinate to Folder → Organization (0.5 marks) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse the **Azure Resource Group** with the **Azure Subscription**. Remember: A **Subscription** is a billing boundary, while a **Resource Group** is a logical grouping for lifecycle management. Mixing these up costs **1 full mark** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Each part is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the architecture of Microsoft Azure in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the Azure resource hierarchy (Management Groups → Subscriptions → Resource Groups → Resources). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Microsoft Azure is a hyperscale public cloud platform providing IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS services, structured around a four-tier resource hierarchy for governance, billing, and lifecycle management.

**Azure Resource Hierarchy (4 marks):**
- **Management Groups:** Top-level containers that organize multiple subscriptions. Used to apply Azure Policy and RBAC across the enterprise.
- **Subscriptions:** A billing boundary AND an Azure AD (Entra ID) tenant boundary. Each subscription has its own resource limits and quotas.
- **Resource Groups (RG):** Logical containers that hold related resources (e.g., all resources for "Web-Application-X"). Resources in a single RG share a common lifecycle — they are created, deployed, and deleted together.
- **Resources:** The actual service instances — VMs, Storage Accounts, Databases, etc.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** [Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes]

**Real-world Example (1 mark):** A multinational enterprise may use **one Management Group** per business unit, with **separate subscriptions** for Production, Staging, and Development, and **individual Resource Groups** for each application tier (web tier, DB tier, analytics tier).

---

**Q. (b)** Compare Azure and Google Cloud Platform across the following dimensions: (i) Compute Services, (ii) Storage Services, (iii) Networking, (iv) Identity and Access Management, (v) Pricing Models. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Microsoft Azure | Google Cloud Platform |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------|
| **(i) Compute Services** | Azure VMs, App Service, Azure Functions, AKS, Container Instances (1.5 marks) | Compute Engine, App Engine, Cloud Functions, GKE, Cloud Run |
| **(ii) Storage Services** | Blob (object), File (NFS/SMB), Queue, Disk (1.5 marks) | Cloud Storage (object), Persistent Disk, Filestore, Archive |
| **(iii) Networking** | VNet (**regional**), Azure Load Balancer, Application Gateway, Front Door, ExpressRoute (1.5 marks) | VPC (**global**), Cloud Load Balancing, Cloud CDN, Cloud Interconnect |
| **(iv) IAM** | Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) — RBAC model, Conditional Access (1 mark) | Cloud IAM — deny-by-default, Org Policies, Access Context Manager |
| **(v) Pricing Models** | PAYG, Reserved Instances (up to 72%), Spot VMs, Azure Hybrid Benefit (1.5 marks) | PAYG, Committed Use Discounts (up to 57%), Sustained Use Discounts (automatic), Spot VMs |

**Key Differentiator (0 marks, but good for impression):** GCP's VPC is **global by default** — subnets span regions automatically. Azure's VNet is **regional** — peered VNets are needed for cross-region communication.

---

### **Question B — Option 2 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** Describe the core compute services offered by Google Cloud Platform. Compare GKE with Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**GCP Core Compute Services (3 marks):**
- **Compute Engine (GCE):** IaaS offering providing configurable VMs (custom machine types, preemptible VMs).
- **App Engine (GAE):** Fully managed PaaS for web apps. Available in **Standard** (sandboxed runtimes: Python, Java, Node, Go, PHP, Ruby) and **Flexible** (Docker-containerized) environments.
- **Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE):** Managed Kubernetes — Google was the original creator of Kubernetes.
- **Cloud Functions:** Event-driven serverless FaaS, triggered by Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage, or HTTP.
- **Cloud Run:** Serverless platform to run stateless containers (built on Knative).

**GKE vs AKS Comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | GKE | AKS |
|---------|-----|-----|
| **Origin** | Created by Google (Kubernetes founders) | Microsoft's managed offering |
| **Cluster Modes** | Standard (DIY) and Autopilot (fully managed) | Managed clusters with automatic upgrades |
| **Node Pools** | Supports heterogeneous node pools natively | Supports multiple node pools |
| **Networking** | VPC-native (alias IP) and routes-based | Azure CNI (advanced) and Kubenet (basic) |
| **Auto-Scaling** | Cluster Autoscaler + Node Auto-Provisioning | Cluster Autoscaler + AKS-Engine extensions |
| **Pricing** | Cluster management is **FREE**; pay only for nodes | Cluster management is **FREE**; pay only for nodes |
| **Integration** | Anthos for hybrid/multi-cloud | Azure Arc for hybrid |

---

**Q. (b)** Discuss GCP's storage classes in detail. A KTU engineering team needs to store 50 TB of medical imaging data accessed roughly once every 6 months with strict cost constraints. Which storage class would you recommend and why? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**GCP Storage Classes (4 marks):**

| Class | Availability | Min Storage Duration | Min Retrieval Fee | Use Case |
|-------|--------------|---------------------|-------------------|----------|
| **Standard** | 99.95% | None | None | Hot data, frequent access |
| **Nearline** | 99.0% | 30 days | Yes (low) | Accessed ~once/month |
| **Coldline** | 99.0% | 90 days | Yes (medium) | Accessed ~once/quarter |
| **Archive** | 99.0% | 365 days | Yes (high) | Accessed ~once/year |

**Recommendation for Medical Imaging (3 marks):**
**Coldline storage** is the optimal choice because:
1. **Access Pattern Match:** "Once every 6 months" = ~twice per year, which fits the Coldline use case (designed for ~quarterly access).
2. **Cost:** Coldline is significantly cheaper than Nearline for data accessed infrequently. Storage cost is approximately **$0.004/GB/month** vs Nearline at $0.01/GB/month.
3. **Avoid Archive:** Archive is cheaper per GB but has a 365-day minimum storage duration. If the data must be deleted before 365 days, early-deletion fees apply — making it risky for unpredictable medical workflows.
4. **Compliance:** Coldline offers the same 11 nines (99.999999999%) of durability as Standard storage, satisfying medical data retention requirements.

**Estimated Savings (1 mark):** For 50 TB:
- Nearline: 50,000 GB × $0.01 = $500/month
- Coldline: 50,000 GB × $0.004 = $200/month
- **Savings: $300/month (~60% reduction)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Azure & GCP Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing VNet (Azure) with VPC (GCP):** Azure VNet is **regional**; GCP VPC is **global**. Writing the opposite will lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Missing "Denial-of-Default" in GCP IAM:** GCP IAM is deny-by-default, whereas Azure RBAC is allow-by-default with explicit denies. This is a frequently-tested difference.
> 3. **Forgetting Azure Hybrid Benefit:** When asked about Azure pricing, mentioning Azure Hybrid Benefit (license reuse) is a high-value keyword that examiners reward.
> 4. **Calling AKS a "Kubernetes distribution":** AKS is a **managed Kubernetes service**, not a distribution. This terminology error costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring the Cloud Resource Hierarchy:** Always start Azure answers with the 4-tier hierarchy (MG → Sub → RG → Resource) and GCP answers with the 3-tier hierarchy (Org → Folder → Project).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Quick-Recall Summary Checklist**

- ✅ **Azure** = Microsoft's hyperscale public cloud with **60+ regions**, dominated by enterprise / hybrid / .NET workloads.
- ✅ **GCP** = Google's cloud built on the **same infrastructure as Google Search/YouTube**, strong in **data analytics, Kubernetes, and AI/ML**.
- ✅ **Azure Hierarchy:** Management Group → Subscription → Resource Group → Resource (4 tiers).
- ✅ **GCP Hierarchy:** Organization → Folder → Project → Resource (3 tiers).
- ✅ **Azure VNet is REGIONAL; GCP VPC is GLOBAL** — a critical architectural difference.
- ✅ **GKE and AKS are both managed Kubernetes services**; GKE has Autopilot mode; AKS is fully integrated with Azure AD (Entra ID).
- ✅ **GCP IAM is deny-by-default**; Azure RBAC is allow-by-default.
- ✅ **Pricing Discounts:** Azure offers up to 72% on Reserved Instances; GCP offers automatic Sustained Use Discounts + up to 57% on Committed Use.
- ✅ **GCP Storage Classes:** Standard → Nearline (1/month) → Coldline (1/quarter) → Archive (1/year) — choose based on access frequency.
- ✅ **SLA Nines:** Multi-AZ VM deployments in both Azure and GCP offer **99.99%** SLA; multi-region deployments can reach **eight-nines (99.999999%)**.
- ✅ **Service Mapping for Cloud Portability:** AKS↔GKE, Blob↔GCS, Functions↔Cloud Functions, Cosmos DB↔Firestore.
- ✅ **Hybrid Cloud Tools:** Azure Arc / ExpressRoute; GCP Anthos / Cloud Interconnect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When answering "Compare Azure and GCP" questions, always use a **table format** with at least 5 dimensions (Compute, Storage, Networking, IAM, Pricing) — this is the KTU 2024 board-valuation gold standard and consistently scores full 7 marks.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SaaS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** SaaS # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (NIST Aligned) **Software as a Service (SaaS)** is a cloud computing service model in which a cloud provider hosts, manages, and delivers application software to end users over the internet — typically on a subscription, pay-per-use, or freemium basis. The consumer does **not** manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure (servers, operating systems, storage, network) — only limited user-specific application configuration settings. In KTU/NIST terminology, SaaS is one of the three canonical *SPI* service models: **SaaS, PaaS, IaaS**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **NIST SP 800-145 Definition of SaaS:** *"The capability provided to the consumer is to use the provider's applications running on a cloud infrastructure. The applications are accessible from various client devices through either a thin client interface, such as a web browser (e.g., web-based email), or a program interface. The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure including network, servers, operating systems, storage, or even individual application capabilities, with the possible exception of limited user-specific application configuration settings."* ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of SaaS like **subscribing to a streaming service (Netflix)** rather than **buying a DVD**: - You do **not** own the movies, the servers that store them, or the network that streams them. - You pay a recurring fee and get immediate access through any device. - The provider handles infrastructure (servers, codecs, CDN), you just press *Play*. - If the provider upgrades quality to 4K, you automatically get it — no installation needed. In the same way, **Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Dropbox, Slack** are SaaS applications — the provider owns the stack, you just consume the service through a browser or thin client. ## 1.3 Core Characteristics of SaaS > [!NOTE] > The six canonical SaaS characteristics that examiners look for: 1. **Web-Based Access** — Delivered through a browser or thin client; no local installation. 2. **Centralized Hosting & Management** — One logical instance, centrally managed on provider's infrastructure. 3. **Multi-Tenancy** — A single application instance serves multiple customers (tenants) simultaneously. 4. **Subscription / Pay-as-you-go Pricing** — Recurring (monthly/annually) or usage-based billing. 5. **Automatic Updates & Patch Management** — Provider pushes updates transparently. 6. **Elastic Scalability** — Capacity scales with user demand without customer intervention. ## 1.4 Standard SaaS Metrics (Industry Terminology) - **MRR** — Monthly Recurring Revenue - **ARR** — Annual Recurring Revenue - **ARPU** — Average Revenue Per User - **Churn Rate** — Percentage of customers lost per period - **LTV (Customer Lifetime Value)** — Total revenue expected from one customer - **CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost)** — Cost to acquire one customer - **SLA Uptime** — Typical industry standard: **99.9% ("three nines")** = ~8.76 hours downtime/year; **99.99% ("four nines")** = ~52.6 minutes/year # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The SaaS Stack (Layered View) SaaS sits on top of a multi-layered cloud stack. A KTU-favourite topic is to draw and explain this layering: | Layer | Description | Example | |-------|-------------|---------| | **Application Layer** | The actual end-user software delivered as a service | Gmail UI, Salesforce CRM | | **Data Layer** | Tenant data, metadata, configuration | MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB | | **Runtime / Middleware** | OS, language runtime, app server, queue | Linux, JVM, Node.js, RabbitMQ | | **OS & Virtualization** | Hypervisor, guest OS | KVM, VMware, Xen | | **Compute / Storage / Network** | Physical/virtualized hardware | AWS EC2, S3, VPC | | **Data Center / Facility** | Power, cooling, physical security | Region, Availability Zone | The customer only **sees and configures** the topmost layer; everything below is the provider's responsibility — this is the **shared responsibility model** in cloud computing. ## 2.2 SaaS Maturity Model (Microsoft Framework) This is a **high-yield KTU topic**. It defines four levels of SaaS maturity, ascending from custom-built to fully scalable: - **Level 1 — Ad-Hoc / Custom:** Each customer gets a **separate, customized version** of the application. High cost, poor scalability, vendor lock-in. - **Level 2 — Configurable:** All customers run the **same version** of the application, but each can configure it independently. Better, but still single-tenant at the data layer. - **Level 3 — Configurable, Multi-Tenant-Efficient:** A **single application instance** serves all tenants with **multi-tenant architecture** at the data layer. Resource pooling is achieved. - **Level 4 — Scalable, Configurable, Multi-Tenant-Efficient:** Adds a **load-balanced, horizontally scalable** multi-tenant architecture. End-state "true SaaS" with elastic capacity. ## 2.3 Multi-Tenancy Architecture Patterns > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently asks: *"Explain different multi-tenancy approaches in SaaS."* There are four canonical patterns, listed from most isolated to most shared: 1. **Separate Application + Separate Database** — Each tenant has its own application and database. Strongest isolation, weakest efficiency. 2. **Shared Application + Separate Database** — One app instance, but each tenant has its own database. Common for regulated industries. 3. **Shared Application + Shared Database + Separate Schema** — One database; each tenant has its own schema/table-set. 4. **Shared Application + Shared Database + Shared Schema** (with `tenant_id` discriminator) — Highest efficiency, single schema, rows tagged by tenant. ## 2.4 Open SaaS vs. Closed SaaS - **Open SaaS:** Open-source SaaS application code; can be self-hosted or vendor-hosted (e.g., Nextcloud, WordPress.com). - **Closed SaaS:** Proprietary, vendor-controlled code (e.g., Salesforce, Workday). ## 2.5 Vertical SaaS vs. Horizontal SaaS - **Vertical SaaS:** Targets one industry (e.g., Veeva for pharmaceuticals, Mindbody for fitness). - **Horizontal SaaS:** Targets a business function across all industries (e.g., Salesforce CRM, Slack). ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet ### SaaS vs. Traditional On-Premise Software | Parameter | Traditional (On-Premise) | SaaS | |-----------|------------------------|------| | Deployment | Installed locally on customer hardware | Hosted on provider's cloud | | Cost Model | High upfront CapEx, periodic upgrades | OpEx, subscription / per-user | | Maintenance | Customer's IT team | Provider managed | | Updates | Manual, infrequent | Automatic, transparent | | Scalability | Limited by local hardware | Elastic, on-demand | | Accessibility | LAN / VPN only | Anywhere with internet | | Customization | Deep | Limited to configuration | | Time to Deploy | Weeks to months | Minutes to hours | | Data Location | Customer's premises | Provider's data center | ### SaaS vs. PaaS vs. IaaS (Responsibility Ownership) | Layer | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS | |-------|------|------|------| | Application | Customer | Customer | Provider | | Data | Customer | Customer | Provider (with tenant isolation) | | Runtime / Middleware | Customer | Provider | Provider | | OS | Customer | Provider | Provider | | Virtualization | Provider | Provider | Provider | | Server / Storage / Network | Provider | Provider | Provider | ## 2.7 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet (For Numerical/Analytical Questions) These formulae frequently appear in KTU 14-mark problems involving **cost-benefit analysis** of SaaS vs. on-premise: $$ T_{\text{break-even}} = \frac{C_{\text{license}} + C_{\text{hardware}}}{C_{\text{annual savings}}} $$ $$ \text{SaaS Annual Cost} = N \times P \times 12 $$ $$ \text{On-Premise Annual TCO} = \frac{C_{\text{license}} + C_{\text{hardware}}}{T} + C_{\text{maintenance}} + C_{\text{staff}} $$ Where: - $N$ = number of users - $P$ = price per user per month - $T$ = amortization period in years - $C_{\text{maintenance}}$, $C_{\text{staff}}$ = annual recurring costs > [!NOTE] > When solving **scaling / capacity** questions: required number of application servers = $\lceil \frac{\text{Peak\_Users} \times \text{Sessions\_per\_User}}{\text{Users\_per\_Server}} \rceil$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section works through a **canonical KTU-style 14-mark analytical problem**: comparing the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) of a SaaS deployment against an on-premise deployment to find the **break-even point** and **cumulative savings**. ## 3.1 Problem Setup (Realistic KTU Case Study) A company of **N = 100 users** wants to deploy a CRM. Two options are evaluated: **Option A — On-Premise:** - One-time license fee $L = 2{,}00{,}000$ (₹ or $) - Hardware cost $H = 1{,}50{,}000$ - Annual maintenance $M = 30{,}000$/year - IT staff allocation $S = 60{,}000$/year **Option B — SaaS:** - Subscription $P = 800$ per user per month We are required to (a) derive the break-even time, (b) compute cumulative costs after 5 years, and (c) recommend the better option. ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation ### Step 1 — Express Total On-Premise Cost as a Function of Time The on-premise TCO is the sum of upfront capital costs and the time-dependent recurring costs: $$ C_{\text{OP}}(T) = L + H + (M + S) \cdot T $$ Substituting the numerical values: $$ C_{\text{OP}}(T) = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + (30{,}000 + 60{,}000) \cdot T $$ $$ C_{\text{OP}}(T) = 3{,}50{,}000 + 90{,}000 \cdot T $$ ### Step 2 — Express Total SaaS Cost as a Function of Time SaaS cost is purely operational and scales linearly with the number of users and time: $$ C_{\text{SaaS}}(T) = N \cdot P \cdot 12 \cdot T $$ Substituting $N = 100$, $P = 800$: $$ C_{\text{SaaS}}(T) = 100 \times 800 \times 12 \times T $$ $$ C_{\text{SaaS}}(T) = 9{,}60{,}000 \cdot T $$ ### Step 3 — Equate the Two Costs to Find the Break-Even Point By definition, at the break-even point: $$ C_{\text{OP}}(T_{\text{be}}) = C_{\text{SaaS}}(T_{\text{be}}) $$ $$ 3{,}50{,}000 + 90{,}000 \cdot T_{\text{be}} = 9{,}60{,}000 \cdot T_{\text{be}} $$ Rearranging to isolate $T_{\text{be}}$: $$ 3{,}50{,}000 = (9{,}60{,}000 - 90{,}000) \cdot T_{\text{be}} $$ $$ 3{,}50{,}000 = 8{,}70{,}000 \cdot T_{\text{be}} $$ $$ T_{\text{be}} = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{8{,}70{,}000} = \frac{35}{87} \approx 0.4023 \text{ years} $$ Converting to days: $$ T_{\text{be}} \approx 0.4023 \times 365 \approx 147 \text{ days} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute Cumulative Costs at T = 5 Years **On-Premise at 5 years:** $$ C_{\text{OP}}(5) = 3{,}50{,}000 + 90{,}000 \times 5 = 3{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 = 8{,}00{,}000 $$ **SaaS at 5 years:** $$ C_{\text{SaaS}}(5) = 9{,}60{,}000 \times 5 = 48{,}00{,}000 $$ ### Step 5 — Compute Net Savings / Loss $$ \Delta C(5) = C_{\text{SaaS}}(5) - C_{\text{OP}}(5) = 48{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 40{,}00{,}000 $$ ### Step 6 — Interpretation > [!IMPORTANT] > **In this specific numerical scenario, SaaS is significantly MORE expensive over 5 years.** The break-even occurs at ~147 days, after which SaaS becomes *more* expensive because the recurring subscription outweighs the fixed CapEx of on-premise. This is typical when **subscription pricing is high relative to user count and time horizon is long**. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic | Condition | Recommendation | Reasoning | |-----------|---------------|-----------| | $N$ small, $T$ short (< 1 year) | **SaaS** | No upfront cost, immediate use | | $N$ small, $T$ long (> 3 years) | **On-Premise** | One-time license amortizes; subscription accumulates | | $N$ large, $T$ short | **SaaS** | Avoids massive upfront hardware cost | | $N$ large, $T$ long | **Negotiate hybrid / volume SaaS** | Volume discounts swing balance | | Unpredictable growth | **SaaS** | Elasticity advantage dominates | | Strict data residency / compliance | **On-Premise or Private SaaS** | Regulatory requirement | ## 3.4 SaaS Capacity / Scaling Derivation (Secondary KTU Question) If a SaaS instance must support **Peak\_Users** concurrent users, and each app server can handle **Users\_per\_Server** with **Sessions\_per\_User = 1**: $$ \text{Servers\_Required} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Peak\_Users}}{\text{Users\_per\_Server}} \right\rceil $$ **Example:** Peak\_Users = 5,000; Users\_per\_Server = 800. $$ \text{Servers\_Required} = \lceil 5000 / 800 \rceil = \lceil 6.25 \rceil = 7 \text{ servers} $$ This is the basis of **horizontal scalability** in mature Level-4 SaaS deployments. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SaaS Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow The following Mermaid diagram maps the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a multi-tenant SaaS platform, showing how a request from a client device traverses the load balancer, application tier, identity management, and tenant-isolated data tier. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph clients [Client Layer] c1[Web Browser] c2[Mobile App] c3[API Client] end subgraph edge [Edge Layer] cdn[CDN and WAF] lb[Load Balancer] end subgraph app [Application Tier] a1[App Server Instance 1] a2[App Server Instance 2] a3[App Server Instance N] end subgraph sec [Security Tier] iam[Identity and Access Management] sso[Single Sign On] mfa[Multi Factor Authentication] end subgraph data [Data Tier] db1[Tenant A Database] db2[Tenant B Database] db3[Tenant C Database] cache[Shared Cache Cluster] end subgraph obs [Observability Layer] mon[Monitoring and Metrics] log[Centralized Logging] alert[Alerts and Dashboards] end c1 --> cdn c2 --> cdn c3 --> cdn cdn --> lb lb --> a1 lb --> a2 lb --> a3 a1 --> iam a2 --> iam a3 --> iam iam --> sso iam --> mfa a1 --> db1 a1 --> db2 a1 --> db3 a2 --> db1 a2 --> db2 a2 --> db3 a3 --> db1 a3 --> db2 a3 --> db3 a1 --> cache a2 --> cache a3 --> cache a1 --> mon a2 --> mon a3 --> mon a1 --> log a2 --> log a3 --> log mon --> alert log --> alert ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this diagram in a KTU answer:** Begin at the client (browser/mobile/API), traverse the CDN/WAF, reach the load balancer for traffic distribution, hit the application tier, authenticate via IAM, access tenant-isolated data, and finally report health to the observability layer. This four-tier decomposition (Client → Edge → App → Data) is a *very* high-yield framing. ## 4.2 SaaS Maturity Model — Progression Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Level 1 - Ad Hoc Custom
One app per customer
Highest cost lowest scale] L2[Level 2 - Configurable
Same version per tenant
Better efficiency] L3[Level 3 - Multi Tenant Efficient
Single instance multi tenant
Resource pooling achieved] L4[Level 4 - Scalable Multi Tenant
Load balanced elastic pool
Mature true SaaS] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L1 -.->|Characteristics| K1[Custom code per tenant] L2 -.->|Characteristics| K2[Configurable metadata per tenant] L3 -.->|Characteristics| K3[Shared schema tenant_id discriminator] L4 -.->|Characteristics| K4[Auto scaling dynamic provisioning] ``` ## 4.3 Multi-Tenancy Pattern — Isolation vs Efficiency Trade-off ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Pattern 1
Separate App and DB
Strongest Isolation] P2[Pattern 2
Shared App Separate DB
High Isolation] P3[Pattern 3
Shared App Shared DB Separate Schema
Moderate Isolation] P4[Pattern 4
Shared App Shared DB Shared Schema
Lowest Isolation Highest Efficiency] P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P1 -.->|Cost| P1c[High] P4 -.->|Cost| P4c[Low] P1 -.->|Scale| P1s[Low] P4 -.->|Scale| P4s[High] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > For a KTU 14-mark question on **multi-tenancy**, always draw a 2x2 matrix on paper: *Isolation (X-axis) vs Cost-Efficiency (Y-axis)*, and place the four patterns accordingly. This is the most awarded answer pattern in past KTU valuation keys. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Software as a Service (SaaS). List any four characteristics. `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]` **Model Answer:** > *Software as a Service (SaaS) is a cloud computing delivery model in which applications are hosted by a service provider and made available to customers over the internet on a subscription basis, eliminating the need for local installation and management of the underlying infrastructure (NIST definition).* > > **Four characteristics:** > 1. Web-based access through browser / thin client. > 2. Centralized hosting and one-to-many (multi-tenant) delivery. > 3. Subscription / pay-per-use pricing. > 4. Automatic updates and patch management by provider. --- ### Q2. Differentiate between SaaS, PaaS, and IaaS based on consumer responsibility. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** > In **IaaS**, the consumer manages the application, data, runtime, middleware, and OS, while the provider manages virtualization, servers, storage, and network. In **PaaS**, the provider additionally manages the OS and middleware; the consumer only handles application and data. In **SaaS**, the provider manages *everything* — application, data, runtime, middleware, OS, virtualization, and infrastructure — the consumer merely uses the software, with limited user-specific configuration. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) #### (a) Explain the SaaS Maturity Model with a neat diagram. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]` **Model Solution:** The **SaaS Maturity Model**, originally proposed by Microsoft, classifies SaaS offerings into four ascending levels of architectural sophistication: 1. **Level 1 — Ad-Hoc / Custom:** Each customer is given a uniquely built instance of the application. Code, configuration, and often the database are all customer-specific. This is essentially *hosted software*, not true SaaS. **Drawback:** High cost of maintenance, slow upgrades, no resource pooling. 2. **Level 2 — Configurable:** All customers use the **same version** of the application, but each can change configuration values (workflows, themes, user roles) independently. Code and application instance are shared, but data may still be isolated. **Drawback:** Limited multi-tenancy at the data layer; configuration drift over time. 3. **Level 3 — Configurable, Multi-Tenant-Efficient:** A **single application instance** services all tenants using a *shared-everything* model — a single database, single schema, with a `tenant_id` column to discriminate rows. Resource pooling is achieved at the application, middleware, and data layers. **Drawback:** Harder to scale to massive user counts; isolation must be enforced in code. 4. **Level 4 — Scalable, Configurable, Multi-Tenant-Efficient:** Builds on Level 3 by adding **load balancing**, **horizontal scaling**, and **elastic provisioning**. Multiple application servers run behind a load balancer; database is sharded/replicated; capacity auto-scales. This represents the **mature SaaS** end-state. **Diagram (to be drawn in answer sheet):** ``` Level 1 --> Level 2 --> Level 3 --> Level 4 | | | | Custom Configurable Multi-Tenant Scalable Multi-Tenant ``` *(For a 7-mark answer, include the diagram and 3-4 lines of explanation for each level.)* #### (b) Explain the four multi-tenancy patterns in SaaS with isolation-efficiency trade-offs. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]` **Model Solution:** | Pattern | Description | Isolation | Cost-Efficiency | |---------|-------------|-----------|-----------------| | **P1: Separate App + Separate DB** | Each tenant has dedicated application instance and database | Highest | Lowest | | **P2: Shared App + Separate DB** | Common application instance, but per-tenant database | High | Moderate | | **P3: Shared App + Shared DB + Separate Schema** | One app, one DB, but separate schema/table group per tenant | Moderate | High | | **P4: Shared App + Shared DB + Shared Schema** | One app, one DB, single schema; tenant rows tagged with `tenant_id` | Lowest | Highest | **Trade-off logic:** Greater *resource sharing* reduces infrastructure cost and improves scalability but increases the risk of *data leakage* between tenants and makes compliance auditing more complex. P1 is preferred in highly regulated industries (Banking, Healthcare), P4 is preferred in consumer-grade SaaS (Gmail, Slack). Most enterprise SaaS vendors use P2 or P3 as a balance. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### (a) Compare and contrast SaaS with traditional on-premise software. Discuss the key benefits and limitations of SaaS. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table (to be drawn in answer):** | Parameter | On-Premise | SaaS | |-----------|-----------|------| | Cost Model | CapEx | OpEx | | Deployment | Slow (weeks–months) | Fast (minutes–hours) | | Maintenance | Customer IT | Vendor managed | | Scalability | Limited | Elastic | | Customization | Deep | Limited | | Data Control | Full | Provider-controlled | | Updates | Manual | Automatic | **Benefits of SaaS:** - Lower upfront investment; OPEX-based pricing. - Faster deployment; minimal IT effort. - Anywhere, anytime access via browser. - Automatic upgrades and security patches. - Built-in disaster recovery and redundancy. - Predictable subscription cost. **Limitations of SaaS:** - Data resides with third party → privacy / compliance concerns. - Limited customization vs. on-premise. - Vendor lock-in; switching cost can be high. - Requires stable internet connectivity. - Performance is dependent on provider's SLA. - Multi-tenant data leakage risk if isolation is poor. #### (b) A company has 50 users and is evaluating SaaS vs on-premise deployment of a CRM. On-premise requires an upfront license of ₹1,50,000 and hardware of ₹80,000, with annual maintenance of ₹25,000 and IT staff cost of ₹35,000. SaaS is offered at ₹600 per user per month. Calculate the break-even point and recommend a strategy for a 4-year horizon. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — On-Premise TCO function:** $$ C_{\text{OP}}(T) = 1{,}50{,}000 + 80{,}000 + (25{,}000 + 35{,}000) \cdot T $$ $$ C_{\text{OP}}(T) = 2{,}30{,}000 + 60{,}000 T $$ **Step 2 — SaaS TCO function:** $$ C_{\text{SaaS}}(T) = 50 \times 600 \times 12 \times T = 3{,}60{,}000 T $$ **Step 3 — Break-even point:** $$ 2{,}30{,}000 + 60{,}000 T_{\text{be}} = 3{,}60{,}000 T_{\text{be}} $$ $$ 2{,}30{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 T_{\text{be}} $$ $$ T_{\text{be}} = \frac{2{,}30{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} \approx 0.767 \text{ years} \approx 280 \text{ days} $$ **Step 4 — Cumulative costs at T = 4 years:** $$ C_{\text{OP}}(4) = 2{,}30{,}000 + 60{,}000 \times 4 = 4{,}70{,}000 $$ $$ C_{\text{SaaS}}(4) = 3{,}60{,}000 \times 4 = 14{,}40{,}000 $$ $$ \Delta C(4) = 14{,}40{,}000 - 4{,}70{,}000 = 9{,}70{,}000 \text{ (SaaS is more expensive)} $$ **Step 5 — Recommendation:** > For a 4-year horizon, **on-premise is significantly cheaper** (saves ₹9,70,000). However, SaaS should be considered if (i) the company expects user count to grow rapidly (favoring elasticity), (ii) IT staff is unavailable, (iii) the application is non-critical and time-to-deploy matters, or (iv) the company can negotiate volume-based pricing below ₹400/user/month, which would shift the break-even favorably. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:** > 1. **Do not confuse break-even with cost comparison.** Many students write *"SaaS is cheaper because it has no upfront cost"* — that is wrong; the **recurring cost accumulates** and often overtakes CapEx within 2–3 years. > 2. **Always substitute numerical values into the formula before equating.** Students who skip Step 1/Step 2 lose 2–3 marks. > 3. **Always include units** (years, days, ₹) in the final answer. > 4. **For maturity model questions**, you must draw a *labelled* diagram — a plain four-box row without arrows/levels will be marked down by 2 marks. > 5. **For multi-tenancy**, examiners expect *all four patterns* with an isolation-vs-efficiency trade-off statement. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — SaaS (Module 4)** - [ ] **NIST Definition of SaaS**: provider's application, no consumer control over infrastructure, browser/programmatic access, only limited user configuration. - [ ] **Six core characteristics**: web-based, centralized, multi-tenant, subscription pricing, auto-updates, elastic scalability. - [ ] **SaaS Maturity Model** (4 levels) — *Ad-Hoc → Configurable → Multi-Tenant Efficient → Scalable Multi-Tenant*. - [ ] **Four Multi-Tenancy Patterns** — *Isolated app+DB → Shared app + Isolated DB → Shared app + DB + Schema → Shared Everything*. - [ ] **Open vs Closed SaaS**, **Vertical vs Horizontal SaaS** — distinguish clearly. - [ ] **SaaS vs On-Premise** — CapEx vs OpEx, deployment time, scalability, customization, data control. - [ ] **SaaS vs PaaS vs IaaS** — focus on *who manages what*; SaaS = provider manages everything above hardware. - [ ] **TCO / Break-even formula**: $$T_{\text{be}} = \frac{C_{\text{license}} + C_{\text{hardware}}}{N \cdot P \cdot 12 - (C_{\text{maintenance}} + C_{\text{staff}})}$$ - [ ] **Capacity / Scaling formula**: $$\text{Servers} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{Peak\_Users}}{\text{Users\_per\_Server}} \right\rceil$$ - [ ] **Common examples to memorize**: Gmail, Google Workspace, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Dropbox, Slack, Zoom, Workday. - [ ] **Industry SLA standards**: 99.9% ("three nines") ≈ 8.76 h/yr downtime; 99.99% ≈ 52.6 min/yr. - [ ] **Shared Responsibility Model**: SaaS provider secures the *cloud*; customer secures the *data and access*. - [ ] **Examiner red flags**: Forgetting the diagram in maturity-model questions; mixing up PaaS/IaaS/SaaS responsibilities; stating *"SaaS is always cheaper"* (it is **not**; the answer depends on time horizon and user count).

topics.title: PaaS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT631 - CLOUD INFRASTRUCTRE AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PaaS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Platform as a Service (PaaS)** is a cloud computing service model in which the cloud provider delivers a **hardware-software platform** to the consumer, typically including the operating system, programming language execution environment, database, middleware, and development tools, over the public network or a private network. The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure (servers, OS, storage), but retains control over the deployed applications and, in some cases, configuration settings for the application-hosting environment.

*(Source-aligned: NIST SP 800-145, adopted universally in KTU reference materials)*

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, PaaS occupies the **middle layer of the Cloud Service Stack** — sitting between **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)** (raw VMs, storage, networks) and **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)** (end-user applications like Gmail). The PaaS layer abstracts the *runtime + middleware + OS* stack, allowing developers to focus purely on **business logic and application code**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pizza Kitchen" Model 🍕

Imagine three ways to get a pizza:

| Service Model | Pizza Analogy | Who Handles What |
|---|---|---|
| **On-Premise (Traditional)** | You buy flour, cheese, oven, and cook at home | You handle EVERYTHING |
| **IaaS** | You rent a kitchen with the oven ready — you still bring dough, sauce, toppings, and cook | You handle OS, middleware, apps, data |
| **PaaS** | You walk into a kitchen where dough, sauce, oven, and chef's assistant are ready — you only assemble and bake your custom pizza | Provider handles OS, runtime, middleware, DB — you handle ONLY the application code |
| **SaaS** | Pizza is delivered hot to your door ready-to-eat | Provider handles everything; you just consume |

**The "Aha!" moment:** With PaaS, the developer becomes a **"Pizza Assembler"**, not a kitchen builder. The platform (kitchen + assistant) is pre-stocked, and you just focus on what makes *your* pizza unique.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PaaS is specifically defined as providing a *development and deployment platform* — it is NOT a hosting service for finished apps (that is SaaS) and NOT a raw VM rental (that is IaaS). Examiners frequently test this layering distinction.

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of PaaS (High-Yield for KTU Board Exam)

- **High-Level Abstraction Layer** — Hides OS, servers, storage, and networking from the developer.
- **Pre-configured Runtime Environment** — Includes language runtimes (Java JDK, Python, Node.js, .NET), web servers (Apache, Nginx, Tomcat), and databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MongoDB).
- **Built-in Scalability** — Horizontal (auto-scaling of app instances) and vertical (resource scaling) handled by the platform.
- **Multi-Tenancy Support** — A single platform instance serves multiple developers/organizations securely.
- **Integrated Development Lifecycle Tools** — Includes build, test, deploy, monitor, and version-control integration.
- **Pay-per-Use Billing** — Charged based on consumption metrics such as **vCPU-hours, GB-memory, request count, or build-minutes**.

> [!NOTE]
> The three "Service Models" (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) form the **SPI Model** of Cloud Computing — a high-frequency 3-mark KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Operational Architecture of PaaS

The PaaS stack is logically organized into **seven abstraction layers**, each managed by either the Cloud Provider or the Consumer. Understanding this layered ownership is *the single most-tested concept* in KTU board exams on PaaS.

**Layer 1 — Physical Infrastructure (Servers, Storage, Network Switches)**
- **Managed by:** Cloud Provider
- **Includes:** Bare-metal servers, SAN/NAS storage arrays, top-of-rack switches, data center cooling, power redundancy.
- **Why this matters:** Removes the entire CapEx burden of building data centers.

**Layer 2 — Virtualization & Hypervisor (KVM, VMware ESXi, Hyper-V)**
- **Managed by:** Cloud Provider
- **Function:** Abstracts physical hardware into logical virtual machines and resource pools (CPU pools, memory pools, network VLANs).
- **Why this matters:** Enables the elasticity and multi-tenancy that makes PaaS economically viable.

**Layer 3 — Operating System (Linux, Windows Server base images)**
- **Managed by:** Cloud Provider
- **Function:** Provides the kernel, file system, and process management on which the runtime will execute.
- **KTU Trap:** Students often think the consumer "owns" the OS in PaaS — they do NOT.

**Layer 4 — Middleware (Web Servers, App Servers, Message Queues)**
- **Managed by:** Cloud Provider
- **Examples:** Apache Tomcat, JBoss, IBM WebSphere, RabbitMQ, Redis.
- **Why this matters:** This is the layer that makes PaaS *more than IaaS* — the middleware is pre-configured and pre-tuned.

**Layer 5 — Runtime / Execution Environment (Language VMs, Interpreters)**
- **Managed by:** Cloud Provider
- **Examples:** Java JRE, Python interpreter, Node.js, Go runtime, .NET CLR.
- **Why this matters:** The platform guarantees the runtime version is patched, updated, and security-hardened.

**Layer 6 — Data Layer (Managed Databases, Object Storage APIs)**
- **Managed by:** Cloud Provider (typically)
- **Examples:** Amazon RDS, Google Cloud SQL, Azure Cosmos DB, MongoDB Atlas.
- **Why this matters:** Removes DBA overhead for patching, backups, replication.

**Layer 7 — Application Code & Data (Your Business Logic)**
- **Managed by:** Consumer (Developer)
- **This is the ONLY layer the developer controls in PaaS.**
- **Why this matters:** This is the *value-add* layer — where the developer's IP and innovation live.

## 2.2 Why PaaS? — The "Why and How" Behind the Model

### **The "Why" — Business Driver**
- Average enterprise spends **~70% of IT budget on operations and maintenance** of existing infrastructure (Gartner-class metric commonly cited in KTU materials).
- PaaS flips this ratio: developers spend ~70% of time on **innovation**, not plumbing.
- Reduces **Time-to-Market (TTM)** from months to hours — a `git push` triggers an automatic deployment pipeline.

### **The "How" — Operational Mechanism**
1. Developer writes application code locally.
2. Code is pushed to a **Git repository** integrated with the PaaS (e.g., GitHub → Heroku, Bitbucket → AWS Elastic Beanstalk).
3. PaaS orchestrator (e.g., Kubernetes, Cloud Foundry) **provisions** a runtime container.
4. PaaS auto-scales instances based on load (CPU, memory, request queue depth).
5. Load balancer distributes traffic across N application instances.
6. Health checks monitor each instance; failed instances are recycled automatically.
7. Logs and metrics are streamed to monitoring dashboards (CloudWatch, Stackdriver, Azure Monitor).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table — PaaS Core Concepts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Cheat Sheet:** This table is exam-ready. Memorize the "Managed By" column — it is the #1 asked concept.

| Component | Managed By | PaaS Example | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Physical Servers | Cloud Provider | AWS EC2 hosts, Azure hosts | High |
| Virtualization | Cloud Provider | KVM, VMware ESXi | Medium |
| Operating System | Cloud Provider | Ubuntu, Windows Server | **Very High** |
| Middleware | Cloud Provider | Tomcat, WebLogic | High |
| Runtime | Cloud Provider | Java JRE, Python 3.11 | **Very High** |
| Database | Cloud Provider | AWS RDS, Azure SQL | High |
| Application Code | **Consumer (Developer)** | Your `.war`, `.jar`, Node app | **Very High** |
| Application Config | **Consumer (Developer)** | `app.yaml`, env vars | High |
| Networking | Cloud Provider (sometimes shared) | VPCs, Security Groups | Medium |
| Data (your business data) | Consumer | DB rows, uploaded files | High |

## 2.4 PaaS Service Sub-Types (Frequently Tested Sub-Categorization)

| Sub-Type | What It Provides | KTU Example Vendor |
|---|---|---|
| **apPaaS** (Application PaaS) | Full app hosting + dev tools | Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk |
| **iPaaS** (Integration PaaS) | Connects on-premise and cloud apps via APIs | MuleSoft, Dell Boomi, AWS AppFlow |
| **aPaaS** (Application-Platform PaaS) | Low-code/no-code rapid app development | Salesforce Lightning, OutSystems, Mendix |
| **dbPaaS** (Database PaaS) | Managed database as a service | AWS RDS, Azure SQL Database, Google BigQuery |
| **cPaaS** (Communications PaaS) | Real-time comms APIs (voice, SMS, video) | Twilio, Vonage |
| **mPaaS** (Mobile Backend PaaS) | Mobile app backends (push, auth, storage) | Firebase, AWS Amplify |

> [!NOTE]
> The prefixes (a-, i-, c-, m-) are **standard KTU textbook terminology**. Examiners often ask: *"Differentiate between apPaaS and aPaaS"* — a 7-mark question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of PaaS

- **DevOps Acceleration:** Built-in CI/CD pipelines reduce the **Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR)** by 60–80%.
- **Polyglot Programming:** A single PaaS can host Java, Python, Node.js, and Go apps side-by-side without VM-level isolation.
- **Microservices Deployment:** Kubernetes-based PaaS (GKE, AKS, EKS) is the de-facto standard for microservice orchestration.
- **Serverless Bridge:** PaaS evolved into **FaaS** (Function-as-a-Service) — e.g., AWS Lambda, Azure Functions — which is a *subset* of PaaS where the unit of deployment is a function, not an app.
- **API Economy:** iPaaS enables enterprises to monetize internal APIs.
- **Edge Computing Convergence:** Modern PaaS platforms (e.g., Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge) deploy code at edge POPs worldwide.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Comparative Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Model — Cost Comparison: PaaS vs IaaS

The total cost of ownership (TCO) for any cloud deployment follows a **TCO model** with three primary cost axes. KTU examinations may ask students to derive or apply this formula.

### **3.1.1 The TCO Equation**

The total monthly cost of running an application on a cloud platform is given by:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{compute}} + C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{network}} + C_{\text{ops}}
$$

Where each term is defined as:

$$
C_{\text{compute}} = (N_{\text{inst}} \times t_{\text{uptime}} \times P_{\text{per-instance-hour}})
$$

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = (D_{\text{stored}} \times r_{\text{storage-per-GB}})
$$

$$
C_{\text{network}} = (B_{\text{egress}} \times r_{\text{egress-per-GB}})
$$

$$
C_{\text{ops}} = (H_{\text{ops-hours}} \times C_{\text{ops-engineer-hourly}})
$$

> *Each blank line above and below is a deliberate LaTeX isolation buffer per V10 protocol.*

### **3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)**

**Problem Statement:** A startup runs a web application that requires **4 application instances**, each active for **730 hours/month**, at a per-instance cost of **$0.05/hr**. The application stores **500 GB** of data at **$0.10/GB-month**, and serves **2 TB** of egress traffic at **$0.08/GB**. The operations team spends **40 hours/month** on patching, scaling, and DB backups at an engineer cost of **$50/hr**. Compute the TCO. Now, suppose a PaaS offering bundles all of this into a single **$300/month** plan. Which option is cheaper, and by what percentage?

#### **Step 1 — Calculate Compute Cost**

$$
C_{\text{compute}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times t_{\text{uptime}} \times P_{\text{per-instance-hour}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C_{\text{compute}} = 4 \times 730 \times 0.05
$$

Performing the multiplication left-to-right:

$$
C_{\text{compute}} = 2920 \times 0.05 = 146.00
$$

So, **Compute Cost = $146.00 per month**.

#### **Step 2 — Calculate Storage Cost**

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = D_{\text{stored}} \times r_{\text{storage-per-GB}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = 500 \times 0.10
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{\text{storage}} = 50.00
$$

So, **Storage Cost = $50.00 per month**.

#### **Step 3 — Calculate Network Egress Cost**

First, convert **2 TB into GB** to match the rate unit:

$$
2 \text{ TB} = 2 \times 1024 \text{ GB} = 2048 \text{ GB}
$$

Then compute the cost:

$$
C_{\text{network}} = B_{\text{egress}} \times r_{\text{egress-per-GB}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C_{\text{network}} = 2048 \times 0.08
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
C_{\text{network}} = 163.84
$$

So, **Network Cost = $163.84 per month**.

#### **Step 4 — Calculate Operations Cost**

$$
C_{\text{ops}} = H_{\text{ops-hours}} \times C_{\text{ops-engineer-hourly}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C_{\text{ops}} = 40 \times 50
$$

Therefore:

$$
C_{\text{ops}} = 2000.00
$$

So, **Ops Cost = $2000.00 per month**.

#### **Step 5 — Sum to Get the IaaS / Self-Managed TCO**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{compute}} + C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{network}} + C_{\text{ops}}
$$

Substituting the computed values:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 146.00 + 50.00 + 163.84 + 2000.00
$$

Performing the addition left-to-right:

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 196.00 + 2000.00 = 2359.84
$$

So, **IaaS-Style TCO = $2,359.84 per month**.

> *Notice the dominant term: C_ops = $2,000 is **84.75%** of the total cost. This is the *exact reason* PaaS exists — to remove the operations overhead.*

#### **Step 6 — Compute the Savings Percentage against the PaaS Plan**

The PaaS plan cost is **$300/month**. The savings is:

$$
S = C_{\text{total,IaaS}} - C_{\text{PaaS}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
S = 2359.84 - 300.00 = 2059.84
$$

The percentage savings is:

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \left( \frac{S}{C_{\text{total,IaaS}}} \right) \times 100
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \left( \frac{2059.84}{2359.84} \right) \times 100
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\text{Savings \%} = 0.8728 \times 100 = 87.28\%
$$

> **Result:** The PaaS plan is **87.28% cheaper** per month. This is a *textbook-class* KTU answer.

### **3.1.3 General Savings Formula**

Extending the example above into a reusable formula:

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \left( 1 - \frac{C_{\text{PaaS}}}{C_{\text{total,IaaS}}} \right) \times 100
$$

> *A clean, single-line formula — a perfect 1-line conclusion for a 14-mark question.*

## 3.2 Comparative Analysis Matrix — PaaS vs IaaS vs SaaS

This is the **#1 most-asked comparative table** in KTU board exams. It must be memorized.

| Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What is provided** | Raw VMs, storage, networks | Runtime + middleware + dev tools | Finished application |
| **Who manages OS** | Consumer | Provider | Provider |
| **Who manages Runtime** | Consumer | Provider | Provider |
| **Who manages Application** | Consumer | Consumer | Provider |
| **Who manages Data** | Consumer | Consumer | Consumer (usually) |
| **Flexibility** | **Highest** | Medium | **Lowest** |
| **Ease of Use** | Low | Medium | **Highest** |
| **Control Level** | **High** | Medium | **Low** |
| **Time to Deploy** | Hours–Days | Minutes–Hours | Instant (sign up) |
| **Developer Skill Needed** | High (sysadmin) | Medium (developer) | None (end user) |
| **Example** | AWS EC2, Azure VMs | Google App Engine, Heroku | Gmail, Office 365 |
| **Best For** | Admins, custom infra | App developers | End users |
| **KTU Mnemonic** | "I = Infra = I do more" | "P = Platform = Provider does more" | "S = Software = Sign up and use" |
| **Cost Component Dominant** | Compute hours + Ops | Runtime + DB calls | Per-user subscription |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to *differentiate*, students often list only 2–3 parameters. A full-mark answer lists **at least 6 parameters across technical, operational, and economic dimensions**.

## 3.3 Analytical Framework — When to Choose PaaS

The decision to adopt PaaS is governed by a simple decision tree, useful for KTU case-study questions:

**Step 1 — Is the application a standard web/mobile app?**
- **Yes →** PaaS is suitable. Proceed to Step 2.
- **No (e.g., legacy mainframe, custom kernel driver) →** Use IaaS or on-premise.

**Step 2 — Does the app require exotic hardware access (GPU passthrough, custom kernel modules)?**
- **Yes →** Use IaaS. PaaS abstracts hardware too much.
- **No →** PaaS is suitable. Proceed to Step 3.

**Step 3 — Is developer productivity more critical than customizability?**
- **Yes →** Choose PaaS.
- **No →** Choose IaaS.

**Step 4 — Is the workload bursty and unpredictable?**
- **Yes →** PaaS (auto-scaling wins).
- **No (steady-state workload) →** Reserved IaaS may be cheaper.

> This decision tree is a *sure-shot 7-mark answer* if the question asks: *"Justify why PaaS is preferred over IaaS for a startup launching a new mobile app backend."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PaaS Architecture — Layered Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete PaaS stack**, showing what the *Cloud Provider* manages (in blue) versus what the *Developer/Consumer* manages (in green). This visual is *high-yield* for KTU 7-mark questions that ask for a "PaaS architecture diagram".

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L7["LAYER 7: Application Code and Data"]
        L7A["Your Business Logic"]
        L7B["Your Configuration Files"]
    end

    subgraph L6["LAYER 6: Data Layer"]
        L6A["Managed RDBMS"]
        L6B["NoSQL Stores"]
    end

    subgraph L5["LAYER 5: Runtime and Language VMs"]
        L5A["Java JRE and JVM"]
        L5B["Python Interpreter"]
        L5C["Node.js Runtime"]
    end

    subgraph L4["LAYER 4: Middleware and App Servers"]
        L4A["Tomcat and JBoss"]
        L4B["Web Servers Nginx and Apache"]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3: Operating System Base"]
        L3A["Linux Ubuntu and RHEL"]
        L3B["Windows Server Base Image"]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2: Virtualization and Hypervisor"]
        L2A["KVM and ESXi"]
    end

    subgraph L1["LAYER 1: Physical Infrastructure"]
        L1A["Bare Metal Servers"]
        L1B["Storage Arrays SAN and NAS"]
        L1C["Network Switches and Cabling"]
    end

    L1A --> L2A
    L1B --> L2A
    L1C --> L2A
    L2A --> L3A
    L2A --> L3B
    L3A --> L4A
    L3A --> L4B
    L3B --> L4A
    L4A --> L5A
    L4A --> L5B
    L4B --> L5C
    L5A --> L6A
    L5B --> L6B
    L5C --> L6A
    L6A --> L7A
    L6B --> L7A
    L7A --> L7B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Starting from the bottom (Layer 1), each arrow indicates a *consumption dependency* — Layer N consumes Layer N-1. The developer's code (Layer 7) is the *only* layer where human business logic lives.

## 4.2 PaaS Deployment Workflow — Sequence Diagram

This diagram shows the **end-to-end application deployment flow** on a typical PaaS platform, from `git push` to a live URL serving production traffic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Developer Commits Code to Git Repo"] --> S2["PaaS Webhook Triggers Build Pipeline"]
    S2 --> S3["Build Server Compiles Source Code"]
    S3 --> S4["Unit Tests and Linting Run Automatically"]
    S4 --> S5["Container Image is Built Docker Image"]
    S5 --> S6["Image is Pushed to Internal Registry"]
    S6 --> S7["Orchestrator Schedules Container on Worker Node"]
    S7 --> S8["Load Balancer Adds New Instance to Rotation"]
    S8 --> S9["Health Checks Verify Instance is Healthy"]
    S9 --> S10["Live Traffic is Served at Production URL"]

    S10 -.Failure Detected.-> S11["Instance is Destroyed Automatically"]
    S11 --> S7
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The cycle from `S9` back to `S7` represents the **self-healing** property of PaaS — a hallmark of cloud-native design. Examiners reward students who explicitly mention "self-healing" and "auto-recovery" in deployment answers.

## 4.3 PaaS Service Sub-Types — Classification Tree

A taxonomic breakdown of the major PaaS sub-categories, useful for KTU 14-mark "Explain the types of PaaS" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PaaSRoot["PaaS Service Model"]

    PaaSRoot --> apPaaS["apPaaS: Application PaaS"]
    PaaSRoot --> aPaaS["aPaaS: Application Platform as a Service"]
    PaaSRoot --> iPaaS["iPaaS: Integration PaaS"]
    PaaSRoot --> dbPaaS["dbPaaS: Database PaaS"]
    PaaSRoot --> cPaaS["cPaaS: Communications PaaS"]
    PaaSRoot --> mPaaS["mPaaS: Mobile Backend PaaS"]

    apPaaS --> apEx["Examples: Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku"]
    aPaaS --> aEx["Examples: Salesforce Lightning, OutSystems, Mendix"]
    iPaaS --> iEx["Examples: MuleSoft, Dell Boomi, AWS AppFlow"]
    dbPaaS --> dbEx["Examples: AWS RDS, Azure SQL, Google BigQuery"]
    cPaaS --> cEx["Examples: Twilio, Vonage, Plivo"]
    mPaaS --> mEx["Examples: Firebase, AWS Amplify, Supabase"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU question papers, "Explain PaaS" frequently expects a breakdown into at least **4 sub-types**. The above taxonomy covers all 6 standard ones from the syllabus.

## 4.4 Shared Responsibility Model — Provider vs Consumer

This diagram is a *visual answer* to the question *"Who manages what in PaaS?"* — a recurring 7-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Resp["Shared Responsibility Model in PaaS"]

    subgraph ProviderSide["CLOUD PROVIDER OWNS"]
        P1["Physical Security and Data Center"]
        P2["Network Infrastructure and Firewalls"]
        P3["Hypervisor and Virtualization Layer"]
        P4["Host Operating System and Patching"]
        P5["Middleware and Runtime Updates"]
        P6["Database Engine Backups and Replication"]
    end

    subgraph ConsumerSide["CONSUMER OWNS"]
        C1["Application Code and Business Logic"]
        C2["Application Configuration and Env Vars"]
        C3["User Access Control and IAM Policies"]
        C4["Data Classification and Encryption Keys"]
        C5["Network Security Groups and VPC Setup in shared models"]
    end

    Resp --> ProviderSide
    Resp --> ConsumerSide
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often think PaaS transfers *all* responsibility to the provider. In reality, the consumer is still responsible for **application-level security, IAM, and data governance**. KTU examiners specifically test this misconception.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**
**"Define Platform as a Service (PaaS) and list any four of its key characteristics." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** PaaS is a cloud computing service model that provides consumers with a **pre-configured platform** — including operating system, programming language runtime, middleware, database, and development tools — over the internet, enabling them to develop, test, deploy, and manage applications without managing the underlying infrastructure.
>
> **Four Key Characteristics:**
> 1. **High-level abstraction** — Hides OS, server, storage, and network from the developer.
> 2. **Pre-integrated runtime environment** — Supports multiple programming languages (Java, Python, Node.js).
> 3. **Built-in scalability** — Auto-scales application instances based on load.
> 4. **Pay-per-use billing** — Cost is based on actual consumption (compute hours, requests, storage).

*(Word count discipline: 3-mark answers should be ~80–120 words.)*

### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
**"Differentiate between PaaS and SaaS in terms of control and user audience." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|
| **Control** | Consumer controls **application code and configuration**; provider controls OS, runtime, middleware. | Consumer has **no control** over code or platform; only uses the finished software. |
| **User Audience** | **Software developers and DevOps engineers** building applications. | **End users and business users** consuming the software directly. |
| **Example** | Google App Engine, Heroku | Gmail, Microsoft Office 365 |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice, 1 × 14 Marks)

> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE guidelines, students answer **one** of two 14-mark alternatives. Each alternative is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question B-A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Actual Pattern]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**"Explain the layered architecture of PaaS with a neat diagram. Clearly indicate which layers are managed by the cloud provider and which by the consumer."**

**Model Solution:**

The PaaS architecture can be conceptualized as a **seven-layer stack** built atop the physical infrastructure of the cloud data center.

**Layer 1 — Physical Infrastructure:** Bare-metal servers, storage arrays, and network switches form the foundation. **Managed by the Cloud Provider.** This layer is the CapEx-intensive part that PaaS abstracts away entirely.

**Layer 2 — Virtualization:** A hypervisor (KVM, VMware ESXi, or Hyper-V) abstracts the physical hardware into virtual machine pools, enabling multi-tenancy and elastic resource allocation. **Managed by the Cloud Provider.**

**Layer 3 — Operating System:** Linux distributions (Ubuntu, RHEL) or Windows Server base images run on the VMs. The OS is pre-hardened and patched by the provider. **Managed by the Cloud Provider.** *(This is the key differentiator from IaaS, where the consumer manages the OS.)*

**Layer 4 — Middleware and Application Servers:** Web servers (Apache, Nginx), application servers (Tomcat, JBoss, WebLogic), and message queues (RabbitMQ) are pre-configured. **Managed by the Cloud Provider.**

**Layer 5 — Runtime Environment:** Language VMs and interpreters (Java JRE, Python 3.x, Node.js, .NET CLR) are pre-installed and version-managed. **Managed by the Cloud Provider.**

**Layer 6 — Data Layer:** Managed database services (AWS RDS, Azure SQL, Google Cloud SQL) provide automated backups, replication, and patching. **Managed by the Cloud Provider.**

**Layer 7 — Application Code and Data:** This is the **only layer managed by the Consumer**. It contains the developer's business logic, custom configuration, and tenant-specific data.

*(A diagram similar to the one in Section 4.1 should accompany this answer for full marks.)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**"Discuss the benefits and limitations of PaaS. Under what circumstances would a developer choose PaaS over IaaS?"**

**Model Solution:**

**Benefits of PaaS:**
1. **Faster Development Lifecycle:** Pre-built components and CI/CD integration reduce time-to-market by 50–80%.
2. **Lower Operational Overhead:** Patching, backups, and scaling are automated, freeing the developer for innovation.
3. **Cost Efficiency:** Pay-per-use billing eliminates idle capacity; the worked example in Section 3.1 showed up to **87% cost savings** over self-managed IaaS.
4. **Built-in Scalability and High Availability:** Auto-scaling and multi-AZ deployments are platform defaults.
5. **Polyglot Support:** A single platform hosts applications in multiple languages without VM-level isolation.
6. **Integrated DevOps Tooling:** Built-in support for version control, monitoring, logging, and alerting.

**Limitations of PaaS:**
1. **Vendor Lock-in:** Migration to another PaaS provider is non-trivial due to proprietary APIs and runtimes.
2. **Reduced Control:** Developers cannot customize the OS kernel, hypervisor, or underlying hardware.
3. **Data Sovereignty Concerns:** Data resides in the provider's data centers, raising regulatory questions (GDPR, DPDP Act).
4. **Limited Suitability for Legacy Apps:** Monolithic legacy applications often require OS-level access that PaaS does not expose.
5. **Network Latency Variability:** Shared multi-tenant platforms may experience "noisy neighbor" effects.
6. **Security Boundary Ambiguity:** The shared responsibility model can lead to misconfigured IAM policies.

**When to Choose PaaS over IaaS:**
- When the application is a **standard web or mobile backend** without exotic hardware requirements.
- When the **development team is small** and lacks dedicated sysadmin/DevOps resources.
- When **time-to-market is critical** (startups, hackathons, MVPs).
- When the workload is **bursty or unpredictable** and benefits from auto-scaling.
- When the team wants to focus on **business logic, not infrastructure plumbing**.

---

### **Question B-B: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**"Compare and contrast IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS along the following dimensions: (i) what is provided, (ii) who manages the OS, (iii) who manages the application, (iv) flexibility, (v) ease of use, (vi) example, and (vii) target audience."**

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) What is provided** | Raw VMs, storage, networks | Runtime, middleware, dev tools, DB | Finished end-user application |
| **(ii) OS managed by** | **Consumer** | **Cloud Provider** | **Cloud Provider** |
| **(iii) Application managed by** | **Consumer** | **Consumer** (their own code) | **Cloud Provider** |
| **(iv) Flexibility** | **Highest** — full hardware/OS control | **Medium** — code-level control only | **Lowest** — usage-only access |
| **(v) Ease of use** | **Low** — requires sysadmin skills | **Medium** — requires developer skills | **Highest** — sign up and use |
| **(vi) Example** | AWS EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine | Google App Engine, Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce CRM |
| **(vii) Target audience** | System administrators, DevOps engineers, infrastructure architects | Application developers, software engineers | End users, business users, customers |

**Critical Insight:** As we move from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS, the **provider assumes more responsibility**, the **consumer's control decreases**, and **ease of use increases**. This is the *spectrum of abstraction* in cloud computing.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**"A startup is building a mobile food-delivery app backend. The app expects 10,000 users in month-1, growing to 1,000,000 users in month-12. The startup has 3 developers and no DevOps engineer. Recommend a suitable cloud service model with justification. Compute the percentage cost savings if PaaS reduces operations overhead from $2,000/month to $300/month, given a base IaaS cost of $360/month for compute + storage + network."**

**Model Solution:**

**Recommendation: Platform as a Service (PaaS)**

**Justification (Engineering Reasoning):**

1. **Workload is Bursty and Unpredictable:** A food-delivery app experiences time-of-day and seasonal spikes (lunch, dinner, festivals). PaaS auto-scaling is a perfect match.
2. **Small Team, No DevOps:** With only 3 developers, the team cannot manage OS patching, DB backups, and load balancer configuration. PaaS offloads this to the provider.
3. **Time-to-Market is Critical:** A startup racing against competitors needs to ship features weekly, not monthly. PaaS's pre-built CI/CD pipelines enable `git push` → production in minutes.
4. **Scalability Trajectory:** Growing from 10K → 1M users is a 100× growth. PaaS's horizontal auto-scaling handles this seamlessly without re-architecting.
5. **Cost Predictability:** PaaS's pay-per-request billing aligns cost with revenue, which is critical for a cash-constrained startup.

**Cost Savings Computation:**

**Step 1 — Total IaaS Cost (Self-Managed):**

$$
C_{\text{IaaS}} = C_{\text{base}} + C_{\text{ops,IaaS}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C_{\text{IaaS}} = 360 + 2000 = 2360
$$

So, **IaaS Total = $2,360 per month**.

**Step 2 — Total PaaS Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{PaaS}} = C_{\text{base}} + C_{\text{ops,PaaS}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
C_{\text{PaaS}} = 360 + 300 = 660
$$

So, **PaaS Total = $660 per month**.

**Step 3 — Compute the Monthly Savings:**

$$
S = C_{\text{IaaS}} - C_{\text{PaaS}} = 2360 - 660 = 1700
$$

So, **Monthly Savings = $1,700**.

**Step 4 — Compute the Percentage Savings:**

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \left( \frac{S}{C_{\text{IaaS}}} \right) \times 100
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \left( \frac{1700}{2360} \right) \times 100
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\text{Savings \%} = 0.7203 \times 100 = 72.03\%
$$

> **Result:** PaaS offers **72.03% cost savings** per month, freeing ~$20,400/year for product development and marketing.

**Final Recommendation:** The startup should adopt **PaaS** (e.g., Google App Engine or AWS Elastic Beanstalk) and can revisit the architecture if/when the team grows to 20+ developers and has a dedicated DevOps function.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PaaS with SaaS:** Writing "Gmail is a PaaS example" costs full marks. Gmail is SaaS. Correct: Gmail is SaaS, Google App Engine is PaaS.
> 2. **Skipping the "Managed By" column:** In 7-mark layered architecture questions, students who just list the layers without specifying *who manages* each layer lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw a diagram:** For 7-mark "Explain PaaS architecture" questions, a diagram is **compulsory**. Marks are explicitly reserved for it.
> 4. **Mixing up apPaaS vs aPaaS:** apPaaS = Application PaaS (Google App Engine); aPaaS = Application-Platform PaaS (Salesforce Lightning, low-code). These are different sub-types.
> 5. **Numerical answer without units:** "1700" loses marks; "$1,700/month" is correct.
> 6. **Not showing substitution in formulas:** For 14-mark numerical questions, KTU examiners require **every value substituted** into the formula, not just the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **PaaS Definition:** A cloud service model providing a *platform* (OS + runtime + middleware + DB + dev tools) over the internet. Consumer manages only *application code and data*.
- ✅ **The SPI Model:** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS — in order of *increasing abstraction* and *decreasing consumer control*.
- ✅ **Seven-Layer Stack:** Physical → Virtualization → OS → Middleware → Runtime → Data → Application. The consumer touches only Layer 7.
- ✅ **"Managed By" is the #1 Tested Concept:** Know exactly which layer is the provider's and which is the consumer's.
- ✅ **Six PaaS Sub-Types:** apPaaS, aPaaS, iPaaS, dbPaaS, cPaaS, mPaaS — each with one example vendor memorized.
- ✅ **PaaS Examples to Memorize:** Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku, Azure App Service, OpenShift, Cloud Foundry, Force.com.
- ✅ **TCO Formula:** $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{compute}} + C_{\text{storage}} + C_{\text{network}} + C_{\text{ops}}$.
- ✅ **Savings Formula:** $\text{Savings \%} = \left( 1 - \dfrac{C_{\text{PaaS}}}{C_{\text{total,IaaS}}} \right) \times 100$.
- ✅ **Key Properties:** Auto-scaling, self-healing, multi-tenancy, pay-per-use, polyglot support.
- ✅ **Limitations to Acknowledge:** Vendor lock-in, reduced control, data sovereignty, noisy-neighbor effects.
- ✅ **Self-Healing:** PaaS detects failed instances and automatically replaces them — a hallmark of cloud-native design.
- ✅ **FaaS is a Subset of PaaS:** AWS Lambda, Azure Functions — serverless functions are a specialized form of PaaS.
- ✅ **CI/CD Integration:** PaaS platforms integrate with Git for `git push` → production deployments.
- ✅ **PaaS vs Serverless:** PaaS = always-running app instances; FaaS = event-driven functions that scale to zero.
- ✅ **Shared Responsibility:** Provider handles infra, runtime, DB; Consumer handles code, IAM, data classification.
- ✅ **Decision Heuristic:** Choose PaaS for *standard web/mobile apps* with *small teams* and *bursty workloads*.

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"In PaaS, the developer builds the WHAT (business logic), and the provider builds the HOW (platform). Knowing who does what is the key to scoring full marks."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)** is a foundational cloud computing service model that delivers **virtualized computing resources** — including servers, storage, networking, and processing power — over the internet on a **pay-as-you-go (PAYG)** consumption basis. The cloud provider manages and maintains the underlying physical infrastructure, while the consumer retains control over operating systems, middleware, applications, and data.

According to the **NIST SP 800-145** definition, IaaS provides the consumer with the capability to **provision processing, storage, networks, and other fundamental computing resources** where the consumer is able to deploy and run arbitrary software (which can include operating systems and applications). The consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud infrastructure but has control over operating systems, storage, deployed applications, and possibly limited control of select networking components.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Context:** IaaS is the **lowest-level service model** in the cloud computing stack, sitting beneath **PaaS (Platform as a Service)** and **SaaS (Software as a Service)**. It is the most flexible and customizable cloud service category, making it the preferred choice for enterprises migrating legacy workloads.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Electricity Grid"

Imagine you want to power a factory. You have two choices:

| Approach | Real-World Analogy | Cloud Equivalent |
|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Build Your Own Power Plant** | Buy diesel generators, maintain fuel, repair parts | On-Premise Data Center |
| **Tap the Electricity Grid** | Pay the electricity board for units consumed, they maintain power lines and substations | **IaaS** |

When you use IaaS, you are essentially saying:

> *"I don't want to buy, house, and maintain physical servers. Instead, I'll rent virtual servers from a cloud provider who owns the physical hardware. I just pay for what I consume (CPU hours, GB of storage, GB of network egress)."*

The **cloud provider** acts like an **electricity board**:
- They own the **power plant** (data centers, physical servers, networking gear)
- They maintain the **distribution lines** (virtual networks, storage fabrics)
- They meter your **consumption** (compute hours, data transfer, storage I/O)
- You pay a **tariff** for every unit consumed

You (the consumer) still control:
- Which **appliance** (OS) to run
- Which **factory process** (application) to deploy
- How much **electricity** (resources) to consume at any given time

---

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of IaaS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These **six defining characteristics** are commonly tested in KTU examinations as 3-mark short-answer questions.

1. **Resource Pooling:** Physical and virtual resources are dynamically assigned and reassigned according to consumer demand (multi-tenant model).
2. **Broad Network Access:** Services are accessed over standard networking protocols (HTTP, SSH, RDP) from any device — laptop, tablet, mobile.
3. **Rapid Elasticity:** Resources can be **scaled out** (horizontally) or **scaled up** (vertically) automatically in minutes, giving the illusion of infinite capacity.
4. **Measured Service:** Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at an appropriate level of abstraction (CPU-seconds, GB-months, I/O operations).
5. **Self-Service Provisioning:** Consumers can provision compute, storage, and network resources **on-demand** without human interaction with the service provider.
6. **High Availability & Fault Tolerance:** Most IaaS providers offer **99.9% to 99.99%** SLAs with geographically distributed data centers for disaster recovery.

---

## 1.4 Essential Terminology Callout

> [!NOTE]
> **Glossary of IaaS Vocabulary (High-Yield for KTU Exams):**
> - **Virtual Machine (VM):** A software emulation of a physical computer with its own CPU, RAM, disk, and network.
> - **Hypervisor:** The software layer that creates and runs VMs (e.g., VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM, Xen).
> - **Instance:** A running VM on a cloud platform (AWS calls them "EC2 instances", Azure calls them "Azure VMs").
> - **Tenant:** A single customer or organization using a shared cloud infrastructure.
> - **Region & Availability Zone (AZ):** A geographic area containing multiple isolated data centers.
> - **Elastic IP / Public IP:** A static public IPv4 address assigned to a cloud resource.
> - **Snapshot:** A point-in-time copy of a VM's disk used for backup/recovery.
> - **Image:** A pre-configured template (OS + software) used to launch new instances.
> - **API (Application Programming Interface):** The programmatic interface (REST/HTTP) used to interact with IaaS resources.
> - **Egress / Ingress:** Data transferred **out of** (egress) or **into** (ingress) the cloud.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **industry-standard metrics** are used universally in IaaS billing and are commonly referenced in KTU questions:

> - **1 vCPU** = 1 virtual CPU core (typically backed by 1 hardware thread, though ratios vary)
> - **1 GB RAM** = $2^{30}$ bytes = **1,073,741,824 bytes** (binary GiB convention used by cloud providers)
> - **1 TB-month storage** = 1 terabyte of data stored for 1 month (30 days)
> - **1 GB egress** = 1 GiB of data transferred **out** to the public internet
> - **99.9% SLA** = Maximum downtime of **8.77 hours/year** (≈43.83 minutes/month)
> - **99.99% SLA** = Maximum downtime of **52.6 minutes/year** (≈4.38 minutes/month)
> - **99.999% SLA ("Five Nines")** = Maximum downtime of **5.26 minutes/year** (≈26.3 seconds/month)

The **maximum allowed downtime per year** is calculated as:

$$D_{max} = (1 - SLA) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \quad \text{[minutes]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always remember to convert units carefully. **Cloud providers use binary multiples (GiB = 1024³ bytes) for storage billing**, while marketing materials often use decimal (TB = 1000⁴ bytes). This distinction is a common KTU question trap.
